According to the [[Tang Code|Tang Rulings and Ordinances]], those who erected shrines or monuments without permission were subject to a penalty of one year of imprisonment.<ref>The [[Tang Code]], Volume 11 (The Law of Office)</ref>。The Tang Dynasty called it "the difficulty of building monuments and shrines on the first day.
In the [[Ming dynasty|Ming]], peasants in [[Tengzhou|Tengzhou]] built a shrine to commemorate [[:zh:趙邦清|Zhao Bangqing]], who was appointed to Yanjing for his clean politics. The shrine was built to commemorate him.
During the reign of the [[Tianqi Emperor]] of the Ming Dynasty, the eunuch [[Wei Zhongxian]], ruled the world by conducting his own politics, and those who flattered him or feared his authority were given a He made a living shrine for him. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing (1627), Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote: "Confucius wrote the Spring and Autumn, and Chung-Hsien wrote the Essentials. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote that Confucius had written the "Spring and Autumn" and Chung-Hsien had written the "Essentials. In April of the 7th year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, [[Yuan Chonghuan]] and {{ill|Yan Mingtai|zh|閻鳴泰}} of the [[Ministry of War (imperial China)|Ministry of War]] made an appeal to the government to praise the merits and demerits of Wei Zhongxian and to establish a living shrine for him in both Qiantu.<ref>"Ming Xi Zong Jizhu": "in April of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianqi, the governor of Thistle Liaoning, Yan Mingtai, and the governor of Fuxing Yuan Chong Huan, praised the merits of Wei Zhongxian, and requested the construction of a shrine in front of Ning, giving the name Mao De." The government of the Liaoning Province has been inscribed in gold and stone, and there is no room for extra duties. The minister's single-mindedness in patrolling, strict warning of the battalion generals, do not dare to greed cowardly selfishness, do not dare to give away the hidden string, change the virtual into the real, into the real, easy timid into brave, to have today. Sustainability ancient internal minister who has the right!" ("Ming Xi Zong seven years of the Imperial Court of the Department of the Experiment" Tianqi six years in October)</ref>。After that, Wei Zhongxian's raw shrine was "all over the world", "the cost of each shrine was at most hundreds of thousands, at least tens of thousands", "stolen the people's fortune, invaded the public safe, and was cut down. There are countless trees. " When Huang Yun Yasushi made a statue and greeted the statue, he said, "Goto Sansho," and "leaded the general of Bunbu, lined up under the stairs, and worshiped as the beginning."
[[Gu Yanwu|Gu Yanwu]] lamented, "In this generation, there is no official who does not build a living shrine. And within a few years of leaving office, he would destroy the statues and change their masters. He said.<ref>《Nichi Chi Roku》 vol. 23 (Sheng Shi), p. 644.</ref>。
=== Shen shrines in Japan === Shosha, the worship of one's own spirit, was performed in order to obtain a long life or to become a god after death. It is said to have originated from the legend of [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi-no-mikoto]], who enshrined his [[Mitama|Chikishin]] and [[Mitama|Kozoshin]] in [[Mount Miwa|Sanmorozan]] ([[Mount Miwa]]).
The earliest documented case of shosha enshrining one's own spirit is that of Haruhiko Matsuki ([[824|824]]), a [[Shinkan (official)|priest]] of the outer shrine of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]] in [[923|923]] of the [[Heian period]]. - [[924|924 years]]), enshrined and enshrined his own spirit on a stone in Obe, Ise [[Watarai District, Mie|Tokai County]]. In the [[Edo period]], [[Matsudaira Sadanobu|Matsudaira Sadanobu]] established his own nascent shrine in [[1797|1797]] at Shirakawa Castle in Oshu. The number of shosshiki increased during the Edo period, probably due to the influence of Chinese thought. In the [[Edo period]], [[Yamazaki Ansai|Yakusai Yamazaki]] devised a [[Festival]] to enshrine his own spirit, referring to the rituals of [[Confucianism]]. The Shusei ritual was established in 1671 at the Tarukarei Shrine in his residence in Kyoto. After this, the [[Shinto]] family and the [[Kokugaku]] scholars of the Hirata school enshrined their own spirits in their own unique rituals.
Kato Genchi ([[1873|1873]] - [[1965|1965]]) is famous for his research on Shusei. According to Kato, there are 670 known Shuso enshrines, and the newest one is that of Nishi Tadayoshi (father of Nishi Yoshikazu, who was enshrined in Urakawa-cho, Hidaka Province, Hokkaido in August [[1931|1931]]). This is the one of [[Emperor Meiji]]
[[Emperor Meiji]] was enshrined at Ishinomaki Port, Miyagi Prefecture in [[1876|1876]] ([[Meiji (era)|明治]]9), and at Yahiko Shrine in Ono Village, [[Kamiina District, Nagano|Kamiina County]] in [[1893|1893]] Yahiko Shrine in Ono Village. Emperor Meiji and [[Empress Shōken|Emperor Shōken]] were enshrined in two places in [[1887|1887]] and [[1904|1904]], and [[Emperor Taishō|Emperor Taisho]] was enshrined in [[1911|1911]] in [[1944]]. [[Emperor Taishō|Taisho]] was enshrined at two locations in [[Hokkaido]], [[Hidaka, Hokkaidō|Hidaka]], [[Kadobetsu]], [[Hokkaido]], [[Hidaka, Hokkaidō|Hidaka]], [[Empress Teimei|Emperor Teimei]] was enshrined in [[1913|1913]], [[Taishō|Taisho]], [[Hirose-cho, Hiroshima Prefecture]], and [[Yasuhito, Prince Chichibu|Prince Chichibu]] and [[Nobuhito, Prince Takamatsu]] were established in [[1924|1924]] (Taisho 13) in [[Himeji|Himeji City]], Doshin-cho.
The birthplace of [[Emperor Hirohito|Showa]] was established in [[1923|1923]] (Taisho 12) in Yoshitsune, Honbetsu Village, [[Tokachi District, Hokkaido|Tokachi]], Hokkaido, and the birthplace of Emperor Showa and [[Empress Kōjun|Empress Kōjun]] was established in [[1928|1928]] (Showa 3) in Hirose-cho, Hiroshima City.
== References == <references />
== See Also ==
* [[Tamaya]] * {{ill|Okuto Castle|ja|奥都城}}

The government of the Liaoning Province has been inscribed in gold and stone, and there is no room for extra duties. The minister's single-mindedness in patrolling, strict warning of the battalion generals, do not dare to greed cowardly selfishness, do not dare to give away the hidden string, change the virtual into the real, into the real, easy timid into brave, to have today. Sustainability ancient internal minister who has the right!" ("Ming Xi Zong seven years of the Imperial Court of the Department of the Experiment" Tianqi six years in October)</ref>。After that, Wei Zhongxian's raw shrine was "all over the world", "the cost of each shrine was at most hundreds of thousands, at least tens of thousands", "stolen the people's fortune, invaded the public safe, and was cut down. There are countless trees. " When Huang Yun Yasushi made a statue and greeted the statue, he said, "Goto Sansho," and "leaded the general of Bunbu, lined up under the stairs, and worshiped as the beginning."
[[Gu Yanwu|Gu Yanwu]] lamented, "In this generation, there is no official who does not build a living shrine. And within a few years of leaving office, he would destroy the statues and change their masters. He said.<ref>《Nichi Chi Roku》 vol. 23 (Sheng Shi), p. 644.</ref>。

In the [[Edo period]], [[Matsudaira Sadanobu|Matsudaira Sadanobu]] established his own nascent shrine in [[1797|1797]] at Shirakawa Castle in Oshu. The number of shosshiki increased during the Edo period, probably due to the influence of Chinese thought. In the [[Edo period]], [[Yamazaki Ansai|Yakusai Yamazaki]] devised a [[Festival]] to enshrine his own spirit, referring to the rituals of [[Confucianism]]. Kato Genchi ([[1873|1873]] - [[1965|1965]]) is famous for his research on Shusei. According to Kato, there are 670 known Shuso enshrines, and the newest one is that of Nishi Tadayoshi (father of Nishi Yoshikazu, who was enshrined in Urakawa-cho, Hidaka Province, Hokkaido in August [[1931|1931]]). This is the one of [[Emperor Meiji]]
[[Emperor Meiji]] was enshrined at Ishinomaki Port, Miyagi Prefecture in [[1876|1876]] ([[Meiji (era)|明治]]9), and at Yahiko Shrine in Ono Village, [[Kamiina District, Nagano|Kamiina County]] in [[1893|1893]] Yahiko Shrine in Ono Village. Emperor Meiji and [[Empress Shōken|Emperor Shōken]] were enshrined in two places in [[1887|1887]] and [[1904|1904]], and [[Emperor Taishō|Emperor Taisho]] was enshrined in [[1911|1911]] in [[1944]]. [[Emperor Taishō|Taisho]] was enshrined at two locations in [[Hokkaido]]: [[Hidaka, Hokkaidō|Hidaka]] and, Monbetsu village, [[Empress Teimei|Emperor Teimei]] was enshrined in [[1913|1913]], [[Taishō|Taisho]], Hirose-cho, [[Hiroshima Prefecture]], and [[Yasuhito, Prince Chichibu|Prince Chichibu]] and [[Nobuhito, Prince Takamatsu]] were established in [[1924|1924]] (Taisho 13) in [[Himeji|Himeji City]], Doshin-cho.
The birthplace of [[Emperor Hirohito|Showa]] was established in [[1923|1923]] (Taisho 12) in Yoshitsune, Honbetsu Village, [[Tokachi District, Hokkaido|Tokachi]], Hokkaido, and the birthplace of Emperor Showa and [[Empress Kōjun|Empress Kōjun]] was established in [[1928|1928]] (Showa 3) in Hirose-cho, Hiroshima City.

{{Short description|Shrine to a living person in Shinto and Chinese folk religion}} {{Draft topics|biography|east-asia}} {{AfC topic|geo}} {{AfC submission|||ts=20220218193823|u=MaitreyaVaruna|ns=118}} {{AfC submission|t||ts=20220218132857|u=SiliconProphet|ns=118|demo=}}
<!-- Note: The following pages were redirects to [[Sheng_Shrine]] before draftification: *[[Seishi (Shinto)]] --> A {{ill|Sheng Shrine|ja|生祀|zh|生祠}} is a [[shrine]] for a living person. There are two types of enshrinement: enshrining the spirit of a living person who has made a significant contribution and enshrining one's own spirit. [[File:Hiraga_Gennai_seishi01s2040.jpg|thumb|200x200px|[[Hiraga Gennai|Hiraga Gennai]]'s birth shrine in [[Hiroshima Prefecture|Hiroshima Prefecture]], [[Fukuyama, Hiroshima|Fukuyama City]], Tomomachi.]]
== Overview ==
=== Sheng shrines in China === When [[Han Dynasty#Western Han|Former Han]]'s Reunobu was the prime minister of [[Yan Kingdom (Han dynasty)|Yan]], he built his shrine between Yan and [[Qi (state)|Qi]] and called it the "Reun Gong Shrine". Also, when [[:zh:石慶|Shiqing]] was the prime minister of Qi, the Qi people built the "Shiqing Shrine". This is the beginning of the birth shrine.<ref>[[Zhao Yi]], "The New New Ancestral Hall", Volume 32, "Ancestral Hall", "This is the beginning of the establishment of an ancestral hall.</ref>
In the [[Tang dynasty]], there were certain restrictions on the construction of monuments and shrines by officials in office. According to the [[Tang Code|Tang Rulings and Ordinances]], those who erected shrines or monuments without permission were subject to a penalty of one year of imprisonment.<ref>The [[Tang Code]], Volume 11 (The Law of Office)</ref>。The Tang Dynasty called it "the difficulty of building monuments and shrines on the first day.
In the [[Ming dynasty|Ming]], peasants in [[Tengzhou|Tengzhou]] built a shrine to commemorate [[:zh:趙邦清|Zhao Bangqing]], who was appointed to Yanjing for his clean politics. The shrine was built to commemorate him.
During the reign of the [[Tianqi Emperor]] of the Ming Dynasty, the eunuch [[Wei Zhongxian]], ruled the world by conducting his own politics, and those who flattered him or feared his authority were given a He made a living shrine for him. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing (1627), Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote: "Confucius wrote the Spring and Autumn, and Chung-Hsien wrote the Essentials. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote that Confucius had written the "Spring and Autumn" and Chung-Hsien had written the "Essentials. In April of the 7th year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, [[Yuan Chonghuan]] and {{ill|Yan Mingtai|zh|閻鳴泰}} of the [[Ministry of War (imperial China)|Ministry of War]] made an appeal to the government to praise the merits and demerits of Wei Zhongxian and to establish a living shrine for him in both Qiantu.<ref>"Ming Xi Zong Jizhu": "in April of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianqi, the governor of Thistle Liaoning, Yan Mingtai, and the governor of Fuxing Yuan Chong Huan, praised the merits of Wei Zhongxian, and requested the construction of a shrine in front of Ning, giving the name Mao De." The government of the Liaoning Province has been inscribed in gold and stone, and there is no room for extra duties. The minister's single-mindedness in patrolling, strict warning of the battalion generals, do not dare to greed cowardly selfishness, do not dare to give away the hidden string, change the virtual into the real, into the real, easy timid into brave, to have today. Sustainability ancient internal minister who has the right!" ("Ming Xi Zong seven years of the Imperial Court of the Department of the Experiment" Tianqi six years in October)</ref>。After that, Wei Zhongxian's raw shrine was "all over the world", "the cost of each shrine was at most hundreds of thousands, at least tens of thousands", "stolen the people's fortune, invaded the public safe, and was cut down. There are countless trees. " When Huang Yun Yasushi made a statue and greeted the statue, he said, "Goto Sansho," and "leaded the general of Bunbu, lined up under the stairs, and worshiped as the beginning."
[[Gu Yanwu|Gu Yanwu]] lamented, "In this generation, there is no official who does not build a living shrine. And within a few years of leaving office, he would destroy the statues and change their masters. He said.<ref>《Nichi Chi Roku》 vol. 23 (Sheng Shi), p. 644.</ref>。
=== Shen shrines in Japan === Shosha, the worship of one's own spirit, was performed in order to obtain a long life or to become a god after death. It is said to have originated from the legend of [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi-no-mikoto]], who enshrined his [[Mitama|Chikishin]] and [[Mitama|Kozoshin]] in [[Mount Miwa]].
The earliest documented case of shosha enshrining one's own spirit is that of Haruhiko Matsuki ([[824|824]]), a [[Shinkan (official)|priest]] of the outer shrine of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]] in [[923|923]] of the [[Heian period]]. - [[924|924 years]]), enshrined and enshrined his own spirit on a stone in Obe, Ise [[Watarai District, Mie|Tokai County]]. In the [[Edo period]], [[Matsudaira Sadanobu|Matsudaira Sadanobu]] established his own nascent shrine in [[1797|1797]] at Shirakawa Castle in Oshu. The number of shosshiki increased during the Edo period, probably due to the influence of Chinese thought. In the [[Edo period]], [[Yamazaki Ansai|Yakusai Yamazaki]] devised a [[Festival]] to enshrine his own spirit, referring to the rituals of [[Confucianism]]. The Shusei ritual was established in 1671 at the Tarukarei Shrine in his residence in Kyoto. After this, the [[Shinto]] family and the [[Kokugaku]] scholars of the Hirata school enshrined their own spirits in their own unique rituals.
Kato Genchi ([[1873|1873]] - [[1965|1965]]) is famous for his research on Shusei. According to Kato, there are 670 known Shuso enshrines, and the newest one is that of Nishi Tadayoshi (father of Nishi Yoshikazu, who was enshrined in Urakawa-cho, Hidaka Province, Hokkaido in August [[1931|1931]]). This is the one of [[Emperor Meiji]]
[[Emperor Meiji]] was enshrined at Ishinomaki Port, Miyagi Prefecture in [[1876|1876]] ([[Meiji (era)|明治]]9), and at Yahiko Shrine in Ono Village, [[Kamiina District, Nagano|Kamiina County]] in [[1893|1893]] Yahiko Shrine in Ono Village. Emperor Meiji and [[Empress Shōken|Emperor Shōken]] were enshrined in two places in [[1887|1887]] and [[1904|1904]], and [[Emperor Taishō|Emperor Taisho]] was enshrined in [[1911|1911]] in [[1944]]. [[Emperor Taishō|Taisho]] was enshrined at two locations in [[Hokkaido]]: [[Hidaka, Hokkaidō|Hidaka]] and, Monbetsu village, [[Empress Teimei|Emperor Teimei]] was enshrined in [[1913|1913]], [[Taishō|Taisho]], Hirose-cho, [[Hiroshima Prefecture]], and [[Yasuhito, Prince Chichibu|Prince Chichibu]] and [[Nobuhito, Prince Takamatsu]] were established in [[1924|1924]] (Taisho 13) in [[Himeji|Himeji City]], Doshin-cho.
The birthplace of [[Emperor Hirohito|Showa]] was established in [[1923|1923]] (Taisho 12) in Yoshitsune, Honbetsu Village, [[Tokachi District, Hokkaido|Tokachi]], Hokkaido, and the birthplace of Emperor Showa and [[Empress Kōjun|Empress Kōjun]] was established in [[1928|1928]] (Showa 3) in Hirose-cho, Hiroshima City.

<!-- Note: The following pages were redirects to [[Sheng_Shrine]] before draftification: *[[Seishi (Shinto)]] --> A {{ill|Sheng Shrine|ja|生祀|zh|生祠}} is a [[shrine]] for a living person. There are two types of enshrinement: enshrining the spirit of a living person who has made a significant contribution and enshrining one's own spirit. [[File:Hiraga_Gennai_seishi01s2040.jpg|thumb|200x200px|[[Hiraga Gennai|Hiraga Gennai]]'s birth shrine in [[Hiroshima Prefecture|Hiroshima Prefecture]], [[Fukuyama, Hiroshima|Fukuyama City]], Tomomachi.]]
== Overview ==
=== Sheng shrines in China === When [[Han Dynasty#Western Han|Former Han]]'s Reunobu was the prime minister of [[Yan Kingdom (Han dynasty)|Yan]], he built his shrine between Yan and [[Qi (state)|Qi]] and called it the "Reun Gong Shrine". Also, when [[:zh:石慶|Shiqing]] was the prime minister of Qi, the Qi people built the "Shiqing Shrine". This is the beginning of the birth shrine.<ref>[[Zhao Yi]], "The New New Ancestral Hall", Volume 32, "Ancestral Hall", "This is the beginning of the establishment of an ancestral hall.</ref>
In the [[Tang dynasty]], there were certain restrictions on the construction of monuments and shrines by officials in office. According to the [[Tang Code|Tang Rulings and Ordinances]], those who erected shrines or monuments without permission were subject to a penalty of one year of imprisonment.<ref>The [[Tang Code]], Volume 11 (The Law of Office)</ref>The Tang Dynasty called it "the difficulty of building monuments and shrines on the first day.
In the [[Ming dynasty|Ming]], peasants in [[Tengzhou|Tengzhou]] built a shrine to commemorate [[:zh:趙邦清|Zhao Bangqing]], who was appointed to Yanjing for his clean politics. The shrine was built to commemorate him.
During the reign of the [[Tianqi Emperor]] of the Ming Dynasty, the eunuch [[Wei Zhongxian]], ruled the world by conducting his own politics, and those who flattered him or feared his authority were given a He made a living shrine for him. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing (1627), Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote: "Confucius wrote the Spring and Autumn, and Chung-Hsien wrote the Essentials. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote that Confucius had written the "Spring and Autumn" and Chung-Hsien had written the "Essentials. In April of the 7th year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, [[Yuan Chonghuan]] and {{ill|Yan Mingtai|zh|閻鳴泰}} of the [[Ministry of War (imperial China)|Ministry of War]] made an appeal to the government to praise the merits and demerits of Wei Zhongxian and to establish a living shrine for him in both Qiantu.<ref>"Ming Xi Zong Jizhu": "in April of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianqi, the governor of Thistle Liaoning, Yan Mingtai, and the governor of Fuxing Yuan Chong Huan, praised the merits of Wei Zhongxian, and requested the construction of a shrine in front of Ning, giving the name Mao De." The government of the Liaoning Province has been inscribed in gold and stone, and there is no room for extra duties. The minister's single-mindedness in patrolling, strict warning of the battalion generals, do not dare to greed cowardly selfishness, do not dare to give away the hidden string, change the virtual into the real, into the real, easy timid into brave, to have today. Sustainability ancient internal minister who has the right!" ("Ming Xi Zong seven years of the Imperial Court of the Department of the Experiment" Tianqi six years in October)</ref>After that, Wei Zhongxian's raw shrine was "all over the world", "the cost of each shrine was at most hundreds of thousands, at least tens of thousands", "stolen the people's fortune, invaded the public safe, and was cut down. There are countless trees. " When Huang Yun Yasushi made a statue and greeted the statue, he said, "Goto Sansho," and "leaded the general of Bunbu, lined up under the stairs, and worshiped as the beginning."
[[Gu Yanwu|Gu Yanwu]] lamented, "In this generation, there is no official who does not build a living shrine. And within a few years of leaving office, he would destroy the statues and change their masters. He said.<ref>《Nichi Chi Roku》 vol. 23 (Sheng Shi), p. 644.</ref>
=== Sheng shrines in Japan === Shosha, the worship of one's own spirit, was performed in order to obtain a long life or to become a god after death. It is said to have originated from the legend of [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi-no-mikoto]], who enshrined his [[Mitama|Chikishin]] and [[Mitama|Kozoshin]] in [[Mount Miwa]].
The earliest documented case of shosha enshrining one's own spirit is that of Haruhiko Matsuki ([[824|824]]), a [[Shinkan (official)|priest]] of the outer shrine of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]] in [[923|923]] of the [[Heian period]]. - [[924|924 years]]), enshrined and enshrined his own spirit on a stone in Obe, Ise [[Watarai District, Mie|Tokai County]]. In the [[Edo period]], [[Matsudaira Sadanobu|Matsudaira Sadanobu]] established his own nascent shrine in [[1797|1797]] at Shirakawa Castle in Oshu. The number of shosshiki increased during the Edo period, probably due to the influence of Chinese thought. In the [[Edo period]], [[Yamazaki Ansai|Yakusai Yamazaki]] devised a [[Festival]] to enshrine his own spirit, referring to the rituals of [[Confucianism]]. The Shusei ritual was established in 1671 at the Tarukarei Shrine in his residence in Kyoto. After this, the [[Shinto]] family and the [[Kokugaku]] scholars of the Hirata school enshrined their own spirits in their own unique rituals.
Kato Genchi ([[1873|1873]] - [[1965|1965]]) is famous for his research on Shusei. According to Kato, there are 670 known Shuso enshrines, and the newest one is that of Nishi Tadayoshi (father of Nishi Yoshikazu, who was enshrined in Urakawa-cho, Hidaka Province, Hokkaido in August [[1931|1931]]). This is the one of [[Emperor Meiji]]
[[Emperor Meiji]] was enshrined at Ishinomaki Port, Miyagi Prefecture in [[1876|1876]] ([[Meiji (era)|明治]]9), and at Yahiko Shrine in Ono Village, [[Kamiina District, Nagano|Kamiina County]] in [[1893|1893]] Yahiko Shrine in Ono Village. Emperor Meiji and [[Empress Shōken|Emperor Shōken]] were enshrined in two places in [[1887|1887]] and [[1904|1904]], and [[Emperor Taishō|Emperor Taisho]] was enshrined in [[1911|1911]] in [[1944]]. [[Emperor Taishō|Taisho]] was enshrined at two locations in [[Hokkaido]]: [[Hidaka, Hokkaidō|Hidaka]] and, Monbetsu village, [[Empress Teimei|Emperor Teimei]] was enshrined in [[1913|1913]], [[Taishō|Taisho]], Hirose-cho, [[Hiroshima Prefecture]], and [[Yasuhito, Prince Chichibu|Prince Chichibu]] and [[Nobuhito, Prince Takamatsu]] were established in [[1924|1924]] (Taisho 13) in [[Himeji|Himeji City]], Doshin-cho.
The birthplace of [[Emperor Hirohito|Showa]] was established in [[1923|1923]] (Taisho 12) in Yoshitsune, Honbetsu Village, [[Tokachi District, Hokkaido|Tokachi]], Hokkaido, and the birthplace of Emperor Showa and [[Empress Kōjun|Empress Kōjun]] was established in [[1928|1928]] (Showa 3) in Hirose-cho, Hiroshima City.

The minister's single-mindedness in patrolling, strict warning of the battalion generals, do not dare to greed cowardly selfishness, do not dare to give away the hidden string, change the virtual into the real, into the real, easy timid into brave, to have today. Sustainability ancient internal minister who has the right!" ("Ming Xi Zong seven years of the Imperial Court of the Department of the Experiment" Tianqi six years in October)</ref>After that, Wei Zhongxian's raw shrine was "all over the world", "the cost of each shrine was at most hundreds of thousands, at least tens of thousands", "stolen the people's fortune, invaded the public safe, and was cut down. There are countless trees. " When Huang Yun Yasushi made a statue and greeted the statue, he said, "Goto Sansho," and "leaded the general of Bunbu, lined up under the stairs, and worshiped as the beginning."
[[Gu Yanwu|Gu Yanwu]] lamented, "In this generation, there is no official who does not build a living shrine. And within a few years of leaving office, he would destroy the statues and change their masters. He said.<ref>《Nichi Chi Roku》 vol. 23 (Sheng Shi), p. 644.</ref>
=== Sheng shrines in Japan === Shosha, the worship of one's own spirit, was performed in order to obtain a long life or to become a god after death. It is said to have originated from the legend of [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi-no-mikoto]], who enshrined his [[Mitama|Chikishin]] and [[Mitama|Kozoshin]] in [[Mount Miwa]].
The earliest documented case of shosha enshrining one's own spirit is that of Haruhiko Matsuki ([[824|824]]), a [[Shinkan (official)|priest]] of the outer shrine of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]] in [[923|923]] of the [[Heian period]]. - [[924|924 years]]), enshrined and enshrined his own spirit on a stone in Obe, Ise [[Watarai District, Mie|Tokai County]]. In the [[Edo period]], [[Matsudaira Sadanobu|Matsudaira Sadanobu]] established his own nascent shrine in [[1797|1797]] at Shirakawa Castle in Oshu. The number of shosshiki increased during the Edo period, probably due to the influence of Chinese thought. In the [[Edo period]], [[Yamazaki Ansai|Yakusai Yamazaki]] devised a [[Festival]] to enshrine his own spirit, referring to the rituals of [[Confucianism]]. The Shusei ritual was established in 1671 at the Tarukarei Shrine in his residence in Kyoto. After this, the [[Shinto]] family and the [[Kokugaku]] scholars of the Hirata school enshrined their own spirits in their own unique rituals.
Kato Genchi ([[1873|1873]] - [[1965|1965]]) is famous for his research on Shusei. According to Kato, there are 670 known Shuso enshrines, and the newest one is that of Nishi Tadayoshi (father of Nishi Yoshikazu, who was enshrined in Urakawa-cho, Hidaka Province, Hokkaido in August [[1931|1931]]). This is the one of [[Emperor Meiji]]
[[Emperor Meiji]] was enshrined at Ishinomaki Port, Miyagi Prefecture in [[1876|1876]] ([[Meiji (era)|Meiji]] 9), and at Yahiko Shrine in Ono Village, [[Kamiina District, Nagano|Kamiina County]] in [[1893|1893]] Yahiko Shrine in Ono Village. Emperor Meiji and [[Empress Shōken|Emperor Shōken]] were enshrined in two places in [[1887|1887]] and [[1904|1904]], and [[Emperor Taishō|Emperor Taisho]] was enshrined in [[1911|1911]] in [[1944]]. [[Emperor Taishō|Taisho]] was enshrined at two locations in [[Hokkaido]]: [[Hidaka, Hokkaidō|Hidaka]] and, Monbetsu village, [[Empress Teimei|Emperor Teimei]] was enshrined in [[1913|1913]], [[Taishō|Taisho]], Hirose-cho, [[Hiroshima Prefecture]], and [[Yasuhito, Prince Chichibu|Prince Chichibu]] and [[Nobuhito, Prince Takamatsu]] were established in [[1924|1924]] (Taisho 13) in [[Himeji|Himeji City]], Doshin-cho.
The birthplace of [[Emperor Hirohito|Showa]] was established in [[1923|1923]] (Taisho 12) in Yoshitsune, Honbetsu Village, [[Tokachi District, Hokkaido|Tokachi]], Hokkaido, and the birthplace of Emperor Showa and [[Empress Kōjun|Empress Kōjun]] was established in [[1928|1928]] (Showa 3) in Hirose-cho, Hiroshima City.

In the [[Tang dynasty]], there were certain restrictions on the construction of monuments and shrines by officials in office. According to the [[Tang Code|Tang Rulings and Ordinances]], those who erected shrines or monuments without permission were subject to a penalty of one year of imprisonment.<ref>The [[Tang Code]], Volume 11 (The Law of Office)</ref>The Tang Dynasty called it "the difficulty of building monuments and shrines on the first day.
In the [[Ming dynasty|Ming]], peasants in [[Tengzhou|Tengzhou]] built a shrine to commemorate {{ill|Zhao Bangqing|zh|趙邦清}}, who was appointed to Yanjing for his clean politics. The shrine was built to commemorate him.

There are countless trees. " When Huang Yun Yasushi made a statue and greeted the statue, he said, "Goto Sansho," and "leaded the general of Bunbu, lined up under the stairs, and worshiped as the beginning."

This is the one of [[Emperor Meiji]]

Emperor Meiji and [[Empress Shōken|Emperor Shōken]] were enshrined in two places in [[1887|1887]] and [[1904|1904]], and [[Emperor Taishō|Emperor Taisho]] was enshrined in [[1911|1911]] in [[1944]]. [[Emperor Taishō|Taisho]] was enshrined at two locations in [[Hokkaido]]: [[Hidaka, Hokkaidō|Hidaka]] and, Monbetsu village, [[Empress Teimei|Emperor Teimei]] was enshrined in [[1913|1913]], [[Taishō|Taisho]], Hirose-cho, [[Hiroshima Prefecture]], and [[Yasuhito, Prince Chichibu|Prince Chichibu]] and [[Nobuhito, Prince Takamatsu]] were established in [[1924|1924]] in [[Himeji|Himeji City]], Doshin-cho.
The birthplace of [[Emperor Hirohito|Showa]] was established in [[1923|1923]] in Yoshitsune, Honbetsu Village, [[Tokachi District, Hokkaido|Tokachi]], Hokkaido, and the birthplace of Emperor Showa and [[Empress Kōjun|Empress Kōjun]] was established in [[1928|1928]] in Hirose-cho, Hiroshima City.

{{Short description|Shrine to a living person in Shinto and Chinese folk religion}} {{Draft topics|biography|east-asia}} {{AfC topic|geo}}
<!-- Note: The following pages were redirects to [[Sheng_Shrine]] before draftification: *[[Seishi (Shinto)]] --> A {{ill|Sheng Shrine|ja|生祀|zh|生祠}} is a [[shrine]] for a living person. There are two types of enshrinement: enshrining the spirit of a living person who has made a significant contribution and enshrining one's own spirit. [[File:Hiraga_Gennai_seishi01s2040.jpg|thumb|200x200px|[[Hiraga Gennai|Hiraga Gennai]]'s birth shrine in [[Hiroshima Prefecture|Hiroshima Prefecture]], [[Fukuyama, Hiroshima|Fukuyama City]], Tomomachi.]]
== Overview ==
=== Sheng shrines in China === When [[Han Dynasty#Western Han|Former Han]]'s Reunobu was the prime minister of [[Yan Kingdom (Han dynasty)|Yan]], he built his shrine between Yan and [[Qi (state)|Qi]] and called it the "Reun Gong Shrine". Also, when {{ill|Shiqing|zh|石慶}} was the prime minister of Qi, the Qi people built the "Shiqing Shrine". This is the beginning of the birth shrine.<ref>[[Zhao Yi]], "The New New Ancestral Hall", Volume 32, "Ancestral Hall", "This is the beginning of the establishment of an ancestral hall.</ref>
In the [[Tang dynasty]], there were certain restrictions on the construction of monuments and shrines by officials in office. According to the [[Tang Code|Tang Rulings and Ordinances]], those who erected shrines or monuments without permission were subject to a penalty of one year of imprisonment.<ref>The [[Tang Code]], Volume 11 (The Law of Office)</ref> The Tang Dynasty called it "the difficulty of building monuments and shrines on the first day.
In the [[Ming dynasty|Ming]], peasants in [[Tengzhou|Tengzhou]] built a shrine to commemorate {{ill|Zhao Bangqing|zh|趙邦清}}, who was appointed to Yanjing for his clean politics. The shrine was built to commemorate him.
During the reign of the [[Tianqi Emperor]] of the Ming Dynasty, the eunuch [[Wei Zhongxian]], ruled the world by conducting his own politics, and those who flattered him or feared his authority were given a He made a living shrine for him. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing (1627), Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote: "Confucius wrote the Spring and Autumn, and Chung-Hsien wrote the Essentials. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote that Confucius had written the "Spring and Autumn" and Chung-Hsien had written the "Essentials. In April of the 7th year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, [[Yuan Chonghuan]] and {{ill|Yan Mingtai|zh|閻鳴泰}} of the [[Ministry of War (imperial China)|Ministry of War]] made an appeal to the government to praise the merits and demerits of Wei Zhongxian and to establish a living shrine for him in both Qiantu.<ref>"Ming Xi Zong Jizhu": "in April of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianqi, the governor of Thistle Liaoning, Yan Mingtai, and the governor of Fuxing Yuan Chong Huan, praised the merits of Wei Zhongxian, and requested the construction of a shrine in front of Ning, giving the name Mao De." The government of the Liaoning Province has been inscribed in gold and stone, and there is no room for extra duties. The minister's single-mindedness in patrolling, strict warning of the battalion generals, do not dare to greed cowardly selfishness, do not dare to give away the hidden string, change the virtual into the real, into the real, easy timid into brave, to have today. Sustainability ancient internal minister who has the right!" ("Ming Xi Zong seven years of the Imperial Court of the Department of the Experiment" Tianqi six years in October)</ref> After that, Wei Zhongxian's raw shrine was "all over the world", "the cost of each shrine was at most hundreds of thousands, at least tens of thousands", "stolen the people's fortune, invaded the public safe, and was cut down. There are countless trees. " When Huang Yun Yasushi made a statue and greeted the statue, he said, "Goto Sansho," and "leaded the general of Bunbu, lined up under the stairs, and worshiped as the beginning."
[[Gu Yanwu|Gu Yanwu]] lamented, "In this generation, there is no official who does not build a living shrine. And within a few years of leaving office, he would destroy the statues and change their masters. He said.<ref>《Nichi Chi Roku》 vol. 23 (Sheng Shi), p. 644.</ref>
=== Sheng shrines in Japan === Shosha, the worship of one's own spirit, was performed in order to obtain a long life or to become a god after death. It is said to have originated from the legend of [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi-no-mikoto]], who enshrined his [[Mitama|Chikishin]] and [[Mitama|Kozoshin]] in [[Mount Miwa]].
The earliest documented case of shosha enshrining one's own spirit is that of Haruhiko Matsuki ([[824|824]]), a [[Shinkan (official)|priest]] of the outer shrine of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]] in [[923|923]] of the [[Heian period]]. - [[924|924 years]]), enshrined and enshrined his own spirit on a stone in Obe, Ise [[Watarai District, Mie|Tokai County]]. In the [[Edo period]], [[Matsudaira Sadanobu|Matsudaira Sadanobu]] established his own nascent shrine in [[1797|1797]] at Shirakawa Castle in Oshu. The number of shosshiki increased during the Edo period, probably due to the influence of Chinese thought. In the [[Edo period]], [[Yamazaki Ansai|Yakusai Yamazaki]] devised a [[Festival]] to enshrine his own spirit, referring to the rituals of [[Confucianism]]. The Shusei ritual was established in 1671 at the Tarukarei Shrine in his residence in Kyoto. After this, the [[Shinto]] family and the [[Kokugaku]] scholars of the Hirata school enshrined their own spirits in their own unique rituals.
Kato Genchi ([[1873|1873]] - [[1965|1965]]) is famous for his research on Shusei. According to Kato, there are 670 known Shuso enshrines, and the newest one is that of Nishi Tadayoshi (father of Nishi Yoshikazu, who was enshrined in Urakawa-cho, Hidaka Province, Hokkaido in August [[1931|1931]]). This is the one of [[Emperor Meiji]]
[[Emperor Meiji]] was enshrined at Ishinomaki Port, Miyagi Prefecture in [[1876|1876]], and at Yahiko Shrine in Ono Village, [[Kamiina District, Nagano|Kamiina County]] in [[1893|1893]] Yahiko Shrine in Ono Village. Emperor Meiji and [[Empress Shōken|Emperor Shōken]] were enshrined in two places in [[1887|1887]] and [[1904|1904]], and [[Emperor Taishō|Emperor Taisho]] was enshrined in [[1911|1911]] in [[1944]]. [[Emperor Taishō|Taisho]] was enshrined at two locations in [[Hokkaido]]: [[Hidaka, Hokkaidō|Hidaka]] and, Monbetsu village, [[Empress Teimei|Emperor Teimei]] was enshrined in [[1913|1913]], [[Taishō|Taisho]], Hirose-cho, [[Hiroshima Prefecture]], and [[Yasuhito, Prince Chichibu|Prince Chichibu]] and [[Nobuhito, Prince Takamatsu]] were established in [[1924|1924]] in [[Himeji|Himeji City]], Doshin-cho.
The birthplace of [[Emperor Hirohito|Showa]] was established in [[1923|1923]] in Yoshitsune, Honbetsu Village, [[Tokachi District, Hokkaido|Tokachi]], Hokkaido, and the birthplace of Emperor Showa and [[Empress Kōjun|Empress Kōjun]] was established in [[1928|1928]] in Hirose-cho, Hiroshima City.

{{Short description|Shrine to a living person in Shinto and Chinese folk religion}} {{Draft topics|biography|east-asia}} {{AfC topic|geo}}
<!-- Note: The following pages were redirects to [[Sheng_Shrine]] before draftification: *[[Seishi (Shinto)]] --> A {{ill|Sheng Shrine|ja|生祀|zh|生祠}} (Seishi, 生祀) (Sheng[[Ci shrine|ci]], 生祠) (living shrine) is a [[shrine]] for a living person.<ref>{{Cite web |title=为关心民生疾苦而设的生祠，在明代起着什么样的作用？_政治_祭祀_陈天祥 |url=https://www.sohu.com/a/www.sohu.com/a/531809387_120005162 |access-date=2022-12-18 |website=www.sohu.com}}</ref> There are two types of enshrinement: enshrining the spirit of a living person who has made a significant contribution and enshrining one's own spirit. [[File:Hiraga_Gennai_seishi01s2040.jpg|thumb|200x200px|[[Hiraga Gennai|Hiraga Gennai]]'s birth shrine in [[Hiroshima Prefecture|Hiroshima Prefecture]], [[Fukuyama, Hiroshima|Fukuyama City]], Tomomachi.]]
== Overview ==
=== Sheng shrines in China === When [[Han Dynasty#Western Han|Former Han]]'s Reunobu was the prime minister of [[Yan Kingdom (Han dynasty)|Yan]], he built his shrine between Yan and [[Qi (state)|Qi]] and called it the "Reun Gong Shrine". Also, when {{ill|Shiqing|zh|石慶}} was the prime minister of Qi, the Qi people built the "Shiqing Shrine". This is the beginning of the birth shrine.<ref>[[Zhao Yi]], "The New New Ancestral Hall", Volume 32, "Ancestral Hall", "This is the beginning of the establishment of an ancestral hall.</ref>
In the [[Tang dynasty]], there were certain restrictions on the construction of monuments and shrines by officials in office. According to the [[Tang Code|Tang Rulings and Ordinances]], those who erected shrines or monuments without permission were subject to a penalty of one year of imprisonment.<ref>The [[Tang Code]], Volume 11 (The Law of Office)</ref> The Tang Dynasty called it "the difficulty of building monuments and shrines on the first day.
In the [[Ming dynasty|Ming]], peasants in [[Tengzhou|Tengzhou]] built a shrine to commemorate {{ill|Zhao Bangqing|zh|趙邦清}}, who was appointed to Yanjing for his clean politics. The shrine was built to commemorate him.
During the reign of the [[Tianqi Emperor]] of the Ming Dynasty, the eunuch [[Wei Zhongxian]], ruled the world by conducting his own politics, and those who flattered him or feared his authority were given a He made a living shrine for him. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing (1627), Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote: "Confucius wrote the Spring and Autumn, and Chung-Hsien wrote the Essentials. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote that Confucius had written the "Spring and Autumn" and Chung-Hsien had written the "Essentials. In April of the 7th year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, [[Yuan Chonghuan]] and {{ill|Yan Mingtai|zh|閻鳴泰}} of the [[Ministry of War (imperial China)|Ministry of War]] made an appeal to the government to praise the merits and demerits of Wei Zhongxian and to establish a living shrine for him in both Qiantu.<ref>"Ming Xi Zong Jizhu": "in April of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianqi, the governor of Thistle Liaoning, Yan Mingtai, and the governor of Fuxing Yuan Chong Huan, praised the merits of Wei Zhongxian, and requested the construction of a shrine in front of Ning, giving the name Mao De." The government of the Liaoning Province has been inscribed in gold and stone, and there is no room for extra duties. The minister's single-mindedness in patrolling, strict warning of the battalion generals, do not dare to greed cowardly selfishness, do not dare to give away the hidden string, change the virtual into the real, into the real, easy timid into brave, to have today. Sustainability ancient internal minister who has the right!" ("Ming Xi Zong seven years of the Imperial Court of the Department of the Experiment" Tianqi six years in October)</ref> After that, Wei Zhongxian's raw shrine was "all over the world", "the cost of each shrine was at most hundreds of thousands, at least tens of thousands", "stolen the people's fortune, invaded the public safe, and was cut down. There are countless trees. " When Huang Yun Yasushi made a statue and greeted the statue, he said, "Goto Sansho," and "leaded the general of Bunbu, lined up under the stairs, and worshiped as the beginning."
[[Gu Yanwu|Gu Yanwu]] lamented, "In this generation, there is no official who does not build a living shrine. And within a few years of leaving office, he would destroy the statues and change their masters. He said.<ref>《Nichi Chi Roku》 vol. 23 (Sheng Shi), p. 644.</ref>
=== Sheng shrines in Japan === Shosha, the worship of one's own spirit, was performed in order to obtain a long life or to become a god after death. It is said to have originated from the legend of [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi-no-mikoto]], who enshrined his [[Mitama|Chikishin]] and [[Mitama|Kozoshin]] in [[Mount Miwa]].
The earliest documented case of shosha enshrining one's own spirit is that of Haruhiko Matsuki ([[824|824]]), a [[Shinkan (official)|priest]] of the outer shrine of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]] in [[923|923]] of the [[Heian period]]. - [[924|924 years]]), enshrined and enshrined his own spirit on a stone in Obe, Ise [[Watarai District, Mie|Tokai County]]. In the [[Edo period]], [[Matsudaira Sadanobu|Matsudaira Sadanobu]] established his own nascent shrine in [[1797|1797]] at Shirakawa Castle in Oshu. The number of shosshiki increased during the Edo period, probably due to the influence of Chinese thought. In the [[Edo period]], [[Yamazaki Ansai|Yakusai Yamazaki]] devised a [[Festival]] to enshrine his own spirit, referring to the rituals of [[Confucianism]]. The Shusei ritual was established in 1671 at the Tarukarei Shrine in his residence in Kyoto. After this, the [[Shinto]] family and the [[Kokugaku]] scholars of the Hirata school enshrined their own spirits in their own unique rituals.
Kato Genchi ([[1873|1873]] - [[1965|1965]]) is famous for his research on Shusei. According to Kato, there are 670 known Shuso enshrines, and the newest one is that of Nishi Tadayoshi (father of Nishi Yoshikazu, who was enshrined in Urakawa-cho, Hidaka Province, Hokkaido in August [[1931|1931]]). This is the one of [[Emperor Meiji]]
[[Emperor Meiji]] was enshrined at Ishinomaki Port, Miyagi Prefecture in [[1876|1876]], and at Yahiko Shrine in Ono Village, [[Kamiina District, Nagano|Kamiina County]] in [[1893|1893]] Yahiko Shrine in Ono Village. Emperor Meiji and [[Empress Shōken|Emperor Shōken]] were enshrined in two places in [[1887|1887]] and [[1904|1904]], and [[Emperor Taishō|Emperor Taisho]] was enshrined in [[1911|1911]] in [[1944]]. [[Emperor Taishō|Taisho]] was enshrined at two locations in [[Hokkaido]]: [[Hidaka, Hokkaidō|Hidaka]] and, Monbetsu village, [[Empress Teimei|Emperor Teimei]] was enshrined in [[1913|1913]], [[Taishō|Taisho]], Hirose-cho, [[Hiroshima Prefecture]], and [[Yasuhito, Prince Chichibu|Prince Chichibu]] and [[Nobuhito, Prince Takamatsu]] were established in [[1924|1924]] in [[Himeji|Himeji City]], Doshin-cho.
The birthplace of [[Emperor Hirohito|Showa]] was established in [[1923|1923]] in Yoshitsune, Honbetsu Village, [[Tokachi District, Hokkaido|Tokachi]], Hokkaido, and the birthplace of Emperor Showa and [[Empress Kōjun|Empress Kōjun]] was established in [[1928|1928]] in Hirose-cho, Hiroshima City.

{{AFC comment|1=Highly unlikely that the sources cited establish notability, and the way they are cited (each only once) is certainly not enough to support the article contents. [[User:DoubleGrazing|DoubleGrazing]] ([[User talk:DoubleGrazing|talk]]) 16:03, 28 June 2022 (UTC)}}
{{AFC comment|1=This appears to be a machine translation of the JA Wikipedia article at [[:ja:生祀]]. ‑‑ [[User:Eirikr|Eiríkr Útlendi]] │<sup>''[[User talk:Eirikr|Tala við mig]]''</sup> 22:05, 21 February 2022 (UTC)}}
----
{{Short description|Shrine to a living person in Shinto and Chinese folk religion}} {{Draft topics|biography|east-asia}} {{AfC topic|geo}}
<!-- Note: The following pages were redirects to [[Sheng_Shrine]] before draftification: *[[Seishi (Shinto)]] --> A {{ill|Sheng Shrine|ja|生祀|zh|生祠}} (Seishi, 生祀) (Sheng[[Ci shrine|ci]], 生祠) (living shrine) is a [[shrine]] for a living person.<ref>{{Cite web |title=为关心民生疾苦而设的生祠，在明代起着什么样的作用？_政治_祭祀_陈天祥 |url=https://www.sohu.com/a/www.sohu.com/a/531809387_120005162 |access-date=2022-12-18 |website=www.sohu.com}}</ref> There are two types of enshrinement: enshrining the spirit of a living person who has made a significant contribution and enshrining one's own spirit. [[File:Hiraga_Gennai_seishi01s2040.jpg|thumb|200x200px|[[Hiraga Gennai|Hiraga Gennai]]'s birth shrine in [[Hiroshima Prefecture|Hiroshima Prefecture]], [[Fukuyama, Hiroshima|Fukuyama City]], Tomomachi.]]
== Overview ==
=== Sheng shrines in China === When [[Han Dynasty#Western Han|Former Han]]'s Reunobu was the prime minister of [[Yan Kingdom (Han dynasty)|Yan]], he built his shrine between Yan and [[Qi (state)|Qi]] and called it the "Reun Gong Shrine". Also, when {{ill|Shiqing|zh|石慶}} was the prime minister of Qi, the Qi people built the "Shiqing Shrine". This is the beginning of the birth shrine.<ref>[[Zhao Yi]], "The New New Ancestral Hall", Volume 32, "Ancestral Hall", "This is the beginning of the establishment of an ancestral hall.</ref>
In the [[Tang dynasty]], there were certain restrictions on the construction of monuments and shrines by officials in office. According to the [[Tang Code|Tang Rulings and Ordinances]], those who erected shrines or monuments without permission were subject to a penalty of one year of imprisonment.<ref>The [[Tang Code]], Volume 11 (The Law of Office)</ref> The Tang Dynasty called it "the difficulty of building monuments and shrines on the first day.
In the [[Ming dynasty|Ming]], peasants in [[Tengzhou|Tengzhou]] built a shrine to commemorate {{ill|Zhao Bangqing|zh|趙邦清}}, who was appointed to Yanjing for his clean politics. The shrine was built to commemorate him.
During the reign of the [[Tianqi Emperor]] of the Ming Dynasty, the eunuch [[Wei Zhongxian]], ruled the world by conducting his own politics, and those who flattered him or feared his authority were given a He made a living shrine for him. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing (1627), Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote: "Confucius wrote the Spring and Autumn, and Chung-Hsien wrote the Essentials. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote that Confucius had written the "Spring and Autumn" and Chung-Hsien had written the "Essentials. In April of the 7th year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, [[Yuan Chonghuan]] and {{ill|Yan Mingtai|zh|閻鳴泰}} of the [[Ministry of War (imperial China)|Ministry of War]] made an appeal to the government to praise the merits and demerits of Wei Zhongxian and to establish a living shrine for him in both Qiantu.<ref>"Ming Xi Zong Jizhu": "in April of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianqi, the governor of Thistle Liaoning, Yan Mingtai, and the governor of Fuxing Yuan Chong Huan, praised the merits of Wei Zhongxian, and requested the construction of a shrine in front of Ning, giving the name Mao De." The government of the Liaoning Province has been inscribed in gold and stone, and there is no room for extra duties. The minister's single-mindedness in patrolling, strict warning of the battalion generals, do not dare to greed cowardly selfishness, do not dare to give away the hidden string, change the virtual into the real, into the real, easy timid into brave, to have today. Sustainability ancient internal minister who has the right!" ("Ming Xi Zong seven years of the Imperial Court of the Department of the Experiment" Tianqi six years in October)</ref> After that, Wei Zhongxian's raw shrine was "all over the world", "the cost of each shrine was at most hundreds of thousands, at least tens of thousands", "stolen the people's fortune, invaded the public safe, and was cut down. There are countless trees. " When Huang Yun Yasushi made a statue and greeted the statue, he said, "Goto Sansho," and "leaded the general of Bunbu, lined up under the stairs, and worshiped as the beginning."
[[Wei Zhongxian]] has been strongly contemned into modern times for his behavior<ref name=":0" />
[[Gu Yanwu|Gu Yanwu]] lamented, "In this generation, there is no official who does not build a living shrine. And within a few years of leaving office, he would destroy the statues and change their masters.<ref>《Nichi Chi Roku》 vol. 23 (Sheng Shi), p. 644.</ref>
Recently{{When|date=2020-12<!--article says it was recently so likely 2020-->}} a person in China created their own Sheng shrine and stirred online controversy<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |last=网易 |date=2020-12-14 |title=魏忠贤为自己生祠揭幕 |url=https://www.163.com/dy/article/FTRIN9CF0521Q1HR.html |access-date=2022-12-18 |website=www.163.com}}</ref>
=== Sheng shrines in Japan === Shosha, the worship of one's own spirit, was performed in order to obtain a long life or to become a god after death. It is said to have originated from the legend of [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi-no-mikoto]], who enshrined his [[Mitama|Chikishin]] and [[Mitama|Kozoshin]] in [[Mount Miwa]].
The earliest documented case of shosha enshrining one's own spirit is that of Haruhiko Matsuki ([[824|824]]), a [[Shinkan (official)|priest]] of the outer shrine of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]] in [[923|923]] of the [[Heian period]]. - [[924|924 years]]), enshrined and enshrined his own spirit on a stone in Obe, Ise [[Watarai District, Mie|Tokai County]]. In the [[Edo period]], [[Matsudaira Sadanobu|Matsudaira Sadanobu]] established his own nascent shrine in [[1797|1797]] at Shirakawa Castle in Oshu. The number of shosshiki increased during the Edo period, probably due to the influence of Chinese thought. In the [[Edo period]], [[Yamazaki Ansai|Yakusai Yamazaki]] devised a [[Festival]] to enshrine his own spirit, referring to the rituals of [[Confucianism]]. The Shusei ritual was established in 1671 at the Tarukarei Shrine in his residence in Kyoto. After this, the [[Shinto]] family and the [[Kokugaku]] scholars of the Hirata school enshrined their own spirits in their own unique rituals.
Kato Genchi ([[1873|1873]] - [[1965|1965]]) is famous for his research on Shusei. According to Kato, there are 670 known Shuso enshrines, and the newest one is that of Nishi Tadayoshi (father of Nishi Yoshikazu, who was enshrined in Urakawa-cho, Hidaka Province, Hokkaido in August [[1931|1931]]). This is the one of [[Emperor Meiji]]
[[Emperor Meiji]] was enshrined at Ishinomaki Port, Miyagi Prefecture in [[1876|1876]], and at Yahiko Shrine in Ono Village, [[Kamiina District, Nagano|Kamiina County]] in [[1893|1893]] Yahiko Shrine in Ono Village. Emperor Meiji and [[Empress Shōken|Emperor Shōken]] were enshrined in two places in [[1887|1887]] and [[1904|1904]], and [[Emperor Taishō|Emperor Taisho]] was enshrined in [[1911|1911]] in [[1944]]. [[Emperor Taishō|Taisho]] was enshrined at two locations in [[Hokkaido]]: [[Hidaka, Hokkaidō|Hidaka]] and, Monbetsu village, [[Empress Teimei|Emperor Teimei]] was enshrined in [[1913|1913]], [[Taishō|Taisho]], Hirose-cho, [[Hiroshima Prefecture]], and [[Yasuhito, Prince Chichibu|Prince Chichibu]] and [[Nobuhito, Prince Takamatsu]] were established in [[1924|1924]] in [[Himeji|Himeji City]], Doshin-cho.
The birthplace of [[Emperor Hirohito|Showa]] was established in [[1923|1923]] in Yoshitsune, Honbetsu Village, [[Tokachi District, Hokkaido|Tokachi]], Hokkaido, and the birthplace of Emperor Showa and [[Empress Kōjun|Empress Kōjun]] was established in [[1928|1928]] in Hirose-cho, Hiroshima City.
== References == <references />
== See Also ==
* [[Tamaya]] * {{ill|Okuto Castle|ja|奥都城}}
[[:Category:Shinto]] {{Drafts moved from mainspace|date=February 2022}} [[:Category:East Asian folk religion]]

Emperor Meiji and [[Empress Shōken|Emperor Shōken]] were enshrined in two places in [[1887|1887]] and [[1904|1904]], and [[Emperor Taishō|Emperor Taisho]] was enshrined in [[1911|1911]] in [[1944]]. [[Emperor Taishō|Taisho]] was enshrined at two locations in [[Hokkaido]]: [[Hidaka, Hokkaidō|Hidaka]] and, Monbetsu village, [[Empress Teimei|Emperor Teimei]] was enshrined in [[1913|1913]], [[Taishō|Taisho]], Hirose-cho, [[Hiroshima Prefecture]], and [[Yasuhito, Prince Chichibu|Prince Chichibu]] and [[Nobuhito, Prince Takamatsu]] were established in [[1924|1924]] in [[Himeji|Himeji City]], Doshin-cho.
The birthplace of [[Emperor Hirohito|Showa]] was established in [[1923|1923]] in Yoshitsune, Honbetsu Village, [[Tokachi District, Hokkaido|Tokachi]], Hokkaido, and the birthplace of Emperor Showa and [[Empress Kōjun|Empress Kōjun]] was established in [[1928|1928]] in Hirose-cho, Hiroshima City.

{{AFC comment|1=Highly unlikely that the sources cited establish notability, and the way they are cited (each only once) is certainly not enough to support the article contents. [[User:DoubleGrazing|DoubleGrazing]] ([[User talk:DoubleGrazing|talk]]) 16:03, 28 June 2022 (UTC)}}
{{AFC comment|1=This appears to be a machine translation of the JA Wikipedia article at [[:ja:生祀]]. ‑‑ [[User:Eirikr|Eiríkr Útlendi]] │<sup>''[[User talk:Eirikr|Tala við mig]]''</sup> 22:05, 21 February 2022 (UTC)}}

{{Short description|Shrine to a living person in Shinto and Chinese folk religion}} {{Draft topics|biography|east-asia}} {{AfC topic|geo}}
<!-- Note: The following pages were redirects to [[Sheng_Shrine]] before draftification: *[[Seishi (Shinto)]] --> A {{ill|Sheng Shrine|ja|生祀|zh|生祠}} (Seishi, 生祀) (Sheng[[Ci shrine|ci]], 生祠) (living shrine) is a [[shrine]] for a living person.<ref>{{Cite web |title=为关心民生疾苦而设的生祠，在明代起着什么样的作用？_政治_祭祀_陈天祥 |url=https://www.sohu.com/a/www.sohu.com/a/531809387_120005162 |access-date=2022-12-18 |website=www.sohu.com}}</ref> There are two types of enshrinement: enshrining the spirit of a living person who has made a significant contribution and enshrining one's own spirit.
Due to the usage by [[Wei Zhongxian]] they became seen as a sign of corruption and declined in China<ref>{{Cite web |title=生祠并非魏忠贤的专利-凤凰周刊 |url=http://www.ifengweekly.com/detil.php?id=16813 |access-date=2022-12-22 |website=www.ifengweekly.com}}</ref>. However the practice continued in Japan with the worship of the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] until 1945 and the end of [[State Shinto]].<ref name="Earhart">{{cite book |last1=Earhart |first1=H. Byron |url=https://archive.org/details/religioninjapane0000earh |title=Religion in the Japanese experience: sources and interpretations |date=1974 |publisher=Dickenson Pub. Co. |isbn=0822101041 |edition=3rd |location=Encino, Calif. |author1-link=H. Byron Earhart |url-access=registration}}</ref> [[File:Hiraga_Gennai_seishi01s2040.jpg|thumb|200x200px|[[Hiraga Gennai|Hiraga Gennai]]'s birth shrine in [[Hiroshima Prefecture|Hiroshima Prefecture]], [[Fukuyama, Hiroshima|Fukuyama City]], Tomomachi.]]
== Overview ==
=== Sheng shrines in China === When [[Han Dynasty#Western Han|Former Han]]'s Reunobu was the prime minister of [[Yan Kingdom (Han dynasty)|Yan]], he built his shrine between Yan and [[Qi (state)|Qi]] and called it the "Reun Gong Shrine". Also, when {{ill|Shiqing|zh|石慶}} was the prime minister of Qi, the Qi people built the "Shiqing Shrine". This is the beginning of the birth shrine.<ref>[[Zhao Yi]], "The New New Ancestral Hall", Volume 32, "Ancestral Hall", "This is the beginning of the establishment of an ancestral hall.</ref>
In the [[Tang dynasty]], there were certain restrictions on the construction of monuments and shrines by officials in office. According to the [[Tang Code|Tang Rulings and Ordinances]], those who erected shrines or monuments without permission were subject to a penalty of one year of imprisonment.<ref>The [[Tang Code]], Volume 11 (The Law of Office)</ref> The Tang Dynasty called it "the difficulty of building monuments and shrines on the first day.
In the [[Ming dynasty|Ming]], peasants in [[Tengzhou|Tengzhou]] built a shrine to commemorate {{ill|Zhao Bangqing|zh|趙邦清}}, who was appointed to Yanjing for his clean politics. The shrine was built to commemorate him.
During the reign of the [[Tianqi Emperor]] of the Ming Dynasty, the eunuch [[Wei Zhongxian]], ruled the world by conducting his own politics, and those who flattered him or feared his authority were given a He made a living shrine for him. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing (1627), Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote: "Confucius wrote the Spring and Autumn, and Chung-Hsien wrote the Essentials. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote that Confucius had written the "Spring and Autumn" and Chung-Hsien had written the "Essentials. In April of the 7th year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, [[Yuan Chonghuan]] and {{ill|Yan Mingtai|zh|閻鳴泰}} of the [[Ministry of War (imperial China)|Ministry of War]] made an appeal to the government to praise the merits and demerits of Wei Zhongxian and to establish a living shrine for him in both Qiantu.<ref>"Ming Xi Zong Jizhu": "in April of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianqi, the governor of Thistle Liaoning, Yan Mingtai, and the governor of Fuxing Yuan Chong Huan, praised the merits of Wei Zhongxian, and requested the construction of a shrine in front of Ning, giving the name Mao De." The government of the Liaoning Province has been inscribed in gold and stone, and there is no room for extra duties. The minister's single-mindedness in patrolling, strict warning of the battalion generals, do not dare to greed cowardly selfishness, do not dare to give away the hidden string, change the virtual into the real, into the real, easy timid into brave, to have today. Sustainability ancient internal minister who has the right!" ("Ming Xi Zong seven years of the Imperial Court of the Department of the Experiment" Tianqi six years in October)</ref> After that, Wei Zhongxian's raw shrine was "all over the world", "the cost of each shrine was at most hundreds of thousands, at least tens of thousands", "stolen the people's fortune, invaded the public safe, and was cut down. There are countless trees. " When Huang Yun Yasushi made a statue and greeted the statue, he said, "Goto Sansho," and "leaded the general of Bunbu, lined up under the stairs, and worshiped as the beginning."
[[Wei Zhongxian]] has been strongly contemned into modern times for his behavior<ref name=":0" />
[[Gu Yanwu|Gu Yanwu]] lamented, "In this generation, there is no official who does not build a living shrine. And within a few years of leaving office, he would destroy the statues and change their masters.<ref>《Nichi Chi Roku》 vol. 23 (Sheng Shi), p. 644.</ref>
Recently{{When|date=2020-12<!--article says it was recently so likely 2020-->}} a person in China created their own Sheng shrine and stirred online controversy<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |last=网易 |date=2020-12-14 |title=魏忠贤为自己生祠揭幕 |url=https://www.163.com/dy/article/FTRIN9CF0521Q1HR.html |access-date=2022-12-18 |website=www.163.com}}</ref>
=== Sheng shrines in Japan === Worship of one's own spirit, was performed in order to obtain a long life or to become a god after death. It is said to have originated from the legend of [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi-no-mikoto]], who enshrined his [[Mitama]] in [[Mount Miwa]].
The earliest documented case of shosha enshrining one's own spirit is that of Haruhiko Matsuki ([[824|824]]), a [[Shinkan (official)|shinkan]] of the outer shrine of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]] in [[923|923]] of the [[Heian period]]. - [[924|924 years]]), enshrined and enshrined his own spirit on a stone in Obe, Ise [[Watarai District, Mie|Tokai County]]. In the [[Edo period]], [[Matsudaira Sadanobu|Matsudaira Sadanobu]] established his own nascent shrine in [[1797|1797]] at Shirakawa Castle in Oshu. The number of shosshiki increased during the Edo period, probably due to the influence of Chinese thought. In the [[Edo period]], [[Yamazaki Ansai|Yakusai Yamazaki]] devised a [[Festival]] to enshrine his own spirit, referring to the rituals of [[Confucianism]]. The Shusei ritual was established in 1671 at the Tarukarei Shrine in his residence in Kyoto. After this, the [[Shinto]] family and the [[Kokugaku]] scholars of the Hirata school enshrined their own spirits in their own unique rituals.
Kato Genchi ([[1873|1873]] - [[1965|1965]]) is famous for his research on Shusei. According to Kato, there are 670 known Shuso enshrines, and the newest one is that of Nishi Tadayoshi (father of Nishi Yoshikazu, who was enshrined in Urakawa-cho, Hidaka Province, Hokkaido in August [[1931|1931]]). This is the one of [[Emperor Meiji]]
[[Emperor Meiji]] was enshrined at Ishinomaki Port, Miyagi Prefecture in [[1876|1876]], and at Yahiko Shrine in Ono Village, [[Kamiina District, Nagano|Kamiina County]] in [[1893|1893]] Yahiko Shrine in Ono Village. Emperor Meiji and [[Empress Shōken|Emperor Shōken]] were enshrined in two places in [[1887|1887]] and [[1904|1904]], and [[Emperor Taishō|Emperor Taisho]] was enshrined in [[1911|1911]] in [[1944]]. [[Emperor Taishō|Taisho]] was enshrined at two locations in [[Hokkaido]]: [[Hidaka, Hokkaidō|Hidaka]] and, Monbetsu village, [[Empress Teimei|Emperor Teimei]] was enshrined in [[1913|1913]], [[Taishō|Taisho]], Hirose-cho, [[Hiroshima Prefecture]], and [[Yasuhito, Prince Chichibu|Prince Chichibu]] and [[Nobuhito, Prince Takamatsu]] were established in [[1924|1924]] in [[Himeji|Himeji City]], Doshin-cho.
The birthplace of [[Emperor Hirohito|Showa]] was established in [[1923|1923]] in Yoshitsune, Honbetsu Village, [[Tokachi District, Hokkaido|Tokachi]], Hokkaido, and the birthplace of Emperor Showa and [[Empress Kōjun|Empress Kōjun]] was established in [[1928|1928]] in Hirose-cho, Hiroshima City.

("Ming Xi Zong seven years of the Imperial Court of the Department of the Experiment" Tianqi six years in October)</ref> After that, Wei Zhongxian's raw shrine was "all over the world", "the cost of each shrine was at most hundreds of thousands, at least tens of thousands", "stolen the people's fortune, invaded the public safe, and was cut down. There are countless trees. " When Huang Yun Yasushi made a statue and greeted the statue, he said, "Goto Sansho," and "leaded the general of Bunbu, lined up under the stairs, and worshiped as the beginning."
[[Wei Zhongxian]] has been strongly contemned into modern times for his behavior<ref name=":0" />
[[Gu Yanwu|Gu Yanwu]] lamented, "In this generation, there is no official who does not build a living shrine. And within a few years of leaving office, he would destroy the statues and change their masters.<ref>《Nichi Chi Roku》 vol. 23 (Sheng Shi), p. 644.</ref>
Recently{{When|date=2020-12<!--article says it was recently so likely 2020-->}} a person in China created their own Sheng shrine and stirred online controversy<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |last=网易 |date=2020-12-14 |title=魏忠贤为自己生祠揭幕 |url=https://www.163.com/dy/article/FTRIN9CF0521Q1HR.html |access-date=2022-12-18 |website=www.163.com}}</ref>
=== Sheng shrines in Japan === {{Nihongo|2=生祀|3=Seishi}} refers to the worship of a living person. It was performed in order to obtain a long life or to become a god after death. It is said to have originated from the legend of [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi-no-mikoto]], who enshrined his [[Mitama]] in [[Mount Miwa]].
The earliest documented case of enshrining one's own spirit is that of Haruhiko Matsuki ([[824|824]]), a [[Shinkan (official)|shinkan]] of the outer shrine of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]] in [[923|923]] of the [[Heian period]]. - [[924|924 years]]), enshrined and enshrined his own spirit on a stone in Obe, Ise [[Watarai District, Mie|Tokai County]]. In the [[Edo period]], [[Matsudaira Sadanobu|Matsudaira Sadanobu]] established his own nascent shrine in [[1797|1797]] at Shirakawa Castle in Oshu. The number of shosshiki increased during the Edo period, probably due to the influence of Chinese thought. In the [[Edo period]], [[Yamazaki Ansai|Yakusai Yamazaki]] devised a [[Festival]] to enshrine his own spirit, referring to the rituals of [[Confucianism]]. The sheng enshrinement ritual was established in 1671 at the Tarukarei Shrine in his residence in Kyoto. After this, the [[Shinto]] family and the [[Kokugaku]] scholars of the Hirata school enshrined their own spirits in their own unique rituals.
Kato Genchi ([[1873|1873]] - [[1965|1965]]) is famous for his research on Sheng Shrines. According to Kato, there are 670 known sheng shrines, and the newest one is that of Nishi Tadayoshi (father of Nishi Yoshikazu, who was enshrined in Urakawa-cho, Hidaka Province, Hokkaido in August [[1931|1931]]). This is the one of [[Emperor Meiji]]
[[Emperor Meiji]] was enshrined at Ishinomaki Port, Miyagi Prefecture in [[1876|1876]], and at Yahiko Shrine in Ono Village, [[Kamiina District, Nagano|Kamiina County]] in [[1893|1893]] Yahiko Shrine in Ono Village. Emperor Meiji and [[Empress Shōken|Emperor Shōken]] were enshrined in two places in [[1887|1887]] and [[1904|1904]], and [[Emperor Taishō|Emperor Taisho]] was enshrined in [[1911|1911]] in [[1944]]. [[Emperor Taishō|Taisho]] was enshrined at two locations in [[Hokkaido]]: [[Hidaka, Hokkaidō|Hidaka]] and, Monbetsu village, [[Empress Teimei|Emperor Teimei]] was enshrined in [[1913|1913]], [[Taishō|Taisho]], Hirose-cho, [[Hiroshima Prefecture]], and [[Yasuhito, Prince Chichibu|Prince Chichibu]] and [[Nobuhito, Prince Takamatsu]] were established in [[1924|1924]] in [[Himeji|Himeji City]], Doshin-cho.
The birthplace of [[Emperor Hirohito|Showa]] was established in [[1923|1923]] in Yoshitsune, Honbetsu Village, [[Tokachi District, Hokkaido|Tokachi]], Hokkaido, and the birthplace of Emperor Showa and [[Empress Kōjun|Empress Kōjun]] was established in [[1928|1928]] in Hirose-cho, Hiroshima City.

{{Short description|Shrine to a living person in Shinto and Chinese folk religion}} {{Draft topics|biography|east-asia}} {{AfC topic|geo}}
<!-- Note: The following pages were redirects to [[Sheng_Shrine]] before draftification: *[[Seishi (Shinto)]] --> A {{ill|Sheng Shrine|ja|生祀|zh|生祠}} (Sheng[[Ci shrine|ci]], 生祠) (living shrine) is a [[shrine]] for a living person.<ref>{{Cite web |title=为关心民生疾苦而设的生祠，在明代起着什么样的作用？_政治_祭祀_陈天祥 |url=https://www.sohu.com/a/www.sohu.com/a/531809387_120005162 |access-date=2022-12-18 |website=www.sohu.com}}</ref> It is where {{Nihongo|2=生祀|3=Seishi}} or worship of a living person is performed. There are two types of enshrinement: enshrining the spirit of a living person who has made a significant contribution and enshrining one's own spirit.
Due to the usage by [[Wei Zhongxian]] they became seen as a sign of corruption and declined in China<ref>{{Cite web |title=生祠并非魏忠贤的专利-凤凰周刊 |url=http://www.ifengweekly.com/detil.php?id=16813 |access-date=2022-12-22 |website=www.ifengweekly.com}}</ref>. However the practice continued in Japan with the worship of the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] until 1945 and the end of [[State Shinto]].<ref name="Earhart">{{cite book |last1=Earhart |first1=H. Byron |url=https://archive.org/details/religioninjapane0000earh |title=Religion in the Japanese experience: sources and interpretations |date=1974 |publisher=Dickenson Pub. Co. |isbn=0822101041 |edition=3rd |location=Encino, Calif. |author1-link=H. Byron Earhart |url-access=registration}}</ref> [[File:Hiraga_Gennai_seishi01s2040.jpg|thumb|200x200px|[[Hiraga Gennai|Hiraga Gennai]]'s birth shrine in [[Hiroshima Prefecture|Hiroshima Prefecture]], [[Fukuyama, Hiroshima|Fukuyama City]], Tomomachi.]]
== Overview ==
=== Sheng shrines in China === When [[Han Dynasty#Western Han|Former Han]]'s Reunobu was the prime minister of [[Yan Kingdom (Han dynasty)|Yan]], he built his shrine between Yan and [[Qi (state)|Qi]] and called it the "Reun Gong Shrine". Also, when {{ill|Shiqing|zh|石慶}} was the prime minister of Qi, the Qi people built the "Shiqing Shrine". This is the beginning of the birth shrine.<ref>[[Zhao Yi]], "The New New Ancestral Hall", Volume 32, "Ancestral Hall", "This is the beginning of the establishment of an ancestral hall.</ref>
In the [[Tang dynasty]], there were certain restrictions on the construction of monuments and shrines by officials in office. According to the [[Tang Code|Tang Rulings and Ordinances]], those who erected shrines or monuments without permission were subject to a penalty of one year of imprisonment.<ref>The [[Tang Code]], Volume 11 (The Law of Office)</ref> The Tang Dynasty called it "the difficulty of building monuments and shrines on the first day.
In the [[Ming dynasty|Ming]], peasants in [[Tengzhou|Tengzhou]] built a shrine to commemorate {{ill|Zhao Bangqing|zh|趙邦清}}, who was appointed to Yanjing for his clean politics. The shrine was built to commemorate him.
During the reign of the [[Tianqi Emperor]] of the Ming Dynasty, the eunuch [[Wei Zhongxian]], ruled the world by conducting his own politics, and those who flattered him or feared his authority were given a He made a living shrine for him. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing (1627), Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote: "Confucius wrote the Spring and Autumn, and Chung-Hsien wrote the Essentials. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote that Confucius had written the "Spring and Autumn" and Chung-Hsien had written the "Essentials. In April of the 7th year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, [[Yuan Chonghuan]] and {{ill|Yan Mingtai|zh|閻鳴泰}} of the [[Ministry of War (imperial China)|Ministry of War]] made an appeal to the government to praise the merits and demerits of Wei Zhongxian and to establish a living shrine for him in both Qiantu.<ref>"Ming Xi Zong Jizhu": "in April of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianqi, the governor of Thistle Liaoning, Yan Mingtai, and the governor of Fuxing Yuan Chong Huan, praised the merits of Wei Zhongxian, and requested the construction of a shrine in front of Ning, giving the name Mao De." The government of the Liaoning Province has been inscribed in gold and stone, and there is no room for extra duties. The minister's single-mindedness in patrolling, strict warning of the battalion generals, do not dare to greed cowardly selfishness, do not dare to give away the hidden string, change the virtual into the real, into the real, easy timid into brave, to have today. Sustainability ancient internal minister who has the right!" ("Ming Xi Zong seven years of the Imperial Court of the Department of the Experiment" Tianqi six years in October)</ref> After that, Wei Zhongxian's raw shrine was "all over the world", "the cost of each shrine was at most hundreds of thousands, at least tens of thousands", "stolen the people's fortune, invaded the public safe, and was cut down. There are countless trees. " When Huang Yun Yasushi made a statue and greeted the statue, he said, "Goto Sansho," and "leaded the general of Bunbu, lined up under the stairs, and worshiped as the beginning."
[[Wei Zhongxian]] has been strongly contemned into modern times for his behavior<ref name=":0" />
[[Gu Yanwu|Gu Yanwu]] lamented, "In this generation, there is no official who does not build a living shrine. And within a few years of leaving office, he would destroy the statues and change their masters.<ref>《Nichi Chi Roku》 vol. 23 (Sheng Shi), p. 644.</ref>
Recently{{When|date=2020-12<!--article says it was recently so likely 2020-->}} a person in China created their own Sheng shrine and stirred online controversy<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |last=网易 |date=2020-12-14 |title=魏忠贤为自己生祠揭幕 |url=https://www.163.com/dy/article/FTRIN9CF0521Q1HR.html |access-date=2022-12-18 |website=www.163.com}}</ref>
=== Worshipping of a living person in Japan === {{Nihongo|2=生祀|3=Seishi}} refers to the worship of a living person. It was performed in order to obtain a long life or to become a god after death. It is said to have originated from the legend of [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi-no-mikoto]], who enshrined his [[Mitama]] in [[Mount Miwa]].
The earliest documented case of enshrining one's own spirit is that of Haruhiko Matsuki ([[824|824]]), a [[Shinkan (official)|shinkan]] of the outer shrine of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]] in [[923|923]] of the [[Heian period]]. - [[924|924 years]]), enshrined and enshrined his own spirit on a stone in Obe, Ise [[Watarai District, Mie|Tokai County]]. In the [[Edo period]], [[Matsudaira Sadanobu|Matsudaira Sadanobu]] established his own nascent shrine in [[1797|1797]] at Shirakawa Castle in Oshu. The number of shosshiki increased during the Edo period, probably due to the influence of Chinese thought. In the [[Edo period]], [[Yamazaki Ansai|Yakusai Yamazaki]] devised a [[Festival]] to enshrine his own spirit, referring to the rituals of [[Confucianism]]. The sheng enshrinement ritual was established in 1671 at the Tarukarei Shrine in his residence in Kyoto. After this, the [[Shinto]] family and the [[Kokugaku]] scholars of the Hirata school enshrined their own spirits in their own unique rituals.
Kato Genchi ([[1873|1873]] - [[1965|1965]]) is famous for his research on Sheng Shrines. According to Kato, there are 670 known sheng shrines, and the newest one is that of Nishi Tadayoshi (father of Nishi Yoshikazu, who was enshrined in Urakawa-cho, Hidaka Province, Hokkaido in August [[1931|1931]]). This is the one of [[Emperor Meiji]]
[[Emperor Meiji]] was enshrined at Ishinomaki Port, Miyagi Prefecture in [[1876|1876]], and at Yahiko Shrine in Ono Village, [[Kamiina District, Nagano|Kamiina County]] in [[1893|1893]] Yahiko Shrine in Ono Village. Emperor Meiji and [[Empress Shōken|Emperor Shōken]] were enshrined in two places in [[1887|1887]] and [[1904|1904]], and [[Emperor Taishō|Emperor Taisho]] was enshrined in [[1911|1911]] in [[1944]]. [[Emperor Taishō|Taisho]] was enshrined at two locations in [[Hokkaido]]: [[Hidaka, Hokkaidō|Hidaka]] and, Monbetsu village, [[Empress Teimei|Emperor Teimei]] was enshrined in [[1913|1913]], [[Taishō|Taisho]], Hirose-cho, [[Hiroshima Prefecture]], and [[Yasuhito, Prince Chichibu|Prince Chichibu]] and [[Nobuhito, Prince Takamatsu]] were established in [[1924|1924]] in [[Himeji|Himeji City]], Doshin-cho.
The birthplace of [[Emperor Hirohito|Showa]] was established in [[1923|1923]] in Yoshitsune, Honbetsu Village, [[Tokachi District, Hokkaido|Tokachi]], Hokkaido, and the birthplace of Emperor Showa and [[Empress Kōjun|Empress Kōjun]] was established in [[1928|1928]] in Hirose-cho, Hiroshima City.

<!-- Note: The following pages were redirects to [[Sheng_Shrine]] before draftification: *[[Seishi (Shinto)]] --> A {{ill|Sheng Shrine|ja|生祀|zh|生祠}} (Sheng[[Ci shrine|ci]], 生祠) {{Nihongo|2=生祀|3=Seishi}} is a [[shrine]] for a living person.<ref>{{Cite web |title=为关心民生疾苦而设的生祠，在明代起着什么样的作用？_政治_祭祀_陈天祥 |url=https://www.sohu.com/a/www.sohu.com/a/531809387_120005162 |access-date=2022-12-18 |website=www.sohu.com}}</ref> It is where or worship of a living person is performed. Due to the usage by [[Wei Zhongxian]] they became seen as a sign of corruption and declined in China<ref>{{Cite web |title=生祠并非魏忠贤的专利-凤凰周刊 |url=http://www.ifengweekly.com/detil.php?id=16813 |access-date=2022-12-22 |website=www.ifengweekly.com}}</ref>.
