Co. |isbn=0822101041 |edition=3rd |location=Encino, Calif. |author1-link=H. Byron Earhart |url-access=registration}}</ref> [[File:Hiraga_Gennai_seishi01s2040.jpg|thumb|200x200px|[[Hiraga Gennai|Hiraga Gennai]]'s birth shrine in [[Hiroshima Prefecture|Hiroshima Prefecture]], [[Fukuyama, Hiroshima|Fukuyama City]], Tomomachi.]]
== Sheng shrines in China == When [[Han Dynasty#Western Han|Former Han]]'s Reunobu was the prime minister of [[Yan Kingdom (Han dynasty)|Yan]], he built his shrine between Yan and [[Qi (state)|Qi]] and called it the "Reun Gong Shrine". Also, when {{ill|Shiqing|zh|石慶}} was the prime minister of Qi, the Qi people built the "Shiqing Shrine". This is the beginning of the birth shrine.<ref>[[Zhao Yi]], "The New New Ancestral Hall", Volume 32, "Ancestral Hall", "This is the beginning of the establishment of an ancestral hall.</ref>
In the [[Tang dynasty]], there were certain restrictions on the construction of monuments and shrines by officials in office. According to the [[Tang Code|Tang Rulings and Ordinances]], those who erected shrines or monuments without permission were subject to a penalty of one year of imprisonment.<ref>The [[Tang Code]], Volume 11 (The Law of Office)</ref> The Tang Dynasty called it "the difficulty of building monuments and shrines on the first day.
In the [[Ming dynasty|Ming]], peasants in [[Tengzhou|Tengzhou]] built a shrine to commemorate {{ill|Zhao Bangqing|zh|趙邦清}}, who was appointed to Yanjing for his clean politics. The shrine was built to commemorate him.
During the reign of the [[Tianqi Emperor]] of the Ming Dynasty, the eunuch [[Wei Zhongxian]], ruled the world by conducting his own politics, and those who flattered him or feared his authority were given a He made a living shrine for him. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing (1627), Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote: "Confucius wrote the Spring and Autumn, and Chung-Hsien wrote the Essentials. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote that Confucius had written the "Spring and Autumn" and Chung-Hsien had written the "Essentials. In April of the 7th year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, [[Yuan Chonghuan]] and {{ill|Yan Mingtai|zh|閻鳴泰}} of the [[Ministry of War (imperial China)|Ministry of War]] made an appeal to the government to praise the merits and demerits of Wei Zhongxian and to establish a living shrine for him in both Qiantu.<ref>"Ming Xi Zong Jizhu": "in April of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianqi, the governor of Thistle Liaoning, Yan Mingtai, and the governor of Fuxing Yuan Chong Huan, praised the merits of Wei Zhongxian, and requested the construction of a shrine in front of Ning, giving the name Mao De." The government of the Liaoning Province has been inscribed in gold and stone, and there is no room for extra duties. The minister's single-mindedness in patrolling, strict warning of the battalion generals, do not dare to greed cowardly selfishness, do not dare to give away the hidden string, change the virtual into the real, into the real, easy timid into brave, to have today. Sustainability ancient internal minister who has the right!" ("Ming Xi Zong seven years of the Imperial Court of the Department of the Experiment" Tianqi six years in October)</ref> After that, Wei Zhongxian's raw shrine was "all over the world", "the cost of each shrine was at most hundreds of thousands, at least tens of thousands", "stolen the people's fortune, invaded the public safe, and was cut down. There are countless trees. " When Huang Yun Yasushi made a statue and greeted the statue, he said, "Goto Sansho," and "leaded the general of Bunbu, lined up under the stairs, and worshiped as the beginning."
[[Wei Zhongxian]] has been strongly contemned into modern times for his behavior<ref name=":0" />
[[Gu Yanwu|Gu Yanwu]] lamented, "In this generation, there is no official who does not build a living shrine. And within a few years of leaving office, he would destroy the statues and change their masters.<ref>《Nichi Chi Roku》 vol. 23 (Sheng Shi), p. 644.</ref>
Recently{{When|date=2020-12<!--article says it was recently so likely 2020-->}} a person in China created their own Sheng shrine and stirred online controversy<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |last=网易 |date=2020-12-14 |title=魏忠贤为自己生祠揭幕 |url=https://www.163.com/dy/article/FTRIN9CF0521Q1HR.html |access-date=2022-12-18 |website=www.163.com}}</ref>
== Worshipping of a living person in Japan == Seishi carried out to worship one's own soul was to live a long life or become Kami (god) after death.
It is thought that this originated in the tradition that [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi]] (chief god of Izumo in southern Honshu Island, Japan, and the central character in the important cycle of myths set in that region) worshipped his own Kushimitama (the wondrous, miraculous, or salubrious) and Sakimitama (the propitious) in [[Mount Miwa]]
The oldest case of someone worshipping their own soul was related to Haruhiko MATSUKI (824 - 924)in the Heian period when he was a Shinto priest of Geku (outer shrine) in Ise-jingu Shrine, he worshipped his soul included in stones or rocks in Obe, Watarai-gun, Ise in 923.
There is a case where Sadanobu MATSUDAIRA carried out Seishi for himself at Oshu Shirakawa-jo Castle in 1797 in the Edo period.
The number of Seishi increased in the Edo period, and this was probably due to the influence of Chinese thought.
In the Edo period, {{Ill|Yamazaki Ansai|ja|山崎闇斎}} established rules for ritual procedures based on a ceremony style for Confucianism and worshipped his soul. The Seishi was carried out in his residence, Suika (Shidemasu)-Reisha Shrine in Kyoto in 1671. After that, Shintoists and scholars of Japanese classical literature in Hirata-ha school worshipped their own souls in accordance with their established ritual procedures.
Genchi KATO (1873-1965) is well-known for his research on Seishi. According to Kato, 60 to 70 Seishi were identified and the latest Seishi is the one for Chugi NISHI which was enshrined in August 1931 in Urakawa-cho, Hidaka Province, Hokkaido Prefecture.
[[Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji's]] Seishi was built in Port of Ishinomaki in Miyagi Prefecture in 1876, and also in the site of Yahiko-jinja Shrine in Ono-mura, Kamiina-gun in 1893. The Seishi of Emperor Meiji and [[Empress Shōken|Empress Dowager Shoken]] was each built in two locations in 1887 and 1904, the Seishi of Emperor Taisho was built in Monbetsu-mura, Hidaka Province, Hokkaido Prefecture in 1911, the Seishi of Empress Teimei was built in Hirose-machi, Hiroshima Prefecture in 1913, and the Seishi of Prince Chichibunomiya and Prince Takamatsunomiya was each built in Doshin-machi, Himeji City in 1924. The Seishi of Emperor Showa was built on Mt. Yoshitsune, Honbetsu-cho, Tokachi Province, Hokkaido Prefecture in 1879, and the Seishi of Emperor Showa and Empress Kojun was each built in Hirose-machi, Hiroshima City in 1928.

Byron |url=https://archive.org/details/religioninjapane0000earh |title=Religion in the Japanese experience: sources and interpretations |date=1974 |publisher=Dickenson Pub. Co. |isbn=0822101041 |edition=3rd |location=Encino, Calif. |author1-link=H. Byron Earhart |url-access=registration}}</ref> [[File:Hiraga_Gennai_seishi01s2040.jpg|thumb|200x200px|[[Hiraga Gennai|Hiraga Gennai]]'s birth shrine in [[Hiroshima Prefecture|Hiroshima Prefecture]], [[Fukuyama, Hiroshima|Fukuyama City]], Tomomachi.]]
== Sheng shrines in China == When [[Han Dynasty#Western Han|Former Han]]'s Reunobu was the prime minister of [[Yan Kingdom (Han dynasty)|Yan]], he built his shrine between Yan and [[Qi (state)|Qi]] and called it the "Reun Gong Shrine". Also, when {{ill|Shiqing|zh|石慶}} was the prime minister of Qi, the Qi people built the "Shiqing Shrine". This is the beginning of the birth shrine.<ref>[[Zhao Yi]], "The New New Ancestral Hall", Volume 32, "Ancestral Hall", "This is the beginning of the establishment of an ancestral hall.</ref>
In the [[Tang dynasty]], there were certain restrictions on the construction of monuments and shrines by officials in office. According to the [[Tang Code|Tang Rulings and Ordinances]], those who erected shrines or monuments without permission were subject to a penalty of one year of imprisonment.<ref>The [[Tang Code]], Volume 11 (The Law of Office)</ref> The Tang Dynasty called it "the difficulty of building monuments and shrines on the first day.
In the [[Ming dynasty|Ming]], peasants in [[Tengzhou|Tengzhou]] built a shrine to commemorate {{ill|Zhao Bangqing|zh|趙邦清}}, who was appointed to Yanjing for his clean politics. The shrine was built to commemorate him.
During the reign of the [[Tianqi Emperor]] of the Ming Dynasty, the eunuch [[Wei Zhongxian]], ruled the world by conducting his own politics, and those who flattered him or feared his authority were given a He made a living shrine for him. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing (1627), Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote: "Confucius wrote the Spring and Autumn, and Chung-Hsien wrote the Essentials. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote that Confucius had written the "Spring and Autumn" and Chung-Hsien had written the "Essentials. In April of the 7th year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, [[Yuan Chonghuan]] and {{ill|Yan Mingtai|zh|閻鳴泰}} of the [[Ministry of War (imperial China)|Ministry of War]] made an appeal to the government to praise the merits and demerits of Wei Zhongxian and to establish a living shrine for him in both Qiantu.<ref>"Ming Xi Zong Jizhu": "in April of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianqi, the governor of Thistle Liaoning, Yan Mingtai, and the governor of Fuxing Yuan Chong Huan, praised the merits of Wei Zhongxian, and requested the construction of a shrine in front of Ning, giving the name Mao De." The government of the Liaoning Province has been inscribed in gold and stone, and there is no room for extra duties. The minister's single-mindedness in patrolling, strict warning of the battalion generals, do not dare to greed cowardly selfishness, do not dare to give away the hidden string, change the virtual into the real, into the real, easy timid into brave, to have today. Sustainability ancient internal minister who has the right!" ("Ming Xi Zong seven years of the Imperial Court of the Department of the Experiment" Tianqi six years in October)</ref> After that, Wei Zhongxian's raw shrine was "all over the world", "the cost of each shrine was at most hundreds of thousands, at least tens of thousands", "stolen the people's fortune, invaded the public safe, and was cut down. There are countless trees. " When Huang Yun Yasushi made a statue and greeted the statue, he said, "Goto Sansho," and "leaded the general of Bunbu, lined up under the stairs, and worshiped as the beginning."
[[Wei Zhongxian]] has been strongly contemned into modern times for his behavior<ref name=":0" />
[[Gu Yanwu|Gu Yanwu]] lamented, "In this generation, there is no official who does not build a living shrine. And within a few years of leaving office, he would destroy the statues and change their masters.<ref>《Nichi Chi Roku》 vol. 23 (Sheng Shi), p. 644.</ref>
Recently{{When|date=2020-12<!--article says it was recently so likely 2020-->}} a person in China created their own Sheng shrine and stirred online controversy<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |last=网易 |date=2020-12-14 |title=魏忠贤为自己生祠揭幕 |url=https://www.163.com/dy/article/FTRIN9CF0521Q1HR.html |access-date=2022-12-18 |website=www.163.com}}</ref>
== Worshipping of a living person in Japan == Seishi carried out to worship one's own soul was to live a long life or become Kami (god) after death.
It is thought that this originated in the tradition that [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi]] (chief god of Izumo in southern Honshu Island, Japan, and the central character in the important cycle of myths set in that region) worshipped his own Kushimitama (the wondrous, miraculous, or salubrious) and Sakimitama (the propitious) in [[Mount Miwa]]
The oldest case of someone worshipping their own soul was related to Haruhiko MATSUKI (824 - 924)in the Heian period when he was a Shinto priest of Geku (outer shrine) in Ise-jingu Shrine, he worshipped his soul included in stones or rocks in Obe, Watarai-gun, Ise in 923.
There is a case where Sadanobu MATSUDAIRA carried out Seishi for himself at Oshu Shirakawa-jo Castle in 1797 in the Edo period.
The number of Seishi increased in the Edo period, and this was probably due to the influence of Chinese thought.
In the Edo period, {{Ill|Yamazaki Ansai|ja|山崎闇斎}} established rules for ritual procedures based on a ceremony style for Confucianism and worshipped his soul. The Seishi was carried out in his residence, Suika (Shidemasu)-Reisha Shrine in Kyoto in 1671. After that, Shintoists and scholars of Japanese classical literature in Hirata-ha school worshipped their own souls in accordance with their established ritual procedures.
Genchi KATO (1873-1965) is well-known for his research on Seishi. According to Kato, 60 to 70 Seishi were identified and the latest Seishi is the one for Chugi NISHI which was enshrined in August 1931 in Urakawa-cho, Hidaka Province, Hokkaido Prefecture.
[[Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji's]] Seishi was built in Port of Ishinomaki in Miyagi Prefecture in 1876, and also in the site of Yahiko-jinja Shrine in Ono-mura, Kamiina-gun in 1893. The Seishi of Emperor Meiji and [[Empress Shōken|Empress Dowager Shoken]] was each built in two locations in 1887 and 1904, the Seishi of Emperor Taisho was built in Monbetsu-mura, Hidaka Province, Hokkaido Prefecture in 1911, the Seishi of Empress Teimei was built in Hirose-machi, Hiroshima Prefecture in 1913, and the Seishi of Prince Chichibunomiya and Prince Takamatsunomiya was each built in Doshin-machi, Himeji City in 1924. The Seishi of Emperor Showa was built on Mt. Yoshitsune, Honbetsu-cho, Tokachi Province, Hokkaido Prefecture in 1879, and the Seishi of Emperor Showa and Empress Kojun was each built in Hirose-machi, Hiroshima City in 1928.

{{Short description|Shrine to a living person in Shinto and Chinese folk religion}} {{Draft topics|biography|east-asia}} {{AfC topic|geo}} {{AfC submission|||ts=20221222235808|u=Immanuelle|ns=118}} {{AFC submission|d|v|u=Immanuelle|ns=118|decliner=DoubleGrazing|declinets=20220628160325|reason2=nn|ts=20220218193823}} <!-- Do not remove this line! -->

{{AFC comment|1=This appears to be a machine translation of the JA Wikipedia article at [[:ja:生祀]]. ‑‑ [[User:Eirikr|Eiríkr Útlendi]] │<sup>''[[User talk:Eirikr|Tala við mig]]''</sup> 22:05, 21 February 2022 (UTC)}}

<!-- Note: The following pages were redirects to [[Sheng_Shrine]] before draftification: *[[Seishi (Shinto)]] --> A {{ill|Sheng Shrine|ja|生祀|zh|生祠}} (Sheng[[Ci shrine|ci]], 生祠) {{Nihongo|2=生祀|3=Seishi}} is a [[shrine]] for a living person.<ref>{{Cite web |title=为关心民生疾苦而设的生祠，在明代起着什么样的作用？_政治_祭祀_陈天祥 |url=https://www.sohu.com/a/www.sohu.com/a/531809387_120005162 |access-date=2022-12-18 |website=www.sohu.com}}</ref> It is where or worship of a living person is performed. There are two types of enshrinement: enshrining the spirit of a living person who has made a significant contribution and enshrining one's own spirit.
Due to the usage by [[Wei Zhongxian]] they became seen as a sign of corruption and declined in China<ref>{{Cite web |title=生祠并非魏忠贤的专利-凤凰周刊 |url=http://www.ifengweekly.com/detil.php?id=16813 |access-date=2022-12-22 |website=www.ifengweekly.com}}</ref>. However the practice continued in Japan with the worship of the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] until 1945 and the end of [[State Shinto]].<ref name="Earhart">{{cite book |last1=Earhart |first1=H. Byron |url=https://archive.org/details/religioninjapane0000earh |title=Religion in the Japanese experience: sources and interpretations |date=1974 |publisher=Dickenson Pub. Co. |isbn=0822101041 |edition=3rd |location=Encino, Calif. |author1-link=H. Byron Earhart |url-access=registration}}</ref> [[File:Hiraga_Gennai_seishi01s2040.jpg|thumb|200x200px|[[Hiraga Gennai|Hiraga Gennai]]'s birth shrine in [[Hiroshima Prefecture|Hiroshima Prefecture]], [[Fukuyama, Hiroshima|Fukuyama City]], Tomomachi.]]

{{AFC comment|1=This appears to be a machine translation of the JA Wikipedia article at [[:ja:生祀]]. ‑‑ [[User:Eirikr|Eiríkr Útlendi]] │<sup>''[[User talk:Eirikr|Tala við mig]]''</sup> 22:05, 21 February 2022 (UTC)}}
----
<!-- Note: The following pages were redirects to [[Sheng_Shrine]] before draftification: *[[Seishi (Shinto)]] --> {{Nihongo|Living worship|2=生祀|3=Seishi}} is worship of a living person in the [[East Asian cultural sphere]], it is done with a {{ill|Sheng Shrine|ja|生祀|zh|生祠}} (Sheng[[Ci shrine|ci]], 生祠).<ref>{{Cite web |title=为关心民生疾苦而设的生祠，在明代起着什么样的作用？_政治_祭祀_陈天祥 |url=https://www.sohu.com/a/www.sohu.com/a/531809387_120005162 |access-date=2022-12-18 |website=www.sohu.com}}{{dead link|date=January 2023}}</ref> It is where or worship of a living person is performed. There are two types of enshrinement: enshrining the spirit of a living person who has made a significant contribution and enshrining one's own spirit.
Due to the usage by [[Wei Zhongxian]] they became seen as a sign of corruption and declined in China<ref>{{Cite web |title=生祠并非魏忠贤的专利-凤凰周刊 |url=http://www.ifengweekly.com/detil.php?id=16813 |access-date=2022-12-22 |website=www.ifengweekly.com}}</ref>. However the practice continued in Japan with the worship of the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] until 1945 and the end of [[State Shinto]].<ref name="Earhart">{{cite book |last1=Earhart |first1=H. Byron |url=https://archive.org/details/religioninjapane0000earh |title=Religion in the Japanese experience: sources and interpretations |date=1974 |publisher=Dickenson Pub. Co. |isbn=0822101041 |edition=3rd |location=Encino, Calif. |author1-link=H. Byron Earhart |url-access=registration}}</ref> [[File:Hiraga_Gennai_seishi01s2040.jpg|thumb|200x200px|[[Hiraga Gennai|Hiraga Gennai]]'s birth shrine in [[Hiroshima Prefecture|Hiroshima Prefecture]], [[Fukuyama, Hiroshima|Fukuyama City]], Tomomachi.]]
== Sheng shrines in China == When [[Han Dynasty#Western Han|Former Han]]'s Reunobu was the prime minister of [[Yan Kingdom (Han dynasty)|Yan]], he built his shrine between Yan and [[Qi (state)|Qi]] and called it the "Reun Gong Shrine". Also, when {{ill|Shiqing|zh|石慶}} was the prime minister of Qi, the Qi people built the "Shiqing Shrine". This is the beginning of the birth shrine.<ref>[[Zhao Yi]], "The New New Ancestral Hall", Volume 32, "Ancestral Hall", "This is the beginning of the establishment of an ancestral hall.</ref>
In the [[Tang dynasty]], there were certain restrictions on the construction of monuments and shrines by officials in office. According to the [[Tang Code|Tang Rulings and Ordinances]], those who erected shrines or monuments without permission were subject to a penalty of one year of imprisonment.<ref>The [[Tang Code]], Volume 11 (The Law of Office)</ref> The Tang Dynasty called it "the difficulty of building monuments and shrines on the first day.
In the [[Ming dynasty|Ming]], peasants in [[Tengzhou|Tengzhou]] built a shrine to commemorate {{ill|Zhao Bangqing|zh|趙邦清}}, who was appointed to Yanjing for his clean politics. The shrine was built to commemorate him.
During the reign of the [[Tianqi Emperor]] of the Ming Dynasty, the eunuch [[Wei Zhongxian]], ruled the world by conducting his own politics, and those who flattered him or feared his authority were given a He made a living shrine for him. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing (1627), Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote: "Confucius wrote the Spring and Autumn, and Chung-Hsien wrote the Essentials. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote that Confucius had written the "Spring and Autumn" and Chung-Hsien had written the "Essentials. In April of the 7th year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, [[Yuan Chonghuan]] and {{ill|Yan Mingtai|zh|閻鳴泰}} of the [[Ministry of War (imperial China)|Ministry of War]] made an appeal to the government to praise the merits and demerits of Wei Zhongxian and to establish a living shrine for him in both Qiantu.<ref>"Ming Xi Zong Jizhu": "in April of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianqi, the governor of Thistle Liaoning, Yan Mingtai, and the governor of Fuxing Yuan Chong Huan, praised the merits of Wei Zhongxian, and requested the construction of a shrine in front of Ning, giving the name Mao De." The government of the Liaoning Province has been inscribed in gold and stone, and there is no room for extra duties. The minister's single-mindedness in patrolling, strict warning of the battalion generals, do not dare to greed cowardly selfishness, do not dare to give away the hidden string, change the virtual into the real, into the real, easy timid into brave, to have today. Sustainability ancient internal minister who has the right!" ("Ming Xi Zong seven years of the Imperial Court of the Department of the Experiment" Tianqi six years in October)</ref> After that, Wei Zhongxian's raw shrine was "all over the world", "the cost of each shrine was at most hundreds of thousands, at least tens of thousands", "stolen the people's fortune, invaded the public safe, and was cut down. There are countless trees. " When Huang Yun Yasushi made a statue and greeted the statue, he said, "Goto Sansho," and "leaded the general of Bunbu, lined up under the stairs, and worshiped as the beginning."
[[Wei Zhongxian]] has been strongly contemned into modern times for his behavior<ref name=":0" />
[[Gu Yanwu|Gu Yanwu]] lamented, "In this generation, there is no official who does not build a living shrine. And within a few years of leaving office, he would destroy the statues and change their masters.<ref>《Nichi Chi Roku》 vol. 23 (Sheng Shi), p. 644.</ref>
Recently{{When|date=2020-12<!--article says it was recently so likely 2020-->}} a person in China created their own Sheng shrine and stirred online controversy<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |last=网易 |date=2020-12-14 |title=魏忠贤为自己生祠揭幕 |url=https://www.163.com/dy/article/FTRIN9CF0521Q1HR.html |access-date=2022-12-18 |website=www.163.com}}</ref>
== Living worship in Japan == Seishi carried out to worship one's own soul was to live a long life or become Kami (god) after death.
It is thought that this originated in the tradition that [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi]] (chief god of Izumo in southern Honshu Island, Japan, and the central character in the important cycle of myths set in that region) worshipped his own Kushimitama (the wondrous, miraculous, or salubrious) and Sakimitama (the propitious) in [[Mount Miwa]]
The oldest case of someone worshipping their own soul was related to Haruhiko MATSUKI (824 - 924)in the Heian period when he was a Shinto priest of Geku (outer shrine) in Ise-jingu Shrine, he worshipped his soul included in stones or rocks in Obe, Watarai-gun, Ise in 923.
There is a case where Sadanobu MATSUDAIRA carried out Seishi for himself at Oshu Shirakawa-jo Castle in 1797 in the Edo period.
The number of Seishi increased in the Edo period, and this was probably due to the influence of Chinese thought.
In the Edo period, {{Ill|Yamazaki Ansai|ja|山崎闇斎}} established rules for ritual procedures based on a ceremony style for Confucianism and worshipped his soul. The Seishi was carried out in his residence, Suika (Shidemasu)-Reisha Shrine in Kyoto in 1671. After that, Shintoists and scholars of Japanese classical literature in Hirata-ha school worshipped their own souls in accordance with their established ritual procedures.
Genchi KATO (1873-1965) is well-known for his research on Seishi. According to Kato, 60 to 70 Seishi were identified and the latest Seishi is the one for Chugi NISHI which was enshrined in August 1931 in Urakawa-cho, Hidaka Province, Hokkaido Prefecture.
[[Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji's]] Seishi was built in Port of Ishinomaki in Miyagi Prefecture in 1876, and also in the site of Yahiko-jinja Shrine in Ono-mura, Kamiina-gun in 1893. The Seishi of Emperor Meiji and [[Empress Shōken|Empress Dowager Shoken]] was each built in two locations in 1887 and 1904, the Seishi of Emperor Taisho was built in Monbetsu-mura, Hidaka Province, Hokkaido Prefecture in 1911, the Seishi of Empress Teimei was built in Hirose-machi, Hiroshima Prefecture in 1913, and the Seishi of Prince Chichibunomiya and Prince Takamatsunomiya was each built in Doshin-machi, Himeji City in 1924. The Seishi of Emperor Showa was built on Mt. Yoshitsune, Honbetsu-cho, Tokachi Province, Hokkaido Prefecture in 1879, and the Seishi of Emperor Showa and Empress Kojun was each built in Hirose-machi, Hiroshima City in 1928.
== References == <references />
== See Also ==
* [[Tamaya]] * {{ill|Okuto Castle|ja|奥都城}}

== Living worship in Japan == Seishi carried out to worship one's own soul was to live a long life or become Kami (god) after death.
It is thought that this originated in the tradition that [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi]] (chief god of Izumo in southern Honshu Island, Japan, and the central character in the important cycle of myths set in that region) worshipped his own Kushimitama (the wondrous, miraculous, or salubrious) and Sakimitama (the propitious) in [[Mount Miwa]]
The oldest case of someone worshipping their own soul was related to Haruhiko MATSUKI (824 - 924)in the Heian period when he was a Shinto priest of Geku (outer shrine) in Ise-jingu Shrine, he worshipped his soul included in stones or rocks in Obe, Watarai-gun, Ise in 923.
There is a case where Sadanobu MATSUDAIRA carried out Seishi for himself at Oshu Shirakawa-jo Castle in 1797 in the Edo period.
The number of Seishi increased in the Edo period, and this was probably due to the influence of Chinese thought.
In the Edo period, {{Ill|Yamazaki Ansai|ja|山崎闇斎}} established rules for ritual procedures based on a ceremony style for Confucianism and worshipped his soul. The Seishi was carried out in his residence, Suika (Shidemasu)-Reisha Shrine in Kyoto in 1671. After that, Shintoists and scholars of Japanese classical literature in Hirata-ha school worshipped their own souls in accordance with their established ritual procedures.
Genchi KATO (1873-1965) is well-known for his research on Seishi. According to Kato, 60 to 70 Seishi were identified and the latest Seishi is the one for Chugi NISHI which was enshrined in August 1931 in Urakawa-cho, Hidaka Province, Hokkaido Prefecture.
[[Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji's]] Seishi was built in Port of Ishinomaki in Miyagi Prefecture in 1876, and also in the site of Yahiko-jinja Shrine in Ono-mura, Kamiina-gun in 1893. The Seishi of Emperor Meiji and [[Empress Shōken|Empress Dowager Shoken]] was each built in two locations in 1887 and 1904, the Seishi of Emperor Taisho was built in Monbetsu-mura, Hidaka Province, Hokkaido Prefecture in 1911, the Seishi of Empress Teimei was built in Hirose-machi, Hiroshima Prefecture in 1913, and the Seishi of Prince Chichibunomiya and Prince Takamatsunomiya was each built in Doshin-machi, Himeji City in 1924. The Seishi of Emperor Showa was built on Mt. Yoshitsune, Honbetsu-cho, Tokachi Province, Hokkaido Prefecture in 1879, and the Seishi of Emperor Showa and Empress Kojun was each built in Hirose-machi, Hiroshima City in 1928.

{{Short description|Shrine to a living person in Shinto and Chinese folk religion}} {{Draft topics|biography|east-asia}} {{AfC topic|geo}}
<!-- Note: The following pages were redirects to [[Sheng_Shrine]] before draftification: *[[Seishi (Shinto)]] --> {{Nihongo|Living worship|2=生祀|3=Seishi}} is worship of a living person in the [[East Asian cultural sphere]], it is done with a {{ill|Sheng Shrine|ja|生祀|zh|生祠}} (Sheng[[Ci shrine|ci]], 生祠).<ref>{{Cite web |title=为关心民生疾苦而设的生祠，在明代起着什么样的作用？_政治_祭祀_陈天祥 |url=https://www.sohu.com/a/www.sohu.com/a/531809387_120005162 |access-date=2022-12-18 |website=www.sohu.com}}{{dead link|date=January 2023}}</ref> It is where or worship of a living person is performed. There are two types of enshrinement: enshrining the spirit of a living person who has made a significant contribution and enshrining one's own spirit.
Due to the usage by [[Wei Zhongxian]] they became seen as a sign of corruption and declined in China.<ref>{{Cite web |title=生祠并非魏忠贤的专利-凤凰周刊 |url=http://www.ifengweekly.com/detil.php?id=16813 |access-date=2022-12-22 |website=www.ifengweekly.com|trans-title=Shengci is not Wei Zhongxian's invention}}</ref> However the practice continued in Japan with the worship of the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] until 1945 and the end of [[State Shinto]].<ref name="Earhart">{{cite book |last1=Earhart |first1=H. Byron |url=https://archive.org/details/religioninjapane0000earh |title=Religion in the Japanese experience: sources and interpretations |date=1974 |publisher=Dickenson Pub. Co. |isbn=0822101041 |edition=3rd |location=Encino, Calif. |author1-link=H. Byron Earhart |url-access=registration}}</ref> [[File:Hiraga_Gennai_seishi01s2040.jpg|thumb|200x200px|[[Hiraga Gennai|Hiraga Gennai]]'s birth shrine in [[Hiroshima Prefecture|Hiroshima Prefecture]], [[Fukuyama, Hiroshima|Fukuyama City]], Tomomachi.]]
== Sheng shrines in China == When [[Han Dynasty#Western Han|Former Han]]'s Reunobu was the prime minister of [[Yan Kingdom (Han dynasty)|Yan]], he built his shrine between Yan and [[Qi (state)|Qi]] and called it the "Reun Gong Shrine". Also, when {{ill|Shiqing|zh|石慶}} was the prime minister of Qi, the Qi people built the "Shiqing Shrine". This is the beginning of the birth shrine.<ref>[[Zhao Yi]], "The New New Ancestral Hall", Volume 32, "Ancestral Hall", "This is the beginning of the establishment of an ancestral hall.</ref>
In the [[Tang dynasty]], there were certain restrictions on the construction of monuments and shrines by officials in office. According to the [[Tang Code|Tang Rulings and Ordinances]], those who erected shrines or monuments without permission were subject to a penalty of one year of imprisonment.<ref>The [[Tang Code]], Volume 11 (The Law of Office)</ref> The Tang Dynasty called it "the difficulty of building monuments and shrines on the first day.
In the [[Ming dynasty|Ming]], peasants in [[Tengzhou|Tengzhou]] built a shrine to commemorate {{ill|Zhao Bangqing|zh|趙邦清}}, who was appointed to Yanjing for his clean politics. The shrine was built to commemorate him.
During the reign of the [[Tianqi Emperor]] of the Ming Dynasty, the eunuch [[Wei Zhongxian]], ruled the world by conducting his own politics, and those who flattered him or feared his authority were given a He made a living shrine for him. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing (1627), Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote: "Confucius wrote the Spring and Autumn, and Chung-Hsien wrote the Essentials. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote that Confucius had written the "Spring and Autumn" and Chung-Hsien had written the "Essentials. In April of the 7th year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, [[Yuan Chonghuan]] and {{ill|Yan Mingtai|zh|閻鳴泰}} of the [[Ministry of War (imperial China)|Ministry of War]] made an appeal to the government to praise the merits and demerits of Wei Zhongxian and to establish a living shrine for him in both Qiantu.<ref>"Ming Xi Zong Jizhu": "in April of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianqi, the governor of Thistle Liaoning, Yan Mingtai, and the governor of Fuxing Yuan Chong Huan, praised the merits of Wei Zhongxian, and requested the construction of a shrine in front of Ning, giving the name Mao De." The government of the Liaoning Province has been inscribed in gold and stone, and there is no room for extra duties. The minister's single-mindedness in patrolling, strict warning of the battalion generals, do not dare to greed cowardly selfishness, do not dare to give away the hidden string, change the virtual into the real, into the real, easy timid into brave, to have today. Sustainability ancient internal minister who has the right!" ("Ming Xi Zong seven years of the Imperial Court of the Department of the Experiment" Tianqi six years in October)</ref> After that, Wei Zhongxian's raw shrine was "all over the world", "the cost of each shrine was at most hundreds of thousands, at least tens of thousands", "stolen the people's fortune, invaded the public safe, and was cut down. There are countless trees. " When Huang Yun Yasushi made a statue and greeted the statue, he said, "Goto Sansho," and "leaded the general of Bunbu, lined up under the stairs, and worshiped as the beginning."
[[Wei Zhongxian]] has been strongly contemned into modern times for his behavior<ref name=":0" />
[[Gu Yanwu|Gu Yanwu]] lamented, "In this generation, there is no official who does not build a living shrine. And within a few years of leaving office, he would destroy the statues and change their masters.<ref>《Nichi Chi Roku》 vol. 23 (Sheng Shi), p. 644.</ref>
Recently{{When|date=2020-12<!--article says it was recently so likely 2020-->}} a person in China created their own Sheng shrine and stirred online controversy<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |last=网易 |date=2020-12-14 |title=魏忠贤为自己生祠揭幕 |url=https://www.163.com/dy/article/FTRIN9CF0521Q1HR.html |access-date=2022-12-18 |website=www.163.com}}</ref>
The sacred and secular life of the Ming Dynasty were always intertwined. "Little Destiny: Shrines and Ming Politics" focuses on two aspects of these debates that are often dismissed as fictional stories. The book examines the political participation of ordinary people and the establishment of shrines for the living. It has been argued that rhetorical expressions of the sanctity of Ming rule actually empowered subjects' political participation. Ancestral halls and temples, large and small, were widely distributed within the territory of the dynasty. <ref name=":1">{{Cite web |title=为关心民生疾苦而设的生祠，在明代起着什么样的作用？_政治_祭祀_陈天祥 |url=https://www.sohu.com/a/www.sohu.com/a/531809387_120005162 |access-date=2023-01-12 |website=www.sohu.com}}</ref>
They worship gods, honor role models, convey traditional values, and provide assistance to those who pray. The concept of shengci was also present in the politics of the Ming Dynasty. Reformers derided living shrines as a corrupt and backward tradition, and historians tend to ignore it or ignore it because of its absurdity. The role of Shengci in the power struggle between Wei Zhongxian and the Donglin party members may have led to their emergence.<ref name=":1" />
Most Ming people were influenced by the idea of the unity of the [[Sanyi teaching|three religions]] - Buddhism, Taoism and China's ancient spiritual traditions. Shengci first appeared in the Eastern Han Dynasty and continued throughout the ages. In the Song Dynasty, various types of ancestral halls increased in large numbers. From the Liaodong Peninsula to Guizhou and Yunnan, from Gansu to Leizhou, Shengci have appeared frequently. <ref name=":1" />
Commemorative buildings can be seen everywhere in the Ming Dynasty. Professional bureaucrats know that they can win local commemorations, including praise and criticism from family members, colleagues, and later generations of scholar-bureaucrats. The establishment of ancestral halls is centered on Confucian filial piety, which is not limited to kindness of nurturing and support between parents and children. Ming people know that the establishment of Shengci includes both sincere gratitude and flattery to the powerful. Study this system can deepen the understanding of Ming people's thinking and behavior.<ref name=":1" />
Research into sheng shrines reveals the possibility of political speech and political action in the Ming state. Since the 1980s, research on the "public sphere" in the late imperial period has flourished. The academic community generally believes it is a recognized field where issues of national governance can be discussed. In the Ming Dynasty, from the emperor down, one could not only have access to cheap books, but also access to religious-related knowledge. Even though a small number of elites still control the publication and writing of political speeches, Yangming Xinxue declares that "everyone can be a Yao and Shun".<ref name=":1" />
Ming embodies what Pierre-E'tienne Will calls a "deeply internalized principle of egalitarianism". Ming Taizu also gave almost all subjects the right to submit a letter to correct the bad governance. In the later period of his rule, he also provided a way for civilians to report and even arrest officials.<ref name=":1" />
Life sacrifice may have become a way of flattery in the officialdom to win the appreciation of superiors. Respecting the ancestors of officials may have the same effect, but the problem of sacrificial offerings is more prominent. Because of the contradictions and tensions it presents, the Shengci Temple in the Ming Dynasty is worthy of careful inspection.<ref name=":1" />
== Living worship in Japan == Seishi carried out to worship one's own soul was to live a long life or become Kami (god) after death.
It is thought that this originated in the tradition that [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi]] (chief god of Izumo in southern Honshu Island, Japan, and the central character in the important cycle of myths set in that region) worshipped his own Kushimitama (the wondrous, miraculous, or salubrious) and Sakimitama (the propitious) in [[Mount Miwa]]
The oldest case of someone worshipping their own soul was related to Haruhiko MATSUKI (824 - 924)in the Heian period when he was a Shinto priest of Geku (outer shrine) in Ise-jingu Shrine, he worshipped his soul included in stones or rocks in Obe, Watarai-gun, Ise in 923.
There is a case where Sadanobu MATSUDAIRA carried out Seishi for himself at Oshu Shirakawa-jo Castle in 1797 in the Edo period.
The number of Seishi increased in the Edo period, and this was probably due to the influence of Chinese thought.
In the Edo period, {{Ill|Yamazaki Ansai|ja|山崎闇斎}} established rules for ritual procedures based on a ceremony style for Confucianism and worshipped his soul. The Seishi was carried out in his residence, Suika (Shidemasu)-Reisha Shrine in Kyoto in 1671. After that, Shintoists and scholars of Japanese classical literature in Hirata-ha school worshipped their own souls in accordance with their established ritual procedures.
Genchi KATO (1873-1965) is well-known for his research on Seishi. According to Kato, 60 to 70 Seishi were identified and the latest Seishi is the one for Chugi NISHI which was enshrined in August 1931 in Urakawa-cho, Hidaka Province, Hokkaido Prefecture.
[[Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji's]] Seishi was built in Port of Ishinomaki in Miyagi Prefecture in 1876, and also in the site of Yahiko-jinja Shrine in Ono-mura, Kamiina-gun in 1893. The Seishi of Emperor Meiji and [[Empress Shōken|Empress Dowager Shoken]] was each built in two locations in 1887 and 1904, the Seishi of Emperor Taisho was built in Monbetsu-mura, Hidaka Province, Hokkaido Prefecture in 1911, the Seishi of Empress Teimei was built in Hirose-machi, Hiroshima Prefecture in 1913, and the Seishi of Prince Chichibunomiya and Prince Takamatsunomiya was each built in Doshin-machi, Himeji City in 1924. The Seishi of Emperor Showa was built on Mt. Yoshitsune, Honbetsu-cho, Tokachi Province, Hokkaido Prefecture in 1879, and the Seishi of Emperor Showa and Empress Kojun was each built in Hirose-machi, Hiroshima City in 1928.

The Seishi of Emperor Meiji and [[Empress Shōken|Empress Dowager Shoken]] was each built in two locations in 1887 and 1904, the Seishi of Emperor Taisho was built in Monbetsu-mura, Hidaka Province, Hokkaido Prefecture in 1911, the Seishi of Empress Teimei was built in Hirose-machi, Hiroshima Prefecture in 1913, and the Seishi of Prince Chichibunomiya and Prince Takamatsunomiya was each built in Doshin-machi, Himeji City in 1924. The Seishi of Emperor Showa was built on Mt. Yoshitsune, Honbetsu-cho, Tokachi Province, Hokkaido Prefecture in 1879, and the Seishi of Emperor Showa and Empress Kojun was each built in Hirose-machi, Hiroshima City in 1928.

{{Short description|Shrine to a living person in Shinto and Chinese folk religion}} {{Draft topics|biography|east-asia}} {{AfC topic|geo}}
<!-- Note: The following pages were redirects to [[Sheng_Shrine]] before draftification: *[[Seishi (Shinto)]] --> {{Nihongo|Living worship|2=生祀|3=Seishi}} is worship of a living person in the [[East Asian cultural sphere]], it is done with a {{ill|Sheng Shrine|ja|生祀|zh|生祠}} (Sheng[[Ci shrine|ci]], 生祠).<ref name=":1" /> It is where or worship of a living person is performed. There are two types of enshrinement: enshrining the spirit of a living person who has made a significant contribution and enshrining one's own spirit.
Due to the usage by [[Wei Zhongxian]] they became seen as a sign of corruption and declined in China.<ref name=":2">{{Cite web |title=生祠并非魏忠贤的专利-凤凰周刊 |url=http://www.ifengweekly.com/detil.php?id=16813 |access-date=2022-12-22 |website=www.ifengweekly.com|trans-title=Shengci is not Wei Zhongxian's invention}}</ref> However the practice continued in Japan with the worship of the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] until 1945 and the end of [[State Shinto]].<ref name="Earhart">{{cite book |last1=Earhart |first1=H. Byron |url=https://archive.org/details/religioninjapane0000earh |title=Religion in the Japanese experience: sources and interpretations |date=1974 |publisher=Dickenson Pub. Co. |isbn=0822101041 |edition=3rd |location=Encino, Calif. |author1-link=H. Byron Earhart |url-access=registration}}</ref> [[File:Hiraga_Gennai_seishi01s2040.jpg|thumb|200x200px|[[Hiraga Gennai|Hiraga Gennai]]'s birth shrine in [[Hiroshima Prefecture|Hiroshima Prefecture]], [[Fukuyama, Hiroshima|Fukuyama City]], Tomomachi.]]
== Sheng shrines in China == When [[Han Dynasty#Western Han|Former Han]]'s Reunobu was the prime minister of [[Yan Kingdom (Han dynasty)|Yan]], he built his shrine between Yan and [[Qi (state)|Qi]] and called it the "Reun Gong Shrine". Also, when {{ill|Shiqing|zh|石慶}} was the prime minister of Qi, the Qi people built the "Shiqing Shrine". This is the beginning of the birth shrine.<ref>[[Zhao Yi]], "The New New Ancestral Hall", Volume 32, "Ancestral Hall", "This is the beginning of the establishment of an ancestral hall.</ref>
In the [[Tang dynasty]], there were certain restrictions on the construction of monuments and shrines by officials in office. According to the [[Tang Code|Tang Rulings and Ordinances]], those who erected shrines or monuments without permission were subject to a penalty of one year of imprisonment.<ref>The [[Tang Code]], Volume 11 (The Law of Office)</ref> The Tang Dynasty called it "the difficulty of building monuments and shrines on the first day.
In the [[Ming dynasty|Ming]], peasants in [[Tengzhou|Tengzhou]] built a shrine to commemorate {{ill|Zhao Bangqing|zh|趙邦清}}, who was appointed to Yanjing for his clean politics. The shrine was built to commemorate him.
During the reign of the [[Tianqi Emperor]] of the Ming Dynasty, the eunuch [[Wei Zhongxian]], ruled the world by conducting his own politics, and those who flattered him or feared his authority were given a He made a living shrine for him. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing (1627), Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote: "Confucius wrote the Spring and Autumn, and Chung-Hsien wrote the Essentials. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote that Confucius had written the "Spring and Autumn" and Chung-Hsien had written the "Essentials. In April of the 7th year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, [[Yuan Chonghuan]] and {{ill|Yan Mingtai|zh|閻鳴泰}} of the [[Ministry of War (imperial China)|Ministry of War]] made an appeal to the government to praise the merits and demerits of Wei Zhongxian and to establish a living shrine for him in both Qiantu.<ref>"Ming Xi Zong Jizhu": "in April of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianqi, the governor of Thistle Liaoning, Yan Mingtai, and the governor of Fuxing Yuan Chong Huan, praised the merits of Wei Zhongxian, and requested the construction of a shrine in front of Ning, giving the name Mao De." The government of the Liaoning Province has been inscribed in gold and stone, and there is no room for extra duties. The minister's single-mindedness in patrolling, strict warning of the battalion generals, do not dare to greed cowardly selfishness, do not dare to give away the hidden string, change the virtual into the real, into the real, easy timid into brave, to have today. Sustainability ancient internal minister who has the right!" ("Ming Xi Zong seven years of the Imperial Court of the Department of the Experiment" Tianqi six years in October)</ref> After that, Wei Zhongxian's raw shrine was "all over the world", "the cost of each shrine was at most hundreds of thousands, at least tens of thousands", "stolen the people's fortune, invaded the public safe, and was cut down. There are countless trees. " When Huang Yun Yasushi made a statue and greeted the statue, he said, "Goto Sansho," and "leaded the general of Bunbu, lined up under the stairs, and worshiped as the beginning."
[[Wei Zhongxian]] has been strongly contemned into modern times for his behavior<ref name=":0" />
[[Gu Yanwu|Gu Yanwu]] lamented, "In this generation, there is no official who does not build a living shrine. And within a few years of leaving office, he would destroy the statues and change their masters.<ref>《Nichi Chi Roku》 vol. 23 (Sheng Shi), p. 644.</ref>
Recently{{When|date=2020-12<!--article says it was recently so likely 2020-->}} a person in China created their own Sheng shrine and stirred online controversy<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |last=网易 |date=2020-12-14 |title=魏忠贤为自己生祠揭幕 |url=https://www.163.com/dy/article/FTRIN9CF0521Q1HR.html |access-date=2022-12-18 |website=www.163.com}}</ref>
The sacred and secular life of the Ming Dynasty were always intertwined. "Little Destiny: Shrines and Ming Politics" focuses on two aspects of these debates that are often dismissed as fictional stories. The book examines the political participation of ordinary people and the establishment of shrines for the living. It has been argued that rhetorical expressions of the sanctity of Ming rule actually empowered subjects' political participation. Ancestral halls and temples, large and small, were widely distributed within the territory of the dynasty. <ref name=":1">{{Cite web |date=2022-12-18 |title=为关心民生疾苦而设的生祠，在明代起着什么样的作用？_政治_祭祀_陈天祥 |url=http://web.archive.org/web/20221218062727/https://www.sohu.com/a/531809387_120005162 |access-date=2023-01-12 |website=web.archive.org}}</ref>
They worship gods, honor role models, convey traditional values, and provide assistance to those who pray. The concept of shengci was also present in the politics of the Ming Dynasty. Reformers derided living shrines as a corrupt and backward tradition, and historians tend to ignore it or ignore it because of its absurdity. The role of Shengci in the power struggle between Wei Zhongxian and the Donglin party members may have led to their emergence.<ref name=":1" />
Most Ming people were influenced by the idea of the unity of the [[Sanyi teaching|three religions]] - Buddhism, Taoism and China's ancient spiritual traditions. Shengci first appeared in the Eastern Han Dynasty and continued throughout the ages. In the Song Dynasty, various types of ancestral halls increased in large numbers. From the Liaodong Peninsula to Guizhou and Yunnan, from Gansu to Leizhou, Shengci have appeared frequently. <ref name=":1" />
Commemorative buildings can be seen everywhere in the Ming Dynasty. Professional bureaucrats know that they can win local commemorations, including praise and criticism from family members, colleagues, and later generations of scholar-bureaucrats. The establishment of ancestral halls is centered on Confucian filial piety, which is not limited to kindness of nurturing and support between parents and children. Ming people know that the establishment of Shengci includes both sincere gratitude and flattery to the powerful. Study this system can deepen the understanding of Ming people's thinking and behavior.<ref name=":1" />
Research into sheng shrines reveals the possibility of political speech and political action in the Ming state. Since the 1980s, research on the "public sphere" in the late imperial period has flourished. The academic community generally believes it is a recognized field where issues of national governance can be discussed. In the Ming Dynasty, from the emperor down, one could not only have access to cheap books, but also access to religious-related knowledge. Even though a small number of elites still control the publication and writing of political speeches, Yangming Xinxue declares that "everyone can be a Yao and Shun".<ref name=":1" />
Ming embodies what Pierre-E'tienne Will calls a "deeply internalized principle of egalitarianism". Ming Taizu also gave almost all subjects the right to submit a letter to correct the bad governance. In the later period of his rule, he also provided a way for civilians to report and even arrest officials.<ref name=":1" />
Life sacrifice may have become a way of flattery in the officialdom to win the appreciation of superiors. Respecting the ancestors of officials may have the same effect, but the problem of sacrificial offerings is more prominent. Because of the contradictions and tensions it presents, the Shengci Temple in the Ming Dynasty is worthy of careful inspection.<ref name=":1" />
The sheng shrine is a form of memorial to an official in recognition of his love for the people and good governance. American sinologist Sarah Schneewind explains the full extent of the practice of living worship in the Ming Dynasty. Shi Shanshan coined the term "small fate" for the phenomenon. Wei Zhongxian's shrine is a special case that is so dazzling that it overshadows most of the other shrines. The people enshrined in the shrine are traitors and traitors, and their motivation was top-down.<ref name=":2" />
Only the deceased were entitled to enjoy the sacrifice, and sacrificing the living was a wrongdoing. The image shows a governor of Zhejiang Province being detained by the gentry when he leaves his post. In the background is a picture of the watery countryside in the south of the Yangtze River. At the top of the picture is an explanation of the words "I spill the tears of all the people". Sheng shrines were a fairly common phenomenon in the Ming generation, and the later they were, the more numerous they became.<ref name=":2" />
One explanation is that the gentry became more and more sycophantic, leading to the proliferation of sheng shrines. The impetus for the establishment of the shrine was bottom-up, and sometimes even conflicted with top-down demands.<ref name=":2" />
Could the sheng shrine be a self-aggrandizement like the "miracle hand" banner in the clinic? One of the more striking features of the act of naming is the lack of bottom-up activism. Shi Shanshan writes: "The Ming dynasty had strict control over the awarding of chastity and martyrdom". The ancestral hall was built in the 36th year of the Wanli reign (1608). Ten government students drafted a fund-raising letter, raising 485 taels.<ref name=":2" />
Under such conditions, it was not uncommon for ancestral halls to become extinct. Some survived for tens or even hundreds of years, even into the Kangxi period. Zhang Juzheng, who was the chief minister of the cabinet in the early Wanli period (1572-1582), opposed the establishment of a sheng shrine. The "achievements" from the perspective of superiors and subordinates, central and local, are often different or even opposite.<ref name=":2" />
== Living worship in Japan == Seishi carried out to worship one's own soul was to live a long life or become Kami (god) after death.
It is thought that this originated in the tradition that [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi]] (chief god of Izumo in southern Honshu Island, Japan, and the central character in the important cycle of myths set in that region) worshipped his own Kushimitama (the wondrous, miraculous, or salubrious) and Sakimitama (the propitious) in [[Mount Miwa]]
The oldest case of someone worshipping their own soul was related to Haruhiko MATSUKI (824 - 924)in the Heian period when he was a Shinto priest of Geku (outer shrine) in Ise-jingu Shrine, he worshipped his soul included in stones or rocks in Obe, Watarai-gun, Ise in 923.
There is a case where Sadanobu MATSUDAIRA carried out Seishi for himself at Oshu Shirakawa-jo Castle in 1797 in the Edo period.
The number of Seishi increased in the Edo period, and this was probably due to the influence of Chinese thought.
In the Edo period, {{Ill|Yamazaki Ansai|ja|山崎闇斎}} established rules for ritual procedures based on a ceremony style for Confucianism and worshipped his soul. The Seishi was carried out in his residence, Suika (Shidemasu)-Reisha Shrine in Kyoto in 1671. After that, Shintoists and scholars of Japanese classical literature in Hirata-ha school worshipped their own souls in accordance with their established ritual procedures.
Genchi KATO (1873-1965) is well-known for his research on Seishi. According to Kato, 60 to 70 Seishi were identified and the latest Seishi is the one for Chugi NISHI which was enshrined in August 1931 in Urakawa-cho, Hidaka Province, Hokkaido Prefecture.
[[Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji's]] Seishi was built in Port of Ishinomaki in Miyagi Prefecture in 1876, and also in the site of Yahiko-jinja Shrine in Ono-mura, Kamiina-gun in 1893. The Seishi of Emperor Meiji and [[Empress Shōken|Empress Dowager Shoken]] was each built in two locations in 1887 and 1904, the Seishi of Emperor Taisho was built in Monbetsu-mura, Hidaka Province, Hokkaido Prefecture in 1911, the Seishi of Empress Teimei was built in Hirose-machi, Hiroshima Prefecture in 1913, and the Seishi of Prince Chichibunomiya and Prince Takamatsunomiya was each built in Doshin-machi, Himeji City in 1924. The Seishi of Emperor Showa was built on Mt. Yoshitsune, Honbetsu-cho, Tokachi Province, Hokkaido Prefecture in 1879, and the Seishi of Emperor Showa and Empress Kojun was each built in Hirose-machi, Hiroshima City in 1928.

Zhang Juzheng, who was the chief minister of the cabinet in the early Wanli period (1572-1582), opposed the establishment of a sheng shrine. The "achievements" from the perspective of superiors and subordinates, central and local, are often different or even opposite.<ref name=":2" />
A living man unveiled a statue of himself, many netizens feel that this is really live. In fact, there is nothing new under the sun, then absurd things in history can find similar cases, can rhyme. This is someone bold innovation, horseshit shot a new height. In Kaifeng, more than 2,000 houses were demolished to build the shrine. The shrine was built with a palace of nine pillars, with the appearance of an emperor. <ref name=":0" />
Even Yuan Chonghuan, a great general at the end of Ming Dynasty, built two of Wei Zhongxian's shrines. It may be true that the incense of the ancestral shrines bent Wei Zhongxian, indeed, not a few years, the death of Emperor Tianqi, soon after Chongzhen came to power, we saw his birth shrine smashed. The first time to build how hard, later smashed how hard. And at the time of smashing the birth shrine, the great savior has become a big bad guy again!<ref name=":0" />
== Living worship in Japan == Seishi carried out to worship one's own soul was to live a long life or become Kami (god) after death.
It is thought that this originated in the tradition that [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi]] (chief god of Izumo in southern Honshu Island, Japan, and the central character in the important cycle of myths set in that region) worshipped his own Kushimitama (the wondrous, miraculous, or salubrious) and Sakimitama (the propitious) in [[Mount Miwa]]
The oldest case of someone worshipping their own soul was related to Haruhiko MATSUKI (824 - 924)in the Heian period when he was a Shinto priest of Geku (outer shrine) in Ise-jingu Shrine, he worshipped his soul included in stones or rocks in Obe, Watarai-gun, Ise in 923.
There is a case where Sadanobu MATSUDAIRA carried out Seishi for himself at Oshu Shirakawa-jo Castle in 1797 in the Edo period.
The number of Seishi increased in the Edo period, and this was probably due to the influence of Chinese thought.
In the Edo period, {{Ill|Yamazaki Ansai|ja|山崎闇斎}} established rules for ritual procedures based on a ceremony style for Confucianism and worshipped his soul. The Seishi was carried out in his residence, Suika (Shidemasu)-Reisha Shrine in Kyoto in 1671. After that, Shintoists and scholars of Japanese classical literature in Hirata-ha school worshipped their own souls in accordance with their established ritual procedures.
Genchi KATO (1873-1965) is well-known for his research on Seishi. According to Kato, 60 to 70 Seishi were identified and the latest Seishi is the one for Chugi NISHI which was enshrined in August 1931 in Urakawa-cho, Hidaka Province, Hokkaido Prefecture.
[[Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji's]] Seishi was built in Port of Ishinomaki in Miyagi Prefecture in 1876, and also in the site of Yahiko-jinja Shrine in Ono-mura, Kamiina-gun in 1893. The Seishi of Emperor Meiji and [[Empress Shōken|Empress Dowager Shoken]] was each built in two locations in 1887 and 1904, the Seishi of Emperor Taisho was built in Monbetsu-mura, Hidaka Province, Hokkaido Prefecture in 1911, the Seishi of Empress Teimei was built in Hirose-machi, Hiroshima Prefecture in 1913, and the Seishi of Prince Chichibunomiya and Prince Takamatsunomiya was each built in Doshin-machi, Himeji City in 1924. The Seishi of Emperor Showa was built on Mt. Yoshitsune, Honbetsu-cho, Tokachi Province, Hokkaido Prefecture in 1879, and the Seishi of Emperor Showa and Empress Kojun was each built in Hirose-machi, Hiroshima City in 1928.

{{Short description|Shrine to a living person in Shinto and Chinese folk religion}} {{Draft topics|biography|east-asia}} {{AfC topic|geo}}
<!-- Note: The following pages were redirects to [[Sheng_Shrine]] before draftification: *[[Seishi (Shinto)]] --> {{Nihongo|Living worship|2=生祀|3=Seishi}} is worship of a living person in the [[East Asian cultural sphere]], it is done with a {{ill|Sheng Shrine|ja|生祀|zh|生祠}} (Sheng[[Ci shrine|ci]], 生祠).<ref name=":1" /> It is where or worship of a living person is performed. There are two types of enshrinement: enshrining the spirit of a living person who has made a significant contribution and enshrining one's own spirit.
Due to the usage by [[Wei Zhongxian]] they became seen as a sign of corruption and declined in China.<ref name=":2">{{Cite web |title=生祠并非魏忠贤的专利-凤凰周刊 |url=http://www.ifengweekly.com/detil.php?id=16813 |access-date=2022-12-22 |website=www.ifengweekly.com|trans-title=Shengci is not Wei Zhongxian's invention}}</ref> However the practice continued in Japan with the worship of the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] until 1945 and the end of [[State Shinto]].<ref name="Earhart">{{cite book |last1=Earhart |first1=H. Byron |url=https://archive.org/details/religioninjapane0000earh |title=Religion in the Japanese experience: sources and interpretations |date=1974 |publisher=Dickenson Pub. Co. |isbn=0822101041 |edition=3rd |location=Encino, Calif. |author1-link=H. Byron Earhart |url-access=registration}}</ref> [[File:Hiraga_Gennai_seishi01s2040.jpg|thumb|200x200px|[[Hiraga Gennai|Hiraga Gennai]]'s birth shrine in [[Hiroshima Prefecture|Hiroshima Prefecture]], [[Fukuyama, Hiroshima|Fukuyama City]], Tomomachi.]]
== Sheng shrines in China == When [[Han Dynasty#Western Han|Former Han]]'s Reunobu was the prime minister of [[Yan Kingdom (Han dynasty)|Yan]], he built his shrine between Yan and [[Qi (state)|Qi]] and called it the "Reun Gong Shrine". Also, when {{ill|Shiqing|zh|石慶}} was the prime minister of Qi, the Qi people built the "Shiqing Shrine". This is the beginning of the birth shrine.<ref>[[Zhao Yi]], "The New New Ancestral Hall", Volume 32, "Ancestral Hall", "This is the beginning of the establishment of an ancestral hall.</ref>
In the [[Tang dynasty]], there were certain restrictions on the construction of monuments and shrines by officials in office. According to the [[Tang Code|Tang Rulings and Ordinances]], those who erected shrines or monuments without permission were subject to a penalty of one year of imprisonment.<ref>The [[Tang Code]], Volume 11 (The Law of Office)</ref> The Tang Dynasty called it "the difficulty of building monuments and shrines on the first day.
In the [[Ming dynasty|Ming]], peasants in [[Tengzhou|Tengzhou]] built a shrine to commemorate {{ill|Zhao Bangqing|zh|趙邦清}}, who was appointed to Yanjing for his clean politics. The shrine was built to commemorate him.
During the reign of the [[Tianqi Emperor]] of the Ming Dynasty, the eunuch [[Wei Zhongxian]], ruled the world by conducting his own politics, and those who flattered him or feared his authority were given a He made a living shrine for him. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing (1627), Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote: "Confucius wrote the Spring and Autumn, and Chung-Hsien wrote the Essentials. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote that Confucius had written the "Spring and Autumn" and Chung-Hsien had written the "Essentials. In April of the 7th year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, [[Yuan Chonghuan]] and {{ill|Yan Mingtai|zh|閻鳴泰}} of the [[Ministry of War (imperial China)|Ministry of War]] made an appeal to the government to praise the merits and demerits of Wei Zhongxian and to establish a living shrine for him in both Qiantu.<ref>"Ming Xi Zong Jizhu": "in April of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianqi, the governor of Thistle Liaoning, Yan Mingtai, and the governor of Fuxing Yuan Chong Huan, praised the merits of Wei Zhongxian, and requested the construction of a shrine in front of Ning, giving the name Mao De." The government of the Liaoning Province has been inscribed in gold and stone, and there is no room for extra duties. The minister's single-mindedness in patrolling, strict warning of the battalion generals, do not dare to greed cowardly selfishness, do not dare to give away the hidden string, change the virtual into the real, into the real, easy timid into brave, to have today. Sustainability ancient internal minister who has the right!" ("Ming Xi Zong seven years of the Imperial Court of the Department of the Experiment" Tianqi six years in October)</ref> After that, Wei Zhongxian's raw shrine was "all over the world", "the cost of each shrine was at most hundreds of thousands, at least tens of thousands", "stolen the people's fortune, invaded the public safe, and was cut down. There are countless trees. " When Huang Yun Yasushi made a statue and greeted the statue, he said, "Goto Sansho," and "leaded the general of Bunbu, lined up under the stairs, and worshiped as the beginning."
[[Wei Zhongxian]] has been strongly contemned into modern times for his behavior<ref name=":0" />
[[Gu Yanwu|Gu Yanwu]] lamented, "In this generation, there is no official who does not build a living shrine. And within a few years of leaving office, he would destroy the statues and change their masters.<ref>《Nichi Chi Roku》 vol. 23 (Sheng Shi), p. 644.</ref>
Recently{{When|date=2020-12<!--article says it was recently so likely 2020-->}} a person in China created their own Sheng shrine and stirred online controversy<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |last=网易 |date=2020-12-14 |title=魏忠贤为自己生祠揭幕 |url=https://www.163.com/dy/article/FTRIN9CF0521Q1HR.html |access-date=2022-12-18 |website=www.163.com}}</ref>
The sacred and secular life of the Ming Dynasty were always intertwined. "Little Destiny: Shrines and Ming Politics" focuses on two aspects of these debates that are often dismissed as fictional stories. The book examines the political participation of ordinary people and the establishment of shrines for the living. It has been argued that rhetorical expressions of the sanctity of Ming rule actually empowered subjects' political participation. Ancestral halls and temples, large and small, were widely distributed within the territory of the dynasty. <ref name=":1">{{Cite web |date=2022-12-18 |title=为关心民生疾苦而设的生祠，在明代起着什么样的作用？_政治_祭祀_陈天祥 |url=http://web.archive.org/web/20221218062727/https://www.sohu.com/a/531809387_120005162 |access-date=2023-01-12 |website=web.archive.org}}</ref>
They worship gods, honor role models, convey traditional values, and provide assistance to those who pray. The concept of shengci was also present in the politics of the Ming Dynasty. Reformers derided living shrines as a corrupt and backward tradition, and historians tend to ignore it or ignore it because of its absurdity. The role of Shengci in the power struggle between Wei Zhongxian and the Donglin party members may have led to their emergence.<ref name=":1" />
Most Ming people were influenced by the idea of the unity of the [[Sanyi teaching|three religions]] - Buddhism, Taoism and China's ancient spiritual traditions. Shengci first appeared in the Eastern Han Dynasty and continued throughout the ages. In the Song Dynasty, various types of ancestral halls increased in large numbers. From the Liaodong Peninsula to Guizhou and Yunnan, from Gansu to Leizhou, Shengci have appeared frequently. <ref name=":1" />
Commemorative buildings can be seen everywhere in the Ming Dynasty. Professional bureaucrats know that they can win local commemorations, including praise and criticism from family members, colleagues, and later generations of scholar-bureaucrats. The establishment of ancestral halls is centered on Confucian filial piety, which is not limited to kindness of nurturing and support between parents and children. Ming people know that the establishment of Shengci includes both sincere gratitude and flattery to the powerful. Study this system can deepen the understanding of Ming people's thinking and behavior.<ref name=":1" />
Research into sheng shrines reveals the possibility of political speech and political action in the Ming state. Since the 1980s, research on the "public sphere" in the late imperial period has flourished. The academic community generally believes it is a recognized field where issues of national governance can be discussed. In the Ming Dynasty, from the emperor down, one could not only have access to cheap books, but also access to religious-related knowledge. Even though a small number of elites still control the publication and writing of political speeches, Yangming Xinxue declares that "everyone can be a Yao and Shun".<ref name=":1" />
Ming embodies what Pierre-E'tienne Will calls a "deeply internalized principle of egalitarianism". Ming Taizu also gave almost all subjects the right to submit a letter to correct the bad governance. In the later period of his rule, he also provided a way for civilians to report and even arrest officials.<ref name=":1" />
Life sacrifice may have become a way of flattery in the officialdom to win the appreciation of superiors. Respecting the ancestors of officials may have the same effect, but the problem of sacrificial offerings is more prominent. Because of the contradictions and tensions it presents, the Shengci Temple in the Ming Dynasty is worthy of careful inspection.<ref name=":1" />
The sheng shrine is a form of memorial to an official in recognition of his love for the people and good governance. American sinologist Sarah Schneewind explains the full extent of the practice of living worship in the Ming Dynasty. Shi Shanshan coined the term "small fate" for the phenomenon. Wei Zhongxian's shrine is a special case that is so dazzling that it overshadows most of the other shrines. The people enshrined in the shrine are traitors and traitors, and their motivation was top-down.<ref name=":2" />
Only the deceased were entitled to enjoy the sacrifice, and sacrificing the living was a wrongdoing. The image shows a governor of Zhejiang Province being detained by the gentry when he leaves his post. In the background is a picture of the watery countryside in the south of the Yangtze River. At the top of the picture is an explanation of the words "I spill the tears of all the people". Sheng shrines were a fairly common phenomenon in the Ming generation, and the later they were, the more numerous they became.<ref name=":2" />
One explanation is that the gentry became more and more sycophantic, leading to the proliferation of sheng shrines. The impetus for the establishment of the shrine was bottom-up, and sometimes even conflicted with top-down demands.<ref name=":2" />
Could the sheng shrine be a self-aggrandizement like the "miracle hand" banner in the clinic? One of the more striking features of the act of naming is the lack of bottom-up activism. Shi Shanshan writes: "The Ming dynasty had strict control over the awarding of chastity and martyrdom". The ancestral hall was built in the 36th year of the Wanli reign (1608). Ten government students drafted a fund-raising letter, raising 485 taels.<ref name=":2" />
Under such conditions, it was not uncommon for ancestral halls to become extinct. Some survived for tens or even hundreds of years, even into the Kangxi period. Zhang Juzheng, who was the chief minister of the cabinet in the early Wanli period (1572-1582), opposed the establishment of a sheng shrine. The "achievements" from the perspective of superiors and subordinates, central and local, are often different or even opposite.<ref name=":2" />
A living man unveiled a statue of himself, many netizens feel that this is really live. In fact, there is nothing new under the sun, then absurd things in history can find similar cases, can rhyme. This is someone bold innovation, horseshit shot a new height. In Kaifeng, more than 2,000 houses were demolished to build the shrine. The shrine was built with a palace of nine pillars, with the appearance of an emperor. <ref name=":0" />
Even Yuan Chonghuan, a great general at the end of Ming Dynasty, built two of Wei Zhongxian's shrines. It may be true that the incense of the ancestral shrines bent Wei Zhongxian, indeed, not a few years, the death of Emperor Tianqi, soon after Chongzhen came to power, we saw his birth shrine smashed. The first time to build how hard, later smashed how hard. And at the time of smashing the birth shrine, the great savior has become a big bad guy again!<ref name=":0" />
== Living worship in Japan == Seishi carried out to worship one's own soul was to live a long life or become Kami (god) after death.
It is thought that this originated in the tradition that [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi]] (chief god of Izumo in southern Honshu Island, Japan, and the central character in the important cycle of myths set in that region) worshipped his own Kushimitama (the wondrous, miraculous, or salubrious) and Sakimitama (the propitious) in [[Mount Miwa]]
The oldest case of someone worshipping their own soul was related to Haruhiko MATSUKI (824 - 924)in the Heian period when he was a Shinto priest of Geku (outer shrine) in Ise-jingu Shrine, he worshipped his soul included in stones or rocks in Obe, Watarai-gun, Ise in 923.
There is a case where Sadanobu MATSUDAIRA carried out Seishi for himself at Oshu Shirakawa-jo Castle in 1797 in the Edo period.
The number of Seishi increased in the Edo period, and this was probably due to the influence of Chinese thought.<ref>{{Cite web |title=「生祀は」の用例・例文集 - 用例.jp |url=http://yourei.jp/%E7%94%9F%E7%A5%80%E3%81%AF |access-date=2023-01-12 |website=yourei.jp}}</ref>
In the Edo period, {{Ill|Yamazaki Ansai|ja|山崎闇斎}} established rules for ritual procedures based on a ceremony style for Confucianism and worshipped his soul. The Seishi was carried out in his residence, Suika (Shidemasu)-Reisha Shrine in Kyoto in 1671. After that, Shintoists and scholars of Japanese classical literature in Hirata-ha school worshipped their own souls in accordance with their established ritual procedures.
Genchi KATO (1873-1965) is well-known for his research on Seishi. According to Kato, 60 to 70 Seishi were identified and the latest Seishi is the one for Chugi NISHI which was enshrined in August 1931 in Urakawa-cho, Hidaka Province, Hokkaido Prefecture.
[[Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji's]] Seishi was built in Port of Ishinomaki in Miyagi Prefecture in 1876, and also in the site of Yahiko-jinja Shrine in Ono-mura, Kamiina-gun in 1893. The Seishi of Emperor Meiji and [[Empress Shōken|Empress Dowager Shoken]] was each built in two locations in 1887 and 1904, the Seishi of Emperor Taisho was built in Monbetsu-mura, Hidaka Province, Hokkaido Prefecture in 1911, the Seishi of Empress Teimei was built in Hirose-machi, Hiroshima Prefecture in 1913, and the Seishi of Prince Chichibunomiya and Prince Takamatsunomiya was each built in Doshin-machi, Himeji City in 1924. The Seishi of Emperor Showa was built on Mt. Yoshitsune, Honbetsu-cho, Tokachi Province, Hokkaido Prefecture in 1879, and the Seishi of Emperor Showa and Empress Kojun was each built in Hirose-machi, Hiroshima City in 1928.
