Reformers derided living shrines as a corrupt and backward tradition, and historians tend to ignore it or ignore it because of its absurdity. The role of Shengci in the power struggle between Wei Zhongxian and the Donglin party members may have led to their emergence.<ref name=":1" />
Most Ming people were influenced by the idea of the unity of the [[Sanyi teaching|three religions]] - Buddhism, Taoism and China's ancient spiritual traditions. Shengci first appeared in the Eastern Han Dynasty and continued throughout the ages. In the Song Dynasty, various types of ancestral halls increased in large numbers. From the Liaodong Peninsula to Guizhou and Yunnan, from Gansu to Leizhou, Shengci have appeared frequently. <ref name=":1" />
Commemorative buildings can be seen everywhere in the Ming Dynasty. Professional bureaucrats know that they can win local commemorations, including praise and criticism from family members, colleagues, and later generations of scholar-bureaucrats. The establishment of ancestral halls is centered on Confucian filial piety, which is not limited to kindness of nurturing and support between parents and children. Ming people know that the establishment of Shengci includes both sincere gratitude and flattery to the powerful. Study this system can deepen the understanding of Ming people's thinking and behavior.<ref name=":1" />
Research into sheng shrines reveals the possibility of political speech and political action in the Ming state. Since the 1980s, research on the "public sphere" in the late imperial period has flourished. The academic community generally believes it is a recognized field where issues of national governance can be discussed. In the Ming Dynasty, from the emperor down, one could not only have access to cheap books, but also access to religious-related knowledge. Even though a small number of elites still control the publication and writing of political speeches, Yangming Xinxue declares that "everyone can be a Yao and Shun".<ref name=":1" />
Ming embodies what Pierre-E'tienne Will calls a "deeply internalized principle of egalitarianism". Ming Taizu also gave almost all subjects the right to submit a letter to correct the bad governance. In the later period of his rule, he also provided a way for civilians to report and even arrest officials.<ref name=":1" />
Life sacrifice may have become a way of flattery in the officialdom to win the appreciation of superiors. Respecting the ancestors of officials may have the same effect, but the problem of sacrificial offerings is more prominent. Because of the contradictions and tensions it presents, the Shengci Temple in the Ming Dynasty is worthy of careful inspection.<ref name=":1" />
The sheng shrine is a form of memorial to an official in recognition of his love for the people and good governance. American sinologist Sarah Schneewind explains the full extent of the practice of living worship in the Ming Dynasty. Shi Shanshan coined the term "small fate" for the phenomenon. Wei Zhongxian's shrine is a special case that is so dazzling that it overshadows most of the other shrines. The people enshrined in the shrine are traitors and traitors, and their motivation was top-down.<ref name=":2" />
Only the deceased were entitled to enjoy the sacrifice, and sacrificing the living was a wrongdoing. The image shows a governor of Zhejiang Province being detained by the gentry when he leaves his post. In the background is a picture of the watery countryside in the south of the Yangtze River. At the top of the picture is an explanation of the words "I spill the tears of all the people". Sheng shrines were a fairly common phenomenon in the Ming generation, and the later they were, the more numerous they became.<ref name=":2" />
One explanation is that the gentry became more and more sycophantic, leading to the proliferation of sheng shrines. The impetus for the establishment of the shrine was bottom-up, and sometimes even conflicted with top-down demands.<ref name=":2" />
Could the sheng shrine be a self-aggrandizement like the "miracle hand" banner in the clinic? One of the more striking features of the act of naming is the lack of bottom-up activism. Shi Shanshan writes: "The Ming dynasty had strict control over the awarding of chastity and martyrdom". The ancestral hall was built in the 36th year of the Wanli reign (1608). Ten government students drafted a fund-raising letter, raising 485 taels.<ref name=":2" />
Under such conditions, it was not uncommon for ancestral halls to become extinct. Some survived for tens or even hundreds of years, even into the Kangxi period. Zhang Juzheng, who was the chief minister of the cabinet in the early Wanli period (1572-1582), opposed the establishment of a sheng shrine. The "achievements" from the perspective of superiors and subordinates, central and local, are often different or even opposite.<ref name=":2" />
A living man unveiled a statue of himself, many netizens feel that this is really live. In fact, there is nothing new under the sun, then absurd things in history can find similar cases, can rhyme. This is someone bold innovation, horseshit shot a new height. In Kaifeng, more than 2,000 houses were demolished to build the shrine. The shrine was built with a palace of nine pillars, with the appearance of an emperor. <ref name=":0" />
Even Yuan Chonghuan, a great general at the end of Ming Dynasty, built two of Wei Zhongxian's shrines. It may be true that the incense of the ancestral shrines bent Wei Zhongxian, indeed, not a few years, the death of Emperor Tianqi, soon after Chongzhen came to power, we saw his birth shrine smashed. The first time to build how hard, later smashed how hard. And at the time of smashing the birth shrine, the great savior has become a big bad guy again!<ref name=":0" />
== Living worship in Japan == Seishi carried out to worship one's own soul was to live a long life or become Kami (god) after death.
It is thought that this originated in the tradition that [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi]] (chief god of Izumo in southern Honshu Island, Japan, and the central character in the important cycle of myths set in that region) worshipped his own Kushimitama (the wondrous, miraculous, or salubrious) and Sakimitama (the propitious) in [[Mount Miwa]]
The oldest case of someone worshipping their own soul was related to Haruhiko MATSUKI (824 - 924)in the Heian period when he was a Shinto priest of Geku (outer shrine) in Ise-jingu Shrine, he worshipped his soul included in stones or rocks in Obe, Watarai-gun, Ise in 923.
There is a case where Sadanobu MATSUDAIRA carried out Seishi for himself at Oshu Shirakawa-jo Castle in 1797 in the Edo period.
The number of Seishi increased in the Edo period, and this was probably due to the influence of Chinese thought.<ref>{{Cite web |title=「生祀は」の用例・例文集 - 用例.jp |url=http://yourei.jp/%E7%94%9F%E7%A5%80%E3%81%AF |access-date=2023-01-12 |website=yourei.jp}}</ref>
In the Edo period, {{Ill|Yamazaki Ansai|ja|山崎闇斎}} established rules for ritual procedures based on a ceremony style for Confucianism and worshipped his soul. The Seishi was carried out in his residence, Suika (Shidemasu)-Reisha Shrine in Kyoto in 1671. After that, Shintoists and scholars of Japanese classical literature in Hirata-ha school worshipped their own souls in accordance with their established ritual procedures.
Genchi KATO (1873-1965) is well-known for his research on Seishi. According to Kato, 60 to 70 Seishi were identified and the latest Seishi is the one for Chugi NISHI which was enshrined in August 1931 in Urakawa-cho, Hidaka Province, Hokkaido Prefecture.
[[Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji's]] Seishi was built in Port of Ishinomaki in Miyagi Prefecture in 1876, and also in the site of Yahiko-jinja Shrine in Ono-mura, Kamiina-gun in 1893. The Seishi of Emperor Meiji and [[Empress Shōken|Empress Dowager Shoken]] was each built in two locations in 1887 and 1904, the Seishi of Emperor Taisho was built in Monbetsu-mura, Hidaka Province, Hokkaido Prefecture in 1911, the Seishi of Empress Teimei was built in Hirose-machi, Hiroshima Prefecture in 1913, and the Seishi of Prince Chichibunomiya and Prince Takamatsunomiya was each built in Doshin-machi, Himeji City in 1924. The Seishi of Emperor Showa was built on Mt. Yoshitsune, Honbetsu-cho, Tokachi Province, Hokkaido Prefecture in 1879, and the Seishi of Emperor Showa and Empress Kojun was each built in Hirose-machi, Hiroshima City in 1928.

<!-- Note: The following pages were redirects to [[Sheng_Shrine]] before draftification: *[[Seishi (Shinto)]] --> {{Nihongo|Living worship|2=生祀|3=Seishi}} is worship of a living person in the [[East Asian cultural sphere]], it is done with a {{ill|Sheng Shrine|ja|生祀|zh|生祠}} (Sheng[[Ci shrine|ci]], 生祠).<ref name=":1" /> It is where or worship of a living person is performed. There are two types of enshrinement: enshrining the spirit of a living person who has made a significant contribution and enshrining one's own spirit.
Due to the usage by [[Wei Zhongxian]] they became seen as a sign of corruption and declined in China.<ref name=":2">{{Cite web |title=生祠并非魏忠贤的专利-凤凰周刊 |url=http://www.ifengweekly.com/detil.php?id=16813 |access-date=2022-12-22 |website=www.ifengweekly.com|trans-title=Shengci is not Wei Zhongxian's invention}}</ref> However the practice continued in Japan with the worship of the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] until 1945 and the end of [[State Shinto]].<ref name="Earhart">{{cite book |last1=Earhart |first1=H. Byron |url=https://archive.org/details/religioninjapane0000earh |title=Religion in the Japanese experience: sources and interpretations |date=1974 |publisher=Dickenson Pub. Co. |isbn=0822101041 |edition=3rd |location=Encino, Calif. |author1-link=H. Byron Earhart |url-access=registration}}</ref> [[File:Hiraga_Gennai_seishi01s2040.jpg|thumb|200x200px|[[Hiraga Gennai|Hiraga Gennai]]'s birth shrine in [[Hiroshima Prefecture|Hiroshima Prefecture]], [[Fukuyama, Hiroshima|Fukuyama City]], Tomomachi.]]
== Sheng shrines in China == When [[Han Dynasty#Western Han|Former Han]]'s Reunobu was the prime minister of [[Yan Kingdom (Han dynasty)|Yan]], he built his shrine between Yan and [[Qi (state)|Qi]] and called it the "Reun Gong Shrine". Also, when {{ill|Shiqing|zh|石慶}} was the prime minister of Qi, the Qi people built the "Shiqing Shrine". This is the beginning of the birth shrine.<ref>[[Zhao Yi]], "The New New Ancestral Hall", Volume 32, "Ancestral Hall", "This is the beginning of the establishment of an ancestral hall.</ref>
In the [[Tang dynasty]], there were certain restrictions on the construction of monuments and shrines by officials in office. According to the [[Tang Code|Tang Rulings and Ordinances]], those who erected shrines or monuments without permission were subject to a penalty of one year of imprisonment.<ref>The [[Tang Code]], Volume 11 (The Law of Office)</ref> The Tang Dynasty called it "the difficulty of building monuments and shrines on the first day.
In the [[Ming dynasty|Ming]], peasants in [[Tengzhou|Tengzhou]] built a shrine to commemorate {{ill|Zhao Bangqing|zh|趙邦清}}, who was appointed to Yanjing for his clean politics. The shrine was built to commemorate him.
During the reign of the [[Tianqi Emperor]] of the Ming Dynasty, the eunuch [[Wei Zhongxian]], ruled the world by conducting his own politics, and those who flattered him or feared his authority were given a He made a living shrine for him. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing (1627), Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote: "Confucius wrote the Spring and Autumn, and Chung-Hsien wrote the Essentials. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote that Confucius had written the "Spring and Autumn" and Chung-Hsien had written the "Essentials. In April of the 7th year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, [[Yuan Chonghuan]] and {{ill|Yan Mingtai|zh|閻鳴泰}} of the [[Ministry of War (imperial China)|Ministry of War]] made an appeal to the government to praise the merits and demerits of Wei Zhongxian and to establish a living shrine for him in both Qiantu.<ref>"Ming Xi Zong Jizhu": "in April of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianqi, the governor of Thistle Liaoning, Yan Mingtai, and the governor of Fuxing Yuan Chong Huan, praised the merits of Wei Zhongxian, and requested the construction of a shrine in front of Ning, giving the name Mao De." The government of the Liaoning Province has been inscribed in gold and stone, and there is no room for extra duties. The minister's single-mindedness in patrolling, strict warning of the battalion generals, do not dare to greed cowardly selfishness, do not dare to give away the hidden string, change the virtual into the real, into the real, easy timid into brave, to have today. Sustainability ancient internal minister who has the right!" ("Ming Xi Zong seven years of the Imperial Court of the Department of the Experiment" Tianqi six years in October)</ref> After that, Wei Zhongxian's raw shrine was "all over the world", "the cost of each shrine was at most hundreds of thousands, at least tens of thousands", "stolen the people's fortune, invaded the public safe, and was cut down. There are countless trees. " When Huang Yun Yasushi made a statue and greeted the statue, he said, "Goto Sansho," and "leaded the general of Bunbu, lined up under the stairs, and worshiped as the beginning."
[[Wei Zhongxian]] has been strongly contemned into modern times for his behavior<ref name=":0" />
[[Gu Yanwu|Gu Yanwu]] lamented, "In this generation, there is no official who does not build a living shrine. And within a few years of leaving office, he would destroy the statues and change their masters.<ref>《Nichi Chi Roku》 vol. 23 (Sheng Shi), p. 644.</ref>
Recently{{When|date=2020-12<!--article says it was recently so likely 2020-->}} a person in China created their own Sheng shrine and stirred online controversy<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |last=网易 |date=2020-12-14 |title=魏忠贤为自己生祠揭幕 |url=https://www.163.com/dy/article/FTRIN9CF0521Q1HR.html |access-date=2022-12-18 |website=www.163.com}}</ref>
== Disorganized but cited content == The sacred and secular life of the Ming Dynasty were always intertwined. "Little Destiny: Shrines and Ming Politics" focuses on two aspects of these debates that are often dismissed as fictional stories. The book examines the political participation of ordinary people and the establishment of shrines for the living. It has been argued that rhetorical expressions of the sanctity of Ming rule actually empowered subjects' political participation. Ancestral halls and temples, large and small, were widely distributed within the territory of the dynasty. <ref name=":1">{{Cite web |date=2022-12-18 |title=为关心民生疾苦而设的生祠，在明代起着什么样的作用？_政治_祭祀_陈天祥 |url=http://web.archive.org/web/20221218062727/https://www.sohu.com/a/531809387_120005162 |access-date=2023-01-12 |website=web.archive.org}}</ref>
They worship gods, honor role models, convey traditional values, and provide assistance to those who pray. The concept of shengci was also present in the politics of the Ming Dynasty. Reformers derided living shrines as a corrupt and backward tradition, and historians tend to ignore it or ignore it because of its absurdity. The role of Shengci in the power struggle between Wei Zhongxian and the Donglin party members may have led to their emergence.<ref name=":1" />

Commemorative buildings can be seen everywhere in the Ming Dynasty. The establishment of ancestral halls is centered on Confucian filial piety, which is not limited to kindness of nurturing and support between parents and children. Ming people know that the establishment of Shengci includes both sincere gratitude and flattery to the powerful. Study this system can deepen the understanding of Ming people's thinking and behavior.<ref name=":1" />
Research into sheng shrines reveals the possibility of political speech and political action in the Ming state. Since the 1980s, research on the "public sphere" in the late imperial period has flourished. The academic community generally believes it is a recognized field where issues of national governance can be discussed. In the Ming Dynasty, from the emperor down, one could not only have access to cheap books, but also access to religious-related knowledge. Even though a small number of elites still control the publication and writing of political speeches, Yangming Xinxue declares that "everyone can be a Yao and Shun".<ref name=":1" />
Ming embodies what Pierre-E'tienne Will calls a "deeply internalized principle of egalitarianism". Ming Taizu also gave almost all subjects the right to submit a letter to correct the bad governance. In the later period of his rule, he also provided a way for civilians to report and even arrest officials.<ref name=":1" />

Respecting the ancestors of officials may have the same effect, but the problem of sacrificial offerings is more prominent. Ten government students drafted a fund-raising letter, raising 485 taels.<ref name=":2" />
Under such conditions, it was not uncommon for ancestral halls to become extinct. Some survived for tens or even hundreds of years, even into the Kangxi period. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote that Confucius had written the "Spring and Autumn" and Chung-Hsien had written the "Essentials. In April of the 7th year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, [[Yuan Chonghuan]] and {{ill|Yan Mingtai|zh|閻鳴泰}} of the [[Ministry of War (imperial China)|Ministry of War]] made an appeal to the government to praise the merits and demerits of Wei Zhongxian and to establish a living shrine for him in both Qiantu.<ref>"Ming Xi Zong Jizhu": "in April of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianqi, the governor of Thistle Liaoning, Yan Mingtai, and the governor of Fuxing Yuan Chong Huan, praised the merits of Wei Zhongxian, and requested the construction of a shrine in front of Ning, giving the name Mao De." The government of the Liaoning Province has been inscribed in gold and stone, and there is no room for extra duties. The minister's single-mindedness in patrolling, strict warning of the battalion generals, do not dare to greed cowardly selfishness, do not dare to give away the hidden string, change the virtual into the real, into the real, easy timid into brave, to have today. Sustainability ancient internal minister who has the right!" ("Ming Xi Zong seven years of the Imperial Court of the Department of the Experiment" Tianqi six years in October)</ref> After that, Wei Zhongxian's raw shrine was "all over the world", "the cost of each shrine was at most hundreds of thousands, at least tens of thousands", "stolen the people's fortune, invaded the public safe, and was cut down. There are countless trees. " When Huang Yun Yasushi made a statue and greeted the statue, he said, "Goto Sansho," and "leaded the general of Bunbu, lined up under the stairs, and worshiped as the beginning."
[[Wei Zhongxian]] has been strongly contemned into modern times for his behavior<ref name=":0" />
[[Gu Yanwu|Gu Yanwu]] lamented, "In this generation, there is no official who does not build a living shrine. And within a few years of leaving office, he would destroy the statues and change their masters.<ref>《Nichi Chi Roku》 vol. 23 (Sheng Shi), p. 644.</ref>
Recently{{When|date=2020-12<!--article says it was recently so likely 2020-->}} a person in China created their own Sheng shrine and stirred online controversy<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |last=网易 |date=2020-12-14 |title=魏忠贤为自己生祠揭幕 |url=https://www.163.com/dy/article/FTRIN9CF0521Q1HR.html |access-date=2022-12-18 |website=www.163.com}}</ref>
== Disorganized but cited content == The sacred and secular life of the Ming Dynasty were always intertwined. "Little Destiny: Shrines and Ming Politics" focuses on two aspects of these debates that are often dismissed as fictional stories. The book examines the political participation of ordinary people and the establishment of shrines for the living. It has been argued that rhetorical expressions of the sanctity of Ming rule actually empowered subjects' political participation. Ancestral halls and temples, large and small, were widely distributed within the territory of the dynasty. <ref name=":1">{{Cite web |date=2022-12-18 |title=为关心民生疾苦而设的生祠，在明代起着什么样的作用？_政治_祭祀_陈天祥 |url=http://web.archive.org/web/20221218062727/https://www.sohu.com/a/531809387_120005162 |access-date=2023-01-12 |website=web.archive.org}}</ref>
They worship gods, honor role models, convey traditional values, and provide assistance to those who pray. The concept of shengci was also present in the politics of the Ming Dynasty. Reformers derided living shrines as a corrupt and backward tradition, and historians tend to ignore it or ignore it because of its absurdity. The role of Shengci in the power struggle between Wei Zhongxian and the Donglin party members may have led to their emergence.<ref name=":1" />
Most Ming people were influenced by the idea of the unity of the [[Sanyi teaching|three religions]] - Buddhism, Taoism and China's ancient spiritual traditions. Shengci first appeared in the Eastern Han Dynasty and continued throughout the ages. In the Song Dynasty, various types of ancestral halls increased in large numbers. From the Liaodong Peninsula to Guizhou and Yunnan, from Gansu to Leizhou, Shengci have appeared frequently. <ref name=":1" />
Commemorative buildings can be seen everywhere in the Ming Dynasty. Professional bureaucrats know that they can win local commemorations, including praise and criticism from family members, colleagues, and later generations of scholar-bureaucrats. The establishment of ancestral halls is centered on Confucian filial piety, which is not limited to kindness of nurturing and support between parents and children. Ming people know that the establishment of Shengci includes both sincere gratitude and flattery to the powerful. Study this system can deepen the understanding of Ming people's thinking and behavior.<ref name=":1" />
Research into sheng shrines reveals the possibility of political speech and political action in the Ming state. Since the 1980s, research on the "public sphere" in the late imperial period has flourished. The academic community generally believes it is a recognized field where issues of national governance can be discussed. In the Ming Dynasty, from the emperor down, one could not only have access to cheap books, but also access to religious-related knowledge. Even though a small number of elites still control the publication and writing of political speeches, Yangming Xinxue declares that "everyone can be a Yao and Shun".<ref name=":1" />
Ming embodies what Pierre-E'tienne Will calls a "deeply internalized principle of egalitarianism". Ming Taizu also gave almost all subjects the right to submit a letter to correct the bad governance. In the later period of his rule, he also provided a way for civilians to report and even arrest officials.<ref name=":1" />
Life sacrifice may have become a way of flattery in the officialdom to win the appreciation of superiors. Respecting the ancestors of officials may have the same effect, but the problem of sacrificial offerings is more prominent. Because of the contradictions and tensions it presents, the Shengci Temple in the Ming Dynasty is worthy of careful inspection.<ref name=":1" />
One explanation is that the gentry became more and more sycophantic, leading to the proliferation of sheng shrines. The impetus for the establishment of the shrine was bottom-up, and sometimes even conflicted with top-down demands.<ref name=":2" />
Could the sheng shrine be a self-aggrandizement like the "miracle hand" banner in the clinic? One of the more striking features of the act of naming is the lack of bottom-up activism. Shi Shanshan writes: "The Ming dynasty had strict control over the awarding of chastity and martyrdom". The ancestral hall was built in the 36th year of the Wanli reign (1608). Ten government students drafted a fund-raising letter, raising 485 taels.<ref name=":2" />
Under such conditions, it was not uncommon for ancestral halls to become extinct. Some survived for tens or even hundreds of years, even into the Kangxi period. Zhang Juzheng, who was the chief minister of the cabinet in the early Wanli period (1572-1582), opposed the establishment of a sheng shrine. The "achievements" from the perspective of superiors and subordinates, central and local, are often different or even opposite.<ref name=":2" />
A living man unveiled a statue of himself, many netizens felt it was really a sheng shrine<ref name=":0" />. In fact, there is nothing new under the sun, then absurd things in history can find similar cases, can rhyme. This is someone bold innovation, horseshit shot a new height. In Kaifeng, more than 2,000 houses were demolished to build the shrine. The shrine was built with a palace of nine pillars, with the appearance of an emperor. <ref name=":0" />
Even Yuan Chonghuan, a great general at the end of Ming Dynasty, built two of Wei Zhongxian's shrines. It may be true that the incense of the ancestral shrines bent Wei Zhongxian, indeed, not a few years, the death of Emperor Tianqi, soon after Chongzhen came to power, we saw his birth shrine smashed. The first time to build how hard, later smashed how hard. And at the time of smashing the birth shrine, the great savior has become a big bad guy again!<ref name=":0" />
== Living worship in Japan == Seishi carried out to worship one's own soul was to live a long life or become Kami (god) after death.
It is thought that this originated in the tradition that [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi]] (chief god of Izumo in southern Honshu Island, Japan, and the central character in the important cycle of myths set in that region) worshipped his own Kushimitama (the wondrous, miraculous, or salubrious) and Sakimitama (the propitious) in [[Mount Miwa]]
The oldest case of someone worshipping their own soul was related to Haruhiko MATSUKI (824 - 924)in the Heian period when he was a Shinto priest of Geku (outer shrine) in Ise-jingu Shrine, he worshipped his soul included in stones or rocks in Obe, Watarai-gun, Ise in 923.
There is a case where Sadanobu MATSUDAIRA carried out Seishi for himself at Oshu Shirakawa-jo Castle in 1797 in the Edo period.
The number of Seishi increased in the Edo period, and this was probably due to the influence of Chinese thought.<ref>{{Cite web |title=「生祀は」の用例・例文集 - 用例.jp |url=http://yourei.jp/%E7%94%9F%E7%A5%80%E3%81%AF |access-date=2023-01-12 |website=yourei.jp}}</ref>
In the Edo period, {{Ill|Yamazaki Ansai|ja|山崎闇斎}} established rules for ritual procedures based on a ceremony style for Confucianism and worshipped his soul. The Seishi was carried out in his residence, Suika (Shidemasu)-Reisha Shrine in Kyoto in 1671. After that, Shintoists and scholars of Japanese classical literature in Hirata-ha school worshipped their own souls in accordance with their established ritual procedures.
Genchi KATO (1873-1965) is well-known for his research on Seishi. According to Kato, 60 to 70 Seishi were identified and the latest Seishi is the one for Chugi NISHI which was enshrined in August 1931 in Urakawa-cho, Hidaka Province, Hokkaido Prefecture.
[[Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji's]] Seishi was built in Port of Ishinomaki in Miyagi Prefecture in 1876, and also in the site of Yahiko-jinja Shrine in Ono-mura, Kamiina-gun in 1893. The Seishi of Emperor Meiji and [[Empress Shōken|Empress Dowager Shoken]] was each built in two locations in 1887 and 1904, the Seishi of Emperor Taisho was built in Monbetsu-mura, Hidaka Province, Hokkaido Prefecture in 1911, the Seishi of Empress Teimei was built in Hirose-machi, Hiroshima Prefecture in 1913, and the Seishi of Prince Chichibunomiya and Prince Takamatsunomiya was each built in Doshin-machi, Himeji City in 1924. The Seishi of Emperor Showa was built on Mt. Yoshitsune, Honbetsu-cho, Tokachi Province, Hokkaido Prefecture in 1879, and the Seishi of Emperor Showa and Empress Kojun was each built in Hirose-machi, Hiroshima City in 1928.

There are two types of enshrinement: enshrining the spirit of a living person who has made a significant contribution and enshrining one's own spirit.
Due to the usage by [[Wei Zhongxian]] they became seen as a sign of corruption and declined in China.<ref name=":2">{{Cite web |title=生祠并非魏忠贤的专利-凤凰周刊 |url=http://www.ifengweekly.com/detil.php?id=16813 |access-date=2022-12-22 |website=www.ifengweekly.com|trans-title=Shengci is not Wei Zhongxian's invention}}</ref> However the practice continued in Japan with the worship of the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] until 1945 and the end of [[State Shinto]].<ref name="Earhart">{{cite book |last1=Earhart |first1=H. Co. |isbn=0822101041 |edition=3rd |location=Encino, Calif. |author1-link=H. Byron Earhart |url-access=registration}}</ref> [[File:Hiraga_Gennai_seishi01s2040.jpg|thumb|200x200px|[[Hiraga Gennai|Hiraga Gennai]]'s birth shrine in [[Hiroshima Prefecture|Hiroshima Prefecture]], [[Fukuyama, Hiroshima|Fukuyama City]], Tomomachi.]]
== Sheng shrines in China == When [[Han Dynasty#Western Han|Former Han]]'s Reunobu was the prime minister of [[Yan Kingdom (Han dynasty)|Yan]], he built his shrine between Yan and [[Qi (state)|Qi]] and called it the "Reun Gong Shrine". Also, when {{ill|Shiqing|zh|石慶}} was the prime minister of Qi, the Qi people built the "Shiqing Shrine". This is the beginning of the birth shrine.<ref>[[Zhao Yi]], "The New New Ancestral Hall", Volume 32, "Ancestral Hall", "This is the beginning of the establishment of an ancestral hall.</ref>
In the [[Tang dynasty]], there were certain restrictions on the construction of monuments and shrines by officials in office. According to the [[Tang Code|Tang Rulings and Ordinances]], those who erected shrines or monuments without permission were subject to a penalty of one year of imprisonment.<ref>The [[Tang Code]], Volume 11 (The Law of Office)</ref> The Tang Dynasty called it "the difficulty of building monuments and shrines on the first day.
In the [[Ming dynasty|Ming]], peasants in [[Tengzhou|Tengzhou]] built a shrine to commemorate {{ill|Zhao Bangqing|zh|趙邦清}}, who was appointed to Yanjing for his clean politics. The shrine was built to commemorate him.
During the reign of the [[Tianqi Emperor]] of the Ming Dynasty, the eunuch [[Wei Zhongxian]], ruled the world by conducting his own politics, and those who flattered him or feared his authority were given a He made a living shrine for him. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing (1627), Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote: "Confucius wrote the Spring and Autumn, and Chung-Hsien wrote the Essentials. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote that Confucius had written the "Spring and Autumn" and Chung-Hsien had written the "Essentials. In April of the 7th year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, [[Yuan Chonghuan]] and {{ill|Yan Mingtai|zh|閻鳴泰}} of the [[Ministry of War (imperial China)|Ministry of War]] made an appeal to the government to praise the merits and demerits of Wei Zhongxian and to establish a living shrine for him in both Qiantu.<ref>"Ming Xi Zong Jizhu": "in April of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianqi, the governor of Thistle Liaoning, Yan Mingtai, and the governor of Fuxing Yuan Chong Huan, praised the merits of Wei Zhongxian, and requested the construction of a shrine in front of Ning, giving the name Mao De." The government of the Liaoning Province has been inscribed in gold and stone, and there is no room for extra duties. The minister's single-mindedness in patrolling, strict warning of the battalion generals, do not dare to greed cowardly selfishness, do not dare to give away the hidden string, change the virtual into the real, into the real, easy timid into brave, to have today. Sustainability ancient internal minister who has the right!" ("Ming Xi Zong seven years of the Imperial Court of the Department of the Experiment" Tianqi six years in October)</ref> After that, Wei Zhongxian's raw shrine was "all over the world", "the cost of each shrine was at most hundreds of thousands, at least tens of thousands", "stolen the people's fortune, invaded the public safe, and was cut down. There are countless trees. " When Huang Yun Yasushi made a statue and greeted the statue, he said, "Goto Sansho," and "leaded the general of Bunbu, lined up under the stairs, and worshiped as the beginning."
[[Wei Zhongxian]] has been strongly contemned into modern times for his behavior<ref name=":0" />

Study this system can deepen the understanding of Ming people's thinking and behavior.<ref name=":1" />

Since the 1980s, research on the "public sphere" in the late imperial period has flourished. In the later period of his rule, he also provided a way for civilians to report and even arrest officials.<ref name=":1" />

Because of the contradictions and tensions it presents, the Shengci Temple in the Ming Dynasty is worthy of careful inspection.<ref name=":1" />

Shi Shanshan writes: "The Ming dynasty had strict control over the awarding of chastity and martyrdom". The ancestral hall was built in the 36th year of the Wanli reign (1608). Ten government students drafted a fund-raising letter, raising 485 taels.<ref name=":2" />

Zhang Juzheng, who was the chief minister of the cabinet in the early Wanli period (1572-1582), opposed the establishment of a sheng shrine. The "achievements" from the perspective of superiors and subordinates, central and local, are often different or even opposite.<ref name=":2" />
A living man unveiled a statue of himself, many netizens felt it was really a sheng shrine<ref name=":0" />. In fact, there is nothing new under the sun, then absurd things in history can find similar cases, can rhyme. This is someone bold innovation, horseshit shot a new height. In Kaifeng, more than 2,000 houses were demolished to build the shrine. The shrine was built with a palace of nine pillars, with the appearance of an emperor. <ref name=":0" />
Even Yuan Chonghuan, a great general at the end of Ming Dynasty, built two of Wei Zhongxian's shrines. It may be true that the incense of the ancestral shrines bent Wei Zhongxian, indeed, not a few years, the death of Emperor Tianqi, soon after Chongzhen came to power, we saw his birth shrine smashed. The first time to build how hard, later smashed how hard. And at the time of smashing the birth shrine, the great savior has become a big bad guy again!<ref name=":0" />
== Living worship in Japan ==
The [[Edo period]], which lasted from 1603 to 1868 in Japan, saw a rise in the practice of "seishi," or the establishment of one's own spiritual memorial site, where one's soul would be enshrined and worshiped after death. One notable example of this practice is that of [[Matsudaira Sadanobu]], who established his own seishi at Okuzawa Hachiman-gu Shrine in 1797.<ref name=":3">{{Cite web |title=「生祀は」の用例・例文集 - 用例.jp |url=http://yourei.jp/%E7%94%9F%E7%A5%80%E3%81%AF |access-date=2023-01-12 |website=yourei.jp}}</ref>
This rise in seishi is thought to have been influenced by Chinese philosophy, specifically [[Edo neo-Confucianism]], which was introduced to Japan during the Edo period. One notable figure of this period, Yamazaki Ansai, is said to have devised rituals for seishi based on Confucian ceremonies, and established his own seishi in 1671 at his residence in Kyoto.<ref name=":3" />
After this, many scholars and practitioners of Shinto and the Hirata school of National Learning also established their own seishi, each with their own unique rituals. These seishi were believed to be a means of obtaining longevity in life or becoming a god after death. The practice is said to have originated from the story of the great ruler Amaterasu, who enshrined her own divine spirit at [[Mount Miwa]].<ref name=":3" />
The study of seishi is particularly associated with Kato Genchi, who has identified 670 known seishi, with the most recent being established in 1931 at the Nishi-Chogo Shrine in Hokkaido's Higashikokura-cho. The Meiji Emperor's seishi was established in 1876 at Ishinomaki Harbor in Miyagi Prefecture, and again in 1893 at the Yahiko Shrine in Ono village, Kamiina County. The seishi of the Meiji Emperor, his consort, the Taisho Emperor, and the Empress Teimei were also established in various locations, including Hokkaido, Hiroshima, and Himeji.<ref name=":3" />

Byron |url=https://archive.org/details/religioninjapane0000earh |title=Religion in the Japanese experience: sources and interpretations |date=1974 |publisher=Dickenson Pub. Co. |isbn=0822101041 |edition=3rd |location=Encino, Calif. |author1-link=H. Byron Earhart |url-access=registration}}</ref> [[File:Hiraga_Gennai_seishi01s2040.jpg|thumb|200x200px|[[Hiraga Gennai|Hiraga Gennai]]'s birth shrine in [[Hiroshima Prefecture|Hiroshima Prefecture]], [[Fukuyama, Hiroshima|Fukuyama City]], Tomomachi.]]
== Sheng shrines in China == When [[Han Dynasty#Western Han|Former Han]]'s Reunobu was the prime minister of [[Yan Kingdom (Han dynasty)|Yan]], he built his shrine between Yan and [[Qi (state)|Qi]] and called it the "Reun Gong Shrine". Also, when {{ill|Shiqing|zh|石慶}} was the prime minister of Qi, the Qi people built the "Shiqing Shrine". This is the beginning of the birth shrine.<ref>[[Zhao Yi]], "The New New Ancestral Hall", Volume 32, "Ancestral Hall", "This is the beginning of the establishment of an ancestral hall.</ref>
In the [[Tang dynasty]], there were certain restrictions on the construction of monuments and shrines by officials in office. According to the [[Tang Code|Tang Rulings and Ordinances]], those who erected shrines or monuments without permission were subject to a penalty of one year of imprisonment.<ref>The [[Tang Code]], Volume 11 (The Law of Office)</ref> The Tang Dynasty called it "the difficulty of building monuments and shrines on the first day.
In the [[Ming dynasty|Ming]], peasants in [[Tengzhou|Tengzhou]] built a shrine to commemorate {{ill|Zhao Bangqing|zh|趙邦清}}, who was appointed to Yanjing for his clean politics. The shrine was built to commemorate him.
During the reign of the [[Tianqi Emperor]] of the Ming Dynasty, the eunuch [[Wei Zhongxian]], ruled the world by conducting his own politics, and those who flattered him or feared his authority were given a He made a living shrine for him. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing (1627), Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote: "Confucius wrote the Spring and Autumn, and Chung-Hsien wrote the Essentials. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote that Confucius had written the "Spring and Autumn" and Chung-Hsien had written the "Essentials. In April of the 7th year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, [[Yuan Chonghuan]] and {{ill|Yan Mingtai|zh|閻鳴泰}} of the [[Ministry of War (imperial China)|Ministry of War]] made an appeal to the government to praise the merits and demerits of Wei Zhongxian and to establish a living shrine for him in both Qiantu.<ref>"Ming Xi Zong Jizhu": "in April of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianqi, the governor of Thistle Liaoning, Yan Mingtai, and the governor of Fuxing Yuan Chong Huan, praised the merits of Wei Zhongxian, and requested the construction of a shrine in front of Ning, giving the name Mao De." The government of the Liaoning Province has been inscribed in gold and stone, and there is no room for extra duties. The minister's single-mindedness in patrolling, strict warning of the battalion generals, do not dare to greed cowardly selfishness, do not dare to give away the hidden string, change the virtual into the real, into the real, easy timid into brave, to have today. Sustainability ancient internal minister who has the right!" ("Ming Xi Zong seven years of the Imperial Court of the Department of the Experiment" Tianqi six years in October)</ref> After that, Wei Zhongxian's raw shrine was "all over the world", "the cost of each shrine was at most hundreds of thousands, at least tens of thousands", "stolen the people's fortune, invaded the public safe, and was cut down. There are countless trees. " When Huang Yun Yasushi made a statue and greeted the statue, he said, "Goto Sansho," and "leaded the general of Bunbu, lined up under the stairs, and worshiped as the beginning."
[[Wei Zhongxian]] has been strongly contemned into modern times for his behavior<ref name=":0" />
[[Gu Yanwu|Gu Yanwu]] lamented, "In this generation, there is no official who does not build a living shrine. And within a few years of leaving office, he would destroy the statues and change their masters.<ref>《Nichi Chi Roku》 vol. 23 (Sheng Shi), p. 644.</ref>
Recently{{When|date=2020-12<!--article says it was recently so likely 2020-->}} a person in China created their own Sheng shrine and stirred online controversy<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |last=网易 |date=2020-12-14 |title=魏忠贤为自己生祠揭幕 |url=https://www.163.com/dy/article/FTRIN9CF0521Q1HR.html |access-date=2022-12-18 |website=www.163.com}}</ref>
== Living worship in Japan ==
The [[Edo period]], which lasted from 1603 to 1868 in Japan, saw a rise in the practice of "seishi," or the establishment of one's own spiritual memorial site, where one's soul would be enshrined and worshiped after death. One notable example of this practice is that of [[Matsudaira Sadanobu]], who established his own seishi at Okuzawa Hachiman-gu Shrine in 1797.<ref name=":3">{{Cite web |title=「生祀は」の用例・例文集 - 用例.jp |url=http://yourei.jp/%E7%94%9F%E7%A5%80%E3%81%AF |access-date=2023-01-12 |website=yourei.jp}}</ref>
This rise in seishi is thought to have been influenced by Chinese philosophy, specifically [[Edo neo-Confucianism]], which was introduced to Japan during the Edo period. One notable figure of this period, Yamazaki Ansai, is said to have devised rituals for seishi based on Confucian ceremonies, and established his own seishi in 1671 at his residence in Kyoto.<ref name=":3" />
After this, many scholars and practitioners of Shinto and the Hirata school of National Learning also established their own seishi, each with their own unique rituals. These seishi were believed to be a means of obtaining longevity in life or becoming a god after death. The practice is said to have originated from the story of the great ruler Amaterasu, who enshrined her own divine spirit at [[Mount Miwa]].<ref name=":3" />
The study of seishi is particularly associated with Kato Genchi, who has identified 670 known seishi, with the most recent being established in 1931 at the Nishi-Chogo Shrine in Hokkaido's Higashikokura-cho. The Meiji Emperor's seishi was established in 1876 at Ishinomaki Harbor in Miyagi Prefecture, and again in 1893 at the Yahiko Shrine in Ono village, Kamiina County. The seishi of the Meiji Emperor, his consort, the Taisho Emperor, and the Empress Teimei were also established in various locations, including Hokkaido, Hiroshima, and Himeji.<ref name=":3" />

{{Short description|Shrine to a living person in Shinto and Chinese folk religion}} {{Draft topics|biography|east-asia}} {{AfC topic|geo}}
<!-- Note: The following pages were redirects to [[Sheng_Shrine]] before draftification: *[[Seishi (Shinto)]] --> {{Nihongo|Living worship|2=生祀|3=Seishi}} is worship of a living person in the [[East Asian cultural sphere]], it is done with a {{ill|Sheng Shrine|ja|生祀|zh|生祠}} (Sheng[[Ci shrine|ci]], 生祠).<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |date=2022-12-18 |title=为关心民生疾苦而设的生祠，在明代起着什么样的作用？_政治_祭祀_陈天祥 |url=http://web.archive.org/web/20221218062727/https://www.sohu.com/a/531809387_120005162 |access-date=2023-01-12 |website=web.archive.org}}</ref> It is where or worship of a living person is performed. There are two types of enshrinement: enshrining the spirit of a living person who has made a significant contribution and enshrining one's own spirit.
Due to the usage by [[Wei Zhongxian]] they became seen as a sign of corruption and declined in China.<ref name=":2">{{Cite web |title=生祠并非魏忠贤的专利-凤凰周刊 |url=http://www.ifengweekly.com/detil.php?id=16813 |access-date=2022-12-22 |website=www.ifengweekly.com|trans-title=Shengci is not Wei Zhongxian's invention}}</ref> However the practice continued in Japan with the worship of the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] until 1945 and the end of [[State Shinto]].<ref name="Earhart">{{cite book |last1=Earhart |first1=H. Byron |url=https://archive.org/details/religioninjapane0000earh |title=Religion in the Japanese experience: sources and interpretations |date=1974 |publisher=Dickenson Pub. Byron Earhart |url-access=registration}}</ref> [[File:Hiraga_Gennai_seishi01s2040.jpg|thumb|200x200px|[[Hiraga Gennai|Hiraga Gennai]]'s birth shrine in [[Hiroshima Prefecture|Hiroshima Prefecture]], [[Fukuyama, Hiroshima|Fukuyama City]], Tomomachi.]]
== Sheng shrines in China == When [[Han Dynasty#Western Han|Former Han]]'s Reunobu was the prime minister of [[Yan Kingdom (Han dynasty)|Yan]], he built his shrine between Yan and [[Qi (state)|Qi]] and called it the "Reun Gong Shrine". This is the beginning of the birth shrine.<ref>[[Zhao Yi]], "The New New Ancestral Hall", Volume 32, "Ancestral Hall", "This is the beginning of the establishment of an ancestral hall.</ref>
In the [[Tang dynasty]], there were certain restrictions on the construction of monuments and shrines by officials in office. According to the [[Tang Code|Tang Rulings and Ordinances]], those who erected shrines or monuments without permission were subject to a penalty of one year of imprisonment.<ref>The [[Tang Code]], Volume 11 (The Law of Office)</ref> The Tang Dynasty called it "the difficulty of building monuments and shrines on the first day.

In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing (1627), Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote: "Confucius wrote the Spring and Autumn, and Chung-Hsien wrote the Essentials. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote that Confucius had written the "Spring and Autumn" and Chung-Hsien had written the "Essentials. In April of the 7th year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, [[Yuan Chonghuan]] and {{ill|Yan Mingtai|zh|閻鳴泰}} of the [[Ministry of War (imperial China)|Ministry of War]] made an appeal to the government to praise the merits and demerits of Wei Zhongxian and to establish a living shrine for him in both Qiantu.<ref>"Ming Xi Zong Jizhu": "in April of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianqi, the governor of Thistle Liaoning, Yan Mingtai, and the governor of Fuxing Yuan Chong Huan, praised the merits of Wei Zhongxian, and requested the construction of a shrine in front of Ning, giving the name Mao De." The government of the Liaoning Province has been inscribed in gold and stone, and there is no room for extra duties. The minister's single-mindedness in patrolling, strict warning of the battalion generals, do not dare to greed cowardly selfishness, do not dare to give away the hidden string, change the virtual into the real, into the real, easy timid into brave, to have today. ("Ming Xi Zong seven years of the Imperial Court of the Department of the Experiment" Tianqi six years in October)</ref> After that, Wei Zhongxian's raw shrine was "all over the world", "the cost of each shrine was at most hundreds of thousands, at least tens of thousands", "stolen the people's fortune, invaded the public safe, and was cut down. There are countless trees. " When Huang Yun Yasushi made a statue and greeted the statue, he said, "Goto Sansho," and "leaded the general of Bunbu, lined up under the stairs, and worshiped as the beginning."
[[Wei Zhongxian]] has been strongly contemned into modern times for his behavior<ref name=":0" />
[[Gu Yanwu|Gu Yanwu]] lamented, "In this generation, there is no official who does not build a living shrine. And within a few years of leaving office, he would destroy the statues and change their masters.<ref>《Nichi Chi Roku》 vol. 23 (Sheng Shi), p. 644.</ref>
Recently{{When|date=2020-12<!--article says it was recently so likely 2020-->}} a person in China created their own Sheng shrine and stirred online controversy<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |last=网易 |date=2020-12-14 |title=魏忠贤为自己生祠揭幕 |url=https://www.163.com/dy/article/FTRIN9CF0521Q1HR.html |access-date=2022-12-18 |website=www.163.com}}</ref>
== China 2 == The Ming Dynasty, which ruled China from 1368 to 1644, saw a blending of the sacred and secular in daily life. Emperors believed in the Mandate of Heaven and thus had to respect ancestors, honor worthy officials, and ensure the well-being of their subjects. Temples and shrines were found throughout palaces and government offices, and complex and intense debates occurred surrounding both political policies and religious rituals.<ref name=":1" />
A book titled "Xiaotianming: Seishi and Ming Dynasty Politics" delves into two aspects of these debates often considered as symbolic gestures: the political participation of commoners and the establishment of seishi for the living. The book is based on research from the series "Gravity of All Things" by Guangdong People's Publishing House and is authorized by the publisher.<ref name=":1" />
Early Ming literature expressed the idea of a harmonious integration of the state and its people. A Yuan dynasty official, Chen Tianxiang, wrote:<ref name=":1" />
"The state and its people are one body. The people are the blood and breath of the state, and the state is the flesh and bones of the people. Therefore, if the people are rich, the state is rich; if the people are poor, the state is poor; if the people are safe, the state is safe; if the people are in distress, the state is in distress. This is logical."<ref name=":1" />
A stable ruling order required a strong economic foundation as a guarantee, which was a consensus among people. The Mandate of Heaven ideology linked the ruler to the power of heaven. Heaven, also known as the Jade Emperor or the Celestial Emperor, would warn dynasties that fail to protect the lives of their subjects and eventually overthrow them. As the Hongwu Emperor, the founder of the Ming Dynasty, said:<ref name=":1" />
"I fear heaven above, the earth below, and the people in between. ... Rulers are the sons of heaven and earth and the parents of the people. ... If one cannot pacify the people within one's own domain, one has lost the heart of the empire. Why fear such things?"<ref name=":1" />
The Hongwu Emperor saw himself as the son of heaven and earth and the father of his subjects, and thus nurturing the people meant taking responsibility for them. But in reality, the relationship between the emperor and his subjects was more complex, and the establishment of seishi for the living was one of the ways in which this relationship was played out.<ref name=":1" />
== Living worship in Japan ==
The [[Edo period]], which lasted from 1603 to 1868 in Japan, saw a rise in the practice of "seishi," or the establishment of one's own spiritual memorial site, where one's soul would be enshrined and worshiped after death. One notable example of this practice is that of [[Matsudaira Sadanobu]], who established his own seishi at Okuzawa Hachiman-gu Shrine in 1797.<ref name=":3">{{Cite web |title=「生祀は」の用例・例文集 - 用例.jp |url=http://yourei.jp/%E7%94%9F%E7%A5%80%E3%81%AF |access-date=2023-01-12 |website=yourei.jp}}</ref>
This rise in seishi is thought to have been influenced by Chinese philosophy, specifically [[Edo neo-Confucianism]], which was introduced to Japan during the Edo period. One notable figure of this period, Yamazaki Ansai, is said to have devised rituals for seishi based on Confucian ceremonies, and established his own seishi in 1671 at his residence in Kyoto.<ref name=":3" />
After this, many scholars and practitioners of Shinto and the Hirata school of National Learning also established their own seishi, each with their own unique rituals. These seishi were believed to be a means of obtaining longevity in life or becoming a god after death. The practice is said to have originated from the story of the great ruler Amaterasu, who enshrined her own divine spirit at [[Mount Miwa]].<ref name=":3" />
The study of seishi is particularly associated with Kato Genchi, who has identified 670 known seishi, with the most recent being established in 1931 at the Nishi-Chogo Shrine in Hokkaido's Higashikokura-cho. The Meiji Emperor's seishi was established in 1876 at Ishinomaki Harbor in Miyagi Prefecture, and again in 1893 at the Yahiko Shrine in Ono village, Kamiina County. The seishi of the Meiji Emperor, his consort, the Taisho Emperor, and the Empress Teimei were also established in various locations, including Hokkaido, Hiroshima, and Himeji.<ref name=":3" />

== China 2 == The Ming Dynasty, which ruled China from 1368 to 1644, saw a blending of the sacred and secular in daily life. Emperors believed in the Mandate of Heaven and thus had to respect ancestors, honor worthy officials, and ensure the well-being of their subjects. Temples and shrines were found throughout palaces and government offices, and complex and intense debates occurred surrounding both political policies and religious rituals.<ref name=":1" />
A book titled "Xiaotianming: Seishi and Ming Dynasty Politics" delves into two aspects of these debates often considered as symbolic gestures: the political participation of commoners and the establishment of seishi for the living. The book is based on research from the series "Gravity of All Things" by Guangdong People's Publishing House and is authorized by the publisher.<ref name=":1" />
Early Ming literature expressed the idea of a harmonious integration of the state and its people. A Yuan dynasty official, Chen Tianxiang, wrote:<ref name=":1" />
"The state and its people are one body. The people are the blood and breath of the state, and the state is the flesh and bones of the people. Therefore, if the people are rich, the state is rich; if the people are poor, the state is poor; if the people are safe, the state is safe; if the people are in distress, the state is in distress. This is logical."<ref name=":1" />
A stable ruling order required a strong economic foundation as a guarantee, which was a consensus among people. The Mandate of Heaven ideology linked the ruler to the power of heaven. Heaven, also known as the Jade Emperor or the Celestial Emperor, would warn dynasties that fail to protect the lives of their subjects and eventually overthrow them. As the Hongwu Emperor, the founder of the Ming Dynasty, said:<ref name=":1" />
"I fear heaven above, the earth below, and the people in between. ... Rulers are the sons of heaven and earth and the parents of the people. ... If one cannot pacify the people within one's own domain, one has lost the heart of the empire. Why fear such things?"<ref name=":1" />

But in reality, the relationship between the emperor and his subjects was more complex, and the establishment of seishi for the living was one of the ways in which this relationship was played out.<ref name=":1" />
The practice of honoring the spirits of deceased ancestors is a common phenomenon in many cultures around the world. However, the practice of building shrines to honor the spirits of the living, known as "shengci" in Chinese, is less well-known. This article will explore the reasons why the study of shengci is important, and provide an overview of the history and significance of this practice.<ref name=":1" />
Firstly, shengci has generated discussions about religious beliefs during the Ming dynasty. In the worldview of the Ming people, certain aspects of the world were closely connected to others, which they referred to as "induction." Politics and religion, including individual or public good and evil, were closely connected through cause and effect, divine induction, and other means, and even various deities were not exceptions. The spirits of the dead also played an important role in the worldview of the Ming people. Temples and shrines were used to worship ancestors and to provide a place for wandering spirits to rest, so as to prevent disasters. But what about the spirits of the living? In the 1930s, Katō Genchi viewed shengci as a universal phenomenon in the history of world religion, closely related to posthumous worship, and referred to it as "worship of mankind" (anthropolatry). However, even if shengci can display holiness, or is similar to posthumous worship or temple of gods and monsters, there are still significant differences between the two. Shengci transcends the basic binary opposition: existence and transcendence, memorial and worship, virtue and spirituality, survival and death. Most Ming people were influenced by the idea of the unity of the three teachings, so the basic Confucian ideas that I discuss also contain elements of Buddhism and Taoism, which are particularly evident in practice.<ref name=":1" />
Secondly, shengci widely exists in East Asian countries, with a history of over two thousand years. As Japanese scholars revealed in the 1930s, shengci first appeared in the Eastern Han Dynasty and was widely practiced in Japan, Korea, and Vietnam, as well as in China. Shengci was also a common practice in other parts of East Asia, and its influence can still be seen in contemporary culture.<ref name=":1" />
In conclusion, the study of shengci is important for understanding the religious beliefs and cultural practices of the Ming dynasty and East Asia. It offers insights into the worldview of the Ming people and the ways in which they honored both the living and the dead. Furthermore, the practice of shengci has a long history and continues to be an important part of East Asian culture today.<ref name=":1" />
== Living worship in Japan ==
The [[Edo period]], which lasted from 1603 to 1868 in Japan, saw a rise in the practice of "seishi," or the establishment of one's own spiritual memorial site, where one's soul would be enshrined and worshiped after death. One notable example of this practice is that of [[Matsudaira Sadanobu]], who established his own seishi at Okuzawa Hachiman-gu Shrine in 1797.<ref name=":3">{{Cite web |title=「生祀は」の用例・例文集 - 用例.jp |url=http://yourei.jp/%E7%94%9F%E7%A5%80%E3%81%AF |access-date=2023-01-12 |website=yourei.jp}}</ref>
This rise in seishi is thought to have been influenced by Chinese philosophy, specifically [[Edo neo-Confucianism]], which was introduced to Japan during the Edo period. One notable figure of this period, Yamazaki Ansai, is said to have devised rituals for seishi based on Confucian ceremonies, and established his own seishi in 1671 at his residence in Kyoto.<ref name=":3" />
After this, many scholars and practitioners of Shinto and the Hirata school of National Learning also established their own seishi, each with their own unique rituals. These seishi were believed to be a means of obtaining longevity in life or becoming a god after death. The practice is said to have originated from the story of the great ruler Amaterasu, who enshrined her own divine spirit at [[Mount Miwa]].<ref name=":3" />
The study of seishi is particularly associated with Kato Genchi, who has identified 670 known seishi, with the most recent being established in 1931 at the Nishi-Chogo Shrine in Hokkaido's Higashikokura-cho. The Meiji Emperor's seishi was established in 1876 at Ishinomaki Harbor in Miyagi Prefecture, and again in 1893 at the Yahiko Shrine in Ono village, Kamiina County. The seishi of the Meiji Emperor, his consort, the Taisho Emperor, and the Empress Teimei were also established in various locations, including Hokkaido, Hiroshima, and Himeji.<ref name=":3" />

Co. |isbn=0822101041 |edition=3rd |location=Encino, Calif. |author1-link=H. Byron Earhart |url-access=registration}}</ref> [[File:Hiraga_Gennai_seishi01s2040.jpg|thumb|200x200px|[[Hiraga Gennai|Hiraga Gennai]]'s birth shrine in [[Hiroshima Prefecture|Hiroshima Prefecture]], [[Fukuyama, Hiroshima|Fukuyama City]], Tomomachi.]]
== Sheng shrines in China == When [[Han Dynasty#Western Han|Former Han]]'s Reunobu was the prime minister of [[Yan Kingdom (Han dynasty)|Yan]], he built his shrine between Yan and [[Qi (state)|Qi]] and called it the "Reun Gong Shrine". Also, when {{ill|Shiqing|zh|石慶}} was the prime minister of Qi, the Qi people built the "Shiqing Shrine". This is the beginning of the birth shrine.<ref>[[Zhao Yi]], "The New New Ancestral Hall", Volume 32, "Ancestral Hall", "This is the beginning of the establishment of an ancestral hall.</ref>
In the [[Tang dynasty]], there were certain restrictions on the construction of monuments and shrines by officials in office. According to the [[Tang Code|Tang Rulings and Ordinances]], those who erected shrines or monuments without permission were subject to a penalty of one year of imprisonment.<ref>The [[Tang Code]], Volume 11 (The Law of Office)</ref> The Tang Dynasty called it "the difficulty of building monuments and shrines on the first day.
In the [[Ming dynasty|Ming]], peasants in [[Tengzhou|Tengzhou]] built a shrine to commemorate {{ill|Zhao Bangqing|zh|趙邦清}}, who was appointed to Yanjing for his clean politics. The shrine was built to commemorate him.
During the reign of the [[Tianqi Emperor]] of the Ming Dynasty, the eunuch [[Wei Zhongxian]], ruled the world by conducting his own politics, and those who flattered him or feared his authority were given a He made a living shrine for him. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing (1627), Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote: "Confucius wrote the Spring and Autumn, and Chung-Hsien wrote the Essentials. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote that Confucius had written the "Spring and Autumn" and Chung-Hsien had written the "Essentials. In April of the 7th year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, [[Yuan Chonghuan]] and {{ill|Yan Mingtai|zh|閻鳴泰}} of the [[Ministry of War (imperial China)|Ministry of War]] made an appeal to the government to praise the merits and demerits of Wei Zhongxian and to establish a living shrine for him in both Qiantu.<ref>"Ming Xi Zong Jizhu": "in April of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianqi, the governor of Thistle Liaoning, Yan Mingtai, and the governor of Fuxing Yuan Chong Huan, praised the merits of Wei Zhongxian, and requested the construction of a shrine in front of Ning, giving the name Mao De." The government of the Liaoning Province has been inscribed in gold and stone, and there is no room for extra duties. ("Ming Xi Zong seven years of the Imperial Court of the Department of the Experiment" Tianqi six years in October)</ref> After that, Wei Zhongxian's raw shrine was "all over the world", "the cost of each shrine was at most hundreds of thousands, at least tens of thousands", "stolen the people's fortune, invaded the public safe, and was cut down. There are countless trees. " When Huang Yun Yasushi made a statue and greeted the statue, he said, "Goto Sansho," and "leaded the general of Bunbu, lined up under the stairs, and worshiped as the beginning."
[[Wei Zhongxian]] has been strongly contemned into modern times for his behavior<ref name=":0" />
[[Gu Yanwu|Gu Yanwu]] lamented, "In this generation, there is no official who does not build a living shrine. And within a few years of leaving office, he would destroy the statues and change their masters.<ref>《Nichi Chi Roku》 vol. 23 (Sheng Shi), p. 644.</ref>
Recently{{When|date=2020-12<!--article says it was recently so likely 2020-->}} a person in China created their own Sheng shrine and stirred online controversy<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |last=网易 |date=2020-12-14 |title=魏忠贤为自己生祠揭幕 |url=https://www.163.com/dy/article/FTRIN9CF0521Q1HR.html |access-date=2022-12-18 |website=www.163.com}}</ref>
== China 2 == The Ming Dynasty, which ruled China from 1368 to 1644, saw a blending of the sacred and secular in daily life. Emperors believed in the Mandate of Heaven and thus had to respect ancestors, honor worthy officials, and ensure the well-being of their subjects. Temples and shrines were found throughout palaces and government offices, and complex and intense debates occurred surrounding both political policies and religious rituals.<ref name=":1" />
A book titled "Xiaotianming: Seishi and Ming Dynasty Politics" delves into two aspects of these debates often considered as symbolic gestures: the political participation of commoners and the establishment of seishi for the living. The book is based on research from the series "Gravity of All Things" by Guangdong People's Publishing House and is authorized by the publisher.<ref name=":1" />
Early Ming literature expressed the idea of a harmonious integration of the state and its people. A Yuan dynasty official, Chen Tianxiang, wrote:<ref name=":1" />
"The state and its people are one body. The people are the blood and breath of the state, and the state is the flesh and bones of the people. Therefore, if the people are rich, the state is rich; if the people are poor, the state is poor; if the people are safe, the state is safe; if the people are in distress, the state is in distress. This is logical."<ref name=":1" />
A stable ruling order required a strong economic foundation as a guarantee, which was a consensus among people. The Mandate of Heaven ideology linked the ruler to the power of heaven. Heaven, also known as the Jade Emperor or the Celestial Emperor, would warn dynasties that fail to protect the lives of their subjects and eventually overthrow them. As the Hongwu Emperor, the founder of the Ming Dynasty, said:<ref name=":1" />
"I fear heaven above, the earth below, and the people in between. ... Rulers are the sons of heaven and earth and the parents of the people. ... If one cannot pacify the people within one's own domain, one has lost the heart of the empire. Why fear such things?"<ref name=":1" />
The Hongwu Emperor saw himself as the son of heaven and earth and the father of his subjects, and thus nurturing the people meant taking responsibility for them. But in reality, the relationship between the emperor and his subjects was more complex, and the establishment of seishi for the living was one of the ways in which this relationship was played out.<ref name=":1" />
The practice of honoring the spirits of deceased ancestors is a common phenomenon in many cultures around the world. However, the practice of building shrines to honor the spirits of the living, known as "shengci" in Chinese, is less well-known. This article will explore the reasons why the study of shengci is important, and provide an overview of the history and significance of this practice.<ref name=":1" />
Firstly, shengci has generated discussions about religious beliefs during the Ming dynasty. In the worldview of the Ming people, certain aspects of the world were closely connected to others, which they referred to as "induction." Politics and religion, including individual or public good and evil, were closely connected through cause and effect, divine induction, and other means, and even various deities were not exceptions. The spirits of the dead also played an important role in the worldview of the Ming people. Temples and shrines were used to worship ancestors and to provide a place for wandering spirits to rest, so as to prevent disasters. But what about the spirits of the living? In the 1930s, Katō Genchi viewed shengci as a universal phenomenon in the history of world religion, closely related to posthumous worship, and referred to it as "worship of mankind" (anthropolatry). However, even if shengci can display holiness, or is similar to posthumous worship or temple of gods and monsters, there are still significant differences between the two. Shengci transcends the basic binary opposition: existence and transcendence, memorial and worship, virtue and spirituality, survival and death. Most Ming people were influenced by the idea of the unity of the three teachings, so the basic Confucian ideas that I discuss also contain elements of Buddhism and Taoism, which are particularly evident in practice.<ref name=":1" />
Secondly, shengci widely exists in East Asian countries, with a history of over two thousand years. As Japanese scholars revealed in the 1930s, shengci first appeared in the Eastern Han Dynasty and was widely practiced in Japan, Korea, and Vietnam, as well as in China. Shengci was also a common practice in other parts of East Asia, and its influence can still be seen in contemporary culture.<ref name=":1" />
In conclusion, the study of shengci is important for understanding the religious beliefs and cultural practices of the Ming dynasty and East Asia. It offers insights into the worldview of the Ming people and the ways in which they honored both the living and the dead. Furthermore, the practice of shengci has a long history and continues to be an important part of East Asian culture today.<ref name=":1" />

Temples and shrines were used to worship ancestors and to provide a place for wandering spirits to rest, so as to prevent disasters. But what about the spirits of the living? In the 1930s, Katō Genchi viewed shengci as a universal phenomenon in the history of world religion, closely related to posthumous worship, and referred to it as "worship of mankind" (anthropolatry). However, even if shengci can display holiness, or is similar to posthumous worship or temple of gods and monsters, there are still significant differences between the two. Shengci transcends the basic binary opposition: existence and transcendence, memorial and worship, virtue and spirituality, survival and death. Most Ming people were influenced by the idea of the unity of the three teachings, so the basic Confucian ideas that I discuss also contain elements of Buddhism and Taoism, which are particularly evident in practice.<ref name=":1" />
Secondly, shengci widely exists in East Asian countries, with a history of over two thousand years. As Japanese scholars revealed in the 1930s, shengci first appeared in the Eastern Han Dynasty and was widely practiced in Japan, Korea, and Vietnam, as well as in China. Shengci was also a common practice in other parts of East Asia, and its influence can still be seen in contemporary culture.<ref name=":1" />
In conclusion, the study of shengci is important for understanding the religious beliefs and cultural practices of the Ming dynasty and East Asia. It offers insights into the worldview of the Ming people and the ways in which they honored both the living and the dead. Furthermore, the practice of shengci has a long history and continues to be an important part of East Asian culture today.<ref name=":1" /> <!--明代的公论与平民主义
研究生祠的第五个原因在于它揭示了在专制制度与官僚体制下明代国家内发表政治言论和采取政治行动的可能性。20世纪80年代以来，关于帝制晚期“公共领域”的研究颇为兴盛，学者们热衷于讨论当时民众自由结社、集会，对普遍感兴趣的问题发表意见的可能性。历史学家和人类学家在质疑从中国历史中寻求欧洲理论结构有效性的同时，也在中国社会的各个层面、宗教领域及其他地方发现了与现代国家不同程度相关的合法社群。这些社群并不一定反对国家，而是如罗威廉（William T.Rowe）所言，“自晚明以来……在培养参与性思维方面发挥了作用”。但学术界普遍认为，帝制晚期的“公论”是一个公认的、合法的可以讨论国家治理问题的场域——不过在某些方面存在着相当的限制。首先，严格意义上来说，它仅仅是地方性的，直到19世纪中叶太平天国之后才正式提出了国家层面的问题。其次，它缺乏出版媒介。再次，它只代表精英人士的意见，更确切地说，只代表官员。最后，和当时的欧洲一样，它可能只意味着一种中立的事实：清朝官员陈宏谋曾认真考虑并试图遵循“公论”，但最终的结论仍是他所权衡的才是正确的。陈氏引述明代改革家吕坤的话说：“公论，非众口一词之谓也。满朝皆非，而一人是，则公论在一人。”这是一种仅限于与国家关系密切的士人的公论。
但这一研究多以清代为中心，正如鲁道夫·瓦格纳（Rudolf Wagner）所指出的，公共参与和政策讨论处于“极度低潮”之中。但如果我们考虑戴福士（Roger V.Des Forges）、周启荣（Chow Kai-wing）、魏斐德（Frederic Wakeman）及其他学者所说的明代“平民主义”（populism），可能会发现颇为不同的明代政治图景。平民主义有许多方面。商业经济和科举考试促进了人员往来和社会流动，科举考试亦然。晚明士绅领导的慈善团体不仅吸纳了平民，而且明确模仿他们的组织形式。纵然少数精英依旧把持着政治言论的发表和书写，阳明心学却宣称“人皆可以为尧舜”，他们的思想及讲学活动大大推动了平民主义在普罗大众间的传播。各类识字阶层喜欢各种各样的出版物，包括政府公报、文学剧本、歌曲小调和涉及公共事务的故事话本等。在明代，自皇帝以下，不仅能接触到廉价的书籍，还能直接接触到宗教相关的知识，这些知识可能经由专职的灵媒——某个平民，通过扶乩的形式创造出新的经文。司徒琳（Lynn Struve）及其他学者指出，明末中国有一种“反叛精神”，表现为奴变、抗租、罢工、兵变、教乱和民变。社会流动、学社林立、哲学运动、廉价书籍、参与性宗教和阶级斗争都促成了“平民主义”的标签在明代，尤其是晚明的流行。
在这种广泛的平民主义文化中，有明确的政治因素存在。贺凯（Charles Hucker）解释说，在明代的政治运作中，来自御史、各地官员乃至平民的上书经过相关部门（吏、户、礼、兵、刑、工六部）的监察渠道上交内阁，经辅臣充分讨论后，拟出皇帝的批复。然后皇帝或者向几个大臣征询意见，或者召集更多的官员进行廷议，在平等的基础上进行讨论，理想情况下，直到他们达成一致意见为止。按照惯例，皇帝应当执行这一致的决定。此外，明太祖还赋予几乎所有臣民上书进言的权利，以纠正弊政；在他统治后期，他还为平民提供了举报甚至逮捕官员的途径。生员（那些在县学及以上就读的学生）是唯一被剥夺进言权利的群体，但尽管如此，他们还是积极参与到政治讨论当中。一位学者称晚明是“一个人们希望舆论尽可能大声的时代，不管决策者是否会听到，人们普遍认为它对国家事务有积极的影响”。财富（阶级）和阶层（以及随之而来的任职可能性）并非总是保持一致——于是会产生富有而有权势的平民群体。除了古老而深刻（全球皆然）的对稳定的社会等级作为秩序之源的信仰之外，明代还体现了魏丕信（Pierre-E’tienne Will）所称的“深刻内化的平等主义原则”，这一原则有时体现在轮流领导或领袖选举之中。我们绝对不能不加考证地就判定清代中期以前的中国民众在政治上都是“传统”的，保持着“绝对臣服”的姿态。我将指出，明代的生祠为平民主义者的“公论”提供了一个制度性焦点。公论十分重要：正如一位明代作者所解释的，就像一个人依靠“气”来维持健康一样，这是一个王朝国家（国）的原始生命力（气），使之延续了240余年。
一旦生祀话语体系发展起来，它就进入了学术与政治讨论的范畴。这是研究生祠的第六个原因。我们可以看到，生祀有可能成为官场上的谄媚之道，以资博取上级的赏识。对官员祖先的尊崇可能会起到同样的效果，但生祀的问题更加突出。真心纪念与阿谀奉承之间存在张力，在特定情况下的处置形成了一系列惯例。这些惯例随后以多种方式促进了对这一现象的阐述。首先，生祀现象对真挚的民众情感的关注，预示了16世纪前期阳明学派关于各阶层人民内在道德能力的论断。其次，如前所述，16世纪晚期东林党与阉党都利用并发展了生祀理论，以谋求其“主权”（米海瑞语）。最后，地方性荣誉的授予，包括生祠的建立，旨在补正官吏频繁调任的缺憾；地方希望将循吏良臣重塑为“贵族”，这一点后来发展为清初思想家顾炎武所主张的“寓封建于郡县之中”。明代的生祀理论产生于旧思想的新语境中，通过将官员的合法性寄托于民众的认可，有助于进一步的观点论证与思想阐述。正因为它所展现的矛盾与紧张，明代的生祠无论在过去还是现在都值得细加考察。-->
== Living worship in Japan ==
The [[Edo period]], which lasted from 1603 to 1868 in Japan, saw a rise in the practice of "seishi," or the establishment of one's own spiritual memorial site, where one's soul would be enshrined and worshiped after death. One notable example of this practice is that of [[Matsudaira Sadanobu]], who established his own seishi at Okuzawa Hachiman-gu Shrine in 1797.<ref name=":3">{{Cite web |title=「生祀は」の用例・例文集 - 用例.jp |url=http://yourei.jp/%E7%94%9F%E7%A5%80%E3%81%AF |access-date=2023-01-12 |website=yourei.jp}}</ref>
This rise in seishi is thought to have been influenced by Chinese philosophy, specifically [[Edo neo-Confucianism]], which was introduced to Japan during the Edo period. One notable figure of this period, Yamazaki Ansai, is said to have devised rituals for seishi based on Confucian ceremonies, and established his own seishi in 1671 at his residence in Kyoto.<ref name=":3" />
After this, many scholars and practitioners of Shinto and the Hirata school of National Learning also established their own seishi, each with their own unique rituals. These seishi were believed to be a means of obtaining longevity in life or becoming a god after death. The practice is said to have originated from the story of the great ruler Amaterasu, who enshrined her own divine spirit at [[Mount Miwa]].<ref name=":3" />
The study of seishi is particularly associated with Kato Genchi, who has identified 670 known seishi, with the most recent being established in 1931 at the Nishi-Chogo Shrine in Hokkaido's Higashikokura-cho. The Meiji Emperor's seishi was established in 1876 at Ishinomaki Harbor in Miyagi Prefecture, and again in 1893 at the Yahiko Shrine in Ono village, Kamiina County. The seishi of the Meiji Emperor, his consort, the Taisho Emperor, and the Empress Teimei were also established in various locations, including Hokkaido, Hiroshima, and Himeji.<ref name=":3" />
