{{Short description|Shrine to a living person in Shinto and Chinese folk religion}} {{Draft topics|biography|east-asia}} {{AfC topic|geo}}
<!-- Note: The following pages were redirects to [[Sheng_Shrine]] before draftification: *[[Seishi (Shinto)]] --> {{Nihongo|Living worship|2=生祀|3=Seishi}} <!-- Chinese Shengsi -->is worship of a living person in the [[East Asian cultural sphere]], it is done with a {{ill|Sheng Shrine|ja|生祀|zh|生祠}} (Sheng[[Ci shrine|ci]], 生祠)<!-- Japanese Seiji -->.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |date=2022-12-18 |title=为关心民生疾苦而设的生祠，在明代起着什么样的作用？_政治_祭祀_陈天祥 |url=http://web.archive.org/web/20221218062727/https://www.sohu.com/a/531809387_120005162 |access-date=2023-01-12 |website=web.archive.org}}</ref> It is where or worship of a living person is performed. There are two types of enshrinement: enshrining the spirit of a living person who has made a significant contribution and enshrining one's own spirit.
Due to the usage by [[Wei Zhongxian]] they became seen as a sign of corruption and declined in China.<ref name=":2">{{Cite web |title=生祠并非魏忠贤的专利-凤凰周刊 |url=http://www.ifengweekly.com/detil.php?id=16813 |access-date=2022-12-22 |website=www.ifengweekly.com|trans-title=Shengci is not Wei Zhongxian's invention}}</ref> However the practice continued in Japan with the worship of the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] until 1945 and the end of [[State Shinto]].<ref name="Earhart">{{cite book |last1=Earhart |first1=H. Byron |url=https://archive.org/details/religioninjapane0000earh |title=Religion in the Japanese experience: sources and interpretations |date=1974 |publisher=Dickenson Pub. Co. |isbn=0822101041 |edition=3rd |location=Encino, Calif. |author1-link=H. Byron Earhart |url-access=registration}}</ref> [[File:Hiraga_Gennai_seishi01s2040.jpg|thumb|200x200px|[[Hiraga Gennai|Hiraga Gennai]]'s birth shrine in [[Hiroshima Prefecture|Hiroshima Prefecture]], [[Fukuyama, Hiroshima|Fukuyama City]], Tomomachi.]]
== Sheng shrines in China == When [[Han Dynasty#Western Han|Former Han]]'s Reunobu was the prime minister of [[Yan Kingdom (Han dynasty)|Yan]], he built his shrine between Yan and [[Qi (state)|Qi]] and called it the "Reun Gong Shrine". Also, when {{ill|Shiqing|zh|石慶}} was the prime minister of Qi, the Qi people built the "Shiqing Shrine". This is the beginning of the birth shrine.<ref>[[Zhao Yi]], "The New New Ancestral Hall", Volume 32, "Ancestral Hall", "This is the beginning of the establishment of an ancestral hall.</ref>
In the [[Tang dynasty]], there were certain restrictions on the construction of monuments and shrines by officials in office. According to the [[Tang Code|Tang Rulings and Ordinances]], those who erected shrines or monuments without permission were subject to a penalty of one year of imprisonment.<ref>The [[Tang Code]], Volume 11 (The Law of Office)</ref> The Tang Dynasty called it "the difficulty of building monuments and shrines on the first day.
In the [[Ming dynasty|Ming]], peasants in [[Tengzhou|Tengzhou]] built a shrine to commemorate {{ill|Zhao Bangqing|zh|趙邦清}}, who was appointed to Yanjing for his clean politics. The shrine was built to commemorate him.
During the reign of the [[Tianqi Emperor]] of the Ming Dynasty, the eunuch [[Wei Zhongxian]], ruled the world by conducting his own politics, and those who flattered him or feared his authority were given a He made a living shrine for him. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing (1627), Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote: "Confucius wrote the Spring and Autumn, and Chung-Hsien wrote the Essentials. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote that Confucius had written the "Spring and Autumn" and Chung-Hsien had written the "Essentials. In April of the 7th year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, [[Yuan Chonghuan]] and {{ill|Yan Mingtai|zh|閻鳴泰}} of the [[Ministry of War (imperial China)|Ministry of War]] made an appeal to the government to praise the merits and demerits of Wei Zhongxian and to establish a living shrine for him in both Qiantu.<ref>"Ming Xi Zong Jizhu": "in April of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianqi, the governor of Thistle Liaoning, Yan Mingtai, and the governor of Fuxing Yuan Chong Huan, praised the merits of Wei Zhongxian, and requested the construction of a shrine in front of Ning, giving the name Mao De." The government of the Liaoning Province has been inscribed in gold and stone, and there is no room for extra duties. The minister's single-mindedness in patrolling, strict warning of the battalion generals, do not dare to greed cowardly selfishness, do not dare to give away the hidden string, change the virtual into the real, into the real, easy timid into brave, to have today. Sustainability ancient internal minister who has the right!" ("Ming Xi Zong seven years of the Imperial Court of the Department of the Experiment" Tianqi six years in October)</ref> After that, Wei Zhongxian's raw shrine was "all over the world", "the cost of each shrine was at most hundreds of thousands, at least tens of thousands", "stolen the people's fortune, invaded the public safe, and was cut down. There are countless trees. " When Huang Yun Yasushi made a statue and greeted the statue, he said, "Goto Sansho," and "leaded the general of Bunbu, lined up under the stairs, and worshiped as the beginning."
[[Wei Zhongxian]] has been strongly contemned into modern times for his behavior<ref name=":0" />
[[Gu Yanwu|Gu Yanwu]] lamented, "In this generation, there is no official who does not build a living shrine. And within a few years of leaving office, he would destroy the statues and change their masters.<ref>《Nichi Chi Roku》 vol. 23 (Sheng Shi), p. 644.</ref>
Recently{{When|date=2020-12<!--article says it was recently so likely 2020-->}} a person in China created their own Sheng shrine and stirred online controversy<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |last=网易 |date=2020-12-14 |title=魏忠贤为自己生祠揭幕 |url=https://www.163.com/dy/article/FTRIN9CF0521Q1HR.html |access-date=2022-12-18 |website=www.163.com}}</ref>
== China 2 == The Ming Dynasty, which ruled China from 1368 to 1644, saw a blending of the sacred and secular in daily life. Emperors believed in the Mandate of Heaven and thus had to respect ancestors, honor worthy officials, and ensure the well-being of their subjects. Temples and shrines were found throughout palaces and government offices, and complex and intense debates occurred surrounding both political policies and religious rituals.<ref name=":1" />
A book titled "Xiaotianming: Seishi and Ming Dynasty Politics" delves into two aspects of these debates often considered as symbolic gestures: the political participation of commoners and the establishment of seishi for the living. The book is based on research from the series "Gravity of All Things" by Guangdong People's Publishing House and is authorized by the publisher.<ref name=":1" />
Early Ming literature expressed the idea of a harmonious integration of the state and its people. A Yuan dynasty official, Chen Tianxiang, wrote:<ref name=":1" />
"The state and its people are one body. The people are the blood and breath of the state, and the state is the flesh and bones of the people. Therefore, if the people are rich, the state is rich; if the people are poor, the state is poor; if the people are safe, the state is safe; if the people are in distress, the state is in distress. This is logical."<ref name=":1" />
A stable ruling order required a strong economic foundation as a guarantee, which was a consensus among people. The Mandate of Heaven ideology linked the ruler to the power of heaven. Heaven, also known as the Jade Emperor or the Celestial Emperor, would warn dynasties that fail to protect the lives of their subjects and eventually overthrow them. As the Hongwu Emperor, the founder of the Ming Dynasty, said:<ref name=":1" />
"I fear heaven above, the earth below, and the people in between. ... Rulers are the sons of heaven and earth and the parents of the people. ... If one cannot pacify the people within one's own domain, one has lost the heart of the empire. Why fear such things?"<ref name=":1" />
The Hongwu Emperor saw himself as the son of heaven and earth and the father of his subjects, and thus nurturing the people meant taking responsibility for them. But in reality, the relationship between the emperor and his subjects was more complex, and the establishment of seishi for the living was one of the ways in which this relationship was played out.<ref name=":1" />
The practice of honoring the spirits of deceased ancestors is a common phenomenon in many cultures around the world. However, the practice of building shrines to honor the spirits of the living, known as "shengci" in Chinese, is less well-known. This article will explore the reasons why the study of shengci is important, and provide an overview of the history and significance of this practice.<ref name=":1" />
Firstly, shengci has generated discussions about religious beliefs during the Ming dynasty. In the worldview of the Ming people, certain aspects of the world were closely connected to others, which they referred to as "induction." Politics and religion, including individual or public good and evil, were closely connected through cause and effect, divine induction, and other means, and even various deities were not exceptions. The spirits of the dead also played an important role in the worldview of the Ming people. Temples and shrines were used to worship ancestors and to provide a place for wandering spirits to rest, so as to prevent disasters. But what about the spirits of the living? In the 1930s, Katō Genchi viewed shengci as a universal phenomenon in the history of world religion, closely related to posthumous worship, and referred to it as "worship of mankind" (anthropolatry). However, even if shengci can display holiness, or is similar to posthumous worship or temple of gods and monsters, there are still significant differences between the two. Shengci transcends the basic binary opposition: existence and transcendence, memorial and worship, virtue and spirituality, survival and death. Most Ming people were influenced by the idea of the unity of the three teachings, so the basic Confucian ideas that I discuss also contain elements of Buddhism and Taoism, which are particularly evident in practice.<ref name=":1" />
Secondly, shengci widely exists in East Asian countries, with a history of over two thousand years. As Japanese scholars revealed in the 1930s, shengci first appeared in the Eastern Han Dynasty and was widely practiced in Japan, Korea, and Vietnam, as well as in China. Shengci was also a common practice in other parts of East Asia, and its influence can still be seen in contemporary culture.<ref name=":1" />
In conclusion, the study of shengci is important for understanding the religious beliefs and cultural practices of the Ming dynasty and East Asia. It offers insights into the worldview of the Ming people and the ways in which they honored both the living and the dead. Furthermore, the practice of shengci has a long history and continues to be an important part of East Asian culture today.<ref name=":1" />
== Living worship in Japan ==
The [[Edo period]], which lasted from 1603 to 1868 in Japan, saw a rise in the practice of "seishi," or the establishment of one's own spiritual memorial site, where one's soul would be enshrined and worshiped after death. One notable example of this practice is that of [[Matsudaira Sadanobu]], who established his own seishi at Okuzawa Hachiman-gu Shrine in 1797.<ref name=":3">{{Cite web |title=「生祀は」の用例・例文集 - 用例.jp |url=http://yourei.jp/%E7%94%9F%E7%A5%80%E3%81%AF |access-date=2023-01-12 |website=yourei.jp}}</ref>
This rise in seishi is thought to have been influenced by Chinese philosophy, specifically [[Edo neo-Confucianism]], which was introduced to Japan during the Edo period. One notable figure of this period, Yamazaki Ansai, is said to have devised rituals for seishi based on Confucian ceremonies, and established his own seishi in 1671 at his residence in Kyoto.<ref name=":3" />
After this, many scholars and practitioners of Shinto and the Hirata school of National Learning also established their own seishi, each with their own unique rituals. These seishi were believed to be a means of obtaining longevity in life or becoming a god after death. The practice is said to have originated from the story of the great ruler Amaterasu, who enshrined her own divine spirit at [[Mount Miwa]].<ref name=":3" />
The study of seishi is particularly associated with Kato Genchi, who has identified 670 known seishi, with the most recent being established in 1931 at the Nishi-Chogo Shrine in Hokkaido's Higashikokura-cho. The Meiji Emperor's seishi was established in 1876 at Ishinomaki Harbor in Miyagi Prefecture, and again in 1893 at the Yahiko Shrine in Ono village, Kamiina County. The seishi of the Meiji Emperor, his consort, the Taisho Emperor, and the Empress Teimei were also established in various locations, including Hokkaido, Hiroshima, and Himeji.<ref name=":3" />

There are two types of enshrinement: enshrining the spirit of a living person who has made a significant contribution and enshrining one's own spirit.
Due to the usage by [[Wei Zhongxian]] they became seen as a sign of corruption and declined in China.<ref name=":2">{{Cite web |title=生祠并非魏忠贤的专利-凤凰周刊 |url=http://www.ifengweekly.com/detil.php?id=16813 |access-date=2022-12-22 |website=www.ifengweekly.com|trans-title=Shengci is not Wei Zhongxian's invention}}</ref> However the practice continued in Japan with the worship of the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] until 1945 and the end of [[State Shinto]].<ref name="Earhart">{{cite book |last1=Earhart |first1=H. Byron Earhart |url-access=registration}}</ref> [[File:Hiraga_Gennai_seishi01s2040.jpg|thumb|200x200px|[[Hiraga Gennai|Hiraga Gennai]]'s birth shrine in [[Hiroshima Prefecture|Hiroshima Prefecture]], [[Fukuyama, Hiroshima|Fukuyama City]], Tomomachi.]]
== Sheng shrines in China == When [[Han Dynasty#Western Han|Former Han]]'s Reunobu was the prime minister of [[Yan Kingdom (Han dynasty)|Yan]], he built his shrine between Yan and [[Qi (state)|Qi]] and called it the "Reun Gong Shrine". Also, when {{ill|Shiqing|zh|石慶}} was the prime minister of Qi, the Qi people built the "Shiqing Shrine". This is the beginning of the birth shrine.<ref>[[Zhao Yi]], "The New New Ancestral Hall", Volume 32, "Ancestral Hall", "This is the beginning of the establishment of an ancestral hall.</ref>
In the [[Tang dynasty]], there were certain restrictions on the construction of monuments and shrines by officials in office. According to the [[Tang Code|Tang Rulings and Ordinances]], those who erected shrines or monuments without permission were subject to a penalty of one year of imprisonment.<ref>The [[Tang Code]], Volume 11 (The Law of Office)</ref> The Tang Dynasty called it "the difficulty of building monuments and shrines on the first day.
In the [[Ming dynasty|Ming]], peasants in [[Tengzhou|Tengzhou]] built a shrine to commemorate {{ill|Zhao Bangqing|zh|趙邦清}}, who was appointed to Yanjing for his clean politics. The shrine was built to commemorate him.
During the reign of the [[Tianqi Emperor]] of the Ming Dynasty, the eunuch [[Wei Zhongxian]], ruled the world by conducting his own politics, and those who flattered him or feared his authority were given a He made a living shrine for him. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing (1627), Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote: "Confucius wrote the Spring and Autumn, and Chung-Hsien wrote the Essentials. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote that Confucius had written the "Spring and Autumn" and Chung-Hsien had written the "Essentials. In April of the 7th year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, [[Yuan Chonghuan]] and {{ill|Yan Mingtai|zh|閻鳴泰}} of the [[Ministry of War (imperial China)|Ministry of War]] made an appeal to the government to praise the merits and demerits of Wei Zhongxian and to establish a living shrine for him in both Qiantu.<ref>"Ming Xi Zong Jizhu": "in April of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianqi, the governor of Thistle Liaoning, Yan Mingtai, and the governor of Fuxing Yuan Chong Huan, praised the merits of Wei Zhongxian, and requested the construction of a shrine in front of Ning, giving the name Mao De." The government of the Liaoning Province has been inscribed in gold and stone, and there is no room for extra duties. The minister's single-mindedness in patrolling, strict warning of the battalion generals, do not dare to greed cowardly selfishness, do not dare to give away the hidden string, change the virtual into the real, into the real, easy timid into brave, to have today. Sustainability ancient internal minister who has the right!" ("Ming Xi Zong seven years of the Imperial Court of the Department of the Experiment" Tianqi six years in October)</ref> After that, Wei Zhongxian's raw shrine was "all over the world", "the cost of each shrine was at most hundreds of thousands, at least tens of thousands", "stolen the people's fortune, invaded the public safe, and was cut down. There are countless trees. " When Huang Yun Yasushi made a statue and greeted the statue, he said, "Goto Sansho," and "leaded the general of Bunbu, lined up under the stairs, and worshiped as the beginning."
[[Wei Zhongxian]] has been strongly contemned into modern times for his behavior<ref name=":0" />
[[Gu Yanwu|Gu Yanwu]] lamented, "In this generation, there is no official who does not build a living shrine. And within a few years of leaving office, he would destroy the statues and change their masters.<ref>《Nichi Chi Roku》 vol. 23 (Sheng Shi), p. 644.</ref>
Recently{{When|date=2020-12<!--article says it was recently so likely 2020-->}} a person in China created their own Sheng shrine and stirred online controversy<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |last=网易 |date=2020-12-14 |title=魏忠贤为自己生祠揭幕 |url=https://www.163.com/dy/article/FTRIN9CF0521Q1HR.html |access-date=2022-12-18 |website=www.163.com}}</ref>
== China 2 == The Ming Dynasty, which ruled China from 1368 to 1644, saw a blending of the sacred and secular in daily life. Emperors believed in the Mandate of Heaven and thus had to respect ancestors, honor worthy officials, and ensure the well-being of their subjects. Temples and shrines were found throughout palaces and government offices, and complex and intense debates occurred surrounding both political policies and religious rituals.<ref name=":1" />
A book titled "Xiaotianming: Seishi and Ming Dynasty Politics" delves into two aspects of these debates often considered as symbolic gestures: the political participation of commoners and the establishment of seishi for the living. The book is based on research from the series "Gravity of All Things" by Guangdong People's Publishing House and is authorized by the publisher.<ref name=":1" />
Early Ming literature expressed the idea of a harmonious integration of the state and its people. A Yuan dynasty official, Chen Tianxiang, wrote:<ref name=":1" />
"The state and its people are one body. The people are the blood and breath of the state, and the state is the flesh and bones of the people. Therefore, if the people are rich, the state is rich; if the people are poor, the state is poor; if the people are safe, the state is safe; if the people are in distress, the state is in distress. This is logical."<ref name=":1" />
A stable ruling order required a strong economic foundation as a guarantee, which was a consensus among people. The Mandate of Heaven ideology linked the ruler to the power of heaven. Heaven, also known as the Jade Emperor or the Celestial Emperor, would warn dynasties that fail to protect the lives of their subjects and eventually overthrow them. As the Hongwu Emperor, the founder of the Ming Dynasty, said:<ref name=":1" />
"I fear heaven above, the earth below, and the people in between. ... Rulers are the sons of heaven and earth and the parents of the people. ... If one cannot pacify the people within one's own domain, one has lost the heart of the empire. Why fear such things?"<ref name=":1" />
The Hongwu Emperor saw himself as the son of heaven and earth and the father of his subjects, and thus nurturing the people meant taking responsibility for them. But in reality, the relationship between the emperor and his subjects was more complex, and the establishment of seishi for the living was one of the ways in which this relationship was played out.<ref name=":1" />
The practice of honoring the spirits of deceased ancestors is a common phenomenon in many cultures around the world. However, the practice of building shrines to honor the spirits of the living, known as "shengci" in Chinese, is less well-known. This article will explore the reasons why the study of shengci is important, and provide an overview of the history and significance of this practice.<ref name=":1" />
Firstly, shengci has generated discussions about religious beliefs during the Ming dynasty. In the worldview of the Ming people, certain aspects of the world were closely connected to others, which they referred to as "induction." Politics and religion, including individual or public good and evil, were closely connected through cause and effect, divine induction, and other means, and even various deities were not exceptions. The spirits of the dead also played an important role in the worldview of the Ming people. Temples and shrines were used to worship ancestors and to provide a place for wandering spirits to rest, so as to prevent disasters. But what about the spirits of the living? In the 1930s, Katō Genchi viewed shengci as a universal phenomenon in the history of world religion, closely related to posthumous worship, and referred to it as "worship of mankind" (anthropolatry). However, even if shengci can display holiness, or is similar to posthumous worship or temple of gods and monsters, there are still significant differences between the two. Shengci transcends the basic binary opposition: existence and transcendence, memorial and worship, virtue and spirituality, survival and death. Most Ming people were influenced by the idea of the unity of the three teachings, so the basic Confucian ideas that I discuss also contain elements of Buddhism and Taoism, which are particularly evident in practice.<ref name=":1" />
Secondly, shengci widely exists in East Asian countries, with a history of over two thousand years. As Japanese scholars revealed in the 1930s, shengci first appeared in the Eastern Han Dynasty and was widely practiced in Japan, Korea, and Vietnam, as well as in China. Shengci was also a common practice in other parts of East Asia, and its influence can still be seen in contemporary culture.<ref name=":1" />
In conclusion, the study of shengci is important for understanding the religious beliefs and cultural practices of the Ming dynasty and East Asia. It offers insights into the worldview of the Ming people and the ways in which they honored both the living and the dead. Furthermore, the practice of shengci has a long history and continues to be an important part of East Asian culture today.<ref name=":1" />
The Ming dynasty's "gonglun" and populism
The fifth reason for the study of the "gonglun" in the Ming dynasty is that it reveals the possibility of political discourse and action within a authoritarian system and bureaucracy. Since the 1980s, there has been a resurgence of research on the "public sphere" in the late imperial period, with scholars interested in discussing the possibility of the freedom of association and assembly for the general public to express their opinions on issues of general interest. Historians and anthropologists have, while questioning the validity of seeking European theoretical structures from Chinese history, found various levels of legally recognized communities in Chinese society, religious realms and other places, that are related to modern states to varying degrees. These communities do not necessarily oppose the state, but as William T. Rowe said, they have played a role in cultivating participatory thinking since the late Ming dynasty. However, the academic community generally recognizes that the "gonglun" of the late imperial period was a recognized and legitimate arena for discussing issues of state governance, albeit with certain limitations. Firstly, strictly speaking, it was only local until the mid-19th century when the Taiping Heavenly Kingdom raised national level issues. Secondly, it lacks publishing media. Thirdly, it only represents the opinions of the elite, more specifically, officials. Finally, like Europe at the time, it may only mean a neutral fact: that officials of the Qing dynasty, such as Chen Hongmou, seriously considered and tried to follow the "gonglun", but ultimately the conclusion was that his judgment was correct. Chen quoted the words of the Ming dynasty reformer Lu Kun:...
== Living worship in Japan ==
The [[Edo period]], which lasted from 1603 to 1868 in Japan, saw a rise in the practice of "seishi," or the establishment of one's own spiritual memorial site, where one's soul would be enshrined and worshiped after death. One notable example of this practice is that of [[Matsudaira Sadanobu]], who established his own seishi at Okuzawa Hachiman-gu Shrine in 1797.<ref name=":3">{{Cite web |title=「生祀は」の用例・例文集 - 用例.jp |url=http://yourei.jp/%E7%94%9F%E7%A5%80%E3%81%AF |access-date=2023-01-12 |website=yourei.jp}}</ref>
This rise in seishi is thought to have been influenced by Chinese philosophy, specifically [[Edo neo-Confucianism]], which was introduced to Japan during the Edo period. One notable figure of this period, Yamazaki Ansai, is said to have devised rituals for seishi based on Confucian ceremonies, and established his own seishi in 1671 at his residence in Kyoto.<ref name=":3" />
After this, many scholars and practitioners of Shinto and the Hirata school of National Learning also established their own seishi, each with their own unique rituals. These seishi were believed to be a means of obtaining longevity in life or becoming a god after death. The practice is said to have originated from the story of the great ruler Amaterasu, who enshrined her own divine spirit at [[Mount Miwa]].<ref name=":3" />
The study of seishi is particularly associated with Kato Genchi, who has identified 670 known seishi, with the most recent being established in 1931 at the Nishi-Chogo Shrine in Hokkaido's Higashikokura-cho. The Meiji Emperor's seishi was established in 1876 at Ishinomaki Harbor in Miyagi Prefecture, and again in 1893 at the Yahiko Shrine in Ono village, Kamiina County. The seishi of the Meiji Emperor, his consort, the Taisho Emperor, and the Empress Teimei were also established in various locations, including Hokkaido, Hiroshima, and Himeji.<ref name=":3" />

{{Short description|Shrine to a living person in Shinto and Chinese folk religion}} {{Draft topics|biography|east-asia}} {{AfC topic|geo}}
<!-- Note: The following pages were redirects to [[Sheng_Shrine]] before draftification: *[[Seishi (Shinto)]] --> {{Nihongo|Living worship|2=生祀|3=Seishi}} <!-- Chinese Shengsi -->is worship of a living person in the [[East Asian cultural sphere]], it is done with a {{ill|Sheng Shrine|ja|生祀|zh|生祠}} (Sheng[[Ci shrine|ci]], 生祠)<!-- Japanese Seiji -->.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |date=2022-12-18 |title=为关心民生疾苦而设的生祠，在明代起着什么样的作用？_政治_祭祀_陈天祥 |url=http://web.archive.org/web/20221218062727/https://www.sohu.com/a/531809387_120005162 |access-date=2023-01-12 |website=web.archive.org}}</ref> It is where or worship of a living person is performed. There are two types of enshrinement: enshrining the spirit of a living person who has made a significant contribution and enshrining one's own spirit.
Due to the usage by [[Wei Zhongxian]] they became seen as a sign of corruption and declined in China.<ref name=":2">{{Cite web |title=生祠并非魏忠贤的专利-凤凰周刊 |url=http://www.ifengweekly.com/detil.php?id=16813 |access-date=2022-12-22 |website=www.ifengweekly.com|trans-title=Shengci is not Wei Zhongxian's invention}}</ref> However the practice continued in Japan with the worship of the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] until 1945 and the end of [[State Shinto]].<ref name="Earhart">{{cite book |last1=Earhart |first1=H. Byron |url=https://archive.org/details/religioninjapane0000earh |title=Religion in the Japanese experience: sources and interpretations |date=1974 |publisher=Dickenson Pub. Co. |isbn=0822101041 |edition=3rd |location=Encino, Calif. |author1-link=H. Byron Earhart |url-access=registration}}</ref> [[File:Hiraga_Gennai_seishi01s2040.jpg|thumb|200x200px|[[Hiraga Gennai|Hiraga Gennai]]'s birth shrine in [[Hiroshima Prefecture|Hiroshima Prefecture]], [[Fukuyama, Hiroshima|Fukuyama City]], Tomomachi.]]
== Sheng shrines in China == When [[Han Dynasty#Western Han|Former Han]]'s Reunobu was the prime minister of [[Yan Kingdom (Han dynasty)|Yan]], he built his shrine between Yan and [[Qi (state)|Qi]] and called it the "Reun Gong Shrine". Also, when {{ill|Shiqing|zh|石慶}} was the prime minister of Qi, the Qi people built the "Shiqing Shrine". This is the beginning of the birth shrine.<ref>[[Zhao Yi]], "The New New Ancestral Hall", Volume 32, "Ancestral Hall", "This is the beginning of the establishment of an ancestral hall.</ref>
In the [[Tang dynasty]], there were certain restrictions on the construction of monuments and shrines by officials in office. According to the [[Tang Code|Tang Rulings and Ordinances]], those who erected shrines or monuments without permission were subject to a penalty of one year of imprisonment.<ref>The [[Tang Code]], Volume 11 (The Law of Office)</ref> The Tang Dynasty called it "the difficulty of building monuments and shrines on the first day.
In the [[Ming dynasty|Ming]], peasants in [[Tengzhou|Tengzhou]] built a shrine to commemorate {{ill|Zhao Bangqing|zh|趙邦清}}, who was appointed to Yanjing for his clean politics. The shrine was built to commemorate him.
During the reign of the [[Tianqi Emperor]] of the Ming Dynasty, the eunuch [[Wei Zhongxian]], ruled the world by conducting his own politics, and those who flattered him or feared his authority were given a He made a sheng shrine for him. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing (1627), Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote: "Confucius wrote the Spring and Autumn, and Chung-Hsien wrote the Essentials. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote that Confucius had written the "Spring and Autumn" and Chung-Hsien had written the "Essentials. In April of the 7th year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, [[Yuan Chonghuan]] and {{ill|Yan Mingtai|zh|閻鳴泰}} of the [[Ministry of War (imperial China)|Ministry of War]] made an appeal to the government to praise the merits and demerits of Wei Zhongxian and to establish a sheng shrine for him in both Qiantu.<ref>"Ming Xi Zong Jizhu": "in April of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianqi, the governor of Thistle Liaoning, Yan Mingtai, and the governor of Fuxing Yuan Chong Huan, praised the merits of Wei Zhongxian, and requested the construction of a shrine in front of Ning, giving the name Mao De." The government of the Liaoning Province has been inscribed in gold and stone, and there is no room for extra duties. The minister's single-mindedness in patrolling, strict warning of the battalion generals, do not dare to greed cowardly selfishness, do not dare to give away the hidden string, change the virtual into the real, into the real, easy timid into brave, to have today. Sustainability ancient internal minister who has the right!" ("Ming Xi Zong seven years of the Imperial Court of the Department of the Experiment" Tianqi six years in October)</ref> After that, Wei Zhongxian's raw shrine was "all over the world", "the cost of each shrine was at most hundreds of thousands, at least tens of thousands", "stolen the people's fortune, invaded the public safe, and was cut down. There are countless trees. " When Huang Yun Yasushi made a statue and greeted the statue, he said, "Goto Sansho," and "leaded the general of Bunbu, lined up under the stairs, and worshiped as the beginning."
[[Wei Zhongxian]] has been strongly contemned into modern times for his behavior<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |last=网易 |date=2020-12-14 |title=魏忠贤为自己生祠揭幕 |url=https://www.163.com/dy/article/FTRIN9CF0521Q1HR.html |access-date=2022-12-18 |website=www.163.com}}</ref>
The Ming Dynasty, which ruled China from 1368 to 1644, saw a blending of the sacred and secular in daily life. Emperors believed in the Mandate of Heaven and thus had to respect ancestors, honor worthy officials, and ensure the well-being of their subjects. Temples and shrines were found throughout palaces and government offices, and complex and intense debates occurred surrounding both political policies and religious rituals.<ref name=":1" />
A book titled "Xiaotianming: Seishi and Ming Dynasty Politics" delves into two aspects of these debates often considered as symbolic gestures: the political participation of commoners and the establishment of seishi for the living. The book is based on research from the series "Gravity of All Things" by Guangdong People's Publishing House and is authorized by the publisher.<ref name=":1" />
Early Ming literature expressed the idea of a harmonious integration of the state and its people. A Yuan dynasty official, Chen Tianxiang, wrote:<ref name=":1" />
"The state and its people are one body. The people are the blood and breath of the state, and the state is the flesh and bones of the people. Therefore, if the people are rich, the state is rich; if the people are poor, the state is poor; if the people are safe, the state is safe; if the people are in distress, the state is in distress. This is logical."<ref name=":1" />
A stable ruling order required a strong economic foundation as a guarantee, which was a consensus among people. The Mandate of Heaven ideology linked the ruler to the power of heaven. Heaven, also known as the Jade Emperor or the Celestial Emperor, would warn dynasties that fail to protect the lives of their subjects and eventually overthrow them. As the Hongwu Emperor, the founder of the Ming Dynasty, said:<ref name=":1" />
"I fear heaven above, the earth below, and the people in between. ... Rulers are the sons of heaven and earth and the parents of the people. ... If one cannot pacify the people within one's own domain, one has lost the heart of the empire. Why fear such things?"<ref name=":1" />
The Hongwu Emperor saw himself as the son of heaven and earth and the father of his subjects, and thus nurturing the people meant taking responsibility for them. But in reality, the relationship between the emperor and his subjects was more complex, and the establishment of seishi for the living was one of the ways in which this relationship was played out.<ref name=":1" />
The practice of honoring the spirits of deceased ancestors is a common phenomenon in many cultures around the world. However, the practice of building shrines to honor the spirits of the living, known as "shengci" in Chinese, is less well-known. This article will explore the reasons why the study of shengci is important, and provide an overview of the history and significance of this practice.<ref name=":1" />
Firstly, shengci has generated discussions about religious beliefs during the Ming dynasty. In the worldview of the Ming people, certain aspects of the world were closely connected to others, which they referred to as "induction." Politics and religion, including individual or public good and evil, were closely connected through cause and effect, divine induction, and other means, and even various deities were not exceptions. The spirits of the dead also played an important role in the worldview of the Ming people. Temples and shrines were used to worship ancestors and to provide a place for wandering spirits to rest, so as to prevent disasters. But what about the spirits of the living? In the 1930s, Katō Genchi viewed shengci as a universal phenomenon in the history of world religion, closely related to posthumous worship, and referred to it as "worship of mankind" (anthropolatry). However, even if shengci can display holiness, or is similar to posthumous worship or temple of gods and monsters, there are still significant differences between the two. Shengci transcends the basic binary opposition: existence and transcendence, memorial and worship, virtue and spirituality, survival and death. Most Ming people were influenced by the idea of the unity of the three teachings, so the basic Confucian ideas that I discuss also contain elements of Buddhism and Taoism, which are particularly evident in practice.<ref name=":1" />
Secondly, shengci widely exists in East Asian countries, with a history of over two thousand years. As Japanese scholars revealed in the 1930s, shengci first appeared in the Eastern Han Dynasty and was widely practiced in Japan, Korea, and Vietnam, as well as in China. Shengci was also a common practice in other parts of East Asia, and its influence can still be seen in contemporary culture.<ref name=":1" />
In conclusion, the study of shengci is important for understanding the religious beliefs and cultural practices of the Ming dynasty and East Asia. It offers insights into the worldview of the Ming people and the ways in which they honored both the living and the dead. Furthermore, the practice of shengci has a long history and continues to be an important part of East Asian culture today.<ref name=":1" />
[[Gu Yanwu]] lamented, "In this generation, there is no official who does not build a sheng shrine. And within a few years of leaving office, he would destroy the statues and change their masters.<ref>《Nichi Chi Roku》 vol. 23 (Sheng Shi), p. 644.</ref>
Recently{{When|date=2020-12<!--article says it was recently so likely 2020-->}} a person in China created their own Sheng shrine and stirred online controversy<ref name=":0" />
== Living worship in Japan ==
The [[Edo period]], which lasted from 1603 to 1868 in Japan, saw a rise in the practice of "seishi," or the establishment of one's own spiritual memorial site, where one's soul would be enshrined and worshiped after death. One notable example of this practice is that of [[Matsudaira Sadanobu]], who established his own seishi at Okuzawa Hachiman-gu Shrine in 1797.<ref name=":3">{{Cite web |title=「生祀は」の用例・例文集 - 用例.jp |url=http://yourei.jp/%E7%94%9F%E7%A5%80%E3%81%AF |access-date=2023-01-12 |website=yourei.jp}}</ref>
This rise in seishi is thought to have been influenced by Chinese philosophy, specifically [[Edo neo-Confucianism]], which was introduced to Japan during the Edo period. One notable figure of this period, Yamazaki Ansai, is said to have devised rituals for seishi based on Confucian ceremonies, and established his own seishi in 1671 at his residence in Kyoto.<ref name=":3" />
After this, many scholars and practitioners of Shinto and the Hirata school of National Learning also established their own seishi, each with their own unique rituals. These seishi were believed to be a means of obtaining longevity in life or becoming a god after death. The practice is said to have originated from the story of the great ruler Amaterasu, who enshrined her own divine spirit at [[Mount Miwa]].<ref name=":3" />
The study of seishi is particularly associated with Kato Genchi, who has identified 670 known seishi, with the most recent being established in 1931 at the Nishi-Chogo Shrine in Hokkaido's Higashikokura-cho. The Meiji Emperor's seishi was established in 1876 at Ishinomaki Harbor in Miyagi Prefecture, and again in 1893 at the Yahiko Shrine in Ono village, Kamiina County. The seishi of the Meiji Emperor, his consort, the Taisho Emperor, and the Empress Teimei were also established in various locations, including Hokkaido, Hiroshima, and Himeji.<ref name=":3" />

revision.comment: /* Sheng shrines in China */
revision.text:
{{Short description|Shrine to a living person in Shinto and Chinese folk religion}} {{Draft topics|east-asia}} {{AfC topic|geo}} {{AfC submission|||ts=20230117230436|u=Immanuelle|ns=118}} {{AFC submission|d|v|u=Immanuelle|ns=118|decliner=Curb Safe Charmer|declinets=20230112174826|reason2=nn|ts=20221222235808}} <!-- Do not remove this line! --> {{AFC submission|d|v|u=Immanuelle|ns=118|decliner=DoubleGrazing|declinets=20220628160325|reason2=nn|small=yes|ts=20220218193823}} <!-- Do not remove this line! -->
{{AFC comment|1=Highly unlikely that the sources cited establish notability, and the way they are cited (each only once) is certainly not enough to support the article contents. [[User:DoubleGrazing|DoubleGrazing]] ([[User talk:DoubleGrazing|talk]]) 16:03, 28 June 2022 (UTC)}}
{{AFC comment|1=This appears to be a machine translation of the JA Wikipedia article at [[:ja:生祀]]. ‑‑ [[User:Eirikr|Eiríkr Útlendi]] │<sup>''[[User talk:Eirikr|Tala við mig]]''</sup> 22:05, 21 February 2022 (UTC)}}
----
<!-- Note: The following pages were redirects to [[Sheng_Shrine]] before draftification: *[[Seishi (Shinto)]] --> {{Nihongo|Living worship|2=生祀|3=Seishi}} <!-- Chinese Shengsi -->is worship of a living person in the [[East Asian cultural sphere]], it is done with a {{ill|Sheng Shrine|ja|生祀|zh|生祠}} (Sheng[[Ci shrine|ci]], 生祠)<!-- Japanese Seiji -->.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |date=2022-12-18 |title=为关心民生疾苦而设的生祠，在明代起着什么样的作用？_政治_祭祀_陈天祥 |url=http://web.archive.org/web/20221218062727/https://www.sohu.com/a/531809387_120005162 |access-date=2023-01-12 |website=web.archive.org}}</ref> It is where or worship of a living person is performed. There are two types of enshrinement: enshrining the spirit of a living person who has made a significant contribution and enshrining one's own spirit.
Due to the usage by [[Wei Zhongxian]] they became seen as a sign of corruption and declined in China.<ref name=":2">{{Cite web |title=生祠并非魏忠贤的专利-凤凰周刊 |url=http://www.ifengweekly.com/detil.php?id=16813 |access-date=2022-12-22 |website=www.ifengweekly.com|trans-title=Shengci is not Wei Zhongxian's invention}}</ref> However the practice continued in Japan with the worship of the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] until 1945 and the end of [[State Shinto]].<ref name="Earhart">{{cite book |last1=Earhart |first1=H. Byron |url=https://archive.org/details/religioninjapane0000earh |title=Religion in the Japanese experience: sources and interpretations |date=1974 |publisher=Dickenson Pub. Co. |isbn=0822101041 |edition=3rd |location=Encino, Calif. |author1-link=H. Byron Earhart |url-access=registration}}</ref> [[File:Hiraga_Gennai_seishi01s2040.jpg|thumb|200x200px|[[Hiraga Gennai|Hiraga Gennai]]'s birth shrine in [[Hiroshima Prefecture|Hiroshima Prefecture]], [[Fukuyama, Hiroshima|Fukuyama City]], Tomomachi.]]
== Sheng shrines in China == When [[Han Dynasty#Western Han|Former Han]]'s Reunobu was the prime minister of [[Yan Kingdom (Han dynasty)|Yan]], he built his shrine between Yan and [[Qi (state)|Qi]] and called it the "Reun Gong Shrine". Also, when {{ill|Shiqing|zh|石慶}} was the prime minister of Qi, the Qi people built the "Shiqing Shrine". This is the beginning of the birth shrine.<ref>[[Zhao Yi]], "The New New Ancestral Hall", Volume 32, "Ancestral Hall", "This is the beginning of the establishment of an ancestral hall.</ref>
In the [[Tang dynasty]], there were certain restrictions on the construction of monuments and shrines by officials in office. According to the [[Tang Code|Tang Rulings and Ordinances]], those who erected shrines or monuments without permission were subject to a penalty of one year of imprisonment.<ref>The [[Tang Code]], Volume 11 (The Law of Office)</ref> The Tang Dynasty called it "the difficulty of building monuments and shrines on the first day.
In the [[Ming dynasty|Ming]], peasants in [[Tengzhou|Tengzhou]] built a shrine to commemorate {{ill|Zhao Bangqing|zh|趙邦清}}, who was appointed to Yanjing for his clean politics. The shrine was built to commemorate him.
During the reign of the [[Tianqi Emperor]] of the Ming Dynasty, the eunuch [[Wei Zhongxian]], ruled the world by conducting his own politics, and those who flattered him or feared his authority were given a He made a sheng shrine for him. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing (1627), Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote: "Confucius wrote the Spring and Autumn, and Chung-Hsien wrote the Essentials. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote that Confucius had written the "Spring and Autumn" and Chung-Hsien had written the "Essentials. In April of the 7th year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, [[Yuan Chonghuan]] and {{ill|Yan Mingtai|zh|閻鳴泰}} of the [[Ministry of War (imperial China)|Ministry of War]] made an appeal to the government to praise the merits and demerits of Wei Zhongxian and to establish a sheng shrine for him in both Qiantu.<ref>"Ming Xi Zong Jizhu": "in April of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianqi, the governor of Thistle Liaoning, Yan Mingtai, and the governor of Fuxing Yuan Chong Huan, praised the merits of Wei Zhongxian, and requested the construction of a shrine in front of Ning, giving the name Mao De." The government of the Liaoning Province has been inscribed in gold and stone, and there is no room for extra duties. The minister's single-mindedness in patrolling, strict warning of the battalion generals, do not dare to greed cowardly selfishness, do not dare to give away the hidden string, change the virtual into the real, into the real, easy timid into brave, to have today. Sustainability ancient internal minister who has the right!" ("Ming Xi Zong seven years of the Imperial Court of the Department of the Experiment" Tianqi six years in October)</ref> After that, Wei Zhongxian's raw shrine was "all over the world", "the cost of each shrine was at most hundreds of thousands, at least tens of thousands", "stolen the people's fortune, invaded the public safe, and was cut down. There are countless trees. " When Huang Yun Yasushi made a statue and greeted the statue, he said, "Goto Sansho," and "leaded the general of Bunbu, lined up under the stairs, and worshiped as the beginning."
[[Wei Zhongxian]] has been strongly contemned into modern times for his behavior<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |last=网易 |date=2020-12-14 |title=魏忠贤为自己生祠揭幕 |url=https://www.163.com/dy/article/FTRIN9CF0521Q1HR.html |access-date=2022-12-18 |website=www.163.com}}</ref>
[[Gu Yanwu]] lamented, "In this generation, there is no official who does not build a sheng shrine. And within a few years of leaving office, he would destroy the statues and change their masters.<ref>《Nichi Chi Roku》 vol. 23 (Sheng Shi), p. 644.</ref>
Recently{{When|date=2020-12<!--article says it was recently so likely 2020-->}} a person in China created their own Sheng shrine and stirred online controversy<ref name=":0" />
== Living worship in Japan ==
The [[Edo period]], which lasted from 1603 to 1868 in Japan, saw a rise in the practice of "seishi," or the establishment of one's own spiritual memorial site, where one's soul would be enshrined and worshiped after death. One notable example of this practice is that of [[Matsudaira Sadanobu]], who established his own seishi at Okuzawa Hachiman-gu Shrine in 1797.<ref name=":3">{{Cite web |title=「生祀は」の用例・例文集 - 用例.jp |url=http://yourei.jp/%E7%94%9F%E7%A5%80%E3%81%AF |access-date=2023-01-12 |website=yourei.jp}}</ref>
This rise in seishi is thought to have been influenced by Chinese philosophy, specifically [[Edo neo-Confucianism]], which was introduced to Japan during the Edo period. One notable figure of this period, Yamazaki Ansai, is said to have devised rituals for seishi based on Confucian ceremonies, and established his own seishi in 1671 at his residence in Kyoto.<ref name=":3" />
After this, many scholars and practitioners of Shinto and the Hirata school of National Learning also established their own seishi, each with their own unique rituals. These seishi were believed to be a means of obtaining longevity in life or becoming a god after death. The practice is said to have originated from the story of the great ruler Amaterasu, who enshrined her own divine spirit at [[Mount Miwa]].<ref name=":3" />
The study of seishi is particularly associated with Kato Genchi, who has identified 670 known seishi, with the most recent being established in 1931 at the Nishi-Chogo Shrine in Hokkaido's Higashikokura-cho. The Meiji Emperor's seishi was established in 1876 at Ishinomaki Harbor in Miyagi Prefecture, and again in 1893 at the Yahiko Shrine in Ono village, Kamiina County. The seishi of the Meiji Emperor, his consort, the Taisho Emperor, and the Empress Teimei were also established in various locations, including Hokkaido, Hiroshima, and Himeji.<ref name=":3" />
== References == <references />
== See Also ==
* [[Tamaya]] * {{ill|Okuto Castle|ja|奥都城}}

These seishi were believed to be a means of obtaining longevity in life or becoming a god after death. The practice is said to have originated from the story of the great ruler Amaterasu, who enshrined her own divine spirit at [[Mount Miwa]].<ref name=":3" />
The study of seishi is particularly associated with Kato Genchi, who has identified 670 known seishi, with the most recent being established in 1931 at the Nishi-Chogo Shrine in Hokkaido's Higashikokura-cho. The Meiji Emperor's seishi was established in 1876 at Ishinomaki Harbor in Miyagi Prefecture, and again in 1893 at the Yahiko Shrine in Ono village, Kamiina County. The seishi of the Meiji Emperor, his consort, the Taisho Emperor, and the Empress Teimei were also established in various locations, including Hokkaido, Hiroshima, and Himeji.<ref name=":3" />

{{AFC submission|||u=Immanuelle|ns=118|ts=20230117230436}} <!-- Do not remove this line! --> {{AFC submission|d|v|u=Immanuelle|ns=118|decliner=Curb Safe Charmer|declinets=20230112174826|reason2=nn|ts=20221222235808}} <!-- Do not remove this line! --> {{AFC submission|d|v|u=Immanuelle|ns=118|decliner=DoubleGrazing|declinets=20220628160325|reason2=nn|small=yes|ts=20220218193823}} <!-- Do not remove this line! -->
{{AFC comment|1=This is certainly notable, but needs editing for language and perhaps further sourcing. [[User:PK650|PK650]] ([[User talk:PK650|talk]]) 07:34, 9 March 2023 (UTC)}}
{{AFC comment|1=Highly unlikely that the sources cited establish notability, and the way they are cited (each only once) is certainly not enough to support the article contents. [[User:DoubleGrazing|DoubleGrazing]] ([[User talk:DoubleGrazing|talk]]) 16:03, 28 June 2022 (UTC)}}
{{AFC comment|1=This appears to be a machine translation of the JA Wikipedia article at [[:ja:生祀]]. ‑‑ [[User:Eirikr|Eiríkr Útlendi]] │<sup>''[[User talk:Eirikr|Tala við mig]]''</sup> 22:05, 21 February 2022 (UTC)}}
----
{{Short description|Shrine to a living person in Shinto and Chinese folk religion}} {{Draft topics|east-asia}} {{AfC topic|geo}}
<!-- Note: The following pages were redirects to [[Sheng_Shrine]] before draftification: *[[Seishi (Shinto)]] --> {{Nihongo|Living worship|2=生祀|3=Seishi}} <!-- Chinese Shengsi -->is worship of a living person in the [[East Asian cultural sphere]], it is done with a {{ill|Sheng Shrine|ja|生祀|zh|生祠}} (Sheng[[Ci shrine|ci]], 生祠)<!-- Japanese Seiji -->.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |date=2022-12-18 |title=为关心民生疾苦而设的生祠，在明代起着什么样的作用？_政治_祭祀_陈天祥 |url=http://web.archive.org/web/20221218062727/https://www.sohu.com/a/531809387_120005162 |access-date=2023-01-12 |website=web.archive.org}}</ref> It is where or worship of a living person is performed. There are two types of enshrinement: enshrining the spirit of a living person who has made a significant contribution and enshrining one's own spirit.
Due to the usage by [[Wei Zhongxian]] they became seen as a sign of corruption and declined in China.<ref name=":2">{{Cite web |title=生祠并非魏忠贤的专利-凤凰周刊 |url=http://www.ifengweekly.com/detil.php?id=16813 |access-date=2022-12-22 |website=www.ifengweekly.com|trans-title=Shengci is not Wei Zhongxian's invention}}</ref> However the practice continued in Japan with the worship of the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] until 1945 and the end of [[State Shinto]].<ref name="Earhart">{{cite book |last1=Earhart |first1=H. Byron |url=https://archive.org/details/religioninjapane0000earh |title=Religion in the Japanese experience: sources and interpretations |date=1974 |publisher=Dickenson Pub. Co. |isbn=0822101041 |edition=3rd |location=Encino, Calif. |author1-link=H. Byron Earhart |url-access=registration}}</ref> [[File:Hiraga_Gennai_seishi01s2040.jpg|thumb|200x200px|[[Hiraga Gennai|Hiraga Gennai]]'s birth shrine in [[Hiroshima Prefecture|Hiroshima Prefecture]], [[Fukuyama, Hiroshima|Fukuyama City]], Tomomachi.]]
== Sheng shrines in China == When [[Han Dynasty#Western Han|Former Han]]'s Reunobu was the prime minister of [[Yan Kingdom (Han dynasty)|Yan]], he built his shrine between Yan and [[Qi (state)|Qi]] and called it the "Reun Gong Shrine". Also, when {{ill|Shiqing|zh|石慶}} was the prime minister of Qi, the Qi people built the "Shiqing Shrine". This is the beginning of the birth shrine.<ref>[[Zhao Yi]], "The New New Ancestral Hall", Volume 32, "Ancestral Hall", "This is the beginning of the establishment of an ancestral hall.</ref>
In the [[Tang dynasty]], there were certain restrictions on the construction of monuments and shrines by officials in office. According to the [[Tang Code|Tang Rulings and Ordinances]], those who erected shrines or monuments without permission were subject to a penalty of one year of imprisonment.<ref>The [[Tang Code]], Volume 11 (The Law of Office)</ref> The Tang Dynasty called it "the difficulty of building monuments and shrines on the first day.
In the [[Ming dynasty|Ming]], peasants in [[Tengzhou|Tengzhou]] built a shrine to commemorate {{ill|Zhao Bangqing|zh|趙邦清}}, who was appointed to Yanjing for his clean politics. The shrine was built to commemorate him.
During the reign of the [[Tianqi Emperor]] of the Ming Dynasty, the eunuch [[Wei Zhongxian]], ruled the world by conducting his own politics, and those who flattered him or feared his authority were given a He made a sheng shrine for him. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing (1627), Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote: "Confucius wrote the Spring and Autumn, and Chung-Hsien wrote the Essentials. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote that Confucius had written the "Spring and Autumn" and Chung-Hsien had written the "Essentials. In April of the 7th year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, [[Yuan Chonghuan]] and {{ill|Yan Mingtai|zh|閻鳴泰}} of the [[Ministry of War (imperial China)|Ministry of War]] made an appeal to the government to praise the merits and demerits of Wei Zhongxian and to establish a sheng shrine for him in both Qiantu.<ref>"Ming Xi Zong Jizhu": "in April of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianqi, the governor of Thistle Liaoning, Yan Mingtai, and the governor of Fuxing Yuan Chong Huan, praised the merits of Wei Zhongxian, and requested the construction of a shrine in front of Ning, giving the name Mao De." The government of the Liaoning Province has been inscribed in gold and stone, and there is no room for extra duties. The minister's single-mindedness in patrolling, strict warning of the battalion generals, do not dare to greed cowardly selfishness, do not dare to give away the hidden string, change the virtual into the real, into the real, easy timid into brave, to have today. Sustainability ancient internal minister who has the right!" ("Ming Xi Zong seven years of the Imperial Court of the Department of the Experiment" Tianqi six years in October)</ref> After that, Wei Zhongxian's raw shrine was "all over the world", "the cost of each shrine was at most hundreds of thousands, at least tens of thousands", "stolen the people's fortune, invaded the public safe, and was cut down. There are countless trees. " When Huang Yun Yasushi made a statue and greeted the statue, he said, "Goto Sansho," and "leaded the general of Bunbu, lined up under the stairs, and worshiped as the beginning."
[[Wei Zhongxian]] has been strongly contemned into modern times for his behavior<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |last=网易 |date=2020-12-14 |title=魏忠贤为自己生祠揭幕 |url=https://www.163.com/dy/article/FTRIN9CF0521Q1HR.html |access-date=2022-12-18 |website=www.163.com}}</ref>
[[Gu Yanwu]] lamented, "In this generation, there is no official who does not build a sheng shrine. And within a few years of leaving office, he would destroy the statues and change their masters.<ref>《Nichi Chi Roku》 vol. 23 (Sheng Shi), p. 644.</ref>
Recently{{When|date=2020-12<!--article says it was recently so likely 2020-->}} a person in China created their own Sheng shrine and stirred online controversy<ref name=":0" />
== Living worship in Japan ==
The [[Edo period]], which lasted from 1603 to 1868 in Japan, saw a rise in the practice of "seishi," or the establishment of one's own spiritual memorial site, where one's soul would be enshrined and worshiped after death. One notable example of this practice is that of [[Matsudaira Sadanobu]], who established his own seishi at Okuzawa Hachiman-gu Shrine in 1797.<ref name=":3">{{Cite web |title=「生祀は」の用例・例文集 - 用例.jp |url=http://yourei.jp/%E7%94%9F%E7%A5%80%E3%81%AF |access-date=2023-01-12 |website=yourei.jp}}</ref>
This rise in seishi is thought to have been influenced by Chinese philosophy, specifically [[Edo neo-Confucianism]], which was introduced to Japan during the Edo period. One notable figure of this period, Yamazaki Ansai, is said to have devised rituals for seishi based on Confucian ceremonies, and established his own seishi in 1671 at his residence in Kyoto.<ref name=":3" />
After this, many scholars and practitioners of Shinto and the Hirata school of National Learning also established their own seishi, each with their own unique rituals. These seishi were believed to be a means of obtaining longevity in life or becoming a god after death. The practice is said to have originated from the story of the great ruler Amaterasu, who enshrined her own divine spirit at [[Mount Miwa]].<ref name=":3" />
The study of seishi is particularly associated with Kato Genchi, who has identified 670 known seishi, with the most recent being established in 1931 at the Nishi-Chogo Shrine in Hokkaido's Higashikokura-cho. The Meiji Emperor's seishi was established in 1876 at Ishinomaki Harbor in Miyagi Prefecture, and again in 1893 at the Yahiko Shrine in Ono village, Kamiina County. The seishi of the Meiji Emperor, his consort, the Taisho Emperor, and the Empress Teimei were also established in various locations, including Hokkaido, Hiroshima, and Himeji.<ref name=":3" />
== References == <references />
== See Also ==
* [[Tamaya]] * {{ill|Okuto Castle|ja|奥都城}}

("Ming Xi Zong seven years of the Imperial Court of the Department of the Experiment" Tianqi six years in October)</ref> After that, Wei Zhongxian's raw shrine was "all over the world", "the cost of each shrine was at most hundreds of thousands, at least tens of thousands", "stolen the people's fortune, invaded the public safe, and was cut down. There are countless trees. " When Huang Yun Yasushi made a statue and greeted the statue, he said, "Goto Sansho," and "leaded the general of Bunbu, lined up under the stairs, and worshiped as the beginning."
[[Wei Zhongxian]] has been strongly contemned into modern times for his behavior<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |last=网易 |date=2020-12-14 |title=魏忠贤为自己生祠揭幕 |url=https://www.163.com/dy/article/FTRIN9CF0521Q1HR.html |access-date=2022-12-18 |website=www.163.com}}</ref>
[[Gu Yanwu]] lamented, "In this generation, there is no official who does not build a sheng shrine. And within a few years of leaving office, he would destroy the statues and change their masters.<ref>《Nichi Chi Roku》 vol. 23 (Sheng Shi), p. 644.</ref>
Recently{{When|date=2020-12<!--article says it was recently so likely 2020-->}} a person in China created their own Sheng shrine and stirred online controversy<ref name=":0" />
== Living worship in Japan ==
The [[Edo period]], which lasted from 1603 to 1868 in Japan, saw a rise in the practice of "seishi," or the establishment of one's own spiritual memorial site, where one's soul would be enshrined and worshiped after death. One notable example of this practice is that of [[Matsudaira Sadanobu]], who established his own seishi at Okuzawa Hachiman-gu Shrine in 1797.<ref name=":3">{{Cite web |title=「生祀は」の用例・例文集 - 用例.jp |url=http://yourei.jp/%E7%94%9F%E7%A5%80%E3%81%AF |access-date=2023-01-12 |website=yourei.jp}}</ref>
This rise in seishi is thought to have been influenced by Chinese philosophy, specifically [[Edo neo-Confucianism]], which was introduced to Japan during the Edo period. One notable figure of this period, Yamazaki Ansai, is said to have devised rituals for seishi based on Confucian ceremonies, and established his own seishi in 1671 at his residence in Kyoto.<ref name=":3" />
After this, many scholars and practitioners of Shinto and the Hirata school of National Learning also established their own seishi, each with their own unique rituals. These seishi were believed to be a means of obtaining longevity in life or becoming a god after death. The practice is said to have originated from the story of the great ruler Amaterasu, who enshrined her own divine spirit at [[Mount Miwa]].<ref name=":3" />
The study of seishi is particularly associated with Kato Genchi, who has identified 670 known seishi, with the most recent being established in 1931 at the Nishi-Chogo Shrine in Hokkaido's Higashikokura-cho. The Meiji Emperor's seishi was established in 1876 at Ishinomaki Harbor in Miyagi Prefecture, and again in 1893 at the Yahiko Shrine in Ono village, Kamiina County. The seishi of the Meiji Emperor, his consort, the Taisho Emperor, and the Empress Teimei were also established in various locations, including Hokkaido, Hiroshima, and Himeji.<ref name=":3" />

{{Short description|Shrine to a living person in Shinto and Chinese folk religion}} {{Draft topics|east-asia}} {{AfC topic|geo}}
<!-- Note: The following pages were redirects to [[Sheng_Shrine]] before draftification: *[[Seishi (Shinto)]] --> '''Living worship''' ([[Japanese language|Japanese]]: 生祀, ''seishi''; [[Chinese language|Chinese]]: 生祠, ''shengci'') is the [[worship]] of living people practiced in the [[East Asian cultural sphere]]. In China, it is practiced at ''sheng'' shrines (Chinese: 生祠, Sheng[[Ci shrine|ci]]).<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |date=2022-12-18 |title=为关心民生疾苦而设的生祠，在明代起着什么样的作用？_政治_祭祀_陈天祥 |url=http://web.archive.org/web/20221218062727/https://www.sohu.com/a/531809387_120005162 |access-date=2023-01-12 |website=web.archive.org}}</ref> There are two types of enshrinement: the enshrinement of the spirit of a living person who has made a significant contribution, and the enshrinement of one's own spirit.
Due to the usage by [[Wei Zhongxian]] the practice became seen as a sign of corruption and declined in China.<ref name=":2">{{Cite web |title=生祠并非魏忠贤的专利-凤凰周刊 |url=http://www.ifengweekly.com/detil.php?id=16813 |access-date=2022-12-22 |website=www.ifengweekly.com|trans-title=Shengci is not Wei Zhongxian's invention}}</ref> In Japan, the practice was most prominent with the worship of the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] during the period of the imperial [[State Shinto]] from the late 19th century until 1945.<ref name="Earhart">{{cite book |last1=Earhart |first1=H. Byron |url=https://archive.org/details/religioninjapane0000earh |title=Religion in the Japanese experience: sources and interpretations |date=1974 |publisher=Dickenson Pub. Co. |isbn=0822101041 |edition=3rd |location=Encino, Calif. |author1-link=H. Byron Earhart |url-access=registration}}</ref> [[File:Hiraga_Gennai_seishi01s2040.jpg|thumb|200x200px|[[Hiraga Gennai|Hiraga Gennai]]'s birth shrine in [[Hiroshima Prefecture|Hiroshima Prefecture]], [[Fukuyama, Hiroshima|Fukuyama City]], Tomomachi.]]
== Sheng shrines in China == When [[Han Dynasty#Western Han|Former Han]]'s Reunobu was the prime minister of [[Yan Kingdom (Han dynasty)|Yan]], he built his shrine between Yan and [[Qi (state)|Qi]] and called it the "Reun Gong Shrine". Also, when {{ill|Shiqing|zh|石慶}} was the prime minister of Qi, the Qi people built the "Shiqing Shrine". This is the beginning of the birth shrine.<ref>[[Zhao Yi]], "The New New Ancestral Hall", Volume 32, "Ancestral Hall", "This is the beginning of the establishment of an ancestral hall.</ref>
In the [[Tang dynasty]], there were certain restrictions on the construction of monuments and shrines by officials in office. According to the [[Tang Code|Tang Rulings and Ordinances]], those who erected shrines or monuments without permission were subject to a penalty of one year of imprisonment.<ref>The [[Tang Code]], Volume 11 (The Law of Office)</ref> The Tang Dynasty called it "the difficulty of building monuments and shrines on the first day.
In the [[Ming dynasty|Ming]], peasants in [[Tengzhou|Tengzhou]] built a shrine to commemorate {{ill|Zhao Bangqing|zh|趙邦清}}, who was appointed to Yanjing for his clean politics. The shrine was built to commemorate him.
During the reign of the [[Tianqi Emperor]] of the Ming Dynasty, the eunuch [[Wei Zhongxian]], ruled the world by conducting his own politics, and those who flattered him or feared his authority were given a He made a sheng shrine for him. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing (1627), Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote: "Confucius wrote the Spring and Autumn, and Chung-Hsien wrote the Essentials. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote that Confucius had written the "Spring and Autumn" and Chung-Hsien had written the "Essentials. In April of the 7th year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, [[Yuan Chonghuan]] and {{ill|Yan Mingtai|zh|閻鳴泰}} of the [[Ministry of War (imperial China)|Ministry of War]] made an appeal to the government to praise the merits and demerits of Wei Zhongxian and to establish a sheng shrine for him in both Qiantu.<ref>"Ming Xi Zong Jizhu": "in April of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianqi, the governor of Thistle Liaoning, Yan Mingtai, and the governor of Fuxing Yuan Chong Huan, praised the merits of Wei Zhongxian, and requested the construction of a shrine in front of Ning, giving the name Mao De." The government of the Liaoning Province has been inscribed in gold and stone, and there is no room for extra duties. The minister's single-mindedness in patrolling, strict warning of the battalion generals, do not dare to greed cowardly selfishness, do not dare to give away the hidden string, change the virtual into the real, into the real, easy timid into brave, to have today. Sustainability ancient internal minister who has the right!" ("Ming Xi Zong seven years of the Imperial Court of the Department of the Experiment" Tianqi six years in October)</ref> After that, Wei Zhongxian's raw shrine was "all over the world", "the cost of each shrine was at most hundreds of thousands, at least tens of thousands", "stolen the people's fortune, invaded the public safe, and was cut down. There are countless trees. " When Huang Yun Yasushi made a statue and greeted the statue, he said, "Goto Sansho," and "leaded the general of Bunbu, lined up under the stairs, and worshiped as the beginning."
[[Wei Zhongxian]] has been strongly contemned into modern times for his behavior<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |last=网易 |date=2020-12-14 |title=魏忠贤为自己生祠揭幕 |url=https://www.163.com/dy/article/FTRIN9CF0521Q1HR.html |access-date=2022-12-18 |website=www.163.com}}</ref>
[[Gu Yanwu]] lamented, "In this generation, there is no official who does not build a sheng shrine. And within a few years of leaving office, he would destroy the statues and change their masters.<ref>《Nichi Chi Roku》 vol. 23 (Sheng Shi), p. 644.</ref>
Recently{{When|date=2020-12<!--article says it was recently so likely 2020-->}} a person in China created their own Sheng shrine and stirred online controversy<ref name=":0" />
== Living worship in Japan ==
The [[Edo period]], which lasted from 1603 to 1868 in Japan, saw a rise in the practice of "seishi," or the establishment of one's own spiritual memorial site, where one's soul would be enshrined and worshiped after death. One notable example of this practice is that of [[Matsudaira Sadanobu]], who established his own seishi at Okuzawa Hachiman-gu Shrine in 1797.<ref name=":3">{{Cite web |title=「生祀は」の用例・例文集 - 用例.jp |url=http://yourei.jp/%E7%94%9F%E7%A5%80%E3%81%AF |access-date=2023-01-12 |website=yourei.jp}}</ref>
This rise in seishi is thought to have been influenced by Chinese philosophy, specifically [[Edo neo-Confucianism]], which was introduced to Japan during the Edo period. One notable figure of this period, Yamazaki Ansai, is said to have devised rituals for seishi based on Confucian ceremonies, and established his own seishi in 1671 at his residence in Kyoto.<ref name=":3" />
After this, many scholars and practitioners of Shinto and the Hirata school of National Learning also established their own seishi, each with their own unique rituals. These seishi were believed to be a means of obtaining longevity in life or becoming a god after death. The practice is said to have originated from the story of the great ruler Amaterasu, who enshrined her own divine spirit at [[Mount Miwa]].<ref name=":3" />
The study of seishi is particularly associated with Kato Genchi, who has identified 670 known seishi, with the most recent being established in 1931 at the Nishi-Chogo Shrine in Hokkaido's Higashikokura-cho. The Meiji Emperor's seishi was established in 1876 at Ishinomaki Harbor in Miyagi Prefecture, and again in 1893 at the Yahiko Shrine in Ono village, Kamiina County. The seishi of the Meiji Emperor, his consort, the Taisho Emperor, and the Empress Teimei were also established in various locations, including Hokkaido, Hiroshima, and Himeji.<ref name=":3" />

This is the beginning of the birth shrine.<ref>[[Zhao Yi]], "The New New Ancestral Hall", Volume 32, "Ancestral Hall", "This is the beginning of the establishment of an ancestral hall.</ref>
In the [[Tang dynasty]], there were certain restrictions on the construction of monuments and shrines by officials in office. According to the [[Tang Code|Tang Rulings and Ordinances]], those who erected shrines or monuments without permission were subject to a penalty of one year of imprisonment.<ref>The [[Tang Code]], Volume 11 (The Law of Office)</ref> The Tang Dynasty called it "the difficulty of building monuments and shrines on the first day.
In the [[Ming dynasty|Ming]], peasants in [[Tengzhou|Tengzhou]] built a shrine to commemorate {{ill|Zhao Bangqing|zh|趙邦清}}, who was appointed to Yanjing for his clean politics. The shrine was built to commemorate him.
During the reign of the [[Tianqi Emperor]] of the Ming Dynasty, the eunuch [[Wei Zhongxian]], ruled the world by conducting his own politics, and those who flattered him or feared his authority were given a He made a sheng shrine for him. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing (1627), Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote: "Confucius wrote the Spring and Autumn, and Chung-Hsien wrote the Essentials. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote that Confucius had written the "Spring and Autumn" and Chung-Hsien had written the "Essentials. In April of the 7th year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, [[Yuan Chonghuan]] and {{ill|Yan Mingtai|zh|閻鳴泰}} of the [[Ministry of War (imperial China)|Ministry of War]] made an appeal to the government to praise the merits and demerits of Wei Zhongxian and to establish a sheng shrine for him in both Qiantu.<ref>"Ming Xi Zong Jizhu": "in April of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianqi, the governor of Thistle Liaoning, Yan Mingtai, and the governor of Fuxing Yuan Chong Huan, praised the merits of Wei Zhongxian, and requested the construction of a shrine in front of Ning, giving the name Mao De." The government of the Liaoning Province has been inscribed in gold and stone, and there is no room for extra duties. The minister's single-mindedness in patrolling, strict warning of the battalion generals, do not dare to greed cowardly selfishness, do not dare to give away the hidden string, change the virtual into the real, into the real, easy timid into brave, to have today. Sustainability ancient internal minister who has the right!" ("Ming Xi Zong seven years of the Imperial Court of the Department of the Experiment" Tianqi six years in October)</ref> After that, Wei Zhongxian's raw shrine was "all over the world", "the cost of each shrine was at most hundreds of thousands, at least tens of thousands", "stolen the people's fortune, invaded the public safe, and was cut down. There are countless trees. " When Huang Yun Yasushi made a statue and greeted the statue, he said, "Goto Sansho," and "leaded the general of Bunbu, lined up under the stairs, and worshiped as the beginning."
[[Wei Zhongxian]] has been strongly contemned into modern times for his behavior<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |last=网易 |date=2020-12-14 |title=魏忠贤为自己生祠揭幕 |url=https://www.163.com/dy/article/FTRIN9CF0521Q1HR.html |access-date=2022-12-18 |website=www.163.com}}</ref>
[[Gu Yanwu]] lamented, "In this generation, there is no official who does not build a sheng shrine. And within a few years of leaving office, he would destroy the statues and change their masters.<ref>《Nichi Chi Roku》 vol. 23 (Sheng Shi), p. 644.</ref>
Recently{{When|date=2020-12<!--article says it was recently so likely 2020-->}} a person in China created their own Sheng shrine and stirred online controversy<ref name=":0" />
== In Japan ==
The [[Edo period]] (1603-1868) saw a rise in the practice of ''seishi'', or the establishment of one's own spiritual memorial site, where one's soul would be enshrined and worshiped after death. One notable example of this practice is that of [[Matsudaira Sadanobu]], who established his own ''seishi'' at Okuzawa Hachiman-gu in 1797.<ref name=":3">{{Cite web |title=「生祀は」の用例・例文集 - 用例.jp |url=http://yourei.jp/%E7%94%9F%E7%A5%80%E3%81%AF |access-date=2023-01-12 |website=yourei.jp}}</ref>
The rise in ''seishi'' is thought to have been influenced by Chinese philosophy, specifically [[Edo neo-Confucianism]], which was introduced to Japan during the Edo period. One notable figure of this period, [[Yamazaki Ansai]], is said to have devised rituals for ''seishi'' based on Confucian ceremonies, and established his own ''seishi'' in 1671 at his residence in [[Kyoto]].<ref name=":3" />
After this, many scholars and practitioners of Shinto and the Hirata school of ''[[kokugaku]]'' ("national study") also established their own ''seishi'', each with their own unique rituals. These ''seishi'' were believed to be a means of obtaining longevity in life or becoming a god after death. The practice is said to have originated from the mythological story of the sun goddess [[Amaterasu]], who enshrined her own divine spirit at [[Mount Miwa]].<ref name=":3" />
The study of ''seishi'' is particularly associated with Genchi Kato, who has identified 670 known ''seishi'', with the most recent being established in 1931 at the Nishi-Chogo Shrine in Higashikokura, [[Hokkaido]]. The ''seishi'' of [[Emperor Meiji]] was established in 1876 in [[Ishinomaki]], [[Miyagi Prefecture]], and again in 1893 at the Yahiko Shrine in [[Tatsuno, Nagano|Tatsuno]], [[Nagano Prefecture]]. The ''seishi'' of Emperor Meiji, [[Empress Shōken]], [[Emperor Taishō|Emperor Taisho]], and [[Empress Teimei]] were also established in various locations, including Hokkaido, [[Hiroshima]], and [[Himeji]].<ref name=":3" />

<!-- Note: The following pages were redirects to [[Sheng_Shrine]] before draftification: *[[Seishi (Shinto)]] --> '''Living worship''' ([[Japanese language|Japanese]]: 生祀, ''seishi''; [[Chinese language|Chinese]]: 生祠, ''shengci'') is the [[worship]] of living people practiced in the [[East Asian cultural sphere]]. In China, it is practiced at ''sheng'' shrines (Chinese: 生祠, Sheng[[Ci shrine|ci]]).<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |date=2022-12-18 |title=为关心民生疾苦而设的生祠，在明代起着什么样的作用？_政治_祭祀_陈天祥 |url=http://web.archive.org/web/20221218062727/https://www.sohu.com/a/531809387_120005162 |access-date=2023-01-12 |website=web.archive.org}}</ref> There are two types of enshrinement: the enshrinement of the spirit of a living person who has made a significant contribution, and the enshrinement of one's own spirit.
Due to the usage by [[Wei Zhongxian]] the practice became seen as a sign of corruption and declined in China.<ref name=":2">{{Cite web |title=生祠并非魏忠贤的专利-凤凰周刊 |url=http://www.ifengweekly.com/detil.php?id=16813 |access-date=2022-12-22 |website=www.ifengweekly.com|trans-title=Shengci is not Wei Zhongxian's invention}}</ref> In Japan, the practice was most prominent with the worship of the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] during the period of the imperial [[State Shinto]] from the late 19th century until 1945.<ref name="Earhart">{{cite book |last1=Earhart |first1=H. Byron |url=https://archive.org/details/religioninjapane0000earh |title=Religion in the Japanese experience: sources and interpretations |date=1974 |publisher=Dickenson Pub. Co. |isbn=0822101041 |edition=3rd |location=Encino, Calif. |author1-link=H. Byron Earhart |url-access=registration}}</ref> [[File:Hiraga_Gennai_seishi01s2040.jpg|thumb|200x200px|[[Hiraga Gennai|Hiraga Gennai]]'s birth shrine in [[Hiroshima Prefecture|Hiroshima Prefecture]], [[Fukuyama, Hiroshima|Fukuyama City]], Tomomachi.]]
