Byron |url=https://archive.org/details/religioninjapane0000earh |title=Religion in the Japanese experience: sources and interpretations |date=1974 |publisher=Dickenson Pub. Co. |isbn=0822101041 |edition=3rd |location=Encino, Calif. |author1-link=H. Byron Earhart |url-access=registration}}</ref> [[File:Hiraga_Gennai_seishi01s2040.jpg|thumb|200x200px|[[Hiraga Gennai]]'s sheng shrine in [[Hiroshima Prefecture]], [[:en:Fukuyama, Hiroshima|Fukuyama City]], Tomomachi.]]
== In China == When [[Han Dynasty#Western Han|Former Han]]'s Reunobu was the prime minister of [[Yan Kingdom (Han dynasty)|Yan]], he built his shrine between Yan and [[Qi (state)|Qi]] and called it the "Reun Gong Shrine". Also, when {{ill|Shiqing|zh|石慶|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiqing|qq=}} was the prime minister of Qi, the Qi people built the "Shiqing Shrine". This is the beginning of the birth shrine.<ref>[[Zhao Yi]], "The New New Ancestral Hall", Volume 32, "Ancestral Hall", "This is the beginning of the establishment of an ancestral hall.</ref>
In the [[Tang dynasty]], there were certain restrictions on the construction of monuments and shrines by officials in office. According to the [[Tang Code|Tang Rulings and Ordinances]], those who erected shrines or monuments without permission were subject to a penalty of one year of imprisonment.<ref>The [[Tang Code]], Volume 11 (The Law of Office)</ref> The Tang Dynasty called it "the difficulty of building monuments and shrines on the first day.
In the [[Ming dynasty|Ming]], peasants in [[Tengzhou]] built a shrine to commemorate {{ill|Zhao Bangqing|zh|趙邦清|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhao Bangqing|qq=}}, who was appointed to Yanjing for his clean politics. The shrine was built to commemorate him.
During the reign of the [[Tianqi Emperor]] of the Ming Dynasty, the eunuch [[Wei Zhongxian]], ruled the world by conducting his own politics, and those who flattered him or feared his authority were given a sheng shrine from him. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing (1627), Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote: "Confucius wrote the Spring and Autumn, and Chung-Hsien wrote the Essentials. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote that Confucius had written the "Spring and Autumn" and Chung-Hsien had written the "Essentials. In April of the 7th year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, [[Yuan Chonghuan]] and {{ill|Yan Mingtai|zh|閻鳴泰|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yan Mingtai|qq=}} of the [[Ministry of War (imperial China)|Ministry of War]] made an appeal to the government to praise the merits and demerits of Wei Zhongxian and to establish a sheng shrine for him in both Qiantu.<ref>"Ming Xi Zong Jizhu": "in April of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianqi, the governor of Thistle Liaoning, Yan Mingtai, and the governor of Fuxing Yuan Chong Huan, praised the merits of Wei Zhongxian, and requested the construction of a shrine in front of Ning, giving the name Mao De." The government of the Liaoning Province has been inscribed in gold and stone, and there is no room for extra duties. The minister's single-mindedness in patrolling, strict warning of the battalion generals, do not dare to greed cowardly selfishness, do not dare to give away the hidden string, change the virtual into the real, into the real, easy timid into brave, to have today. Sustainability ancient internal minister who has the right!" ("Ming Xi Zong seven years of the Imperial Court of the Department of the Experiment" Tianqi six years in October)</ref> After that, Wei Zhongxian's raw shrine was "all over the world", "the cost of each shrine was at most hundreds of thousands, at least tens of thousands", "stolen the people's fortune, invaded the public safe, and was cut down. There are countless trees. " When Huang Yun Yasushi made a statue and greeted the statue, he said, "Goto Sansho," and "led the general of Bunbu, lined up under the stairs, and worshipped as the beginning."
[[Wei Zhongxian]] has been strongly condemned in modern times for his behavior.{{by whom|date=March 2024}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |last=网易 |date=2020-12-14 |title=魏忠贤为自己生祠揭幕 |url=https://www.163.com/dy/article/FTRIN9CF0521Q1HR.html |access-date=2022-12-18 |website=www.163.com}}</ref>
[[Gu Yanwu]] lamented, "In this generation, there is no official who does not build a sheng shrine. And within a few years of leaving office, he would destroy the statues and change their masters.<ref>《Nichi Chi Roku》 vol. 23 (Sheng Shi), p. 644.</ref>
Recently{{When|date=December 2020}} a person{{whom|date=March 2024}} in China created their own Sheng shrine and stirred online controversy.<ref name=":0" />
== In Japan ==
The [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] (1603-1868) saw a rise in the practice of ''seishi'', or the establishment of one's own spiritual memorial site, where one's soul would be enshrined and worshipped after death. One notable example of this practice is that of [[Matsudaira Sadanobu]], who established his own ''seishi'' at Okuzawa Hachiman-gu in 1797.<ref name=":3">{{Cite web |title=「生祀は」の用例・例文集 - 用例.jp |url=http://yourei.jp/%E7%94%9F%E7%A5%80%E3%81%AF |access-date=2023-01-12 |website=yourei.jp}}</ref>
The rise in ''seishi'' is thought to have been influenced by Chinese philosophy, specifically [[:en:Edo neo-Confucianism|Edo neo-Confucianism]], which was introduced to Japan during the Edo period. One notable figure of this period, [[Yamazaki Ansai]], is said to have devised rituals for ''seishi'' based on Confucian ceremonies, and established his own ''seishi'' in 1671 at his residence in [[Kyoto]].<ref name=":3" />
After this, many scholars and practitioners of Shinto and the Hirata school of ''[[kokugaku]]'' ("national study") also established their own ''seishi'', each with their own unique rituals. These ''seishi'' were believed to be a means of obtaining longevity in life or becoming a god after death. The practice is said to have originated from the mythological story of the sun goddess [[Amaterasu]], who enshrined her own divine spirit at [[Mount Miwa]].<ref name=":3" />
The study of ''seishi'' is particularly associated with Genchi Kato, who has identified 670 known ''seishi'', with the most recent being established in 1931 at the Nishi-Chogo Shrine in Higashikokura, [[Hokkaido]]. The ''seishi'' of [[:en:Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]] was established in 1876 in [[Ishinomaki]], [[Miyagi Prefecture]], and again in 1893 at the Yahiko Shrine in [[Tatsuno, Nagano|Tatsuno]], [[Nagano Prefecture]]. The ''seishi'' of Emperor Meiji, [[Empress Shōken]], [[:en:Emperor Taishō|Emperor Taisho]], and [[Empress Teimei]] were also established in various locations, including Hokkaido, [[Hiroshima]], and [[Himeji]].<ref name=":3" />
== See also == * [[Tamaya]] * {{ill|Okutsuki|ja|奥都城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okutsuki|qq=}} * [[Hitogami]]
== References == <references />

{{Short description|Worshipping living people in East Asian culture}}
'''Worship of the living''' ([[Japanese language|Japanese]]: 生祀, ''seishi''; [[:en:Chinese language|Chinese]]: 生祠, ''shēngsì'') is the [[worship]] of living people practiced in the [[:en:East Asian cultural sphere|East Asian cultural sphere]]. In China, it is practiced at ''sheng'' shrines (Chinese: 生祠, shēng[[:en:Ci Shrine|cí]]).<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |date=2022-12-18 |title=为关心民生疾苦而设的生祠，在明代起着什么样的作用？_政治_祭祀_陈天祥 |website=[[Sohu]] |url=https://www.sohu.com/a/531809387_120005162 |access-date=2023-01-12 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221218062727/https://www.sohu.com/a/531809387_120005162 |archive-date=2022-12-18 }}</ref> There are two types of enshrinement: the enshrinement of the spirit of a living person who has made a significant contribution, and the enshrinement of one's own spirit.
Due to the usage by [[Wei Zhongxian]] the practice became seen as a sign of corruption and declined in China.<ref name=":2">{{Cite web |title=生祠并非魏忠贤的专利-凤凰周刊 |url=http://www.ifengweekly.com/detil.php?id=16813 |access-date=2022-12-22 |website=www.ifengweekly.com|trans-title=Shengci is not Wei Zhongxian's invention}}</ref> In Japan, the practice was most prominent with the worship of the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] during the period of the imperial [[State Shinto]] from the late 19th century until 1945.<ref name="Earhart">{{cite book |last1=Earhart |first1=H. Byron |url=https://archive.org/details/religioninjapane0000earh |title=Religion in the Japanese experience: sources and interpretations |date=1974 |publisher=Dickenson Pub. Co. |isbn=0822101041 |edition=3rd |location=Encino, Calif. |author1-link=H. Byron Earhart |url-access=registration}}</ref> [[File:Hiraga_Gennai_seishi01s2040.jpg|thumb|200x200px|[[Hiraga Gennai]]'s sheng shrine in [[Hiroshima Prefecture]], [[:en:Fukuyama, Hiroshima|Fukuyama City]], Tomomachi.]]

In April of the 7th year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, [[Yuan Chonghuan]] and {{ill|Yan Mingtai|zh|閻鳴泰|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yan Mingtai|qq=}} of the [[Ministry of War (imperial China)|Ministry of War]] made an appeal to the government to praise the merits and demerits of Wei Zhongxian and to establish a sheng shrine for him in both Qiantu.<ref>"Ming Xi Zong Jizhu": "in April of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianqi, the governor of Thistle Liaoning, Yan Mingtai, and the governor of Fuxing Yuan Chong Huan, praised the merits of Wei Zhongxian, and requested the construction of a shrine in front of Ning, giving the name Mao De." The government of the Liaoning Province has been inscribed in gold and stone, and there is no room for extra duties. The minister's single-mindedness in patrolling, strict warning of the battalion generals, do not dare to greed cowardly selfishness, do not dare to give away the hidden string, change the virtual into the real, into the real, easy timid into brave, to have today. ("Ming Xi Zong seven years of the Imperial Court of the Department of the Experiment" Tianqi six years in October)</ref> After that, Wei Zhongxian's raw shrine was "all over the world", "the cost of each shrine was at most hundreds of thousands, at least tens of thousands", "stolen the people's fortune, invaded the public safe, and was cut down. {{Short description|Worshipping living people in East Asian culture}}
'''Worship of the living''' ([[Japanese language|Japanese]]: 生祀, ''seishi''; [[:en:Chinese language|Chinese]]: 生祠, ''shēngsì'') is the [[worship]] of living people practiced in the [[:en:East Asian cultural sphere|East Asian cultural sphere]]. In China, it is practiced at ''sheng'' shrines (Chinese: 生祠, shēng[[:en:Ci Shrine|cí]]).<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |date=2022-12-18 |title=为关心民生疾苦而设的生祠，在明代起着什么样的作用？_政治_祭祀_陈天祥 |website=[[Sohu]] |url=https://www.sohu.com/a/531809387_120005162 |access-date=2023-01-12 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221218062727/https://www.sohu.com/a/531809387_120005162 |archive-date=2022-12-18 }}</ref> There are two types of enshrinement: the enshrinement of the spirit of a living person who has made a significant contribution, and the enshrinement of one's own spirit.
Due to the usage by [[Wei Zhongxian]] the practice became seen as a sign of corruption and declined in China.<ref name=":2">{{Cite web |title=生祠并非魏忠贤的专利-凤凰周刊 |url=http://www.ifengweekly.com/detil.php?id=16813 |access-date=2022-12-22 |website=www.ifengweekly.com|trans-title=Shengci is not Wei Zhongxian's invention}}</ref> In Japan, the practice was most prominent with the worship of the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] during the period of the imperial [[State Shinto]] from the late 19th century until 1945.<ref name="Earhart">{{cite book |last1=Earhart |first1=H. Byron |url=https://archive.org/details/religioninjapane0000earh |title=Religion in the Japanese experience: sources and interpretations |date=1974 |publisher=Dickenson Pub. Co. |isbn=0822101041 |edition=3rd |location=Encino, Calif. |author1-link=H. Byron Earhart |url-access=registration}}</ref> [[File:Hiraga_Gennai_seishi01s2040.jpg|thumb|200x200px|[[Hiraga Gennai]]'s sheng shrine in [[Hiroshima Prefecture]], [[:en:Fukuyama, Hiroshima|Fukuyama City]], Tomomachi.]]
== In China == When [[Han Dynasty#Western Han|Former Han]]'s Reunobu was the prime minister of [[Yan Kingdom (Han dynasty)|Yan]], he built his shrine between Yan and [[Qi (state)|Qi]] and called it the "Reun Gong Shrine". Also, when {{ill|Shiqing|zh|石慶|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiqing|qq=}} was the prime minister of Qi, the Qi people built the "Shiqing Shrine". This is the beginning of the birth shrine.<ref>[[Zhao Yi]], "The New New Ancestral Hall", Volume 32, "Ancestral Hall", "This is the beginning of the establishment of an ancestral hall.</ref>
In the [[Tang dynasty]], there were certain restrictions on the construction of monuments and shrines by officials in office. According to the [[Tang Code|Tang Rulings and Ordinances]], those who erected shrines or monuments without permission were subject to a penalty of one year of imprisonment.<ref>The [[Tang Code]], Volume 11 (The Law of Office)</ref> The Tang Dynasty called it "the difficulty of building monuments and shrines on the first day.
In the [[Ming dynasty|Ming]], peasants in [[Tengzhou]] built a shrine to commemorate {{ill|Zhao Bangqing|zh|趙邦清|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhao Bangqing|qq=}}, who was appointed to Yanjing for his clean politics. The shrine was built to commemorate him.
During the reign of the [[Tianqi Emperor]] of the Ming Dynasty, the eunuch [[Wei Zhongxian]], ruled the world by conducting his own politics, and those who flattered him or feared his authority were given a sheng shrine from him. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing (1627), Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote: "Confucius wrote the Spring and Autumn, and Chung-Hsien wrote the Essentials. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote that Confucius had written the "Spring and Autumn" and Chung-Hsien had written the "Essentials. In April of the 7th year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, [[Yuan Chonghuan]] and {{ill|Yan Mingtai|zh|閻鳴泰|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yan Mingtai|qq=}} of the [[Ministry of War (imperial China)|Ministry of War]] made an appeal to the government to praise the merits and demerits of Wei Zhongxian and to establish a sheng shrine for him in both Qiantu.<ref>"Ming Xi Zong Jizhu": "in April of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianqi, the governor of Thistle Liaoning, Yan Mingtai, and the governor of Fuxing Yuan Chong Huan, praised the merits of Wei Zhongxian, and requested the construction of a shrine in front of Ning, giving the name Mao De." The government of the Liaoning Province has been inscribed in gold and stone, and there is no room for extra duties. The minister's single-mindedness in patrolling, strict warning of the battalion generals, do not dare to greed cowardly selfishness, do not dare to give away the hidden string, change the virtual into the real, into the real, easy timid into brave, to have today. Sustainability ancient internal minister who has the right!" ("Ming Xi Zong seven years of the Imperial Court of the Department of the Experiment" Tianqi six years in October)</ref> After that, Wei Zhongxian's raw shrine was "all over the world", "the cost of each shrine was at most hundreds of thousands, at least tens of thousands", "stolen the people's fortune, invaded the public safe, and was cut down. There are countless trees. " When Huang Yun Yasushi made a statue and greeted the statue, he said, "Goto Sansho," and "led the general of Bunbu, lined up under the stairs, and worshipped as the beginning."
[[Wei Zhongxian]] has been strongly condemned in modern times for his behavior.{{by whom|date=March 2024}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |last=网易 |date=2020-12-14 |title=魏忠贤为自己生祠揭幕 |url=https://www.163.com/dy/article/FTRIN9CF0521Q1HR.html |access-date=2022-12-18 |website=www.163.com}}</ref>
[[Gu Yanwu]] lamented, "In this generation, there is no official who does not build a sheng shrine. And within a few years of leaving office, he would destroy the statues and change their masters.<ref>《Nichi Chi Roku》 vol. 23 (Sheng Shi), p. 644.</ref>
Recently{{When|date=December 2020}} a person{{whom|date=March 2024}} in China created their own Sheng shrine and stirred online controversy.<ref name=":0" />
== In Japan ==
The [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] (1603-1868) saw a rise in the practice of ''seishi'', or the establishment of one's own spiritual memorial site, where one's soul would be enshrined and worshipped after death. One notable example of this practice is that of [[Matsudaira Sadanobu]], who established his own ''seishi'' at Okuzawa Hachiman-gu in 1797.<ref name=":3">{{Cite web |title=「生祀は」の用例・例文集 - 用例.jp |url=http://yourei.jp/%E7%94%9F%E7%A5%80%E3%81%AF |access-date=2023-01-12 |website=yourei.jp}}</ref>
The rise in ''seishi'' is thought to have been influenced by Chinese philosophy, specifically [[:en:Edo neo-Confucianism|Edo neo-Confucianism]], which was introduced to Japan during the Edo period. One notable figure of this period, [[Yamazaki Ansai]], is said to have devised rituals for ''seishi'' based on Confucian ceremonies, and established his own ''seishi'' in 1671 at his residence in [[Kyoto]].<ref name=":3" />
After this, many scholars and practitioners of Shinto and the Hirata school of ''[[kokugaku]]'' ("national study") also established their own ''seishi'', each with their own unique rituals. These ''seishi'' were believed to be a means of obtaining longevity in life or becoming a god after death. The practice is said to have originated from the mythological story of the sun goddess [[Amaterasu]], who enshrined her own divine spirit at [[Mount Miwa]].<ref name=":3" />
The study of ''seishi'' is particularly associated with Genchi Kato, who has identified 670 known ''seishi'', with the most recent being established in 1931 at the Nishi-Chogo Shrine in Higashikokura, [[Hokkaido]]. The ''seishi'' of [[:en:Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]] was established in 1876 in [[Ishinomaki]], [[Miyagi Prefecture]], and again in 1893 at the Yahiko Shrine in [[Tatsuno, Nagano|Tatsuno]], [[Nagano Prefecture]]. The ''seishi'' of Emperor Meiji, [[:en:Empress Shōken|Empress Shōken]], [[:en:Emperor Taishō|Emperor Taisho]], and [[:en:Empress Teimei|Empress Teimei]] were also established in various locations, including Hokkaido, [[Hiroshima]], and [[:en:Himeji|Himeji]].<ref name=":3" />
== See also == * [[Tamaya]] * {{ill|Okutsuki|ja|奥都城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okutsuki|qq=}} * [[Hitogami]]
== References == <references />
*

The study of ''seishi'' is particularly associated with Genchi Kato, who has identified 670 known ''seishi'', with the most recent being established in 1931 at the Nishi-Chogo Shrine in Higashikokura, [[Hokkaido]]. The ''seishi'' of [[:en:Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]] was established in 1876 in [[Ishinomaki]], [[Miyagi Prefecture]], and again in 1893 at the Yahiko Shrine in [[Tatsuno, Nagano|Tatsuno]], [[Nagano Prefecture]]. The ''seishi'' of Emperor Meiji, [[:en:Empress Shōken|Empress Shōken]], [[:en:Emperor Taishō|Emperor Taisho]], and [[:en:Empress Teimei|Empress Teimei]] were also established in various locations, including Hokkaido, [[:en:Hiroshima|Hiroshima]], and [[:en:Himeji|Himeji]].<ref name=":3" />

[[Category:Pages without translation templates]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ja:生祀]] [[simple:Worship of the living]] [[zh:生祠]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]]

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
{{Short description|Worshipping living people in East Asian culture}}
'''Worship of the living''' ([[Japanese language|Japanese]]: 生祀, ''seishi''; [[:en:Chinese language|Chinese]]: 生祠, ''shēngsì'') is the [[worship]] of living people practiced in the [[:en:East Asian cultural sphere|East Asian cultural sphere]]. In China, it is practiced at ''sheng'' shrines (Chinese: 生祠, shēng[[:en:Ci Shrine|cí]]).<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |date=2022-12-18 |title=为关心民生疾苦而设的生祠，在明代起着什么样的作用？_政治_祭祀_陈天祥 |website=[[Sohu]] |url=https://www.sohu.com/a/531809387_120005162 |access-date=2023-01-12 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221218062727/https://www.sohu.com/a/531809387_120005162 |archive-date=2022-12-18 }}</ref> There are two types of enshrinement: the enshrinement of the spirit of a living person who has made a significant contribution, and the enshrinement of one's own spirit.
Due to the usage by [[Wei Zhongxian]] the practice became seen as a sign of corruption and declined in China.<ref name=":2">{{Cite web |title=生祠并非魏忠贤的专利-凤凰周刊 |url=http://www.ifengweekly.com/detil.php?id=16813 |access-date=2022-12-22 |website=www.ifengweekly.com|trans-title=Shengci is not Wei Zhongxian's invention}}</ref> In Japan, the practice was most prominent with the worship of the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] during the period of the imperial [[State Shinto]] from the late 19th century until 1945.<ref name="Earhart">{{cite book |last1=Earhart |first1=H. Byron |url=https://archive.org/details/religioninjapane0000earh |title=Religion in the Japanese experience: sources and interpretations |date=1974 |publisher=Dickenson Pub. Co. |isbn=0822101041 |edition=3rd |location=Encino, Calif. |author1-link=H. Byron Earhart |url-access=registration}}</ref> [[File:Hiraga_Gennai_seishi01s2040.jpg|thumb|200x200px|[[:en:Hiraga Gennai|Hiraga Gennai]]'s sheng shrine in [[:en:Hiroshima Prefecture|Hiroshima Prefecture]], [[:en:Fukuyama, Hiroshima|Fukuyama City]], Tomomachi.]]
== In China == When [[Han Dynasty#Western Han|Former Han]]'s Reunobu was the prime minister of [[Yan Kingdom (Han dynasty)|Yan]], he built his shrine between Yan and [[Qi (state)|Qi]] and called it the "Reun Gong Shrine". Also, when {{ill|Shiqing|zh|石慶|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiqing|qq=}} was the prime minister of Qi, the Qi people built the "Shiqing Shrine". This is the beginning of the birth shrine.<ref>[[Zhao Yi]], "The New New Ancestral Hall", Volume 32, "Ancestral Hall", "This is the beginning of the establishment of an ancestral hall.</ref>
In the [[Tang dynasty]], there were certain restrictions on the construction of monuments and shrines by officials in office. According to the [[Tang Code|Tang Rulings and Ordinances]], those who erected shrines or monuments without permission were subject to a penalty of one year of imprisonment.<ref>The [[Tang Code]], Volume 11 (The Law of Office)</ref> The Tang Dynasty called it "the difficulty of building monuments and shrines on the first day.
In the [[Ming dynasty|Ming]], peasants in [[Tengzhou]] built a shrine to commemorate {{ill|Zhao Bangqing|zh|趙邦清|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhao Bangqing|qq=}}, who was appointed to Yanjing for his clean politics. The shrine was built to commemorate him.
During the reign of the [[Tianqi Emperor]] of the Ming Dynasty, the eunuch [[Wei Zhongxian]], ruled the world by conducting his own politics, and those who flattered him or feared his authority were given a sheng shrine from him. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing (1627), Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote: "Confucius wrote the Spring and Autumn, and Chung-Hsien wrote the Essentials. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote that Confucius had written the "Spring and Autumn" and Chung-Hsien had written the "Essentials. In April of the 7th year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, [[Yuan Chonghuan]] and {{ill|Yan Mingtai|zh|閻鳴泰|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yan Mingtai|qq=}} of the [[Ministry of War (imperial China)|Ministry of War]] made an appeal to the government to praise the merits and demerits of Wei Zhongxian and to establish a sheng shrine for him in both Qiantu.<ref>"Ming Xi Zong Jizhu": "in April of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianqi, the governor of Thistle Liaoning, Yan Mingtai, and the governor of Fuxing Yuan Chong Huan, praised the merits of Wei Zhongxian, and requested the construction of a shrine in front of Ning, giving the name Mao De." The government of the Liaoning Province has been inscribed in gold and stone, and there is no room for extra duties. The minister's single-mindedness in patrolling, strict warning of the battalion generals, do not dare to greed cowardly selfishness, do not dare to give away the hidden string, change the virtual into the real, into the real, easy timid into brave, to have today. Sustainability ancient internal minister who has the right!" ("Ming Xi Zong seven years of the Imperial Court of the Department of the Experiment" Tianqi six years in October)</ref> After that, Wei Zhongxian's raw shrine was "all over the world", "the cost of each shrine was at most hundreds of thousands, at least tens of thousands", "stolen the people's fortune, invaded the public safe, and was cut down. There are countless trees. " When Huang Yun Yasushi made a statue and greeted the statue, he said, "Goto Sansho," and "led the general of Bunbu, lined up under the stairs, and worshipped as the beginning."
[[Wei Zhongxian]] has been strongly condemned in modern times for his behavior.{{by whom|date=March 2024}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |last=网易 |date=2020-12-14 |title=魏忠贤为自己生祠揭幕 |url=https://www.163.com/dy/article/FTRIN9CF0521Q1HR.html |access-date=2022-12-18 |website=www.163.com}}</ref>
[[Gu Yanwu]] lamented, "In this generation, there is no official who does not build a sheng shrine. And within a few years of leaving office, he would destroy the statues and change their masters.<ref>《Nichi Chi Roku》 vol. 23 (Sheng Shi), p. 644.</ref>
Recently{{When|date=December 2020}} a person{{whom|date=March 2024}} in China created their own Sheng shrine and stirred online controversy.<ref name=":0" />
== In Japan ==
The [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] (1603-1868) saw a rise in the practice of ''seishi'', or the establishment of one's own spiritual memorial site, where one's soul would be enshrined and worshipped after death. One notable example of this practice is that of [[Matsudaira Sadanobu]], who established his own ''seishi'' at Okuzawa Hachiman-gu in 1797.<ref name=":3">{{Cite web |title=「生祀は」の用例・例文集 - 用例.jp |url=http://yourei.jp/%E7%94%9F%E7%A5%80%E3%81%AF |access-date=2023-01-12 |website=yourei.jp}}</ref>
The rise in ''seishi'' is thought to have been influenced by Chinese philosophy, specifically [[:en:Edo neo-Confucianism|Edo neo-Confucianism]], which was introduced to Japan during the Edo period. One notable figure of this period, [[Yamazaki Ansai]], is said to have devised rituals for ''seishi'' based on Confucian ceremonies, and established his own ''seishi'' in 1671 at his residence in [[Kyoto]].<ref name=":3" />
After this, many scholars and practitioners of Shinto and the Hirata school of ''[[kokugaku]]'' ("national study") also established their own ''seishi'', each with their own unique rituals. These ''seishi'' were believed to be a means of obtaining longevity in life or becoming a god after death. The practice is said to have originated from the mythological story of the sun goddess [[Amaterasu]], who enshrined her own divine spirit at [[Mount Miwa]].<ref name=":3" />
The study of ''seishi'' is particularly associated with Genchi Kato, who has identified 670 known ''seishi'', with the most recent being established in 1931 at the Nishi-Chogo Shrine in Higashikokura, [[Hokkaido]]. The ''seishi'' of [[:en:Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]] was established in 1876 in [[Ishinomaki]], [[Miyagi Prefecture]], and again in 1893 at the Yahiko Shrine in [[Tatsuno, Nagano|Tatsuno]], [[Nagano Prefecture]]. The ''seishi'' of Emperor Meiji, [[:en:Empress Shōken|Empress Shōken]], [[:en:Emperor Taishō|Emperor Taisho]], and [[:en:Empress Teimei|Empress Teimei]] were also established in various locations, including Hokkaido, [[:en:Hiroshima|Hiroshima]], and [[:en:Himeji|Himeji]].<ref name=":3" />
== See also == * [[Tamaya]] * {{ill|Okutsuki|ja|奥都城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okutsuki|qq=}} * [[Hitogami]]
== References == <references />

There are countless trees. " When Huang Yun Yasushi made a statue and greeted the statue, he said, "Goto Sansho," and "led the general of Bunbu, lined up under the stairs, and worshipped as the beginning."
[[Wei Zhongxian]] has been strongly condemned in modern times for his behavior.{{by whom|date=March 2024}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |last=网易 |date=2020-12-14 |title=魏忠贤为自己生祠揭幕 |url=https://www.163.com/dy/article/FTRIN9CF0521Q1HR.html |access-date=2022-12-18 |website=www.163.com}}</ref>
[[Gu Yanwu]] lamented, "In this generation, there is no official who does not build a sheng shrine. And within a few years of leaving office, he would destroy the statues and change their masters.<ref>《Nichi Chi Roku》 vol. 23 (Sheng Shi), p. 644.</ref>
Recently{{When|date=December 2020}} a person{{whom|date=March 2024}} in China created their own Sheng shrine and stirred online controversy.<ref name=":0" />
== In Japan ==
The [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] (1603-1868) saw a rise in the practice of ''seishi'', or the establishment of one's own spiritual memorial site, where one's soul would be enshrined and worshipped after death. One notable example of this practice is that of [[Matsudaira Sadanobu]], who established his own ''seishi'' at Okuzawa Hachiman-gu in 1797.<ref name=":3">{{Cite web |title=「生祀は」の用例・例文集 - 用例.jp |url=http://yourei.jp/%E7%94%9F%E7%A5%80%E3%81%AF |access-date=2023-01-12 |website=yourei.jp}}</ref>
The rise in ''seishi'' is thought to have been influenced by Chinese philosophy, specifically [[:en:Edo neo-Confucianism|Edo neo-Confucianism]], which was introduced to Japan during the Edo period. One notable figure of this period, [[Yamazaki Ansai]], is said to have devised rituals for ''seishi'' based on Confucian ceremonies, and established his own ''seishi'' in 1671 at his residence in [[Kyoto]].<ref name=":3" />
After this, many scholars and practitioners of Shinto and the Hirata school of ''[[kokugaku]]'' ("national study") also established their own ''seishi'', each with their own unique rituals. These ''seishi'' were believed to be a means of obtaining longevity in life or becoming a god after death. The practice is said to have originated from the mythological story of the sun goddess [[Amaterasu]], who enshrined her own divine spirit at [[Mount Miwa]].<ref name=":3" />
The study of ''seishi'' is particularly associated with Genchi Kato, who has identified 670 known ''seishi'', with the most recent being established in 1931 at the Nishi-Chogo Shrine in Higashikokura, [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]]. The ''seishi'' of [[:en:Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]] was established in 1876 in [[Ishinomaki]], [[Miyagi Prefecture]], and again in 1893 at the Yahiko Shrine in [[Tatsuno, Nagano|Tatsuno]], [[Nagano Prefecture]]. The ''seishi'' of Emperor Meiji, [[:en:Empress Shōken|Empress Shōken]], [[:en:Emperor Taishō|Emperor Taisho]], and [[:en:Empress Teimei|Empress Teimei]] were also established in various locations, including Hokkaido, [[:en:Hiroshima|Hiroshima]], and [[:en:Himeji|Himeji]].<ref name=":3" />

{{Short description|Worshipping living people in East Asian culture}}
'''Worship of the living''' ([[Japanese language|Japanese]]: 生祀, ''seishi''; [[:en:Chinese language|Chinese]]: 生祠, ''shēngsì'') is the [[worship]] of living people practiced in the [[:en:East Asian cultural sphere|East Asian cultural sphere]]. In China, it is practiced at ''sheng'' shrines (Chinese: 生祠, shēng[[:en:Ci Shrine|cí]]).<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |date=2022-12-18 |title=为关心民生疾苦而设的生祠，在明代起着什么样的作用？_政治_祭祀_陈天祥 |website=[[Sohu]] |url=https://www.sohu.com/a/531809387_120005162 |access-date=2023-01-12 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221218062727/https://www.sohu.com/a/531809387_120005162 |archive-date=2022-12-18 }}</ref> There are two types of enshrinement: the enshrinement of the spirit of a living person who has made a significant contribution, and the enshrinement of one's own spirit.
Due to the usage by [[Wei Zhongxian]] the practice became seen as a sign of corruption and declined in China.<ref name=":2">{{Cite web |title=生祠并非魏忠贤的专利-凤凰周刊 |url=http://www.ifengweekly.com/detil.php?id=16813 |access-date=2022-12-22 |website=www.ifengweekly.com|trans-title=Shengci is not Wei Zhongxian's invention}}</ref> In Japan, the practice was most prominent with the worship of the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] during the period of the imperial [[State Shinto]] from the late 19th century until 1945.<ref name="Earhart">{{cite book |last1=Earhart |first1=H. Byron |url=https://archive.org/details/religioninjapane0000earh |title=Religion in the Japanese experience: sources and interpretations |date=1974 |publisher=Dickenson Pub. Co. |isbn=0822101041 |edition=3rd |location=Encino, Calif. |author1-link=H. Byron Earhart |url-access=registration}}</ref> [[File:Hiraga_Gennai_seishi01s2040.jpg|thumb|200x200px|[[:en:Hiraga Gennai|Hiraga Gennai]]'s sheng shrine in [[:en:Hiroshima Prefecture|Hiroshima Prefecture]], [[:en:Fukuyama, Hiroshima|Fukuyama City]], Tomomachi.]]
== In China == When [[Han Dynasty#Western Han|Former Han]]'s Reunobu was the prime minister of [[Yan Kingdom (Han dynasty)|Yan]], he built his shrine between Yan and [[Qi (state)|Qi]] and called it the "Reun Gong Shrine". Also, when {{ill|Shiqing|zh|石慶|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiqing|qq=}} was the prime minister of Qi, the Qi people built the "Shiqing Shrine". This is the beginning of the birth shrine.<ref>[[Zhao Yi]], "The New New Ancestral Hall", Volume 32, "Ancestral Hall", "This is the beginning of the establishment of an ancestral hall.</ref>
In the [[Tang dynasty]], there were certain restrictions on the construction of monuments and shrines by officials in office. According to the [[Tang Code|Tang Rulings and Ordinances]], those who erected shrines or monuments without permission were subject to a penalty of one year of imprisonment.<ref>The [[Tang Code]], Volume 11 (The Law of Office)</ref> The Tang Dynasty called it "the difficulty of building monuments and shrines on the first day.
In the [[Ming dynasty|Ming]], peasants in [[Tengzhou]] built a shrine to commemorate {{ill|Zhao Bangqing|zh|趙邦清|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhao Bangqing|qq=}}, who was appointed to Yanjing for his clean politics. The shrine was built to commemorate him.
During the reign of the [[Tianqi Emperor]] of the Ming Dynasty, the eunuch [[Wei Zhongxian]], ruled the world by conducting his own politics, and those who flattered him or feared his authority were given a sheng shrine from him. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing (1627), Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote: "Confucius wrote the Spring and Autumn, and Chung-Hsien wrote the Essentials. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote that Confucius had written the "Spring and Autumn" and Chung-Hsien had written the "Essentials. In April of the 7th year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, [[Yuan Chonghuan]] and {{ill|Yan Mingtai|zh|閻鳴泰|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yan Mingtai|qq=}} of the [[Ministry of War (imperial China)|Ministry of War]] made an appeal to the government to praise the merits and demerits of Wei Zhongxian and to establish a sheng shrine for him in both Qiantu.<ref>"Ming Xi Zong Jizhu": "in April of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianqi, the governor of Thistle Liaoning, Yan Mingtai, and the governor of Fuxing Yuan Chong Huan, praised the merits of Wei Zhongxian, and requested the construction of a shrine in front of Ning, giving the name Mao De." The government of the Liaoning Province has been inscribed in gold and stone, and there is no room for extra duties. The minister's single-mindedness in patrolling, strict warning of the battalion generals, do not dare to greed cowardly selfishness, do not dare to give away the hidden string, change the virtual into the real, into the real, easy timid into brave, to have today. Sustainability ancient internal minister who has the right!" ("Ming Xi Zong seven years of the Imperial Court of the Department of the Experiment" Tianqi six years in October)</ref> After that, Wei Zhongxian's raw shrine was "all over the world", "the cost of each shrine was at most hundreds of thousands, at least tens of thousands", "stolen the people's fortune, invaded the public safe, and was cut down. There are countless trees. " When Huang Yun Yasushi made a statue and greeted the statue, he said, "Goto Sansho," and "led the general of Bunbu, lined up under the stairs, and worshipped as the beginning."
[[Wei Zhongxian]] has been strongly condemned in modern times for his behavior.{{by whom|date=March 2024}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |last=网易 |date=2020-12-14 |title=魏忠贤为自己生祠揭幕 |url=https://www.163.com/dy/article/FTRIN9CF0521Q1HR.html |access-date=2022-12-18 |website=www.163.com}}</ref>
[[Gu Yanwu]] lamented, "In this generation, there is no official who does not build a sheng shrine. And within a few years of leaving office, he would destroy the statues and change their masters.<ref>《Nichi Chi Roku》 vol. 23 (Sheng Shi), p. 644.</ref>
Recently{{When|date=December 2020}} a person{{whom|date=March 2024}} in China created their own Sheng shrine and stirred online controversy.<ref name=":0" />
== In Japan ==
The [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] (1603-1868) saw a rise in the practice of ''seishi'', or the establishment of one's own spiritual memorial site, where one's soul would be enshrined and worshipped after death. One notable example of this practice is that of [[Matsudaira Sadanobu]], who established his own ''seishi'' at Okuzawa Hachiman-gu in 1797.<ref name=":3">{{Cite web |title=「生祀は」の用例・例文集 - 用例.jp |url=http://yourei.jp/%E7%94%9F%E7%A5%80%E3%81%AF |access-date=2023-01-12 |website=yourei.jp}}</ref>
The rise in ''seishi'' is thought to have been influenced by Chinese philosophy, specifically [[:en:Edo neo-Confucianism|Edo neo-Confucianism]], which was introduced to Japan during the Edo period. One notable figure of this period, [[Yamazaki Ansai]], is said to have devised rituals for ''seishi'' based on Confucian ceremonies, and established his own ''seishi'' in 1671 at his residence in [[Kyoto]].<ref name=":3" />
After this, many scholars and practitioners of Shinto and the Hirata school of ''[[kokugaku]]'' ("national study") also established their own ''seishi'', each with their own unique rituals. These ''seishi'' were believed to be a means of obtaining longevity in life or becoming a god after death. The practice is said to have originated from the mythological story of the sun goddess [[Amaterasu]], who enshrined her own divine spirit at [[Mount Miwa]].<ref name=":3" />
The study of ''seishi'' is particularly associated with Genchi Kato, who has identified 670 known ''seishi'', with the most recent being established in 1931 at the Nishi-Chogo Shrine in Higashikokura, [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]]. The ''seishi'' of [[:en:Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]] was established in 1876 in [[:en:Ishinomaki|Ishinomaki]], [[Miyagi Prefecture]], and again in 1893 at the Yahiko Shrine in [[Tatsuno, Nagano|Tatsuno]], [[Nagano Prefecture]]. The ''seishi'' of Emperor Meiji, [[:en:Empress Shōken|Empress Shōken]], [[:en:Emperor Taishō|Emperor Taisho]], and [[:en:Empress Teimei|Empress Teimei]] were also established in various locations, including Hokkaido, [[:en:Hiroshima|Hiroshima]], and [[:en:Himeji|Himeji]].<ref name=":3" />
== See also == * [[Tamaya]] * {{ill|Okutsuki|ja|奥都城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okutsuki|qq=}} * [[:en:Hitogami|Hitogami]]

In the [[Tang dynasty]], there were certain restrictions on the construction of monuments and shrines by officials in office. According to the [[Tang Code|Tang Rulings and Ordinances]], those who erected shrines or monuments without permission were subject to a penalty of one year of imprisonment.<ref>The [[Tang Code]], Volume 11 (The Law of Office)</ref> The Tang Dynasty called it "the difficulty of building monuments and shrines on the first day.
In the [[Ming dynasty|Ming]], peasants in [[Tengzhou]] built a shrine to commemorate {{ill|Zhao Bangqing|zh|趙邦清|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhao Bangqing|qq=}}, who was appointed to Yanjing for his clean politics. The shrine was built to commemorate him.
During the reign of the [[Tianqi Emperor]] of the Ming Dynasty, the eunuch [[Wei Zhongxian]], ruled the world by conducting his own politics, and those who flattered him or feared his authority were given a sheng shrine from him. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing (1627), Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote: "Confucius wrote the Spring and Autumn, and Chung-Hsien wrote the Essentials. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote that Confucius had written the "Spring and Autumn" and Chung-Hsien had written the "Essentials. In April of the 7th year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, [[Yuan Chonghuan]] and {{ill|Yan Mingtai|zh|閻鳴泰|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yan Mingtai|qq=}} of the [[Ministry of War (imperial China)|Ministry of War]] made an appeal to the government to praise the merits and demerits of Wei Zhongxian and to establish a sheng shrine for him in both Qiantu.<ref>"Ming Xi Zong Jizhu": "in April of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianqi, the governor of Thistle Liaoning, Yan Mingtai, and the governor of Fuxing Yuan Chong Huan, praised the merits of Wei Zhongxian, and requested the construction of a shrine in front of Ning, giving the name Mao De." The government of the Liaoning Province has been inscribed in gold and stone, and there is no room for extra duties. The minister's single-mindedness in patrolling, strict warning of the battalion generals, do not dare to greed cowardly selfishness, do not dare to give away the hidden string, change the virtual into the real, into the real, easy timid into brave, to have today. Sustainability ancient internal minister who has the right!" ("Ming Xi Zong seven years of the Imperial Court of the Department of the Experiment" Tianqi six years in October)</ref> After that, Wei Zhongxian's raw shrine was "all over the world", "the cost of each shrine was at most hundreds of thousands, at least tens of thousands", "stolen the people's fortune, invaded the public safe, and was cut down. There are countless trees. " When Huang Yun Yasushi made a statue and greeted the statue, he said, "Goto Sansho," and "led the general of Bunbu, lined up under the stairs, and worshipped as the beginning."
[[Wei Zhongxian]] has been strongly condemned in modern times for his behavior.{{by whom|date=March 2024}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |last=网易 |date=2020-12-14 |title=魏忠贤为自己生祠揭幕 |url=https://www.163.com/dy/article/FTRIN9CF0521Q1HR.html |access-date=2022-12-18 |website=www.163.com}}</ref>
[[:en:Gu Yanwu|Gu Yanwu]] lamented, "In this generation, there is no official who does not build a sheng shrine. And within a few years of leaving office, he would destroy the statues and change their masters.<ref>《Nichi Chi Roku》 vol. 23 (Sheng Shi), p. 644.</ref>
Recently{{When|date=December 2020}} a person{{whom|date=March 2024}} in China created their own Sheng shrine and stirred online controversy.<ref name=":0" />
== In Japan ==
The [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] (1603-1868) saw a rise in the practice of ''seishi'', or the establishment of one's own spiritual memorial site, where one's soul would be enshrined and worshipped after death. One notable example of this practice is that of [[Matsudaira Sadanobu]], who established his own ''seishi'' at Okuzawa Hachiman-gu in 1797.<ref name=":3">{{Cite web |title=「生祀は」の用例・例文集 - 用例.jp |url=http://yourei.jp/%E7%94%9F%E7%A5%80%E3%81%AF |access-date=2023-01-12 |website=yourei.jp}}</ref>
The rise in ''seishi'' is thought to have been influenced by Chinese philosophy, specifically [[:en:Edo neo-Confucianism|Edo neo-Confucianism]], which was introduced to Japan during the Edo period. One notable figure of this period, [[Yamazaki Ansai]], is said to have devised rituals for ''seishi'' based on Confucian ceremonies, and established his own ''seishi'' in 1671 at his residence in [[Kyoto]].<ref name=":3" />
After this, many scholars and practitioners of Shinto and the Hirata school of ''[[kokugaku]]'' ("national study") also established their own ''seishi'', each with their own unique rituals. These ''seishi'' were believed to be a means of obtaining longevity in life or becoming a god after death. The practice is said to have originated from the mythological story of the sun goddess [[Amaterasu]], who enshrined her own divine spirit at [[Mount Miwa]].<ref name=":3" />
The study of ''seishi'' is particularly associated with Genchi Kato, who has identified 670 known ''seishi'', with the most recent being established in 1931 at the Nishi-Chogo Shrine in Higashikokura, [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]]. The ''seishi'' of [[:en:Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]] was established in 1876 in [[:en:Ishinomaki|Ishinomaki]], [[Miyagi Prefecture]], and again in 1893 at the Yahiko Shrine in [[Tatsuno, Nagano|Tatsuno]], [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]]. The ''seishi'' of Emperor Meiji, [[:en:Empress Shōken|Empress Shōken]], [[:en:Emperor Taishō|Emperor Taisho]], and [[:en:Empress Teimei|Empress Teimei]] were also established in various locations, including Hokkaido, [[:en:Hiroshima|Hiroshima]], and [[:en:Himeji|Himeji]].<ref name=":3" />

{{Short description|Worshipping living people in East Asian culture}}
'''Worship of the living''' ([[Japanese language|Japanese]]: 生祀, ''seishi''; [[:en:Chinese language|Chinese]]: 生祠, ''shēngsì'') is the [[worship]] of living people practiced in the [[:en:East Asian cultural sphere|East Asian cultural sphere]]. In China, it is practiced at ''sheng'' shrines (Chinese: 生祠, shēng[[:en:Ci Shrine|cí]]).<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |date=2022-12-18 |title=为关心民生疾苦而设的生祠，在明代起着什么样的作用？_政治_祭祀_陈天祥 |website=[[Sohu]] |url=https://www.sohu.com/a/531809387_120005162 |access-date=2023-01-12 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221218062727/https://www.sohu.com/a/531809387_120005162 |archive-date=2022-12-18 }}</ref> There are two types of enshrinement: the enshrinement of the spirit of a living person who has made a significant contribution, and the enshrinement of one's own spirit.
Due to the usage by [[Wei Zhongxian]] the practice became seen as a sign of corruption and declined in China.<ref name=":2">{{Cite web |title=生祠并非魏忠贤的专利-凤凰周刊 |url=http://www.ifengweekly.com/detil.php?id=16813 |access-date=2022-12-22 |website=www.ifengweekly.com|trans-title=Shengci is not Wei Zhongxian's invention}}</ref> In Japan, the practice was most prominent with the worship of the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] during the period of the imperial [[State Shinto]] from the late 19th century until 1945.<ref name="Earhart">{{cite book |last1=Earhart |first1=H. Byron |url=https://archive.org/details/religioninjapane0000earh |title=Religion in the Japanese experience: sources and interpretations |date=1974 |publisher=Dickenson Pub. Co. |isbn=0822101041 |edition=3rd |location=Encino, Calif. |author1-link=H. Byron Earhart |url-access=registration}}</ref> [[File:Hiraga_Gennai_seishi01s2040.jpg|thumb|200x200px|[[:en:Hiraga Gennai|Hiraga Gennai]]'s sheng shrine in [[:en:Hiroshima Prefecture|Hiroshima Prefecture]], [[:en:Fukuyama, Hiroshima|Fukuyama City]], Tomomachi.]]
== In China == When [[Han Dynasty#Western Han|Former Han]]'s Reunobu was the prime minister of [[Yan Kingdom (Han dynasty)|Yan]], he built his shrine between Yan and [[Qi (state)|Qi]] and called it the "Reun Gong Shrine". Also, when {{ill|Shiqing|zh|石慶|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiqing|qq=}} was the prime minister of Qi, the Qi people built the "Shiqing Shrine". This is the beginning of the birth shrine.<ref>[[Zhao Yi]], "The New New Ancestral Hall", Volume 32, "Ancestral Hall", "This is the beginning of the establishment of an ancestral hall.</ref>
In the [[Tang dynasty]], there were certain restrictions on the construction of monuments and shrines by officials in office. According to the [[Tang Code|Tang Rulings and Ordinances]], those who erected shrines or monuments without permission were subject to a penalty of one year of imprisonment.<ref>The [[Tang Code]], Volume 11 (The Law of Office)</ref> The Tang Dynasty called it "the difficulty of building monuments and shrines on the first day.
In the [[Ming dynasty|Ming]], peasants in [[Tengzhou]] built a shrine to commemorate {{ill|Zhao Bangqing|zh|趙邦清|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhao Bangqing|qq=}}, who was appointed to Yanjing for his clean politics. The shrine was built to commemorate him.
During the reign of the [[Tianqi Emperor]] of the Ming Dynasty, the eunuch [[Wei Zhongxian]], ruled the world by conducting his own politics, and those who flattered him or feared his authority were given a sheng shrine from him. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing (1627), Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote: "Confucius wrote the Spring and Autumn, and Chung-Hsien wrote the Essentials. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote that Confucius had written the "Spring and Autumn" and Chung-Hsien had written the "Essentials. In April of the 7th year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, [[Yuan Chonghuan]] and {{ill|Yan Mingtai|zh|閻鳴泰|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yan Mingtai|qq=}} of the [[Ministry of War (imperial China)|Ministry of War]] made an appeal to the government to praise the merits and demerits of Wei Zhongxian and to establish a sheng shrine for him in both Qiantu.<ref>"Ming Xi Zong Jizhu": "in April of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianqi, the governor of Thistle Liaoning, Yan Mingtai, and the governor of Fuxing Yuan Chong Huan, praised the merits of Wei Zhongxian, and requested the construction of a shrine in front of Ning, giving the name Mao De." The government of the Liaoning Province has been inscribed in gold and stone, and there is no room for extra duties. The minister's single-mindedness in patrolling, strict warning of the battalion generals, do not dare to greed cowardly selfishness, do not dare to give away the hidden string, change the virtual into the real, into the real, easy timid into brave, to have today. Sustainability ancient internal minister who has the right!" ("Ming Xi Zong seven years of the Imperial Court of the Department of the Experiment" Tianqi six years in October)</ref> After that, Wei Zhongxian's raw shrine was "all over the world", "the cost of each shrine was at most hundreds of thousands, at least tens of thousands", "stolen the people's fortune, invaded the public safe, and was cut down. There are countless trees. " When Huang Yun Yasushi made a statue and greeted the statue, he said, "Goto Sansho," and "led the general of Bunbu, lined up under the stairs, and worshipped as the beginning."
[[Wei Zhongxian]] has been strongly condemned in modern times for his behavior.{{by whom|date=March 2024}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |last=网易 |date=2020-12-14 |title=魏忠贤为自己生祠揭幕 |url=https://www.163.com/dy/article/FTRIN9CF0521Q1HR.html |access-date=2022-12-18 |website=www.163.com}}</ref>
[[:en:Gu Yanwu|Gu Yanwu]] lamented, "In this generation, there is no official who does not build a sheng shrine. And within a few years of leaving office, he would destroy the statues and change their masters.<ref>《Nichi Chi Roku》 vol. 23 (Sheng Shi), p. 644.</ref>
Recently{{When|date=December 2020}} a person{{whom|date=March 2024}} in China created their own Sheng shrine and stirred online controversy.<ref name=":0" />

After this, many scholars and practitioners of Shinto and the Hirata school of ''[[kokugaku]]'' ("national study") also established their own ''seishi'', each with their own unique rituals. The study of ''seishi'' is particularly associated with Genchi Kato, who has identified 670 known ''seishi'', with the most recent being established in 1931 at the Nishi-Chogo Shrine in Higashikokura, [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]]. The ''seishi'' of Emperor Meiji, [[:en:Empress Shōken|Empress Shōken]], [[:en:Emperor Taishō|Emperor Taisho]], and [[:en:Empress Teimei|Empress Teimei]] were also established in various locations, including Hokkaido, [[:en:Hiroshima|Hiroshima]], and [[:en:Himeji|Himeji]].<ref name=":3" />

{{Short description|Worshipping living people in East Asian culture}}
'''Worship of the living''' ([[:en:Japanese language|Japanese]]: 生祀, ''seishi''; [[:en:Chinese language|Chinese]]: 生祠, ''shēngsì'') is the [[worship]] of living people practiced in the [[:en:East Asian cultural sphere|East Asian cultural sphere]]. In China, it is practiced at ''sheng'' shrines (Chinese: 生祠, shēng[[:en:Ci Shrine|cí]]).<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |date=2022-12-18 |title=为关心民生疾苦而设的生祠，在明代起着什么样的作用？_政治_祭祀_陈天祥 |website=[[Sohu]] |url=https://www.sohu.com/a/531809387_120005162 |access-date=2023-01-12 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221218062727/https://www.sohu.com/a/531809387_120005162 |archive-date=2022-12-18 }}</ref> There are two types of enshrinement: the enshrinement of the spirit of a living person who has made a significant contribution, and the enshrinement of one's own spirit.
Due to the usage by [[Wei Zhongxian]] the practice became seen as a sign of corruption and declined in China.<ref name=":2">{{Cite web |title=生祠并非魏忠贤的专利-凤凰周刊 |url=http://www.ifengweekly.com/detil.php?id=16813 |access-date=2022-12-22 |website=www.ifengweekly.com|trans-title=Shengci is not Wei Zhongxian's invention}}</ref> In Japan, the practice was most prominent with the worship of the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] during the period of the imperial [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] from the late 19th century until 1945.<ref name="Earhart">{{cite book |last1=Earhart |first1=H. Byron |url=https://archive.org/details/religioninjapane0000earh |title=Religion in the Japanese experience: sources and interpretations |date=1974 |publisher=Dickenson Pub. Co. |isbn=0822101041 |edition=3rd |location=Encino, Calif. |author1-link=H. Byron Earhart |url-access=registration}}</ref> [[File:Hiraga_Gennai_seishi01s2040.jpg|thumb|200x200px|[[:en:Hiraga Gennai|Hiraga Gennai]]'s sheng shrine in [[:en:Hiroshima Prefecture|Hiroshima Prefecture]], [[:en:Fukuyama, Hiroshima|Fukuyama City]], Tomomachi.]]
== In China == When [[Han Dynasty#Western Han|Former Han]]'s Reunobu was the prime minister of [[Yan Kingdom (Han dynasty)|Yan]], he built his shrine between Yan and [[Qi (state)|Qi]] and called it the "Reun Gong Shrine". Also, when {{ill|Shiqing|zh|石慶|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiqing|qq=}} was the prime minister of Qi, the Qi people built the "Shiqing Shrine". This is the beginning of the birth shrine.<ref>[[Zhao Yi]], "The New New Ancestral Hall", Volume 32, "Ancestral Hall", "This is the beginning of the establishment of an ancestral hall.</ref>
In the [[Tang dynasty]], there were certain restrictions on the construction of monuments and shrines by officials in office. According to the [[Tang Code|Tang Rulings and Ordinances]], those who erected shrines or monuments without permission were subject to a penalty of one year of imprisonment.<ref>The [[Tang Code]], Volume 11 (The Law of Office)</ref> The Tang Dynasty called it "the difficulty of building monuments and shrines on the first day.
In the [[Ming dynasty|Ming]], peasants in [[Tengzhou]] built a shrine to commemorate {{ill|Zhao Bangqing|zh|趙邦清|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhao Bangqing|qq=}}, who was appointed to Yanjing for his clean politics. The shrine was built to commemorate him.
During the reign of the [[Tianqi Emperor]] of the Ming Dynasty, the eunuch [[Wei Zhongxian]], ruled the world by conducting his own politics, and those who flattered him or feared his authority were given a sheng shrine from him. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing (1627), Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote: "Confucius wrote the Spring and Autumn, and Chung-Hsien wrote the Essentials. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote that Confucius had written the "Spring and Autumn" and Chung-Hsien had written the "Essentials. In April of the 7th year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, [[Yuan Chonghuan]] and {{ill|Yan Mingtai|zh|閻鳴泰|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yan Mingtai|qq=}} of the [[Ministry of War (imperial China)|Ministry of War]] made an appeal to the government to praise the merits and demerits of Wei Zhongxian and to establish a sheng shrine for him in both Qiantu.<ref>"Ming Xi Zong Jizhu": "in April of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianqi, the governor of Thistle Liaoning, Yan Mingtai, and the governor of Fuxing Yuan Chong Huan, praised the merits of Wei Zhongxian, and requested the construction of a shrine in front of Ning, giving the name Mao De." The government of the Liaoning Province has been inscribed in gold and stone, and there is no room for extra duties. The minister's single-mindedness in patrolling, strict warning of the battalion generals, do not dare to greed cowardly selfishness, do not dare to give away the hidden string, change the virtual into the real, into the real, easy timid into brave, to have today. Sustainability ancient internal minister who has the right!" ("Ming Xi Zong seven years of the Imperial Court of the Department of the Experiment" Tianqi six years in October)</ref> After that, Wei Zhongxian's raw shrine was "all over the world", "the cost of each shrine was at most hundreds of thousands, at least tens of thousands", "stolen the people's fortune, invaded the public safe, and was cut down. There are countless trees. " When Huang Yun Yasushi made a statue and greeted the statue, he said, "Goto Sansho," and "led the general of Bunbu, lined up under the stairs, and worshipped as the beginning."
[[Wei Zhongxian]] has been strongly condemned in modern times for his behavior.{{by whom|date=March 2024}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |last=网易 |date=2020-12-14 |title=魏忠贤为自己生祠揭幕 |url=https://www.163.com/dy/article/FTRIN9CF0521Q1HR.html |access-date=2022-12-18 |website=www.163.com}}</ref>
[[:en:Gu Yanwu|Gu Yanwu]] lamented, "In this generation, there is no official who does not build a sheng shrine. And within a few years of leaving office, he would destroy the statues and change their masters.<ref>《Nichi Chi Roku》 vol. 23 (Sheng Shi), p. 644.</ref>
Recently{{When|date=December 2020}} a person{{whom|date=March 2024}} in China created their own Sheng shrine and stirred online controversy.<ref name=":0" />
== In Japan ==
The [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] (1603-1868) saw a rise in the practice of ''seishi'', or the establishment of one's own spiritual memorial site, where one's soul would be enshrined and worshipped after death. One notable example of this practice is that of [[Matsudaira Sadanobu]], who established his own ''seishi'' at Okuzawa Hachiman-gu in 1797.<ref name=":3">{{Cite web |title=「生祀は」の用例・例文集 - 用例.jp |url=http://yourei.jp/%E7%94%9F%E7%A5%80%E3%81%AF |access-date=2023-01-12 |website=yourei.jp}}</ref>
The rise in ''seishi'' is thought to have been influenced by Chinese philosophy, specifically [[:en:Edo neo-Confucianism|Edo neo-Confucianism]], which was introduced to Japan during the Edo period. One notable figure of this period, [[Yamazaki Ansai]], is said to have devised rituals for ''seishi'' based on Confucian ceremonies, and established his own ''seishi'' in 1671 at his residence in [[Kyoto]].<ref name=":3" />
After this, many scholars and practitioners of Shinto and the Hirata school of ''[[kokugaku]]'' ("national study") also established their own ''seishi'', each with their own unique rituals. These ''seishi'' were believed to be a means of obtaining longevity in life or becoming a god after death. The practice is said to have originated from the mythological story of the sun goddess [[Amaterasu]], who enshrined her own divine spirit at [[Mount Miwa]].<ref name=":3" />
The study of ''seishi'' is particularly associated with Genchi Kato, who has identified 670 known ''seishi'', with the most recent being established in 1931 at the Nishi-Chogo Shrine in Higashikokura, [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]]. The ''seishi'' of [[:en:Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]] was established in 1876 in [[:en:Ishinomaki|Ishinomaki]], [[:en:Miyagi Prefecture|Miyagi Prefecture]], and again in 1893 at the Yahiko Shrine in [[Tatsuno, Nagano|Tatsuno]], [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]]. The ''seishi'' of Emperor Meiji, [[:en:Empress Shōken|Empress Shōken]], [[:en:Emperor Taishō|Emperor Taisho]], and [[:en:Empress Teimei|Empress Teimei]] were also established in various locations, including Hokkaido, [[:en:Hiroshima|Hiroshima]], and [[:en:Himeji|Himeji]].<ref name=":3" />
== See also == * [[Tamaya]] * {{ill|Okutsuki|ja|奥都城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okutsuki|qq=}} * [[:en:Hitogami|Hitogami]]

One notable figure of this period, [[Yamazaki Ansai]], is said to have devised rituals for ''seishi'' based on Confucian ceremonies, and established his own ''seishi'' in 1671 at his residence in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]].<ref name=":3" />
After this, many scholars and practitioners of Shinto and the Hirata school of ''[[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]]'' ("national study") also established their own ''seishi'', each with their own unique rituals. These ''seishi'' were believed to be a means of obtaining longevity in life or becoming a god after death. The practice is said to have originated from the mythological story of the sun goddess [[Amaterasu]], who enshrined her own divine spirit at [[Mount Miwa]].<ref name=":3" />
The study of ''seishi'' is particularly associated with Genchi Kato, who has identified 670 known ''seishi'', with the most recent being established in 1931 at the Nishi-Chogo Shrine in Higashikokura, [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]]. The ''seishi'' of [[:en:Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]] was established in 1876 in [[:en:Ishinomaki|Ishinomaki]], [[:en:Miyagi Prefecture|Miyagi Prefecture]], and again in 1893 at the Yahiko Shrine in [[Tatsuno, Nagano|Tatsuno]], [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]]. The ''seishi'' of Emperor Meiji, [[:en:Empress Shōken|Empress Shōken]], [[:en:Emperor Taishō|Emperor Taisho]], and [[:en:Empress Teimei|Empress Teimei]] were also established in various locations, including Hokkaido, [[:en:Hiroshima|Hiroshima]], and [[:en:Himeji|Himeji]].<ref name=":3" />

[[Category:Pages without translation templates]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ja:生祀]] [[simple:Worship of the living]] [[zh:生祠]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]]

revision.comment: Bot: remove obsolete category Pages with valid en interwikis
revision.text:
{{Short description|Worshipping living people in East Asian culture}}
'''Worship of the living''' ([[:en:Japanese language|Japanese]]: 生祀, ''seishi''; [[:en:Chinese language|Chinese]]: 生祠, ''shēngsì'') is the [[worship]] of living people practiced in the [[:en:East Asian cultural sphere|East Asian cultural sphere]]. In China, it is practiced at ''sheng'' shrines (Chinese: 生祠, shēng[[:en:Ci Shrine|cí]]).<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |date=2022-12-18 |title=为关心民生疾苦而设的生祠，在明代起着什么样的作用？_政治_祭祀_陈天祥 |website=[[Sohu]] |url=https://www.sohu.com/a/531809387_120005162 |access-date=2023-01-12 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221218062727/https://www.sohu.com/a/531809387_120005162 |archive-date=2022-12-18 }}</ref> There are two types of enshrinement: the enshrinement of the spirit of a living person who has made a significant contribution, and the enshrinement of one's own spirit.
Due to the usage by [[Wei Zhongxian]] the practice became seen as a sign of corruption and declined in China.<ref name=":2">{{Cite web |title=生祠并非魏忠贤的专利-凤凰周刊 |url=http://www.ifengweekly.com/detil.php?id=16813 |access-date=2022-12-22 |website=www.ifengweekly.com|trans-title=Shengci is not Wei Zhongxian's invention}}</ref> In Japan, the practice was most prominent with the worship of the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] during the period of the imperial [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] from the late 19th century until 1945.<ref name="Earhart">{{cite book |last1=Earhart |first1=H. Byron |url=https://archive.org/details/religioninjapane0000earh |title=Religion in the Japanese experience: sources and interpretations |date=1974 |publisher=Dickenson Pub. Co. |isbn=0822101041 |edition=3rd |location=Encino, Calif. |author1-link=H. Byron Earhart |url-access=registration}}</ref> [[File:Hiraga_Gennai_seishi01s2040.jpg|thumb|200x200px|[[:en:Hiraga Gennai|Hiraga Gennai]]'s sheng shrine in [[:en:Hiroshima Prefecture|Hiroshima Prefecture]], [[:en:Fukuyama, Hiroshima|Fukuyama City]], Tomomachi.]]
== In China == When [[Han Dynasty#Western Han|Former Han]]'s Reunobu was the prime minister of [[Yan Kingdom (Han dynasty)|Yan]], he built his shrine between Yan and [[Qi (state)|Qi]] and called it the "Reun Gong Shrine". Also, when {{ill|Shiqing|zh|石慶|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiqing|qq=}} was the prime minister of Qi, the Qi people built the "Shiqing Shrine". This is the beginning of the birth shrine.<ref>[[Zhao Yi]], "The New New Ancestral Hall", Volume 32, "Ancestral Hall", "This is the beginning of the establishment of an ancestral hall.</ref>
In the [[Tang dynasty]], there were certain restrictions on the construction of monuments and shrines by officials in office. According to the [[Tang Code|Tang Rulings and Ordinances]], those who erected shrines or monuments without permission were subject to a penalty of one year of imprisonment.<ref>The [[Tang Code]], Volume 11 (The Law of Office)</ref> The Tang Dynasty called it "the difficulty of building monuments and shrines on the first day.
In the [[Ming dynasty|Ming]], peasants in [[Tengzhou]] built a shrine to commemorate {{ill|Zhao Bangqing|zh|趙邦清|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhao Bangqing|qq=}}, who was appointed to Yanjing for his clean politics. The shrine was built to commemorate him.
During the reign of the [[Tianqi Emperor]] of the Ming Dynasty, the eunuch [[Wei Zhongxian]], ruled the world by conducting his own politics, and those who flattered him or feared his authority were given a sheng shrine from him. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing (1627), Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote: "Confucius wrote the Spring and Autumn, and Chung-Hsien wrote the Essentials. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote that Confucius had written the "Spring and Autumn" and Chung-Hsien had written the "Essentials. In April of the 7th year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, [[Yuan Chonghuan]] and {{ill|Yan Mingtai|zh|閻鳴泰|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yan Mingtai|qq=}} of the [[Ministry of War (imperial China)|Ministry of War]] made an appeal to the government to praise the merits and demerits of Wei Zhongxian and to establish a sheng shrine for him in both Qiantu.<ref>"Ming Xi Zong Jizhu": "in April of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianqi, the governor of Thistle Liaoning, Yan Mingtai, and the governor of Fuxing Yuan Chong Huan, praised the merits of Wei Zhongxian, and requested the construction of a shrine in front of Ning, giving the name Mao De." The government of the Liaoning Province has been inscribed in gold and stone, and there is no room for extra duties. The minister's single-mindedness in patrolling, strict warning of the battalion generals, do not dare to greed cowardly selfishness, do not dare to give away the hidden string, change the virtual into the real, into the real, easy timid into brave, to have today. Sustainability ancient internal minister who has the right!" ("Ming Xi Zong seven years of the Imperial Court of the Department of the Experiment" Tianqi six years in October)</ref> After that, Wei Zhongxian's raw shrine was "all over the world", "the cost of each shrine was at most hundreds of thousands, at least tens of thousands", "stolen the people's fortune, invaded the public safe, and was cut down. There are countless trees. " When Huang Yun Yasushi made a statue and greeted the statue, he said, "Goto Sansho," and "led the general of Bunbu, lined up under the stairs, and worshipped as the beginning."
[[Wei Zhongxian]] has been strongly condemned in modern times for his behavior.{{by whom|date=March 2024}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |last=网易 |date=2020-12-14 |title=魏忠贤为自己生祠揭幕 |url=https://www.163.com/dy/article/FTRIN9CF0521Q1HR.html |access-date=2022-12-18 |website=www.163.com}}</ref>
[[:en:Gu Yanwu|Gu Yanwu]] lamented, "In this generation, there is no official who does not build a sheng shrine. And within a few years of leaving office, he would destroy the statues and change their masters.<ref>《Nichi Chi Roku》 vol. 23 (Sheng Shi), p. 644.</ref>
Recently{{When|date=December 2020}} a person{{whom|date=March 2024}} in China created their own Sheng shrine and stirred online controversy.<ref name=":0" />
== In Japan ==
The [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] (1603-1868) saw a rise in the practice of ''seishi'', or the establishment of one's own spiritual memorial site, where one's soul would be enshrined and worshipped after death. One notable example of this practice is that of [[Matsudaira Sadanobu]], who established his own ''seishi'' at Okuzawa Hachiman-gu in 1797.<ref name=":3">{{Cite web |title=「生祀は」の用例・例文集 - 用例.jp |url=http://yourei.jp/%E7%94%9F%E7%A5%80%E3%81%AF |access-date=2023-01-12 |website=yourei.jp}}</ref>
The rise in ''seishi'' is thought to have been influenced by Chinese philosophy, specifically [[:en:Edo neo-Confucianism|Edo neo-Confucianism]], which was introduced to Japan during the Edo period. One notable figure of this period, [[Yamazaki Ansai]], is said to have devised rituals for ''seishi'' based on Confucian ceremonies, and established his own ''seishi'' in 1671 at his residence in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]].<ref name=":3" />
After this, many scholars and practitioners of Shinto and the Hirata school of ''[[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]]'' ("national study") also established their own ''seishi'', each with their own unique rituals. These ''seishi'' were believed to be a means of obtaining longevity in life or becoming a god after death. The practice is said to have originated from the mythological story of the sun goddess [[Amaterasu]], who enshrined her own divine spirit at [[Mount Miwa]].<ref name=":3" />
The study of ''seishi'' is particularly associated with Genchi Kato, who has identified 670 known ''seishi'', with the most recent being established in 1931 at the Nishi-Chogo Shrine in Higashikokura, [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]]. The ''seishi'' of [[:en:Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]] was established in 1876 in [[:en:Ishinomaki|Ishinomaki]], [[:en:Miyagi Prefecture|Miyagi Prefecture]], and again in 1893 at the Yahiko Shrine in [[Tatsuno, Nagano|Tatsuno]], [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]]. The ''seishi'' of Emperor Meiji, [[:en:Empress Shōken|Empress Shōken]], [[:en:Emperor Taishō|Emperor Taisho]], and [[:en:Empress Teimei|Empress Teimei]] were also established in various locations, including Hokkaido, [[:en:Hiroshima|Hiroshima]], and [[:en:Himeji|Himeji]].<ref name=":3" />
== See also == * [[Tamaya]] * {{ill|Okutsuki|ja|奥都城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okutsuki|qq=}} * [[:en:Hitogami|Hitogami]]
== References == <references />
*

In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing (1627), Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote: "Confucius wrote the Spring and Autumn, and Chung-Hsien wrote the Essentials. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote that Confucius had written the "Spring and Autumn" and Chung-Hsien had written the "Essentials. In April of the 7th year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, [[Yuan Chonghuan]] and {{ill|Yan Mingtai|zh|閻鳴泰|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yan Mingtai|qq=}} of the [[Ministry of War (imperial China)|Ministry of War]] made an appeal to the government to praise the merits and demerits of Wei Zhongxian and to establish a sheng shrine for him in both Qiantu.<ref>"Ming Xi Zong Jizhu": "in April of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianqi, the governor of Thistle Liaoning, Yan Mingtai, and the governor of Fuxing Yuan Chong Huan, praised the merits of Wei Zhongxian, and requested the construction of a shrine in front of Ning, giving the name Mao De." The government of the Liaoning Province has been inscribed in gold and stone, and there is no room for extra duties. The minister's single-mindedness in patrolling, strict warning of the battalion generals, do not dare to greed cowardly selfishness, do not dare to give away the hidden string, change the virtual into the real, into the real, easy timid into brave, to have today. Sustainability ancient internal minister who has the right!" ("Ming Xi Zong seven years of the Imperial Court of the Department of the Experiment" Tianqi six years in October)</ref> After that, Wei Zhongxian's raw shrine was "all over the world", "the cost of each shrine was at most hundreds of thousands, at least tens of thousands", "stolen the people's fortune, invaded the public safe, and was cut down. There are countless trees. " When Huang Yun Yasushi made a statue and greeted the statue, he said, "Goto Sansho," and "led the general of Bunbu, lined up under the stairs, and worshipped as the beginning."
[[Wei Zhongxian]] has been strongly condemned in modern times for his behavior.{{by whom|date=March 2024}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |last=网易 |date=2020-12-14 |title=魏忠贤为自己生祠揭幕 |url=https://www.163.com/dy/article/FTRIN9CF0521Q1HR.html |access-date=2022-12-18 |website=www.163.com}}</ref>
[[:en:Gu Yanwu|Gu Yanwu]] lamented, "In this generation, there is no official who does not build a sheng shrine. And within a few years of leaving office, he would destroy the statues and change their masters.<ref>《Nichi Chi Roku》 vol. 23 (Sheng Shi), p. 644.</ref>
Recently{{When|date=December 2020}} a person{{whom|date=March 2024}} in China created their own Sheng shrine and stirred online controversy.<ref name=":0" />
== In Japan ==
The [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] (1603-1868) saw a rise in the practice of ''seishi'', or the establishment of one's own spiritual memorial site, where one's soul would be enshrined and worshipped after death. One notable example of this practice is that of [[:en:Matsudaira Sadanobu|Matsudaira Sadanobu]], who established his own ''seishi'' at Okuzawa Hachiman-gu in 1797.<ref name=":3">{{Cite web |title=「生祀は」の用例・例文集 - 用例.jp |url=http://yourei.jp/%E7%94%9F%E7%A5%80%E3%81%AF |access-date=2023-01-12 |website=yourei.jp}}</ref>
The rise in ''seishi'' is thought to have been influenced by Chinese philosophy, specifically [[:en:Edo neo-Confucianism|Edo neo-Confucianism]], which was introduced to Japan during the Edo period. One notable figure of this period, [[Yamazaki Ansai]], is said to have devised rituals for ''seishi'' based on Confucian ceremonies, and established his own ''seishi'' in 1671 at his residence in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]].<ref name=":3" />
After this, many scholars and practitioners of Shinto and the Hirata school of ''[[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]]'' ("national study") also established their own ''seishi'', each with their own unique rituals. These ''seishi'' were believed to be a means of obtaining longevity in life or becoming a god after death. The practice is said to have originated from the mythological story of the sun goddess [[Amaterasu]], who enshrined her own divine spirit at [[Mount Miwa]].<ref name=":3" />
The study of ''seishi'' is particularly associated with Genchi Kato, who has identified 670 known ''seishi'', with the most recent being established in 1931 at the Nishi-Chogo Shrine in Higashikokura, [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]]. The ''seishi'' of [[:en:Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]] was established in 1876 in [[:en:Ishinomaki|Ishinomaki]], [[:en:Miyagi Prefecture|Miyagi Prefecture]], and again in 1893 at the Yahiko Shrine in [[Tatsuno, Nagano|Tatsuno]], [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]]. The ''seishi'' of Emperor Meiji, [[:en:Empress Shōken|Empress Shōken]], [[:en:Emperor Taishō|Emperor Taisho]], and [[:en:Empress Teimei|Empress Teimei]] were also established in various locations, including Hokkaido, [[:en:Hiroshima|Hiroshima]], and [[:en:Himeji|Himeji]].<ref name=":3" />
