<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Worship of the living]][[ja:生祀]][[simple:Worship of the living]][[zh:生祠]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q110226457]] Wikidata name: Sheng Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Worshipping living people in East Asian culture}}
'''Worship of the living''' ([[:en:Japanese language|Japanese]]: 生祀, ''seishi''; [[:en:Chinese language|Chinese]]: 生祠, ''shēngsì'') is the [[:en:Worship|Worship]] of living people practiced in the [[:en:East Asian cultural sphere|East Asian cultural sphere]]. In China, it is practiced at ''sheng'' shrines (Chinese: 生祠, shēng[[:en:Ci Shrine|cí]]).<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |date=2022-12-18 |title=为关心民生疾苦而设的生祠，在明代起着什么样的作用？_政治_祭祀_陈天祥 |website=[[Sohu]] |url=https://www.sohu.com/a/531809387_120005162 |access-date=2023-01-12 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221218062727/https://www.sohu.com/a/531809387_120005162 |archive-date=2022-12-18 }}</ref> There are two types of enshrinement: the enshrinement of the spirit of a living person who has made a significant contribution, and the enshrinement of one's own spirit.
Due to the usage by [[:en:Wei Zhongxian|Wei Zhongxian]] the practice became seen as a sign of corruption and declined in China.<ref name=":2">{{Cite web |title=生祠并非魏忠贤的专利-凤凰周刊 |url=http://www.ifengweekly.com/detil.php?id=16813 |access-date=2022-12-22 |website=www.ifengweekly.com|trans-title=Shengci is not Wei Zhongxian's invention}}</ref> In Japan, the practice was most prominent with the worship of the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] during the period of the imperial [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] from the late 19th century until 1945.<ref name="Earhart">{{cite book |last1=Earhart |first1=H. Byron |url=https://archive.org/details/religioninjapane0000earh |title=Religion in the Japanese experience: sources and interpretations |date=1974 |publisher=Dickenson Pub. Co. |isbn=0822101041 |edition=3rd |location=Encino, Calif. |author1-link=H. Byron Earhart |url-access=registration}}</ref> [[File:Hiraga_Gennai_seishi01s2040.jpg|thumb|200x200px|[[:en:Hiraga Gennai|Hiraga Gennai]]'s sheng shrine in [[:en:Hiroshima Prefecture|Hiroshima Prefecture]], [[:en:Fukuyama, Hiroshima|Fukuyama City]], Tomomachi.]]
== In China == When [[Han Dynasty#Western Han|Former Han]]'s Reunobu was the prime minister of [[:en:Yan Kingdom (Han dynasty)|Yan]], he built his shrine between Yan and [[:en:Qi (state)|Qi]] and called it the "Reun Gong Shrine". Also, when {{ill|Shiqing|zh|石慶|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiqing|qq=}} was the prime minister of Qi, the Qi people built the "Shiqing Shrine". This is the beginning of the birth shrine.<ref>[[:en:Zhao Yi|Zhao Yi]], "The New New Ancestral Hall", Volume 32, "Ancestral Hall", "This is the beginning of the establishment of an ancestral hall.</ref>
In the [[:en:Tang dynasty|Tang dynasty]], there were certain restrictions on the construction of monuments and shrines by officials in office. According to the [[:en:Tang Code|Tang Rulings and Ordinances]], those who erected shrines or monuments without permission were subject to a penalty of one year of imprisonment.<ref>The [[:en:Tang Code|Tang Code]], Volume 11 (The Law of Office)</ref> The Tang Dynasty called it "the difficulty of building monuments and shrines on the first day.
In the [[:en:Ming dynasty|Ming]], peasants in [[:en:Tengzhou|Tengzhou]] built a shrine to commemorate {{ill|Zhao Bangqing|zh|趙邦清|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhao Bangqing|qq=}}, who was appointed to Yanjing for his clean politics. The shrine was built to commemorate him.
During the reign of the [[:en:Tianqi Emperor|Tianqi Emperor]] of the Ming Dynasty, the eunuch [[:en:Wei Zhongxian|Wei Zhongxian]], ruled the world by conducting his own politics, and those who flattered him or feared his authority were given a sheng shrine from him. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing (1627), Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote: "Confucius wrote the Spring and Autumn, and Chung-Hsien wrote the Essentials. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote that Confucius had written the "Spring and Autumn" and Chung-Hsien had written the "Essentials. In April of the 7th year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, [[:en:Yuan Chonghuan|Yuan Chonghuan]] and {{ill|Yan Mingtai|zh|閻鳴泰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yan Mingtai|qq=}} of the [[:en:Ministry of War (imperial China)|Ministry of War]] made an appeal to the government to praise the merits and demerits of Wei Zhongxian and to establish a sheng shrine for him in both Qiantu.<ref>"Ming Xi Zong Jizhu": "in April of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianqi, the governor of Thistle Liaoning, Yan Mingtai, and the governor of Fuxing Yuan Chong Huan, praised the merits of Wei Zhongxian, and requested the construction of a shrine in front of Ning, giving the name Mao De." The government of the Liaoning Province has been inscribed in gold and stone, and there is no room for extra duties. The minister's single-mindedness in patrolling, strict warning of the battalion generals, do not dare to greed cowardly selfishness, do not dare to give away the hidden string, change the virtual into the real, into the real, easy timid into brave, to have today. Sustainability ancient internal minister who has the right!" ("Ming Xi Zong seven years of the Imperial Court of the Department of the Experiment" Tianqi six years in October)</ref> After that, Wei Zhongxian's raw shrine was "all over the world", "the cost of each shrine was at most hundreds of thousands, at least tens of thousands", "stolen the people's fortune, invaded the public safe, and was cut down. There are countless trees. " When Huang Yun Yasushi made a statue and greeted the statue, he said, "Goto Sansho," and "led the general of Bunbu, lined up under the stairs, and worshipped as the beginning."
[[:en:Wei Zhongxian|Wei Zhongxian]] has been strongly condemned in modern times for his behavior.{{by whom|date=March 2024}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |last=网易 |date=2020-12-14 |title=魏忠贤为自己生祠揭幕 |url=https://www.163.com/dy/article/FTRIN9CF0521Q1HR.html |access-date=2022-12-18 |website=www.163.com}}</ref>
[[:en:Gu Yanwu|Gu Yanwu]] lamented, "In this generation, there is no official who does not build a sheng shrine. And within a few years of leaving office, he would destroy the statues and change their masters.<ref>《Nichi Chi Roku》 vol. 23 (Sheng Shi), p. 644.</ref>
Recently{{When|date=December 2020}} a person{{whom|date=March 2024}} in China created their own Sheng shrine and stirred online controversy.<ref name=":0" />
== In Japan ==
The [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] (1603-1868) saw a rise in the practice of ''seishi'', or the establishment of one's own spiritual memorial site, where one's soul would be enshrined and worshipped after death. One notable example of this practice is that of [[:en:Matsudaira Sadanobu|Matsudaira Sadanobu]], who established his own ''seishi'' at Okuzawa Hachiman-gu in 1797.<ref name=":3">{{Cite web |title=「生祀は」の用例・例文集 - 用例.jp |url=http://yourei.jp/%E7%94%9F%E7%A5%80%E3%81%AF |access-date=2023-01-12 |website=yourei.jp}}</ref>
The rise in ''seishi'' is thought to have been influenced by Chinese philosophy, specifically [[:en:Edo neo-Confucianism|Edo neo-Confucianism]], which was introduced to Japan during the Edo period. One notable figure of this period, [[:en:Yamazaki Ansai|Yamazaki Ansai]], is said to have devised rituals for ''seishi'' based on Confucian ceremonies, and established his own ''seishi'' in 1671 at his residence in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]].<ref name=":3" />
After this, many scholars and practitioners of Shinto and the Hirata school of ''[[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]]'' ("national study") also established their own ''seishi'', each with their own unique rituals. These ''seishi'' were believed to be a means of obtaining longevity in life or becoming a god after death. The practice is said to have originated from the mythological story of the sun goddess [[Amaterasu]], who enshrined her own divine spirit at [[:en:Mount Miwa|Mount Miwa]].<ref name=":3" />
The study of ''seishi'' is particularly associated with Genchi Kato, who has identified 670 known ''seishi'', with the most recent being established in 1931 at the Nishi-Chogo Shrine in Higashikokura, [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]]. The ''seishi'' of [[:en:Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]] was established in 1876 in [[:en:Ishinomaki|Ishinomaki]], [[:en:Miyagi Prefecture|Miyagi Prefecture]], and again in 1893 at the Yahiko Shrine in [[:en:Tatsuno, Nagano|Tatsuno]], [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]]. The ''seishi'' of Emperor Meiji, [[:en:Empress Shōken|Empress Shōken]], [[:en:Emperor Taishō|Emperor Taisho]], and [[:en:Empress Teimei|Empress Teimei]] were also established in various locations, including Hokkaido, [[:en:Hiroshima|Hiroshima]], and [[:en:Himeji|Himeji]].<ref name=":3" />

In April of the 7th year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, [[:en:Yuan Chonghuan|Yuan Chonghuan]] and {{ill|Yan Mingtai|zh|閻鳴泰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yan Mingtai|qq=}} of the [[:en:Ministry of War (imperial China)|Ministry of War]] made an appeal to the government to praise the merits and demerits of Wei Zhongxian and to establish a sheng shrine for him in both Qiantu.<ref>"Ming Xi Zong Jizhu": "in April of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianqi, the governor of Thistle Liaoning, Yan Mingtai, and the governor of Fuxing Yuan Chong Huan, praised the merits of Wei Zhongxian, and requested the construction of a shrine in front of Ning, giving the name Mao De." The government of the Liaoning Province has been inscribed in gold and stone, and there is no room for extra duties. The minister's single-mindedness in patrolling, strict warning of the battalion generals, do not dare to greed cowardly selfishness, do not dare to give away the hidden string, change the virtual into the real, into the real, easy timid into brave, to have today. Sustainability ancient internal minister who has the right!" ("Ming Xi Zong seven years of the Imperial Court of the Department of the Experiment" Tianqi six years in October)</ref> After that, Wei Zhongxian's raw shrine was "all over the world", "the cost of each shrine was at most hundreds of thousands, at least tens of thousands", "stolen the people's fortune, invaded the public safe, and was cut down. There are countless trees. " When Huang Yun Yasushi made a statue and greeted the statue, he said, "Goto Sansho," and "led the general of Bunbu, lined up under the stairs, and worshipped as the beginning."
[[:en:Wei Zhongxian|Wei Zhongxian]] has been strongly condemned in modern times for his behavior.{{by whom|date=March 2024}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |last=网易 |date=2020-12-14 |title=魏忠贤为自己生祠揭幕 |url=https://www.163.com/dy/article/FTRIN9CF0521Q1HR.html |access-date=2022-12-18 |website=www.163.com}}</ref>
[[:en:Gu Yanwu|Gu Yanwu]] lamented, "In this generation, there is no official who does not build a sheng shrine. And within a few years of leaving office, he would destroy the statues and change their masters.<ref>《Nichi Chi Roku》 vol. 23 (Sheng Shi), p. 644.</ref>
Recently{{When|date=December 2020}} a person{{whom|date=March 2024}} in China created their own Sheng shrine and stirred online controversy.<ref name=":0" />
== In Japan ==
The [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] (1603-1868) saw a rise in the practice of ''seishi'', or the establishment of one's own spiritual memorial site, where one's soul would be enshrined and worshipped after death. One notable example of this practice is that of [[:en:Matsudaira Sadanobu|Matsudaira Sadanobu]], who established his own ''seishi'' at Okuzawa Hachiman-gu in 1797.<ref name=":3">{{Cite web |title=「生祀は」の用例・例文集 - 用例.jp |url=http://yourei.jp/%E7%94%9F%E7%A5%80%E3%81%AF |access-date=2023-01-12 |website=yourei.jp}}</ref>
The rise in ''seishi'' is thought to have been influenced by Chinese philosophy, specifically [[:en:Edo neo-Confucianism|Edo neo-Confucianism]], which was introduced to Japan during the Edo period. One notable figure of this period, [[:en:Yamazaki Ansai|Yamazaki Ansai]], is said to have devised rituals for ''seishi'' based on Confucian ceremonies, and established his own ''seishi'' in 1671 at his residence in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]].<ref name=":3" />
After this, many scholars and practitioners of Shinto and the Hirata school of ''[[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]]'' ("national study") also established their own ''seishi'', each with their own unique rituals. These ''seishi'' were believed to be a means of obtaining longevity in life or becoming a god after death. The practice is said to have originated from the mythological story of the sun goddess [[Amaterasu]], who enshrined her own divine spirit at [[:en:Mount Miwa|Mount Miwa]].<ref name=":3" />
The study of ''seishi'' is particularly associated with Genchi Kato, who has identified 670 known ''seishi'', with the most recent being established in 1931 at the Nishi-Chogo Shrine in Higashikokura, [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]]. The ''seishi'' of [[:en:Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]] was established in 1876 in [[:en:Ishinomaki|Ishinomaki]], [[:en:Miyagi Prefecture|Miyagi Prefecture]], and again in 1893 at the Yahiko Shrine in [[:en:Tatsuno, Nagano|Tatsuno]], [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]]. The ''seishi'' of Emperor Meiji, [[:en:Empress Shōken|Empress Shōken]], [[:en:Emperor Taishō|Emperor Taisho]], and [[:en:Empress Teimei|Empress Teimei]] were also established in various locations, including Hokkaido, [[:en:Hiroshima|Hiroshima]], and [[:en:Himeji|Himeji]].<ref name=":3" />

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Worship of the living]][[ja:生祀]][[simple:Worship of the living]][[zh:生祠]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Worship of the living]][[ja:生祀]][[simple:Worship of the living]][[zh:生祠]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Worship of the living]][[ja:生祀]][[simple:Worship of the living]][[zh:生祠]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q110226457]] Wikidata name: Sheng Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Worshipping living people in East Asian culture}}
'''Worship of the living''' ([[:en:Japanese language|Japanese]]: 生祀, ''seishi''; [[:en:Chinese language|Chinese]]: 生祠, ''shēngsì'') is the [[:en:Worship|Worship]] of living people practiced in the [[:en:East Asian cultural sphere|East Asian cultural sphere]]. In China, it is practiced at ''sheng'' shrines (Chinese: 生祠, shēng[[:en:Ci Shrine|cí]]).<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |date=2022-12-18 |title=为关心民生疾苦而设的生祠，在明代起着什么样的作用？_政治_祭祀_陈天祥 |website=[[Sohu]] |url=https://www.sohu.com/a/531809387_120005162 |access-date=2023-01-12 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221218062727/https://www.sohu.com/a/531809387_120005162 |archive-date=2022-12-18 }}</ref> There are two types of enshrinement: the enshrinement of the spirit of a living person who has made a significant contribution, and the enshrinement of one's own spirit.
Due to the usage by [[:en:Wei Zhongxian|Wei Zhongxian]] the practice became seen as a sign of corruption and declined in China.<ref name=":2">{{Cite web |title=生祠并非魏忠贤的专利-凤凰周刊 |url=http://www.ifengweekly.com/detil.php?id=16813 |access-date=2022-12-22 |website=www.ifengweekly.com|trans-title=Shengci is not Wei Zhongxian's invention}}</ref> In Japan, the practice was most prominent with the worship of the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] during the period of the imperial [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] from the late 19th century until 1945.<ref name="Earhart">{{cite book |last1=Earhart |first1=H. Byron |url=https://archive.org/details/religioninjapane0000earh |title=Religion in the Japanese experience: sources and interpretations |date=1974 |publisher=Dickenson Pub. Co. |isbn=0822101041 |edition=3rd |location=Encino, Calif. |author1-link=H. Byron Earhart |url-access=registration}}</ref> [[File:Hiraga_Gennai_seishi01s2040.jpg|thumb|200x200px|[[:en:Hiraga Gennai|Hiraga Gennai]]'s sheng shrine in [[:en:Hiroshima Prefecture|Hiroshima Prefecture]], [[:en:Fukuyama, Hiroshima|Fukuyama City]], Tomomachi.]]
== In China == When [[Han Dynasty#Western Han|Former Han]]'s Reunobu was the prime minister of [[:en:Yan Kingdom (Han dynasty)|Yan]], he built his shrine between Yan and [[:en:Qi (state)|Qi]] and called it the "Reun Gong Shrine". Also, when {{ill|Shiqing|zh|石慶|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiqing|qq=}} was the prime minister of Qi, the Qi people built the "Shiqing Shrine". This is the beginning of the birth shrine.<ref>[[:en:Zhao Yi|Zhao Yi]], "The New New Ancestral Hall", Volume 32, "Ancestral Hall", "This is the beginning of the establishment of an ancestral hall.</ref>
In the [[:en:Tang dynasty|Tang dynasty]], there were certain restrictions on the construction of monuments and shrines by officials in office. According to the [[:en:Tang Code|Tang Rulings and Ordinances]], those who erected shrines or monuments without permission were subject to a penalty of one year of imprisonment.<ref>The [[:en:Tang Code|Tang Code]], Volume 11 (The Law of Office)</ref> The Tang Dynasty called it "the difficulty of building monuments and shrines on the first day.
In the [[:en:Ming dynasty|Ming]], peasants in [[:en:Tengzhou|Tengzhou]] built a shrine to commemorate {{ill|Zhao Bangqing|zh|趙邦清|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhao Bangqing|qq=}}, who was appointed to Yanjing for his clean politics. The shrine was built to commemorate him.
During the reign of the [[:en:Tianqi Emperor|Tianqi Emperor]] of the Ming Dynasty, the eunuch [[:en:Wei Zhongxian|Wei Zhongxian]], ruled the world by conducting his own politics, and those who flattered him or feared his authority were given a sheng shrine from him. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing (1627), Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote: "Confucius wrote the Spring and Autumn, and Chung-Hsien wrote the Essentials. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote that Confucius had written the "Spring and Autumn" and Chung-Hsien had written the "Essentials. In April of the 7th year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, [[:en:Yuan Chonghuan|Yuan Chonghuan]] and {{ill|Yan Mingtai|zh|閻鳴泰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yan Mingtai|qq=}} of the [[:en:Ministry of War (imperial China)|Ministry of War]] made an appeal to the government to praise the merits and demerits of Wei Zhongxian and to establish a sheng shrine for him in both Qiantu.<ref>"Ming Xi Zong Jizhu": "in April of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianqi, the governor of Thistle Liaoning, Yan Mingtai, and the governor of Fuxing Yuan Chong Huan, praised the merits of Wei Zhongxian, and requested the construction of a shrine in front of Ning, giving the name Mao De." The government of the Liaoning Province has been inscribed in gold and stone, and there is no room for extra duties. The minister's single-mindedness in patrolling, strict warning of the battalion generals, do not dare to greed cowardly selfishness, do not dare to give away the hidden string, change the virtual into the real, into the real, easy timid into brave, to have today. Sustainability ancient internal minister who has the right!" ("Ming Xi Zong seven years of the Imperial Court of the Department of the Experiment" Tianqi six years in October)</ref> After that, Wei Zhongxian's raw shrine was "all over the world", "the cost of each shrine was at most hundreds of thousands, at least tens of thousands", "stolen the people's fortune, invaded the public safe, and was cut down. There are countless trees. " When Huang Yun Yasushi made a statue and greeted the statue, he said, "Goto Sansho," and "led the general of Bunbu, lined up under the stairs, and worshipped as the beginning."
[[:en:Wei Zhongxian|Wei Zhongxian]] has been strongly condemned in modern times for his behavior.{{by whom|date=March 2024}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |last=网易 |date=2020-12-14 |title=魏忠贤为自己生祠揭幕 |url=https://www.163.com/dy/article/FTRIN9CF0521Q1HR.html |access-date=2022-12-18 |website=www.163.com}}</ref>
[[:en:Gu Yanwu|Gu Yanwu]] lamented, "In this generation, there is no official who does not build a sheng shrine. And within a few years of leaving office, he would destroy the statues and change their masters.<ref>《Nichi Chi Roku》 vol. 23 (Sheng Shi), p. 644.</ref>
Recently{{When|date=December 2020}} a person{{whom|date=March 2024}} in China created their own Sheng shrine and stirred online controversy.<ref name=":0" />

The study of ''seishi'' is particularly associated with Genchi Kato, who has identified 670 known ''seishi'', with the most recent being established in 1931 at the Nishi-Chogo Shrine in Higashikokura, [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]]. The ''seishi'' of Emperor Meiji, [[:en:Empress Shōken|Empress Shōken]], [[:en:Emperor Taishō|Emperor Taisho]], and [[:en:Empress Teimei|Empress Teimei]] were also established in various locations, including Hokkaido, [[:en:Hiroshima|Hiroshima]], and [[:en:Himeji|Himeji]].<ref name=":3" />

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Worship of the living]][[ja:生祀]][[simple:Worship of the living]][[zh:生祠]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Worship of the living]][[ja:生祀]][[simple:Worship of the living]][[zh:生祠]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Worship of the living]][[ja:生祀]][[simple:Worship of the living]][[zh:生祠]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q110226457]] Wikidata name: Sheng Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Worshipping living people in East Asian culture}}
'''Worship of the living''' ([[:en:Japanese language|Japanese]]: 生祀, ''seishi''; [[:en:Chinese language|Chinese]]: 生祠, ''shēngsì'') is the [[:en:Worship|Worship]] of living people practiced in the [[:en:East Asian cultural sphere|East Asian cultural sphere]]. In China, it is practiced at ''sheng'' shrines (Chinese: 生祠, shēng[[:en:Ci Shrine|cí]]).<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |date=2022-12-18 |title=为关心民生疾苦而设的生祠，在明代起着什么样的作用？_政治_祭祀_陈天祥 |website=[[Sohu]] |url=https://www.sohu.com/a/531809387_120005162 |access-date=2023-01-12 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221218062727/https://www.sohu.com/a/531809387_120005162 |archive-date=2022-12-18 }}</ref> There are two types of enshrinement: the enshrinement of the spirit of a living person who has made a significant contribution, and the enshrinement of one's own spirit.
Due to the usage by [[:en:Wei Zhongxian|Wei Zhongxian]] the practice became seen as a sign of corruption and declined in China.<ref name=":2">{{Cite web |title=生祠并非魏忠贤的专利-凤凰周刊 |url=http://www.ifengweekly.com/detil.php?id=16813 |access-date=2022-12-22 |website=www.ifengweekly.com|trans-title=Shengci is not Wei Zhongxian's invention}}</ref> In Japan, the practice was most prominent with the worship of the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] during the period of the imperial [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] from the late 19th century until 1945.<ref name="Earhart">{{cite book |last1=Earhart |first1=H. Byron |url=https://archive.org/details/religioninjapane0000earh |title=Religion in the Japanese experience: sources and interpretations |date=1974 |publisher=Dickenson Pub. Co. |isbn=0822101041 |edition=3rd |location=Encino, Calif. |author1-link=H. Byron Earhart |url-access=registration}}</ref> [[File:Hiraga_Gennai_seishi01s2040.jpg|thumb|200x200px|[[:en:Hiraga Gennai|Hiraga Gennai]]'s sheng shrine in [[:en:Hiroshima Prefecture|Hiroshima Prefecture]], [[:en:Fukuyama, Hiroshima|Fukuyama City]], Tomomachi.]]
== In China == When [[Han Dynasty#Western Han|Former Han]]'s Reunobu was the prime minister of [[:en:Yan Kingdom (Han dynasty)|Yan]], he built his shrine between Yan and [[:en:Qi (state)|Qi]] and called it the "Reun Gong Shrine". Also, when {{ill|Shiqing|zh|石慶|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiqing|qq=}} was the prime minister of Qi, the Qi people built the "Shiqing Shrine". This is the beginning of the birth shrine.<ref>[[:en:Zhao Yi|Zhao Yi]], "The New New Ancestral Hall", Volume 32, "Ancestral Hall", "This is the beginning of the establishment of an ancestral hall.</ref>
In the [[:en:Tang dynasty|Tang dynasty]], there were certain restrictions on the construction of monuments and shrines by officials in office. According to the [[:en:Tang Code|Tang Rulings and Ordinances]], those who erected shrines or monuments without permission were subject to a penalty of one year of imprisonment.<ref>The [[:en:Tang Code|Tang Code]], Volume 11 (The Law of Office)</ref> The Tang Dynasty called it "the difficulty of building monuments and shrines on the first day.
In the [[:en:Ming dynasty|Ming]], peasants in [[:en:Tengzhou|Tengzhou]] built a shrine to commemorate {{ill|Zhao Bangqing|zh|趙邦清|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhao Bangqing|qq=}}, who was appointed to Yanjing for his clean politics. The shrine was built to commemorate him.
During the reign of the [[:en:Tianqi Emperor|Tianqi Emperor]] of the Ming Dynasty, the eunuch [[:en:Wei Zhongxian|Wei Zhongxian]], ruled the world by conducting his own politics, and those who flattered him or feared his authority were given a sheng shrine from him. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing (1627), Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote: "Confucius wrote the Spring and Autumn, and Chung-Hsien wrote the Essentials. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote that Confucius had written the "Spring and Autumn" and Chung-Hsien had written the "Essentials. In April of the 7th year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, [[:en:Yuan Chonghuan|Yuan Chonghuan]] and {{ill|Yan Mingtai|zh|閻鳴泰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yan Mingtai|qq=}} of the [[:en:Ministry of War (imperial China)|Ministry of War]] made an appeal to the government to praise the merits and demerits of Wei Zhongxian and to establish a sheng shrine for him in both Qiantu.<ref>"Ming Xi Zong Jizhu": "in April of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianqi, the governor of Thistle Liaoning, Yan Mingtai, and the governor of Fuxing Yuan Chong Huan, praised the merits of Wei Zhongxian, and requested the construction of a shrine in front of Ning, giving the name Mao De." The government of the Liaoning Province has been inscribed in gold and stone, and there is no room for extra duties. The minister's single-mindedness in patrolling, strict warning of the battalion generals, do not dare to greed cowardly selfishness, do not dare to give away the hidden string, change the virtual into the real, into the real, easy timid into brave, to have today. Sustainability ancient internal minister who has the right!" ("Ming Xi Zong seven years of the Imperial Court of the Department of the Experiment" Tianqi six years in October)</ref> After that, Wei Zhongxian's raw shrine was "all over the world", "the cost of each shrine was at most hundreds of thousands, at least tens of thousands", "stolen the people's fortune, invaded the public safe, and was cut down. There are countless trees. " When Huang Yun Yasushi made a statue and greeted the statue, he said, "Goto Sansho," and "led the general of Bunbu, lined up under the stairs, and worshipped as the beginning."
[[:en:Wei Zhongxian|Wei Zhongxian]] has been strongly condemned in modern times for his behavior.{{by whom|date=March 2024}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |last=网易 |date=2020-12-14 |title=魏忠贤为自己生祠揭幕 |url=https://www.163.com/dy/article/FTRIN9CF0521Q1HR.html |access-date=2022-12-18 |website=www.163.com}}</ref>
[[:en:Gu Yanwu|Gu Yanwu]] lamented, "In this generation, there is no official who does not build a sheng shrine. And within a few years of leaving office, he would destroy the statues and change their masters.<ref>《Nichi Chi Roku》 vol. 23 (Sheng Shi), p. 644.</ref>
Recently{{When|date=December 2020}} a person{{whom|date=March 2024}} in China created their own Sheng shrine and stirred online controversy.<ref name=":0" />
== In Japan ==
The [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] (1603-1868) saw a rise in the practice of ''seishi'', or the establishment of one's own spiritual memorial site, where one's soul would be enshrined and worshipped after death. One notable example of this practice is that of [[:en:Matsudaira Sadanobu|Matsudaira Sadanobu]], who established his own ''seishi'' at Okuzawa Hachiman-gu in 1797.<ref name=":3">{{Cite web |title=「生祀は」の用例・例文集 - 用例.jp |url=http://yourei.jp/%E7%94%9F%E7%A5%80%E3%81%AF |access-date=2023-01-12 |website=yourei.jp}}</ref>
The rise in ''seishi'' is thought to have been influenced by Chinese philosophy, specifically [[:en:Edo neo-Confucianism|Edo neo-Confucianism]], which was introduced to Japan during the Edo period. One notable figure of this period, [[:en:Yamazaki Ansai|Yamazaki Ansai]], is said to have devised rituals for ''seishi'' based on Confucian ceremonies, and established his own ''seishi'' in 1671 at his residence in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]].<ref name=":3" />
After this, many scholars and practitioners of Shinto and the Hirata school of ''[[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]]'' ("national study") also established their own ''seishi'', each with their own unique rituals. These ''seishi'' were believed to be a means of obtaining longevity in life or becoming a god after death. The practice is said to have originated from the mythological story of the sun goddess [[Amaterasu]], who enshrined her own divine spirit at [[:en:Mount Miwa|Mount Miwa]].<ref name=":3" />
The study of ''seishi'' is particularly associated with Genchi Kato, who has identified 670 known ''seishi'', with the most recent being established in 1931 at the Nishi-Chogo Shrine in Higashikokura, [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]]. The ''seishi'' of [[:en:Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]] was established in 1876 in [[:en:Ishinomaki|Ishinomaki]], [[:en:Miyagi Prefecture|Miyagi Prefecture]], and again in 1893 at the Yahiko Shrine in [[:en:Tatsuno, Nagano|Tatsuno]], [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]]. The ''seishi'' of Emperor Meiji, [[:en:Empress Shōken|Empress Shōken]], [[:en:Emperor Taishō|Emperor Taisho]], and [[:en:Empress Teimei|Empress Teimei]] were also established in various locations, including Hokkaido, [[:en:Hiroshima|Hiroshima]], and [[:en:Himeji|Himeji]].<ref name=":3" />
== See also == * [[:en:Tamaya|Tamaya]] * {{ill|Okutsuki|ja|奥都城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okutsuki|qq=}} * [[:en:Hitogami|Hitogami]]

Byron Earhart |url-access=registration}}</ref> [[File:Hiraga_Gennai_seishi01s2040.jpg|thumb|200x200px|[[:en:Hiraga Gennai|Hiraga Gennai]]'s sheng shrine in [[:en:Hiroshima Prefecture|Hiroshima Prefecture]], [[:en:Fukuyama, Hiroshima|Fukuyama City]], Tomomachi.]]
== In China == When [[Han Dynasty#Western Han|Former Han]]'s Reunobu was the prime minister of [[:en:Yan Kingdom (Han dynasty)|Yan]], he built his shrine between Yan and [[:en:Qi (state)|Qi]] and called it the "Reun Gong Shrine". Also, when {{ill|Shiqing|zh|石慶|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiqing|qq=}} was the prime minister of Qi, the Qi people built the "Shiqing Shrine". This is the beginning of the birth shrine.<ref>[[:en:Zhao Yi|Zhao Yi]], "The New New Ancestral Hall", Volume 32, "Ancestral Hall", "This is the beginning of the establishment of an ancestral hall.</ref>
In the [[:en:Tang dynasty|Tang dynasty]], there were certain restrictions on the construction of monuments and shrines by officials in office. According to the [[:en:Tang Code|Tang Rulings and Ordinances]], those who erected shrines or monuments without permission were subject to a penalty of one year of imprisonment.<ref>The [[:en:Tang Code|Tang Code]], Volume 11 (The Law of Office)</ref> The Tang Dynasty called it "the difficulty of building monuments and shrines on the first day.
In the [[:en:Ming dynasty|Ming]], peasants in [[:en:Tengzhou|Tengzhou]] built a shrine to commemorate {{ill|Zhao Bangqing|zh|趙邦清|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhao Bangqing|qq=}}, who was appointed to Yanjing for his clean politics. The shrine was built to commemorate him.
During the reign of the [[:en:Tianqi Emperor|Tianqi Emperor]] of the Ming Dynasty, the eunuch [[:en:Wei Zhongxian|Wei Zhongxian]], ruled the world by conducting his own politics, and those who flattered him or feared his authority were given a sheng shrine from him. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing (1627), Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote: "Confucius wrote the Spring and Autumn, and Chung-Hsien wrote the Essentials. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote that Confucius had written the "Spring and Autumn" and Chung-Hsien had written the "Essentials. In April of the 7th year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, [[:en:Yuan Chonghuan|Yuan Chonghuan]] and {{ill|Yan Mingtai|zh|閻鳴泰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yan Mingtai|qq=}} of the [[:en:Ministry of War (imperial China)|Ministry of War]] made an appeal to the government to praise the merits and demerits of Wei Zhongxian and to establish a sheng shrine for him in both Qiantu.<ref>"Ming Xi Zong Jizhu": "in April of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianqi, the governor of Thistle Liaoning, Yan Mingtai, and the governor of Fuxing Yuan Chong Huan, praised the merits of Wei Zhongxian, and requested the construction of a shrine in front of Ning, giving the name Mao De." The government of the Liaoning Province has been inscribed in gold and stone, and there is no room for extra duties. The minister's single-mindedness in patrolling, strict warning of the battalion generals, do not dare to greed cowardly selfishness, do not dare to give away the hidden string, change the virtual into the real, into the real, easy timid into brave, to have today. Sustainability ancient internal minister who has the right!" ("Ming Xi Zong seven years of the Imperial Court of the Department of the Experiment" Tianqi six years in October)</ref> After that, Wei Zhongxian's raw shrine was "all over the world", "the cost of each shrine was at most hundreds of thousands, at least tens of thousands", "stolen the people's fortune, invaded the public safe, and was cut down. There are countless trees. " When Huang Yun Yasushi made a statue and greeted the statue, he said, "Goto Sansho," and "led the general of Bunbu, lined up under the stairs, and worshipped as the beginning."
[[:en:Wei Zhongxian|Wei Zhongxian]] has been strongly condemned in modern times for his behavior.{{by whom|date=March 2024}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |last=网易 |date=2020-12-14 |title=魏忠贤为自己生祠揭幕 |url=https://www.163.com/dy/article/FTRIN9CF0521Q1HR.html |access-date=2022-12-18 |website=www.163.com}}</ref>
[[:en:Gu Yanwu|Gu Yanwu]] lamented, "In this generation, there is no official who does not build a sheng shrine. And within a few years of leaving office, he would destroy the statues and change their masters.<ref>《Nichi Chi Roku》 vol. 23 (Sheng Shi), p. 644.</ref>
Recently{{When|date=December 2020}} a person{{whom|date=March 2024}} in China created their own Sheng shrine and stirred online controversy.<ref name=":0" />
== In Japan ==
The [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] (1603-1868) saw a rise in the practice of ''seishi'', or the establishment of one's own spiritual memorial site, where one's soul would be enshrined and worshipped after death. One notable example of this practice is that of [[:en:Matsudaira Sadanobu|Matsudaira Sadanobu]], who established his own ''seishi'' at Okuzawa Hachiman-gu in 1797.<ref name=":3">{{Cite web |title=「生祀は」の用例・例文集 - 用例.jp |url=http://yourei.jp/%E7%94%9F%E7%A5%80%E3%81%AF |access-date=2023-01-12 |website=yourei.jp}}</ref>
The rise in ''seishi'' is thought to have been influenced by Chinese philosophy, specifically [[:en:Edo neo-Confucianism|Edo neo-Confucianism]], which was introduced to Japan during the Edo period. One notable figure of this period, [[:en:Yamazaki Ansai|Yamazaki Ansai]], is said to have devised rituals for ''seishi'' based on Confucian ceremonies, and established his own ''seishi'' in 1671 at his residence in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]].<ref name=":3" />
After this, many scholars and practitioners of Shinto and the Hirata school of ''[[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]]'' ("national study") also established their own ''seishi'', each with their own unique rituals. These ''seishi'' were believed to be a means of obtaining longevity in life or becoming a god after death. The practice is said to have originated from the mythological story of the sun goddess [[Amaterasu]], who enshrined her own divine spirit at [[:en:Mount Miwa|Mount Miwa]].<ref name=":3" />
The study of ''seishi'' is particularly associated with Genchi Kato, who has identified 670 known ''seishi'', with the most recent being established in 1931 at the Nishi-Chogo Shrine in Higashikokura, [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]]. The ''seishi'' of [[:en:Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]] was established in 1876 in [[:en:Ishinomaki|Ishinomaki]], [[:en:Miyagi Prefecture|Miyagi Prefecture]], and again in 1893 at the Yahiko Shrine in [[:en:Tatsuno, Nagano|Tatsuno]], [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]]. The ''seishi'' of Emperor Meiji, [[:en:Empress Shōken|Empress Shōken]], [[:en:Emperor Taishō|Emperor Taisho]], and [[:en:Empress Teimei|Empress Teimei]] were also established in various locations, including Hokkaido, [[:en:Hiroshima|Hiroshima]], and [[:en:Himeji|Himeji]].<ref name=":3" />

'''Worship of the living''' ([[:en:Japanese language|Japanese]]: 生祀, ''seishi''; [[:en:Chinese language|Chinese]]: 生祠, ''shēngsì'') is the [[:en:Worship|Worship]] of living people practiced in the [[:en:East Asian cultural sphere|East Asian cultural sphere]]. In China, it is practiced at ''sheng'' shrines (Chinese: 生祠, shēng[[:en:Ci Shrine|cí]]).<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |date=2022-12-18 |title=为关心民生疾苦而设的生祠，在明代起着什么样的作用？_政治_祭祀_陈天祥 |website=[[Sohu]] |url=https://www.sohu.com/a/531809387_120005162 |access-date=2023-01-12 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221218062727/https://www.sohu.com/a/531809387_120005162 |archive-date=2022-12-18 }}</ref> There are two types of enshrinement: the enshrinement of the spirit of a living person who has made a significant contribution, and the enshrinement of one's own spirit.
Due to the usage by [[:en:Wei Zhongxian|Wei Zhongxian]] the practice became seen as a sign of corruption and declined in China.<ref name=":2">{{Cite web |title=生祠并非魏忠贤的专利-凤凰周刊 |url=http://www.ifengweekly.com/detil.php?id=16813 |access-date=2022-12-22 |website=www.ifengweekly.com|trans-title=Shengci is not Wei Zhongxian's invention}}</ref> In Japan, the practice was most prominent with the worship of the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] during the period of the imperial [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] from the late 19th century until 1945.<ref name="Earhart">{{cite book |last1=Earhart |first1=H. Byron |url=https://archive.org/details/religioninjapane0000earh |title=Religion in the Japanese experience: sources and interpretations |date=1974 |publisher=Dickenson Pub. Co. |isbn=0822101041 |edition=3rd |location=Encino, Calif. |author1-link=H. Byron Earhart |url-access=registration}}</ref> [[File:Hiraga_Gennai_seishi01s2040.jpg|thumb|200x200px|[[:en:Hiraga Gennai|Hiraga Gennai]]'s sheng shrine in [[:en:Hiroshima Prefecture|Hiroshima Prefecture]], [[:en:Fukuyama, Hiroshima|Fukuyama City]], Tomomachi.]]
== In China == When [[Han Dynasty#Western Han|Former Han]]'s Reunobu was the prime minister of [[:en:Yan Kingdom (Han dynasty)|Yan]], he built his shrine between Yan and [[:en:Qi (state)|Qi]] and called it the "Reun Gong Shrine". Also, when {{ill|Shiqing|zh|石慶|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiqing|qq=}} was the prime minister of Qi, the Qi people built the "Shiqing Shrine". This is the beginning of the birth shrine.<ref>[[:en:Zhao Yi|Zhao Yi]], "The New New Ancestral Hall", Volume 32, "Ancestral Hall", "This is the beginning of the establishment of an ancestral hall.</ref>

In the [[:en:Ming dynasty|Ming]], peasants in [[:en:Tengzhou|Tengzhou]] built a shrine to commemorate {{ill|Zhao Bangqing|zh|趙邦清|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhao Bangqing|qq=}}, who was appointed to Yanjing for his clean politics. The shrine was built to commemorate him.

In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing (1627), Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote: "Confucius wrote the Spring and Autumn, and Chung-Hsien wrote the Essentials. == In Japan ==
The [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] (1603-1868) saw a rise in the practice of ''seishi'', or the establishment of one's own spiritual memorial site, where one's soul would be enshrined and worshipped after death. One notable example of this practice is that of [[:en:Matsudaira Sadanobu|Matsudaira Sadanobu]], who established his own ''seishi'' at Okuzawa Hachiman-gu in 1797.<ref name=":3">{{Cite web |title=「生祀は」の用例・例文集 - 用例.jp |url=http://yourei.jp/%E7%94%9F%E7%A5%80%E3%81%AF |access-date=2023-01-12 |website=yourei.jp}}</ref>
The rise in ''seishi'' is thought to have been influenced by Chinese philosophy, specifically [[:en:Edo neo-Confucianism|Edo neo-Confucianism]], which was introduced to Japan during the Edo period. One notable figure of this period, [[:en:Yamazaki Ansai|Yamazaki Ansai]], is said to have devised rituals for ''seishi'' based on Confucian ceremonies, and established his own ''seishi'' in 1671 at his residence in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]].<ref name=":3" />
After this, many scholars and practitioners of Shinto and the Hirata school of ''[[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]]'' ("national study") also established their own ''seishi'', each with their own unique rituals. These ''seishi'' were believed to be a means of obtaining longevity in life or becoming a god after death. The practice is said to have originated from the mythological story of the sun goddess [[Amaterasu]], who enshrined her own divine spirit at [[:en:Mount Miwa|Mount Miwa]].<ref name=":3" />
The study of ''seishi'' is particularly associated with Genchi Kato, who has identified 670 known ''seishi'', with the most recent being established in 1931 at the Nishi-Chogo Shrine in Higashikokura, [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]]. The ''seishi'' of [[:en:Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]] was established in 1876 in [[:en:Ishinomaki|Ishinomaki]], [[:en:Miyagi Prefecture|Miyagi Prefecture]], and again in 1893 at the Yahiko Shrine in [[:en:Tatsuno, Nagano|Tatsuno]], [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]]. The ''seishi'' of Emperor Meiji, [[:en:Empress Shōken|Empress Shōken]], [[:en:Emperor Taishō|Emperor Taisho]], and [[:en:Empress Teimei|Empress Teimei]] were also established in various locations, including Hokkaido, [[:en:Hiroshima|Hiroshima]], and [[:en:Himeji|Himeji]].<ref name=":3" />

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Worship of the living]][[ja:生祀]][[simple:Worship of the living]][[zh:生祠]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Worship of the living]][[ja:生祀]][[simple:Worship of the living]][[zh:生祠]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Worship of the living]][[ja:生祀]][[simple:Worship of the living]][[zh:生祠]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q110226457]] Wikidata name: Sheng Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Worshipping living people in East Asian culture}}
'''Worship of the living''' ({{ill|Japanese language|en|Japanese language|WD=Q5287|lt=Japanese}}: 生祀, ''seishi''; {{ill|Chinese language|en|Chinese language|WD=Q7850|lt=Chinese}}: 生祠, ''shēngsì'') is the {{ill|Worship|en|Worship|WD=Q2514663|lt=Worship}} of living people practiced in the [[:en:East Asian cultural sphere|East Asian cultural sphere]]. In China, it is practiced at ''sheng'' shrines (Chinese: 生祠, shēng[[:en:Ci Shrine|cí]]).<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |date=2022-12-18 |title=为关心民生疾苦而设的生祠，在明代起着什么样的作用？_政治_祭祀_陈天祥 |website=[[Sohu]] |url=https://www.sohu.com/a/531809387_120005162 |access-date=2023-01-12 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221218062727/https://www.sohu.com/a/531809387_120005162 |archive-date=2022-12-18 }}</ref> There are two types of enshrinement: the enshrinement of the spirit of a living person who has made a significant contribution, and the enshrinement of one's own spirit.
Due to the usage by {{ill|Wei Zhongxian|en|Wei Zhongxian|WD=Q702101|lt=Wei Zhongxian}} the practice became seen as a sign of corruption and declined in China.<ref name=":2">{{Cite web |title=生祠并非魏忠贤的专利-凤凰周刊 |url=http://www.ifengweekly.com/detil.php?id=16813 |access-date=2022-12-22 |website=www.ifengweekly.com|trans-title=Shengci is not Wei Zhongxian's invention}}</ref> In Japan, the practice was most prominent with the worship of the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor}} during the period of the imperial {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto}} from the late 19th century until 1945.<ref name="Earhart">{{cite book |last1=Earhart |first1=H. Byron |url=https://archive.org/details/religioninjapane0000earh |title=Religion in the Japanese experience: sources and interpretations |date=1974 |publisher=Dickenson Pub. Co. |isbn=0822101041 |edition=3rd |location=Encino, Calif. |author1-link=H. Byron Earhart |url-access=registration}}</ref> [[File:Hiraga_Gennai_seishi01s2040.jpg|thumb|200x200px|{{ill|Hiraga Gennai|en|Hiraga Gennai|WD=Q968242|lt=Hiraga Gennai}}'s sheng shrine in {{ill|Hiroshima Prefecture|en|Hiroshima Prefecture|WD=Q617375|lt=Hiroshima Prefecture}}, {{ill|Fukuyama, Hiroshima|en|Fukuyama, Hiroshima|WD=Q661038|lt=Fukuyama City}}, Tomomachi.]]
== In China == When [[Han Dynasty#Western Han|Former Han]]'s Reunobu was the prime minister of {{ill|Yan Kingdom (Han dynasty)|en|Yan Kingdom (Han dynasty)|WD=Q11569258|lt=Yan}}, he built his shrine between Yan and {{ill|Qi (state)|en|Qi (state)|WD=Q837855|lt=Qi}} and called it the "Reun Gong Shrine". Also, when {{ill|Shiqing|zh|石慶|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiqing|qq=}} was the prime minister of Qi, the Qi people built the "Shiqing Shrine". This is the beginning of the birth shrine.<ref>{{ill|Zhao Yi|en|Zhao Yi|WD=Q4388724|lt=Zhao Yi}}, "The New New Ancestral Hall", Volume 32, "Ancestral Hall", "This is the beginning of the establishment of an ancestral hall.</ref>
In the {{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|WD=Q9683|lt=Tang dynasty}}, there were certain restrictions on the construction of monuments and shrines by officials in office. According to the {{ill|Tang Code|en|Tang Code|WD=Q1195751|lt=Tang Rulings and Ordinances}}, those who erected shrines or monuments without permission were subject to a penalty of one year of imprisonment.<ref>The {{ill|Tang Code|en|Tang Code|WD=Q1195751|lt=Tang Code}}, Volume 11 (The Law of Office)</ref> The Tang Dynasty called it "the difficulty of building monuments and shrines on the first day.
In the {{ill|Ming dynasty|en|Ming dynasty|WD=Q9903|lt=Ming}}, peasants in {{ill|Tengzhou|en|Tengzhou|WD=Q1207076|lt=Tengzhou}} built a shrine to commemorate {{ill|Zhao Bangqing|zh|趙邦清|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhao Bangqing|qq=}}, who was appointed to Yanjing for his clean politics. The shrine was built to commemorate him.
During the reign of the {{ill|Tianqi Emperor|en|Tianqi Emperor|WD=Q10066|lt=Tianqi Emperor}} of the Ming Dynasty, the eunuch {{ill|Wei Zhongxian|en|Wei Zhongxian|WD=Q702101|lt=Wei Zhongxian}}, ruled the world by conducting his own politics, and those who flattered him or feared his authority were given a sheng shrine from him. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing (1627), Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote: "Confucius wrote the Spring and Autumn, and Chung-Hsien wrote the Essentials. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote that Confucius had written the "Spring and Autumn" and Chung-Hsien had written the "Essentials. In April of the 7th year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, {{ill|Yuan Chonghuan|en|Yuan Chonghuan|WD=Q715553|lt=Yuan Chonghuan}} and {{ill|Yan Mingtai|zh|閻鳴泰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yan Mingtai|qq=}} of the {{ill|Ministry of War (imperial China)|en|Ministry of War (imperial China)|WD=Q718044|lt=Ministry of War}} made an appeal to the government to praise the merits and demerits of Wei Zhongxian and to establish a sheng shrine for him in both Qiantu.<ref>"Ming Xi Zong Jizhu": "in April of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianqi, the governor of Thistle Liaoning, Yan Mingtai, and the governor of Fuxing Yuan Chong Huan, praised the merits of Wei Zhongxian, and requested the construction of a shrine in front of Ning, giving the name Mao De." The government of the Liaoning Province has been inscribed in gold and stone, and there is no room for extra duties. The minister's single-mindedness in patrolling, strict warning of the battalion generals, do not dare to greed cowardly selfishness, do not dare to give away the hidden string, change the virtual into the real, into the real, easy timid into brave, to have today. Sustainability ancient internal minister who has the right!" ("Ming Xi Zong seven years of the Imperial Court of the Department of the Experiment" Tianqi six years in October)</ref> After that, Wei Zhongxian's raw shrine was "all over the world", "the cost of each shrine was at most hundreds of thousands, at least tens of thousands", "stolen the people's fortune, invaded the public safe, and was cut down. There are countless trees. " When Huang Yun Yasushi made a statue and greeted the statue, he said, "Goto Sansho," and "led the general of Bunbu, lined up under the stairs, and worshipped as the beginning."
{{ill|Wei Zhongxian|en|Wei Zhongxian|WD=Q702101|lt=Wei Zhongxian}} has been strongly condemned in modern times for his behavior.{{by whom|date=March 2024}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |last=网易 |date=2020-12-14 |title=魏忠贤为自己生祠揭幕 |url=https://www.163.com/dy/article/FTRIN9CF0521Q1HR.html |access-date=2022-12-18 |website=www.163.com}}</ref>
{{ill|Gu Yanwu|en|Gu Yanwu|WD=Q1195906|lt=Gu Yanwu}} lamented, "In this generation, there is no official who does not build a sheng shrine. And within a few years of leaving office, he would destroy the statues and change their masters.<ref>《Nichi Chi Roku》 vol. 23 (Sheng Shi), p. 644.</ref>
Recently{{When|date=December 2020}} a person{{whom|date=March 2024}} in China created their own Sheng shrine and stirred online controversy.<ref name=":0" />
== In Japan ==
The {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}} (1603-1868) saw a rise in the practice of ''seishi'', or the establishment of one's own spiritual memorial site, where one's soul would be enshrined and worshipped after death. One notable example of this practice is that of {{ill|Matsudaira Sadanobu|en|Matsudaira Sadanobu|WD=Q284038|lt=Matsudaira Sadanobu}}, who established his own ''seishi'' at Okuzawa Hachiman-gu in 1797.<ref name=":3">{{Cite web |title=「生祀は」の用例・例文集 - 用例.jp |url=http://yourei.jp/%E7%94%9F%E7%A5%80%E3%81%AF |access-date=2023-01-12 |website=yourei.jp}}</ref>
The rise in ''seishi'' is thought to have been influenced by Chinese philosophy, specifically {{ill|Edo neo-Confucianism|en|Edo neo-Confucianism|WD=Q5340141|lt=Edo neo-Confucianism}}, which was introduced to Japan during the Edo period. One notable figure of this period, {{ill|Yamazaki Ansai|en|Yamazaki Ansai|WD=Q6565636|lt=Yamazaki Ansai}}, is said to have devised rituals for ''seishi'' based on Confucian ceremonies, and established his own ''seishi'' in 1671 at his residence in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}.<ref name=":3" />
After this, many scholars and practitioners of Shinto and the Hirata school of ''{{ill|Kokugaku|en|Kokugaku|WD=Q855159|lt=Kokugaku}}'' ("national study") also established their own ''seishi'', each with their own unique rituals. These ''seishi'' were believed to be a means of obtaining longevity in life or becoming a god after death. The practice is said to have originated from the mythological story of the sun goddess [[Amaterasu]], who enshrined her own divine spirit at {{ill|Mount Miwa|en|Mount Miwa|WD=Q860627|lt=Mount Miwa}}.<ref name=":3" />
The study of ''seishi'' is particularly associated with Genchi Kato, who has identified 670 known ''seishi'', with the most recent being established in 1931 at the Nishi-Chogo Shrine in Higashikokura, [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]]. The ''seishi'' of {{ill|Emperor Meiji|en|Emperor Meiji|WD=Q174231|lt=Emperor Meiji}} was established in 1876 in {{ill|Ishinomaki|en|Ishinomaki|WD=Q270814|lt=Ishinomaki}}, {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|en|Miyagi Prefecture|WD=Q47896|lt=Miyagi Prefecture}}, and again in 1893 at the Yahiko Shrine in {{ill|Tatsuno, Nagano|en|Tatsuno, Nagano|WD=Q1347504|lt=Tatsuno}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|WD=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture}}. The ''seishi'' of Emperor Meiji, {{ill|Empress Shōken|en|Empress Shōken|WD=Q269520|lt=Empress Shōken}}, {{ill|Emperor Taishō|en|Emperor Taishō|WD=Q157052|lt=Emperor Taisho}}, and {{ill|Empress Teimei|en|Empress Teimei|WD=Q1197656|lt=Empress Teimei}} were also established in various locations, including Hokkaido, {{ill|Hiroshima|en|Hiroshima|WD=Q34664|lt=Hiroshima}}, and {{ill|Himeji|en|Himeji|WD=Q209801|lt=Himeji}}.<ref name=":3" />

These ''seishi'' were believed to be a means of obtaining longevity in life or becoming a god after death. The practice is said to have originated from the mythological story of the sun goddess [[Amaterasu]], who enshrined her own divine spirit at {{ill|Mount Miwa|en|Mount Miwa|WD=Q860627|lt=Mount Miwa}}.<ref name=":3" />
The study of ''seishi'' is particularly associated with Genchi Kato, who has identified 670 known ''seishi'', with the most recent being established in 1931 at the Nishi-Chogo Shrine in Higashikokura, [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]]. The ''seishi'' of {{ill|Emperor Meiji|en|Emperor Meiji|WD=Q174231|lt=Emperor Meiji}} was established in 1876 in {{ill|Ishinomaki|en|Ishinomaki|WD=Q270814|lt=Ishinomaki}}, {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|en|Miyagi Prefecture|WD=Q47896|lt=Miyagi Prefecture}}, and again in 1893 at the Yahiko Shrine in {{ill|Tatsuno, Nagano|en|Tatsuno, Nagano|WD=Q1347504|lt=Tatsuno}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|WD=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture}}. The ''seishi'' of Emperor Meiji, {{ill|Empress Shōken|en|Empress Shōken|WD=Q269520|lt=Empress Shōken}}, {{ill|Emperor Taishō|en|Emperor Taishō|WD=Q157052|lt=Emperor Taisho}}, and {{ill|Empress Teimei|en|Empress Teimei|WD=Q1197656|lt=Empress Teimei}} were also established in various locations, including Hokkaido, {{ill|Hiroshima|en|Hiroshima|WD=Q34664|lt=Hiroshima}}, and {{ill|Himeji|en|Himeji|WD=Q209801|lt=Himeji}}.<ref name=":3" />

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Worship of the living]][[ja:生祀]][[simple:Worship of the living]][[zh:生祠]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Worship of the living]][[ja:生祀]][[simple:Worship of the living]][[zh:生祠]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Worship of the living]][[ja:生祀]][[simple:Worship of the living]][[zh:生祠]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q110226457]] Wikidata name: Sheng Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Worshipping living people in East Asian culture}}
'''Worship of the living''' ({{ill|Japanese language|en|Japanese language|WD=Q5287|lt=Japanese}}: 生祀, ''seishi''; {{ill|Chinese language|en|Chinese language|WD=Q7850|lt=Chinese}}: 生祠, ''shēngsì'') is the {{ill|Worship|en|Worship|WD=Q2514663|lt=Worship}} of living people practiced in the [[:en:East Asian cultural sphere|East Asian cultural sphere]]. In China, it is practiced at ''sheng'' shrines (Chinese: 生祠, shēng[[:en:Ci Shrine|cí]]).<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |date=2022-12-18 |title=为关心民生疾苦而设的生祠，在明代起着什么样的作用？_政治_祭祀_陈天祥 |website=[[Sohu]] |url=https://www.sohu.com/a/531809387_120005162 |access-date=2023-01-12 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221218062727/https://www.sohu.com/a/531809387_120005162 |archive-date=2022-12-18 }}</ref> There are two types of enshrinement: the enshrinement of the spirit of a living person who has made a significant contribution, and the enshrinement of one's own spirit.
Due to the usage by {{ill|Wei Zhongxian|en|Wei Zhongxian|WD=Q702101|lt=Wei Zhongxian}} the practice became seen as a sign of corruption and declined in China.<ref name=":2">{{Cite web |title=生祠并非魏忠贤的专利-凤凰周刊 |url=http://www.ifengweekly.com/detil.php?id=16813 |access-date=2022-12-22 |website=www.ifengweekly.com|trans-title=Shengci is not Wei Zhongxian's invention}}</ref> In Japan, the practice was most prominent with the worship of the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor}} during the period of the imperial {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto}} from the late 19th century until 1945.<ref name="Earhart">{{cite book |last1=Earhart |first1=H. Byron |url=https://archive.org/details/religioninjapane0000earh |title=Religion in the Japanese experience: sources and interpretations |date=1974 |publisher=Dickenson Pub. Co. |isbn=0822101041 |edition=3rd |location=Encino, Calif. |author1-link=H. Byron Earhart |url-access=registration}}</ref> [[File:Hiraga_Gennai_seishi01s2040.jpg|thumb|200x200px|{{ill|Hiraga Gennai|en|Hiraga Gennai|WD=Q968242|lt=Hiraga Gennai}}'s sheng shrine in {{ill|Hiroshima Prefecture|en|Hiroshima Prefecture|WD=Q617375|lt=Hiroshima Prefecture}}, {{ill|Fukuyama, Hiroshima|en|Fukuyama, Hiroshima|WD=Q661038|lt=Fukuyama City}}, Tomomachi.]]
== In China == When [[Han Dynasty#Western Han|Former Han]]'s Reunobu was the prime minister of {{ill|Yan Kingdom (Han dynasty)|en|Yan Kingdom (Han dynasty)|WD=Q11569258|lt=Yan}}, he built his shrine between Yan and {{ill|Qi (state)|en|Qi (state)|WD=Q837855|lt=Qi}} and called it the "Reun Gong Shrine". Also, when {{ill|Shiqing|zh|石慶|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiqing|qq=}} was the prime minister of Qi, the Qi people built the "Shiqing Shrine". This is the beginning of the birth shrine.<ref>{{ill|Zhao Yi|en|Zhao Yi|WD=Q4388724|lt=Zhao Yi}}, "The New New Ancestral Hall", Volume 32, "Ancestral Hall", "This is the beginning of the establishment of an ancestral hall.</ref>
In the {{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|WD=Q9683|lt=Tang dynasty}}, there were certain restrictions on the construction of monuments and shrines by officials in office. According to the {{ill|Tang Code|en|Tang Code|WD=Q1195751|lt=Tang Rulings and Ordinances}}, those who erected shrines or monuments without permission were subject to a penalty of one year of imprisonment.<ref>The {{ill|Tang Code|en|Tang Code|WD=Q1195751|lt=Tang Code}}, Volume 11 (The Law of Office)</ref> The Tang Dynasty called it "the difficulty of building monuments and shrines on the first day.
In the {{ill|Ming dynasty|en|Ming dynasty|WD=Q9903|lt=Ming}}, peasants in {{ill|Tengzhou|en|Tengzhou|WD=Q1207076|lt=Tengzhou}} built a shrine to commemorate {{ill|Zhao Bangqing|zh|趙邦清|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhao Bangqing|qq=}}, who was appointed to Yanjing for his clean politics. The shrine was built to commemorate him.
During the reign of the {{ill|Tianqi Emperor|en|Tianqi Emperor|WD=Q10066|lt=Tianqi Emperor}} of the Ming Dynasty, the eunuch {{ill|Wei Zhongxian|en|Wei Zhongxian|WD=Q702101|lt=Wei Zhongxian}}, ruled the world by conducting his own politics, and those who flattered him or feared his authority were given a sheng shrine from him. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing (1627), Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote: "Confucius wrote the Spring and Autumn, and Chung-Hsien wrote the Essentials. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote that Confucius had written the "Spring and Autumn" and Chung-Hsien had written the "Essentials. In April of the 7th year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, {{ill|Yuan Chonghuan|en|Yuan Chonghuan|WD=Q715553|lt=Yuan Chonghuan}} and {{ill|Yan Mingtai|zh|閻鳴泰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yan Mingtai|qq=}} of the {{ill|Ministry of War (imperial China)|en|Ministry of War (imperial China)|WD=Q718044|lt=Ministry of War}} made an appeal to the government to praise the merits and demerits of Wei Zhongxian and to establish a sheng shrine for him in both Qiantu.<ref>"Ming Xi Zong Jizhu": "in April of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianqi, the governor of Thistle Liaoning, Yan Mingtai, and the governor of Fuxing Yuan Chong Huan, praised the merits of Wei Zhongxian, and requested the construction of a shrine in front of Ning, giving the name Mao De." The government of the Liaoning Province has been inscribed in gold and stone, and there is no room for extra duties. The minister's single-mindedness in patrolling, strict warning of the battalion generals, do not dare to greed cowardly selfishness, do not dare to give away the hidden string, change the virtual into the real, into the real, easy timid into brave, to have today. Sustainability ancient internal minister who has the right!" ("Ming Xi Zong seven years of the Imperial Court of the Department of the Experiment" Tianqi six years in October)</ref> After that, Wei Zhongxian's raw shrine was "all over the world", "the cost of each shrine was at most hundreds of thousands, at least tens of thousands", "stolen the people's fortune, invaded the public safe, and was cut down. There are countless trees. " When Huang Yun Yasushi made a statue and greeted the statue, he said, "Goto Sansho," and "led the general of Bunbu, lined up under the stairs, and worshipped as the beginning."
{{ill|Wei Zhongxian|en|Wei Zhongxian|WD=Q702101|lt=Wei Zhongxian}} has been strongly condemned in modern times for his behavior.{{by whom|date=March 2024}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |last=网易 |date=2020-12-14 |title=魏忠贤为自己生祠揭幕 |url=https://www.163.com/dy/article/FTRIN9CF0521Q1HR.html |access-date=2022-12-18 |website=www.163.com}}</ref>
{{ill|Gu Yanwu|en|Gu Yanwu|WD=Q1195906|lt=Gu Yanwu}} lamented, "In this generation, there is no official who does not build a sheng shrine. And within a few years of leaving office, he would destroy the statues and change their masters.<ref>《Nichi Chi Roku》 vol. 23 (Sheng Shi), p. 644.</ref>
Recently{{When|date=December 2020}} a person{{whom|date=March 2024}} in China created their own Sheng shrine and stirred online controversy.<ref name=":0" />
== In Japan ==
The {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}} (1603-1868) saw a rise in the practice of ''seishi'', or the establishment of one's own spiritual memorial site, where one's soul would be enshrined and worshipped after death. One notable example of this practice is that of {{ill|Matsudaira Sadanobu|en|Matsudaira Sadanobu|WD=Q284038|lt=Matsudaira Sadanobu}}, who established his own ''seishi'' at Okuzawa Hachiman-gu in 1797.<ref name=":3">{{Cite web |title=「生祀は」の用例・例文集 - 用例.jp |url=http://yourei.jp/%E7%94%9F%E7%A5%80%E3%81%AF |access-date=2023-01-12 |website=yourei.jp}}</ref>
The rise in ''seishi'' is thought to have been influenced by Chinese philosophy, specifically {{ill|Edo neo-Confucianism|en|Edo neo-Confucianism|WD=Q5340141|lt=Edo neo-Confucianism}}, which was introduced to Japan during the Edo period. One notable figure of this period, {{ill|Yamazaki Ansai|en|Yamazaki Ansai|WD=Q6565636|lt=Yamazaki Ansai}}, is said to have devised rituals for ''seishi'' based on Confucian ceremonies, and established his own ''seishi'' in 1671 at his residence in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}.<ref name=":3" />
After this, many scholars and practitioners of Shinto and the Hirata school of ''{{ill|Kokugaku|en|Kokugaku|WD=Q855159|lt=Kokugaku}}'' ("national study") also established their own ''seishi'', each with their own unique rituals. These ''seishi'' were believed to be a means of obtaining longevity in life or becoming a god after death. The practice is said to have originated from the mythological story of the sun goddess [[Amaterasu]], who enshrined her own divine spirit at {{ill|Mount Miwa|en|Mount Miwa|WD=Q860627|lt=Mount Miwa}}.<ref name=":3" />
The study of ''seishi'' is particularly associated with Genchi Kato, who has identified 670 known ''seishi'', with the most recent being established in 1931 at the Nishi-Chogo Shrine in Higashikokura, [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]]. The ''seishi'' of {{ill|Emperor Meiji|en|Emperor Meiji|WD=Q174231|lt=Emperor Meiji}} was established in 1876 in {{ill|Ishinomaki|en|Ishinomaki|WD=Q270814|lt=Ishinomaki}}, {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|en|Miyagi Prefecture|WD=Q47896|lt=Miyagi Prefecture}}, and again in 1893 at the Yahiko Shrine in {{ill|Tatsuno, Nagano|en|Tatsuno, Nagano|WD=Q1347504|lt=Tatsuno}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|WD=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture}}. The ''seishi'' of Emperor Meiji, {{ill|Empress Shōken|en|Empress Shōken|WD=Q269520|lt=Empress Shōken}}, {{ill|Emperor Taishō|en|Emperor Taishō|WD=Q157052|lt=Emperor Taisho}}, and {{ill|Empress Teimei|en|Empress Teimei|WD=Q1197656|lt=Empress Teimei}} were also established in various locations, including Hokkaido, {{ill|Hiroshima|en|Hiroshima|WD=Q34664|lt=Hiroshima}}, and {{ill|Himeji|en|Himeji|WD=Q209801|lt=Himeji}}.<ref name=":3" />

The study of ''seishi'' is particularly associated with Genchi Kato, who has identified 670 known ''seishi'', with the most recent being established in 1931 at the Nishi-Chogo Shrine in Higashikokura, [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]]. The ''seishi'' of {{ill|Emperor Meiji|en|Emperor Meiji|WD=Q174231|lt=Emperor Meiji}} was established in 1876 in {{ill|Ishinomaki|en|Ishinomaki|WD=Q270814|lt=Ishinomaki}}, {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|en|Miyagi Prefecture|WD=Q47896|lt=Miyagi Prefecture}}, and again in 1893 at the Yahiko Shrine in {{ill|Tatsuno, Nagano|en|Tatsuno, Nagano|WD=Q1347504|lt=Tatsuno}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|WD=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture}}. The ''seishi'' of Emperor Meiji, {{ill|Empress Shōken|en|Empress Shōken|WD=Q269520|lt=Empress Shōken}}, {{ill|Emperor Taishō|en|Emperor Taishō|WD=Q157052|lt=Emperor Taisho}}, and {{ill|Empress Teimei|en|Empress Teimei|WD=Q1197656|lt=Empress Teimei}} were also established in various locations, including Hokkaido, {{ill|Hiroshima|en|Hiroshima|WD=Q34664|lt=Hiroshima}}, and {{ill|Himeji|en|Himeji|WD=Q209801|lt=Himeji}}.<ref name=":3" />

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Worship of the living]][[ja:生祀]][[simple:Worship of the living]][[zh:生祠]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Worship of the living]][[ja:生祀]][[simple:Worship of the living]][[zh:生祠]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Worship of the living]][[ja:生祀]][[simple:Worship of the living]][[zh:生祠]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q110226457]] Wikidata name: Sheng Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Worshipping living people in East Asian culture}}
'''Worship of the living''' ({{ill|Japanese language|en|Japanese language|WD=Q5287|lt=Japanese}}: 生祀, ''seishi''; {{ill|Chinese language|en|Chinese language|WD=Q7850|lt=Chinese}}: 生祠, ''shēngsì'') is the {{ill|Worship|en|Worship|WD=Q2514663|lt=Worship}} of living people practiced in the [[:en:East Asian cultural sphere|East Asian cultural sphere]]. In China, it is practiced at ''sheng'' shrines (Chinese: 生祠, shēng[[:en:Ci Shrine|cí]]).<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |date=2022-12-18 |title=为关心民生疾苦而设的生祠，在明代起着什么样的作用？_政治_祭祀_陈天祥 |website=[[Sohu]] |url=https://www.sohu.com/a/531809387_120005162 |access-date=2023-01-12 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221218062727/https://www.sohu.com/a/531809387_120005162 |archive-date=2022-12-18 }}</ref> There are two types of enshrinement: the enshrinement of the spirit of a living person who has made a significant contribution, and the enshrinement of one's own spirit.
Due to the usage by {{ill|Wei Zhongxian|en|Wei Zhongxian|WD=Q702101|lt=Wei Zhongxian}} the practice became seen as a sign of corruption and declined in China.<ref name=":2">{{Cite web |title=生祠并非魏忠贤的专利-凤凰周刊 |url=http://www.ifengweekly.com/detil.php?id=16813 |access-date=2022-12-22 |website=www.ifengweekly.com|trans-title=Shengci is not Wei Zhongxian's invention}}</ref> In Japan, the practice was most prominent with the worship of the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor}} during the period of the imperial {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto}} from the late 19th century until 1945.<ref name="Earhart">{{cite book |last1=Earhart |first1=H. Byron |url=https://archive.org/details/religioninjapane0000earh |title=Religion in the Japanese experience: sources and interpretations |date=1974 |publisher=Dickenson Pub. Co. |isbn=0822101041 |edition=3rd |location=Encino, Calif. |author1-link=H. Byron Earhart |url-access=registration}}</ref> [[File:Hiraga_Gennai_seishi01s2040.jpg|thumb|200x200px|{{ill|Hiraga Gennai|en|Hiraga Gennai|WD=Q968242|lt=Hiraga Gennai}}'s sheng shrine in {{ill|Hiroshima Prefecture|en|Hiroshima Prefecture|WD=Q617375|lt=Hiroshima Prefecture}}, {{ill|Fukuyama, Hiroshima|en|Fukuyama, Hiroshima|WD=Q661038|lt=Fukuyama City}}, Tomomachi.]]
== In China == When [[Han Dynasty#Western Han|Former Han]]'s Reunobu was the prime minister of {{ill|Yan Kingdom (Han dynasty)|en|Yan Kingdom (Han dynasty)|WD=Q11569258|lt=Yan}}, he built his shrine between Yan and {{ill|Qi (state)|en|Qi (state)|WD=Q837855|lt=Qi}} and called it the "Reun Gong Shrine". Also, when {{ill|Shiqing|zh|石慶|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiqing|qq=}} was the prime minister of Qi, the Qi people built the "Shiqing Shrine". This is the beginning of the birth shrine.<ref>{{ill|Zhao Yi|en|Zhao Yi|WD=Q4388724|lt=Zhao Yi}}, "The New New Ancestral Hall", Volume 32, "Ancestral Hall", "This is the beginning of the establishment of an ancestral hall.</ref>
In the {{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|WD=Q9683|lt=Tang dynasty}}, there were certain restrictions on the construction of monuments and shrines by officials in office. According to the {{ill|Tang Code|en|Tang Code|WD=Q1195751|lt=Tang Rulings and Ordinances}}, those who erected shrines or monuments without permission were subject to a penalty of one year of imprisonment.<ref>The {{ill|Tang Code|en|Tang Code|WD=Q1195751|lt=Tang Code}}, Volume 11 (The Law of Office)</ref> The Tang Dynasty called it "the difficulty of building monuments and shrines on the first day.
In the {{ill|Ming dynasty|en|Ming dynasty|WD=Q9903|lt=Ming}}, peasants in {{ill|Tengzhou|en|Tengzhou|WD=Q1207076|lt=Tengzhou}} built a shrine to commemorate {{ill|Zhao Bangqing|zh|趙邦清|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhao Bangqing|qq=}}, who was appointed to Yanjing for his clean politics. The shrine was built to commemorate him.
During the reign of the {{ill|Tianqi Emperor|en|Tianqi Emperor|WD=Q10066|lt=Tianqi Emperor}} of the Ming Dynasty, the eunuch {{ill|Wei Zhongxian|en|Wei Zhongxian|WD=Q702101|lt=Wei Zhongxian}}, ruled the world by conducting his own politics, and those who flattered him or feared his authority were given a sheng shrine from him. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing (1627), Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote: "Confucius wrote the Spring and Autumn, and Chung-Hsien wrote the Essentials. In May of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, Lu Wanling, the prefect of the State Council, wrote that Confucius had written the "Spring and Autumn" and Chung-Hsien had written the "Essentials. In April of the 7th year of the reign of Emperor Tianjing, {{ill|Yuan Chonghuan|en|Yuan Chonghuan|WD=Q715553|lt=Yuan Chonghuan}} and {{ill|Yan Mingtai|zh|閻鳴泰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yan Mingtai|qq=}} of the {{ill|Ministry of War (imperial China)|en|Ministry of War (imperial China)|WD=Q718044|lt=Ministry of War}} made an appeal to the government to praise the merits and demerits of Wei Zhongxian and to establish a sheng shrine for him in both Qiantu.<ref>"Ming Xi Zong Jizhu": "in April of the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Tianqi, the governor of Thistle Liaoning, Yan Mingtai, and the governor of Fuxing Yuan Chong Huan, praised the merits of Wei Zhongxian, and requested the construction of a shrine in front of Ning, giving the name Mao De." The government of the Liaoning Province has been inscribed in gold and stone, and there is no room for extra duties. The minister's single-mindedness in patrolling, strict warning of the battalion generals, do not dare to greed cowardly selfishness, do not dare to give away the hidden string, change the virtual into the real, into the real, easy timid into brave, to have today. Sustainability ancient internal minister who has the right!" ("Ming Xi Zong seven years of the Imperial Court of the Department of the Experiment" Tianqi six years in October)</ref> After that, Wei Zhongxian's raw shrine was "all over the world", "the cost of each shrine was at most hundreds of thousands, at least tens of thousands", "stolen the people's fortune, invaded the public safe, and was cut down. There are countless trees. " When Huang Yun Yasushi made a statue and greeted the statue, he said, "Goto Sansho," and "led the general of Bunbu, lined up under the stairs, and worshipped as the beginning."
{{ill|Wei Zhongxian|en|Wei Zhongxian|WD=Q702101|lt=Wei Zhongxian}} has been strongly condemned in modern times for his behavior.{{by whom|date=March 2024}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |last=网易 |date=2020-12-14 |title=魏忠贤为自己生祠揭幕 |url=https://www.163.com/dy/article/FTRIN9CF0521Q1HR.html |access-date=2022-12-18 |website=www.163.com}}</ref>
{{ill|Gu Yanwu|en|Gu Yanwu|WD=Q1195906|lt=Gu Yanwu}} lamented, "In this generation, there is no official who does not build a sheng shrine. And within a few years of leaving office, he would destroy the statues and change their masters.<ref>《Nichi Chi Roku》 vol. 23 (Sheng Shi), p. 644.</ref>
Recently{{When|date=December 2020}} a person{{whom|date=March 2024}} in China created their own Sheng shrine and stirred online controversy.<ref name=":0" />
== In Japan ==
The {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}} (1603-1868) saw a rise in the practice of ''seishi'', or the establishment of one's own spiritual memorial site, where one's soul would be enshrined and worshipped after death. One notable example of this practice is that of {{ill|Matsudaira Sadanobu|en|Matsudaira Sadanobu|WD=Q284038|lt=Matsudaira Sadanobu}}, who established his own ''seishi'' at Okuzawa Hachiman-gu in 1797.<ref name=":3">{{Cite web |title=「生祀は」の用例・例文集 - 用例.jp |url=http://yourei.jp/%E7%94%9F%E7%A5%80%E3%81%AF |access-date=2023-01-12 |website=yourei.jp}}</ref>
The rise in ''seishi'' is thought to have been influenced by Chinese philosophy, specifically {{ill|Edo neo-Confucianism|en|Edo neo-Confucianism|WD=Q5340141|lt=Edo neo-Confucianism}}, which was introduced to Japan during the Edo period. One notable figure of this period, {{ill|Yamazaki Ansai|en|Yamazaki Ansai|WD=Q6565636|lt=Yamazaki Ansai}}, is said to have devised rituals for ''seishi'' based on Confucian ceremonies, and established his own ''seishi'' in 1671 at his residence in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}.<ref name=":3" />
After this, many scholars and practitioners of Shinto and the Hirata school of ''{{ill|Kokugaku|en|Kokugaku|WD=Q855159|lt=Kokugaku}}'' ("national study") also established their own ''seishi'', each with their own unique rituals. These ''seishi'' were believed to be a means of obtaining longevity in life or becoming a god after death. The practice is said to have originated from the mythological story of the sun goddess [[Amaterasu]], who enshrined her own divine spirit at {{ill|Mount Miwa|en|Mount Miwa|WD=Q860627|lt=Mount Miwa}}.<ref name=":3" />
The study of ''seishi'' is particularly associated with Genchi Kato, who has identified 670 known ''seishi'', with the most recent being established in 1931 at the Nishi-Chogo Shrine in Higashikokura, [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]]. The ''seishi'' of {{ill|Emperor Meiji|en|Emperor Meiji|WD=Q174231|lt=Emperor Meiji}} was established in 1876 in {{ill|Ishinomaki|en|Ishinomaki|WD=Q270814|lt=Ishinomaki}}, {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|en|Miyagi Prefecture|WD=Q47896|lt=Miyagi Prefecture}}, and again in 1893 at the Yahiko Shrine in {{ill|Tatsuno, Nagano|en|Tatsuno, Nagano|WD=Q1347504|lt=Tatsuno}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|WD=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture}}. The ''seishi'' of Emperor Meiji, {{ill|Empress Shōken|en|Empress Shōken|WD=Q269520|lt=Empress Shōken}}, {{ill|Emperor Taishō|en|Emperor Taishō|WD=Q157052|lt=Emperor Taisho}}, and {{ill|Empress Teimei|en|Empress Teimei|WD=Q1197656|lt=Empress Teimei}} were also established in various locations, including Hokkaido, {{ill|Hiroshima|en|Hiroshima|WD=Q34664|lt=Hiroshima}}, and {{ill|Himeji|en|Himeji|WD=Q209801|lt=Himeji}}.<ref name=":3" />

The study of ''seishi'' is particularly associated with Genchi Kato, who has identified 670 known ''seishi'', with the most recent being established in 1931 at the Nishi-Chogo Shrine in Higashikokura, [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]]. The ''seishi'' of {{ill|Emperor Meiji|en|Emperor Meiji|WD=Q174231|lt=Emperor Meiji}} was established in 1876 in {{ill|Ishinomaki|en|Ishinomaki|WD=Q270814|lt=Ishinomaki}}, {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|en|Miyagi Prefecture|WD=Q47896|lt=Miyagi Prefecture}}, and again in 1893 at the Yahiko Shrine in {{ill|Tatsuno, Nagano|en|Tatsuno, Nagano|WD=Q1347504|lt=Tatsuno}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|WD=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture}}. The ''seishi'' of Emperor Meiji, {{ill|Empress Shōken|en|Empress Shōken|WD=Q269520|lt=Empress Shōken}}, {{ill|Emperor Taishō|en|Emperor Taishō|WD=Q157052|lt=Emperor Taisho}}, and {{ill|Empress Teimei|en|Empress Teimei|WD=Q1197656|lt=Empress Teimei}} were also established in various locations, including Hokkaido, {{ill|Hiroshima|en|Hiroshima|WD=Q34664|lt=Hiroshima}}, and {{ill|Himeji|en|Himeji|WD=Q209801|lt=Himeji}}.<ref name=":3" />
